action: Reframe the funnel as a sampler-geometry problem: note that non-centered reparameterization is equivalent to Riemannian/second-order HMC with a specific non-constant mass matter, and treat reparameterization (not prior surgery) as the fix
discriminates: H3 (geometry/sampler fix via reparameterization or second-order info) vs H2 (change the model's prior)
expected_findings: Under H3: changing the parameterization (or adding second-order curvature info) removes divergences while leaving the posterior unchanged — confirmed by the clean non-centered baseline. Under H2 the model itself would have to change. The principled fix is to keep the model and fix the geometry.

action: Localize the divergence source: separate kernel-hyperparameter non-identifiability from geometry introduced by the approximation, by toggling which hyperparameters are estimated and comparing against exact / alternative models.
discriminates: Length-scale/output-scale funnel vs. approximation-induced bad geometry vs. likelihood-side issues.
expected_findings: Estimating BOTH length scale and covariance(output) scale gives the most divergences (~100+); the two are non-identified and produce funnel-like changing curvature. Fixing the output scale (estimate only the length scale) cuts but does NOT eliminate divergences (~40-50 remain). The same data fit with an exact full GP (Wahba/Yuedong spline form) or with RFF shows ZERO divergences (at far higher runtime: 5-7h exact, ~1h RFF k=80, vs minutes for the approx) — so the residual divergences are a property of THIS approximation's posterior geometry, not the likelihood.

action: Try a dense (full) mass matrix as an alternative to reparameterization, isolating 'metric can't capture the correlation' from 'geometry is intrinsically curved.'
discriminates: linear-correlation-fixable-by-a-better-metric (H4) vs intrinsic-nonlinear-funnel-curvature-no-constant-metric-can-fix (H1/H2)
expected_findings: H4: a dense metric removes the pathology (the bad geometry was just a fixed linear correlation). H1/H2: a dense metric does NOT help because the curvature varies across the posterior (a global constant metric cannot adapt to a funnel). Observed: dense metric still diverges -> rules out H4, confirms intrinsic varying curvature -> reinforces the regime-split resolution.

action: Generate the brms Stan data + Stan code (make_standata / make_stancode) and read the model block; cross-check by refitting via rstan
discriminates: H4: pins the exact decomposition, dimensions, prior assignment, and the linear-vs-penalized split that pure notation-guessing cannot settle
expected_findings: mu = Xs*bs + Zs_1_1*s_1_1: Xs (N x Ks) is the unpenalized linear design with coefficient bs (the 'sx_1' / beta); Zs_1_1 (N x knots) is the penalized basis matrix; s_1_1 = sds_1_1 * zs_1_1 is the non-centered penalized coefficient vector with zs_1_1 ~ std_normal; sds_1_1 (= sigma_tau) is the smooth SD. brms's DEFAULT prior on sds is student_t(3,0,2.5), not the user's stated normal(0,2) unless overridden. Raw x does not appear in the data — mgcv pre-builds Xs and Zs.

action: Test the zero-avoiding-prior remedy empirically: sweep the strength/location of the boundary-avoiding prior on sigma and tabulate divergence counts AND the resulting posterior for tau (the scale)
discriminates: H2 (zero-avoiding prior fixes the geometry cheaply) vs the rival that the prior only removes divergences by paying an unacceptable price in the posterior
expected_findings: Under H2: a mild zero-avoiding prior should drop divergences to ~0 while leaving the tau posterior roughly where the weak-prior baseline puts it. Observed instead: divergences only vanish for extreme priors (prior median pushed to ~50-150+, i.e. mean 5-7), and exactly those priors massively shift the tau posterior to the right (50% for tau climbing from ~1.2 up to 15-40). Mild zaps (mean 0-2) still throw 50-500 divergences. So H2 fails: you cannot exclude enough mass near zero to fix the funnel without grossly distorting the inference.

action: Reparameterize: move the removed column-mean content out of the basis and into the intercept's prior so that the marginal prior is preserved while the parameters are decorrelated.

This restores the correct implied prior, but couples the intercept's location to the basis weights, introducing nasty prior dependencies that may or may not propagate to the posterior depending on model+data; not generally recommended.

action: Re-attribute the divergences to a different geometry pathology and scope it to its own investigation rather than forcing the NCP tool.

action: Inspect sampler adaptation: get_adaptation_info(fit) — read step size and inverse-metric (unconstrained variance) elements and their spread across params and chains

action: Constrain the random initialization range — set init_r below its default of 2 — to keep starting points out of the pathological tails; START with this lever before hand-specifying per-parameter inits

action: Audit the likelihood for an off-by-one in the censoring index and for numerical stability of the log-sum-exp.

branches.if_observe: Anomaly persists after the index/stability fix

action: Identify the model as hierarchical and attribute the curvature to the centered/non-centered scale-vs-intercept funnel, not to the marginal prior shapes

If the priors really were trivially well-conditioned: no parametrization sensitivity would exist (contradicted by the hierarchical structure).

action: Diagnose WHERE the funnel lives: distinguish a subject<->population funnel (reparameterizable) from a funnel between population-level hyperparameters (not reparameterizable).
discriminates: Whether ANY local centering/non-centering can ever help, vs whether the bad geometry is intrinsic to the population block.
expected_findings: Subject-population funnel: some centered/non-centered blend removes divergences. Hyperparameter funnel: no parameterization helps; divergences and a specific population-spread parameter (e.g. population-SD of one partition coefficient) stay pathological.

action: Perturb the sampler controls (max_treedepth, adapt_delta) and watch whether 'cleanness' and the treedepth/step-size adaptation are stable.
discriminates: Whether treedepth exceedance reflects a fixable tuning issue vs an intrinsic race-to-infinity; tests H4 (adaptation luck) against H1/H2.
expected_findings: H4/H1: 'clean' is random; raising adapt_delta lowers step size and INCREASES treedepth exceedances (3762 at max_treedepth 15) and surfaces Rhat=1.21 + tail/bulk ESS. Stable-fix world: raising treedepth should monotonically clean things up.

action: Reason carefully about the change-of-variables: is there a Jacobian, is it constant, and is the map a bijection between the (K-1)-dim unconstrained space and the K-dim zero-sum subspace?
discriminates: H3 (Jacobian mishandling is the cause) vs H2 (Jacobian is a constant and irrelevant; bug is elsewhere, in the prior centering) — and a pedagogical sub-issue about how priors push forward through a constrained type
expected_findings: Under H3-as-cause: a missing non-constant adjustment would distort posteriors. Expected actual finding: the transform IS a bijection between (K-1)-dim unconstrained vectors and the K-dim sum-to-zero subspace; its Jacobian is constant (drops out as an additive log-constant), analogous to simplex/correlation-matrix constrained types. So the Jacobian is NOT the source of the discrepancy — the syntactic prior simply doesn't push forward to independent normals on the K elements, which is a *prior-specification* subtlety, not a missing adjustment.
situations: Want to give a prior (e.g. Dirichlet) to a simplex via an unconstrained vector mapped through softmax, adding a Jacobian correction by hand (target += log_deter
An additive/varying-intercept regression (alpha_k per group, plus a global intercept mu) is non-identified in the likelihood because mu and the alpha levels tra
leaves: Betancourt: 'one-to-many' still presumes equal input/output dimension; a Jacobian determinant transforming densities is NOT well-defined for maps between spaces of different dimension. Must either map

Jacobian is non-square only if you mis-frame the map; correct framing is unconstrained^(K-1) <-> zero-sum^K, giving a square, constant Jacobian. Caveat (Socolar): constrained types are arrays-plus-con

branches.if_observe: Jacobian is constant / bijection holds
branches.then: rule out H3 as the cause of the posterior discrepancy; redirect the audit to the prior's location/centering (H2)

action: Reparameterize: split the count matrix into condition-A and condition-B sub-matrices, estimate a per-row baseline mu and phi for A, and model B as mu*delta with a truncated mixture over delta<1 vs delta>1; build up complexity incrementally with shared priors across rows

Final per-row effect can be summarized as pi*delta1 + (1-pi)*delta0

action: Read the posterior to assess identifiability/flexibility, and recommend posterior-predictive / retrodictive checks as the arbiter of structure choices.

PPC/retrodictive checks (Visualisation paper / principled-Bayesian-workflow case study) are the right tool to decide whether structure is adequate.

action: Reframe persistent slowness as a diagnostic signal, not something to wait out; prescribe short cheap runs and posterior inspection of parameter correlations.

expected_findings: Unusually slow Stan runs usually flag model weirdness (non-identifiability, bad geometry); ~100 iterations suffice to tell if a model is working; step-size collapse means parameterization, not coding efficiency. Pairs/ShinyStan should reveal which parameters are super-correlated.

A brms GAM-style spline regression (s(age, by=congruent) + main effect + (1|id), bernoulli) on a very large dataset (~15k subjects, ~10-20 trials/level) is pain
leaves: Tell: high max_treedepth=15 + adapt_delta=0.95 are usually compensating for divergences. 'Models that have issues are usually very slow.' Rule out the model before clusters/GPUs.
Run ~100-300 iterations; inspect pairplots / ShinyStan for highly correlated parameters; if step size is crashing, stop and fix parameterization rather than running longer.

branches.if_observe: Pairplots show strong ridges among interaction-related betas / intercept
branches.then: Attribute to predictor collinearity; decorrelate (QR) or simplify the design

branches.if_observe: Geometry looks clean but a parameter is flat/unidentified
branches.then: Tighten priors or drop/merge that term

action: Correct the definition of R-hat: it compares within-chain to between-chain variance (mixing), i.e. (1) is first-half distribution == second-half, (2) do chains from different starts converge to the same distribution — autocorrelation is more tied to ESS than to R-hat
discriminates: H4 — whether the dismissal rests on a category error; a very high R-hat is NOT removed by 'collapsing over iterations' and signals chains disagreeing, not benign autocorrelation
expected_findings: H4 confirmed: 'collapsing over iterations puts samples back in the posterior range' is false when chains aren't sampling the same post-warmup distribution; high R-hat means non-stationary/disagreeing chains, a genuine problem.
situations: Nested R-hat (posterior::rhat_nested) comes out systematically lower than ordinary R-hat (posterior::rhat) across all chain counts (2/4/8/16/32), even when each
A published Bayesian model (mathematical-psychology, fit in JAGS/Stan/brms) shows a clean-looking "parameter recovery" plot, yet when re-fit it produces high R-
leaves: Vehtari: 'ESS=4368 with S=24000 indicates high autocorrelation in each chain dominating loss of efficiency'; 1.011 is 0.1% away, no meaningful effect; nested-Rhat-based ESS does not yet exist because
Cite Gelman's two questions R-hat was built to answer; note ESS (not R-hat) is the autocorrelation-tied diagnostic. Prefer the term 'mixing' over 'convergence'.

branches.if_observe: R-hat high AND persists after 50k iterations
branches.then: rule out 'just needs more samples' -> something structural (multimodality/non-identifiability/bad geometry), escalate to model inspection

branches.if_observe: R-hat near 1 but ESS low
branches.then: that is the autocorrelation regime — more iterations may suffice; do not conflate with the high-R-hat case

action: Run algorithmic-faithfulness checks at the right scope — self-consistency diagnostics on the single fit, and SBC (simulation-based algorithmic calibration) across an ensemble of prior-predictive datasets
discriminates: H1 vs H2 — distinguishes computational corruption (SBC rank non-uniformity / divergences / high R-hat) from a faithfully-computed but weakly-identified posterior (clean diagnostics, but wide/multimodal posterior)
expected_findings: H1: SBC ranks non-uniform and/or divergences present -> computation corrupt; any recovery or retrodictive check downstream is meaningless because comparing corrupted posteriors to truth doesn't constrain true-posterior behavior. H2: SBC ranks uniform, no divergences, low R-hat -> computation trustworthy, so a wide posterior is honest non-identifiability, not a bug.
situations: Running Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC) on a Bayesian model, but the realistic/weak prior produces prior-predictive datasets that cause fitting failures (div
A published Bayesian model (mathematical-psychology, fit in JAGS/Stan/brms) shows a clean-looking "parameter recovery" plot, yet when re-fit it produces high R-
leaves: SBC = Simulation-Based (Algorithmic) Calibration: simulate params from prior, data from params, refit, check rank uniformity over the ensemble. Also catches some coding errors. Per Betancourt, diverge
Betancourt: each statistical-computation benchmark is a specific posterior with validated expectations, NOT a generic model; SBC consistency usually requires fitting all prior-predictive posteriors

branches.if_observe: SBC ranks non-uniform OR divergences present
branches.then: computation untrustworthy -> STOP; do not interpret recovery or posterior; fix sampler/model first

branches.if_observe: SBC uniform, no divergences, low R-hat
branches.then: computation trusted -> NOW parameter recovery / inferential calibration becomes well-defined and worth doing

action: Start from a uniform LKJ(eta=1) Cholesky (gives a PSD matrix uniform over valid matrices) then ADD normal density on the off-diagonal correlation elements toward a target, defining a joint prior p(R)=LKJ(R|1)*prod_u N(R_u|mu_u,sigma_u)
discriminates: whether you can keep PSD + assembled matrix + tunable per-element prior (defeating the identity pull) in one simple model
expected_findings: gives basically identical estimates to the spherical 'nearest-PD' approach but is simpler/more intuitive; lets you encode a-priori info on specific correlations; equivalent whether you write target_cor ~ N(estimated_cor,s) or estimated_cor ~ N(target_cor,s)
situations: Want a hierarchical / partial-pooling prior over a correlation (or covariance) matrix in Stan — group-specific correlation matrices that share a common componen
A standard multivariate-normal model with a cholesky_factor_corr / LKJ parameterization (multi_normal_cholesky + lkj_corr_cholesky) is fit to data whose true co
leaves: LKJ-cholesky(1) for the PSD scaffold; get_lower_tri(cormat) ~ normal(cors, corpriorscale) adds density to elements away from identity; this is the converged recommended pattern

Charles Driver: 'LKJ really doesn't like high correlations... not flat, some complex notion of uniform on a strange space.' Mike: variants with no prior on correlations show the same pathology. Socola

action: Read out the actual E-BFMI value (not just the boolean warning) and validate inference against ground truth via simulation; add/inspect priors for identifiability
discriminates: genuine non-identifiability / heavy-tailed energy vs benign near-threshold false positive
expected_findings: If false positive: numeric E-BFMI sits just under 0.3 (e.g. 0.24-0.34) AND posterior recovers the simulated truth -> safe to proceed. If genuine: estimates biased / heavy tails persist -> need tighter priors or reparameterization.
situations: Fitting a hierarchical normal model in Stan/HMC; get "estimated Bayesian Fraction of Missing Information was low" (E-BFMI low) warning on all chains; pairs plot
A Bayesian "animal model" / hierarchical GLMM with a KNOWN between-individual covariance (relationship/pedigree matrix A) and an UNKNOWN between-trait covarianc
leaves: Betancourt: warning threshold 0.3 is conservative and likely too high; compute the numeric E-BFMI — if near 0.3 you are probably fine. Validate with the simulate-from-prior -> simulate-data -> refit a
betanalpha stan_utility.R check_energy(); E-BFMI(E-FMI) quantifies how well momentum resampling explores energy sets; low -> possible biased exploration

branches.if_observe: E-BFMI 0.24-0.34 and all estimates consistent with simulated values
branches.then: declare false positive in this rare high-heritability regime; proceed

branches.if_observe: estimates inconsistent or E-BFMI far below 0.3
branches.then: add informative priors / reparameterize variance partition to identify the model

action: Apply the order-of-operations rule for nonlinear summaries of posterior draws.
discriminates: H5 — transform-then-average vs average-then-transform (Jensen's inequality).
expected_findings: exp(mean(beta)) != mean(exp(beta)) because exp is convex; the correct summary takes the mean LAST: compute the quantity of interest per draw, then summarize. exp(coef(post)) (point/mean estimate exponentiated) is the wrong, frequentist-flavored shortcut. Same rule governs RR: compute Pr_1/Pr_0 per draw, then summarize, never ratio the posterior-mean probabilities.
situations: A (zero-inflated) beta regression fitted in brms runs fine, but the user is confused about: which beta parameterization brms uses (mu/phi vs a/b shape), whether
Binomial/Bernoulli regression fit with a LOG link to directly estimate a relative risk (RR = exp(beta)). Sampler keeps hitting bad/invalid initializations becau
leaves: Goodrich: 'Always take the mean last (irrespective of what function produced the posterior draws).' mean(exp(as.data.frame(post)$treat)) is right; exp(mean(...)) or exp(coef(post)['treat']) are 'horri

Work on individual draws; summarize last. Helper functions posterior_linpred (link scale), posterior_epred (expected response scale), prepare_predictions automate this and avoid the manual-summary tra

branches.if_observe: Bayesian estimate must match the frequentist exp(coef) point estimate
branches.then: It should not match in general; the discrepancy is expected and the draw-wise mean is the correct posterior summary.

action: Simulate a dataset from the model with known/recovered parameters and refit; check whether divergences reproduce and whether generative parameters are recovered.
discriminates: data-driven pathology (poor fit / a few bad real observations) vs intrinsic model-specification/geometry pathology
expected_findings: If misspecification-vs-data H: synthetic data samples cleanly and recovers params -> problem is the real data. If degeneracy/geometry H: synthetic data STILL diverges -> problem is in the model specification itself.
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model (interval-censored insect development times; temperature-dependent rate curve with an iid latent lack-of-fit term mapped throu
A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: charlesm93: fixing one/two params or population variance 'greatly simplifies the problem' and gives reasonable results; the full model still diverges. Funko_Unko adopts synthetic data drawn from the p
Generate synthetic data from the assumed generative process (curve + lognormal individual variation + Cauchy lack-of-fit), run the same sampler, inspect divergences and parameter recovery.

branches.if_observe: synthetic data still diverges
branches.then: rule out 'bad real data'; treat as model-specification/geometry problem and move to localizing the degeneracy among upsilon

branches.if_observe: synthetic data is clean and recovers parameters
branches.then: suspect specific real observations / lack of fit; examine which data points / temperature combinations drive the divergences

action: Reframe via the folk theorem: treat the slowdown as a geometry/identifiability symptom, and isolate by building up complexity one layer at a time from the known-good single-subject model
discriminates: whether the slowdown is intrinsic compute (ODE cost scaling) vs a hierarchy-induced posterior pathology
expected_findings: If raw compute, time scales ~linearly with #subjects; the 200x jump for 10 subjects instead points to a pathology (folk theorem). Single-subject fits fast and clean, confirming the ODE+likelihood core is sound and the problem entered with the hierarchy.
situations: A brms/make_stan-generated hierarchical regression (correlated random slopes per group, non-centered Cholesky parameterization) on ~26-30k sales observations ru
A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje
leaves: Slow HMC iterations indicate model is misspecified for the data (Andrew's Folk Theorem). Suspects: many zeros in regressors, bathtub-shaped covariates, non-stationary time series, mismatched informati

Andrew Gelman folk theorem: slow sampling usually means a mis-specified / non-identifiable model. Add one layer at a time; keep the single-subject model as the fast reference for all later tests (test

branches.if_observe: time roughly linear in #subjects, diagnostics clean
branches.then: genuine compute -> pursue parallelism/solver-tolerance moves

branches.if_observe: super-linear blow-up and/or warmup-dominated slowness
branches.then: pursue geometry: bounds, parametrization, discontinuity, identifiability

action: Diagnose identifiability with pairs plots at BOTH global and subject level; probe parameter redundancy by perturbing the ODE and watching state trajectories; consider reparametrizing the ODE to break the v-kappa ridge
discriminates: structural non-identifiability (different params -> nearly identical trajectories -> likelihood ridge) vs a fittable but awkward funnel
expected_findings: If increasing v and kappa together yields nearly identical state trajectories, the likelihood has a ridge -> correlated posterior -> slow exploration; a trajectory-based reparametrization removes it. Clean global pairs + dirty subject-level pairs localize the pathology to the per-fish layer. Use ESS/second and average treedepth (nuts_params), not wall-time, as the speed metric.
situations: Hierarchical multinomial/softmax regression (categorical outcome over parties x districts x dates, with a zero-inflation mask for unavailable categories): NUTS
A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje
leaves: Plot global+local params together; play with the ODE to see which parameter combinations leave trajectories invariant (those are the non-identified directions). v vs kappa correlation suggests a repar
Inspect the softmax->mask->renormalize pipeline; note the linear predictor feeds softmax (already simplex-valued)

branches.if_observe: very different (v,kappa) give near-identical trajectories
branches.then: reparametrize ODE to a identified combination

branches.if_observe: trajectories differ, ridge is mild
branches.then: it is a funnel -> adaptive centeredness move

action: Reframe the change-of-variables to transform the positive_ordered vector to the FIRST K-1 simplex elements only (the last = 1 - sum has constant Jacobian and can be dropped), then use the analytic (K-1)x(K-1) log-determinant
discriminates: H4 (softmax route fixable with reduced Jacobian) vs the earlier failing full-KxK softmax attempt (H2 manifestation)
expected_findings: With K-1 params, a pinned 0, and the reduced log-det Jacobian target += sum(y) - K*log_sum_exp(append_row(0,y)), SBC passes. Confirms the earlier softmax failure was exactly the rank-deficient full KxK Jacobian (det 0). Slightly slower / worse geometry than the order-statistic construction.
situations: A custom Stan transform maps an unconstrained / positive_ordered vector to an ordered simplex, with a hand-derived target += log|J| Jacobian. It compiles, runs,
Want to give a prior (e.g. Dirichlet) to a simplex via an unconstrained vector mapped through softmax, adding a Jacobian correction by hand (target += log_deter
leaves: softmax(append_row(0,y)) with positive_ordered[K-1] y; reduced Jacobian sum(y)-K*log_sum_exp(append_row(0,y)); SBC-verified. Diagnosis stated explicitly: the full KxK softmax Jacobian is not full rank

With v0 = append_row(0, v), s = log_sum_exp(v0): the log-Jacobian-determinant is sum(v) - K*log_sum_exp(v0). Stan function: target += sum(v) - K*log_sum_exp(v0); return softmax(v0). Avoids costly log_

branches.if_observe: softmax variant passes SBC but is slower / worse-conditioned
branches.then: prefer the order-statistic recursive transform for production; keep softmax as a documented alternative

action: Reframe runtime in terms of gradient evaluations: inspect leapfrog steps per iteration (get_num_leapfrog_per_iteration) and treedepth rather than walltime
discriminates: slow because of poor mixing (low n_eff) vs slow because of very long trajectories per draw (geometry forces many leapfrog steps)
expected_findings: n_eff is actually high (~1800-2000) so mixing is fine; 255-511 leapfrog steps per iteration is a LOT -> the cost is per-trajectory length, a geometry signature, not a mixing failure
situations: A Stan GP model (latent 1D function f drawn via Cholesky of an exponentiated-quadratic kernel, convolved with a known binary event vector via a Toeplitz matrix,
After non-centered reparameterization of correlated parameters in a latent AR(1) state-space model with sigmoid (inv_logit / soft-clipping) link components (inp
leaves: Expert: 'It's more about number of gradient evaluations than walltime... 255+ leapfrog steps before a U-turn is a lot... sounds like it's sampling really efficiently, just taking a lot of leapfrog ste
Use total time AND sampler behavior — total leapfrog steps and the per-iteration distribution; new profiling tools for per-block cost. Cf. Betancourt 'Identity Crisis' case study diagnostics.

branches.if_observe: high n_eff but huge leapfrog counts
branches.then: attack trajectory length via a better metric (dense) and/or model geometry; consider shorter chains

branches.if_observe: low n_eff
branches.then: different problem -- mixing/identifiability, longer or better-tuned warmup

action: Classify the multimodality: ask whether the modes are mathematically equivalent (symmetry-induced) or represent genuinely different solutions
discriminates: exchangeable/sign/rotation symmetry (fixable by a constraint) vs non-equivalent distinct solutions (must pick one / live with it)
expected_findings: If modes are equivalent (like mixture label-switching: mode means at +1/-1), a single ordering/sign constraint collapses them; if non-equivalent, no constraint helps and you must report the ambiguity
situations: A latent linear-dynamical-system / state-space model (latent x_t evolving via A x_t + B u_t, emitting theta via C, then Bernoulli observations) where per-chain
A Bayesian neural network (or an overparameterized exchangeable-component model such as a Gaussian mixture) has a posterior that MCMC/HMC cannot explore: it is
leaves: Vehtari (#2): beyond multimodality, also weak identifiability (narrow twisted ridges) and funnels if prior hyperparameters are sampled. Carpenter (#3): hidden layers act like a mixed-membership/factor
bbbales2 gives the mixture-of-two-normals label-switching example and the 'NY-to-MN, multiple roads' analogy for non-equivalent modes; points to the 'Identifying Bayesian Mixture Models' case study

branches.if_observe: modes mathematically equivalent
branches.then: impose an identifying constraint (e.g. ordered[2] mu, or sign/ordering on C) to pin one labelling

branches.if_observe: modes non-equivalent / constraint can't be found
branches.then: pick one solution or report results with an explicit multimodality caveat

action: Reparameterize the group-level effects to non-centered form (gamma_raw ~ std_normal, gamma = mu + gamma_raw * diag_pre_multiply(sigma, L)') and align to the Stan User's Guide multivariate-hierarchical template
discriminates: centered-funnel hypothesis: if non-centering fixes BFMI/treedepth, the pathology was the centered hierarchical geometry, not priors or tails
expected_findings: Under centered-funnel hypothesis, non-centered version samples cleanly: E-BFMI ~0.63 (healthy), 0 divergences, 0 treedepth saturations at adapt_delta=0.99 / max_treedepth=16 with only ~1000 iterations
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) 4-parameter log-logistic / sigmoid dose-response model (Poisson likelihood) fit with Stan NUTS: the single-group / no-pooling v
A multilevel mediation model with crossed random intercepts (two grouping factors, 3x3 correlated random effects via Cholesky-factored LKJ) fit in rstan keeps t

andre.pfeuffer model_zrn_modern.stan: non-centered, diag_pre_multiply instead of diag_matrix*chol, added mean mu1/mu2 to each multivariate group dist, vectorized the mu loops; verify with get_bfmi() a

branches.then: geometry was the cause; proceed to extend model (random slopes)

branches.if_observe: cmdstanr install fails (bin/stanc not found)
branches.then: fall back to rstan by reverting array[N] int syntax to int Group[N]; trust HMC diagnostics over the rstan summary warning

action: Compute the true log pointwise predictive density from scratch (average the likelihood over posterior draws per observation, then log and sum) and compare to elpd_loo + p_loo
discriminates: H5/correct-definition vs H6 (numerical artifact / wrong quantity)
expected_findings: Manual lppd via Vehtari-Gelman-Gabry (2017) Eq. 3 reproduces elpd_loo + p_loo to the decimal; the quantity is the in-sample lppd, an honest posterior-predictive score that legitimately differs from a single-point likelihood.
situations: Hierarchical/multilevel Stan model; user computes out-of-sample predictive likelihood (log predictive density) in generated quantities to compare two candidate
A Bayesian Bernoulli/logistic regression (brms) reports a log predictive score (elpd_loo + p_loo, equivalently a manual lppd from fitted values) that is HIGHER/
leaves: lppd = sum_n log( (1/S) sum_S dbinom(y_n, 1, plogis(X_n . theta^(S))) ) == -1453.191 == baymod_loo$elpd_loo + p_loo; matches Eq.3 exactly
log p(y_test,1..N | y_train) (joint) is NOT comparable to sum_n log p(y_n | y_{n-1}) style pointwise WAIC; keep log_p_news pointwise, drop the sum; move sum outside loop if ever needed

branches.if_observe: manual lppd matches official (observed)
branches.then: confirmed: lppd vs MLE-LL compare different objects; no bug; the 'paradox' dissolves

action: Treat the warning as a diagnostic signal, not noise: switch from WAIC to LOO because they are asymptotically equivalent but LOO carries the pareto-k self-diagnostic
discriminates: Whether the high-p_waic warning is ignorable vs a real signal that the importance-sampling/plug-in approximation is unreliable for some observations
expected_findings: Experts expect WAIC's p_waic>0.4 to co-occur with LOO pareto_k problems; the value of LOO is that it tells you WHICH observations are problematic and lets you fix them (reloo)
situations: A newcomer fits a Bayesian multinomial/categorical logistic mixed model (brms, family=categorical, 4 categories) and tries to reproduce the familiar frequentist
Hierarchical/multilevel Stan model; user computes out-of-sample predictive likelihood (log predictive density) in generated quantities to compare two candidate
leaves: p_waic>0.4 = effective-number-of-parameters diagnostic from Vehtari/Gelman/Gabry LOO+WAIC paper; loo/PSIS Pareto-k is a separate, better diagnostic; 'same WAIC as my paper code' confirms code but valu
bgoodri: do NOT use waic; use loo because asymptotically equivalent and loo has better diagnostics. First run loo(fit) on a single model to read its diagnostics before comparing.

branches.if_observe: loo(fit) runs and flags pareto_k > 0.7 for a few obs
branches.then: Use reloo=TRUE to refit those observations exactly

branches.if_observe: loo errors out
branches.then: Isolate whether it is a model/data problem or a software bug (next move)

action: Check that every estimated parameter actually receives a proper prior (category intercepts; all elements of length-M vectors).
discriminates: H3 (improper/missing priors -> implicit uniform) vs H1 (priors present but too wide)
expected_findings: Under H3: category intercepts had no prior, and any length-M param using only element [1] leaves the rest on implicit uniform. Adding the missing intercept priors removes flat directions and is part of the big speed-up.
situations: A brms-generated hierarchical multinomial-logit model (8 response categories, ~16 group-level grouping structures, ~4000 observations) samples extremely slowly
A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar
leaves: LucC: 'higher mu runs to infinity because you did not specify a prior -> implicit uniform; be super careful with uniform/improper priors'; with mu1=20,mu2=19,sd=3 a single normal fits all the data and
add priors on category intercepts; verify M_Condition/M_Group == 1 so unused vector elements aren't silently uniform; if M>1, prior the whole vector

branches.if_observe: M==1 for all intercept-only group terms
branches.then: no hidden uniform priors there; missing-intercept-prior fix alone resolves the H3 portion

branches.if_observe: any M>1 with only element [1] priored
branches.then: remaining elements get implicit uniform priors -> add vectorized prior over the whole parameter

action: Replace the uniform_lpdf term with the hand-computed log-density constant and re-sample; cross-check against a developer on whether _lpdf keeps normalizing constants.
discriminates: constant-dropping artifact vs. a deeper numerical issue
expected_findings: If propto-dropping were the cause, hand-coding -log(max-min) would fix it. Developer note: _lpdf forms DO compute the constants, so this hypothesis is wrong.
situations: A custom Stan user-defined _lpdf function combines two priors on a parameter, and the posterior collapses far from the data-generating truth (Weibull shape k re
A two-component mixture likelihood (Wiener-diffusion response-time process mixed with a uniform contaminant, combined via log_mix / log_sum_exp) produces 100% d

Three hand-coded variants (-log(max_Y-min_Y), explicit log_sum_exp, density-scale log()) all still diverge for lambda>0; Daniel Lee confirms _lpdf includes constants, so the all-data term equals -N*lo

branches.if_observe: hand-coded constant still diverges
branches.then: the constant/propto idea is a red herring; look at the OTHER (Wiener) component's behavior on the data

action: Compare the parameter's actual data/inferential scale against the prior's assumed unit scale; check whether the model parameters are standardized.
discriminates: H1 (scale mismatch) vs H2/H3 (a problem with the prior's shape/mode rather than its scale)
expected_findings: H1: parameters are on a non-unit scale (e.g. logistic-regression random effects far from O(1)), so half-normal(0,1) is genuinely too tight and the posterior pulls the scale upward — the prior just needs to be consistent with the parameter scale, or the parameters rescaled. H2/H3: parameters are roughly unit-scale yet the user still objects to the mode being at zero, pointing instead to a conceptual misread.
situations: In rstan a subset of parameters (here A, B, delta_H, declared with lower bounds and given normal(0, 1e-3) priors) come out as flat/constant chains: Rhat reporte
Using the Stan-recommended default half-normal(0,1) / half-t(4,0,1) prior on the group-level scale (sigma) of a hierarchical (e.g. logistic regression) model, t
leaves: Bob: 'Those priors assume parameters are on a roughly unit scale. Bigger parameter values need bigger priors to be consistent. Or rescale the parameters, which can simplify Stan's adaptation.' Default
Stan normal(mu, sigma) uses SD, not precision; JAGS dnorm(0,0.001) is precision=0.001 i.e. variance=1000. Convert: normal(0, sqrt(1/0.001)) = normal(0, sqrt(1000)). This is the accepted fix that made

branches.if_observe: params not on unit scale
branches.then: rescale parameters and keep default prior (preferred), else scale the prior to match — pursue H1

branches.if_observe: params unit-scale but user still wants positive mode
branches.then: move to the mode-vs-mass / density-vs-mass clarification (H2)

action: Compare prior mass under the (now correct) prior against the hard constraint and the parameter's plausible range
discriminates: H4 residual prior/constraint mismatch vs 'fully solved'
expected_findings: Even Normal(0, sqrt(1000)) puts ~99.9% of mass below 100, so a lower=100 parameter is still pushed against its boundary; recommend setting the prior mean near the plausible value (and/or reconsidering the bound) for a genuinely weakly-informative prior
situations: In rstan a subset of parameters (here A, B, delta_H, declared with lower bounds and given normal(0, 1e-3) priors) come out as flat/constant chains: Rhat reporte
A Stan model places a prior (sampling statement) directly on a transformed parameter that is a non-linear function of the raw parameters (e.g. Group1Share = inv
leaves: advise shifting the normal mean to a domain-reasonable value consistent with the lower bound instead of mean 0, since 'non-informative around 0' contradicts a >=100 constraint
Switch Group[i]Share ~ beta(a,b); pick shapes to hit mean 1/3 with intended dispersion (beta(7,14) ~ sd close to original normal sd 0.1, vs over-tight beta(50,100)).

branches.if_observe: user wants truly weakly-informative priors
branches.then: set mean to a physically plausible value, widen SD modestly, and prior-predictive check rather than copy JAGS diffuse priors

action: Try a centered parameterization for strongly-informed upsilon (and keep non-centered for weakly-informed ones), i.e. per-element parameterization choice.
discriminates: prior-dominated (non-centering helps) vs likelihood-dominated (centering helps) regime per random effect
expected_findings: If likelihood-dominated H: centered parameterization for informed upsilon removes their funnels. If prior-dominated: centering makes things worse.
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model (interval-censored insect development times; temperature-dependent rate curve with an iid latent lack-of-fit term mapped throu
Hierarchical (population) one-compartment PK model fit in Stan with the non-centered parameterization STILL produces divergent transitions; pairs plot shows the
leaves: wds15: 'against the usual advice... the centered parameterization performs better... whenever you see the PK profiles clearly.' Diagnostic tell = visible/identifiable PK profiles => prefer CP.
Switch well-identified upsilon to centered; leave poorly-identified ones non-centered; compare divergence behavior.

branches.if_observe: centering the informed effects helps
branches.then: adopt a mixed centered/non-centered parameterization keyed to information content

branches.if_observe: no improvement either way
branches.then: the pathology is identifiability between effects, not a CP/NCP regime mismatch

action: Reframe the prior onto the latent linear predictor and put the scale on TOTAL model variance rather than per-coefficient
discriminates: whether the difficulty is 'find the right sd(y) number' (lookup) vs 'choose the scale of total latent variation' (modeling choice)
expected_findings: Under the latent-scale hypothesis: a clean reparameterization that needs only sigma_gamma (interpretable as how separated a 3-sigma observation's probability is, e.g. inv_logit(3)=0.95); under the pi^2/3 hypothesis: a dead end because the logistic variance is not a free sd(y) you can set.

action: classify the three densities by which variable is the sampled parameter (P(x|y)P(y) vs P(x_raw)P(y))

discriminates: which specific coupling (H2 generic correlation vs H3 funnel vs H4 non-convexity) is responsible, and where it lives
expected_findings: ESS-ranking surfaces the hierarchical/multiplicative culprits; targeted pairs plots reveal the elongated or funnel geometry. Blind traceplots/pairs are expected to be uninterpretable and waste effort. Note OP claims to have already checked location-vs-log(scale) for funnels on small-n and large-n and is 'reasonably happy', so simple 2D funnels are partly ruled out — pushing suspicion toward 3D funnels / functions-of-parameters couplings.
situations: A high-dimensional nonlinear model (tens of thousands of parameters, strong correlations, weak identifiability) samples cleanly — NO divergences, no R-hat/ESS/t
leaves: Find parameters with low estimated ESS — typically hierarchical parameters correlated to many lower-level ones, or pairs with multiplicative non-identification (two parameters whose product conditions

branches.if_observe: low-ESS params are hierarchical scale + its children with a neck
branches.then: diagnose funnel (H3); reparameterize non-centered on the unconstrained scale

branches.if_observe: elongated ridge between two parameters that multiply
branches.then: multiplicative non-identifiability; reparameterize or tighten priors to break the ridge

branches.if_observe: no 2D structure found but coupling implied
branches.then: search 3D combinations and functions of parameters guided by model structure, since 2D pairs already looked clean

branches.if_observe: elongation cannot be removed by any reparameterization
branches.then: it's intrinsic; accept small step size + deep trees, or tighten priors / add data to shrink the correlated direction

action: Probe local log-convexity by computing the condition number of the inverse Hessian of the log density at posterior draws
discriminates: H4 (non-convex / non-positive-definite curvature, saddle points) vs H2/H3 (positive-definite but elongated/funnel geometry)
expected_findings: Under H4 the inverse Hessian is non-PD or wildly ill-conditioned at some draws, pinpointing the hard region. Under H2/H3 curvature stays PD but anisotropic (large condition number along the correlated direction). Under H1 condition numbers are benign once scales are absorbed.
situations: A high-dimensional nonlinear model (tens of thousands of parameters, strong correlations, weak identifiability) samples cleanly — NO divergences, no R-hat/ESS/t
leaves: At each (or sampled) posterior point compute the inverse Hessian of the log density and its condition number; high condition number flags badly-scaled curvature, and failure to be positive-definite fl

branches.if_observe: inverse Hessian non-positive-definite at some points
branches.then: non-convexity / saddle — reparameterize that block; consider tighter priors

branches.if_observe: PD everywhere but high condition number along specific directions
branches.then: elongated geometry confirmed — back to targeted reparameterization of that coupled direction

action: Run a controlled data-strength sweep: vary dataset size and watch divergence incidence.
discriminates: Geometry-only vs likelihood-strength: does the pathology track how informative the data is, independent of parameterization?
expected_findings: If pure geometry -> divergences roughly constant across sizes. If weak-likelihood-driven -> divergences vanish when data is plentiful and reappear in the small/extreme cases. Observed: 100x10 clean, 2-anything or 10x10 diverges -> divergences scale with WEAKNESS of the likelihood, the signature of an under-informed funnel that NCP alone can't save.
situations: Few but persistent post-warmup divergences in a hierarchical model that partial-pools the group/individual SCALE parameters (a hyperprior on the sds, e.g. indiv
leaves: Simulate at several (num_individuals x num_replicates) settings (e.g. 2x2, 10x10, 100x10) keeping the model fixed, and record where divergences appear.

branches.if_observe: clean at large N, diverges at small N
branches.then: frame as data-scarcity + funnel; need stronger priors or match sim size to the real experiment, not just reparameterization

branches.if_observe: diverges at all N
branches.then: pure structural geometry / coding bug -> isolate with a minimal reproducer

action: Reduce to a minimal reproducer (strip to Neal's funnel) and test both parameterizations at the worst-case data size.
discriminates: Whether the pathology is intrinsic to a funnel starved of data (no parameterization wins) vs an artifact of the larger model's extra structure.
expected_findings: If intrinsic small-data funnel -> even the canonical Neal funnel diverges under both CP and NCP at nI=2,nY=4 (NCP's advantage disappears when the likelihood is too weak to pin sigma). Confirmed. This validates 'it's the geometry + scarcity', not the surrounding code.
situations: Few but persistent post-warmup divergences in a hierarchical model that partial-pools the group/individual SCALE parameters (a hyperprior on the sds, e.g. indiv
leaves: Collapse to mu, sigma, imu ~ normal(mu,sigma) with nI=2, nY=4 and a sample-time centered/non-centered toggle; confirm neither CP nor NCP is consistently divergence-free.

branches.if_observe: minimal funnel also diverges under both parameterizations
branches.then: accept it as a fundamentally under-identified funnel; remedy is regularization (zero-avoiding/informative prior on sigma) or more data, not reparameterization

branches.if_observe: minimal funnel is clean
branches.then: the extra model structure is to blame -> reintroduce pieces until divergences return

action: Reframe how the pooling is induced: prefer a regression (design-matrix) structure over an explicit normal-variate hyperprior on the scales, and add a zero-avoiding prior on the noise/scale.
discriminates: Whether the pooling can be achieved with more robust geometry under low information (regression-induced pooling) vs the fragile explicit hyper-scale parameterization.
expected_findings: Regression-style pooling -> more robust in low-information regimes (no explicit sd-of-scales neck). Explicit normal-variate pooling of variances -> only worthwhile / well-behaved when there are many coefficients to inform the hyperparameters; otherwise it re-creates the under-identified funnel.
situations: Few but persistent post-warmup divergences in a hierarchical model that partial-pools the group/individual SCALE parameters (a hyperprior on the sds, e.g. indiv
leaves: Induce mutual information through coef ~ std_normal() with mu = X * mu_coef and psi = X * psi_coef (log-variance via regression on a contrast matrix), instead of psi ~ normal(psi_mean, psi_sd); keep w

branches.if_observe: regression-induced pooling samples cleanly at realistic data sizes
branches.then: adopt it; reserve normal-variate variance-pooling for many-coefficient settings

branches.if_observe: still diverges
branches.then: the binding constraint is data scarcity -> strengthen priors or do not attempt to pool the variances at this sample size

action: Lower the divergence energy-error threshold and re-sample, then compare divergence counts.
discriminates: Whether the default threshold is masking divergences (loose-cutoff hypothesis) vs. the geometry being genuinely fine.
expected_findings: If the cutoff is masking and trajectories spin out late: divergence count jumps dramatically (the funnel demo went 177 -> 986) and the new divergences cluster closer to the source. If geometry were fine, count stays low even at the tighter threshold.
situations: A model has few flagged divergences and they are scattered across the posterior, so you cannot pin down WHERE in parameter space the geometry breaks — the diver

branches.then: Treat that region as the candidate source; the default 1000 was flagging late. Inspect that region's geometry (e.g. tail of a hierarchical scale / neck of a funnel).

branches.if_observe: Count barely changes at the lower threshold
branches.then: Threshold is not the bottleneck; suspect trajectories are being truncated by max tree depth or U-turn before error accumulates — go to the tree-depth move.

action: Switch which point along the divergent trajectory you report as the divergence location.
discriminates: Whether scattered/uninformative locations are an artifact of WHICH point is saved (reporting-point hypothesis) vs. a real lack of concentration.
expected_findings: If it is a reporting artifact: the first-crossing point clusters tightly at the geometric source even when the saved-draw locations were scattered. The max-error point, by contrast, lands at far-flung low-density states.
situations: A model has few flagged divergences and they are scattered across the posterior, so you cannot pin down WHERE in parameter space the geometry breaks — the diver
leaves: Do not report the iteration's saved draw or starting point (Stan default), and do NOT report the point of maximum energy error or maximum error-increase. Instead report the point where the energy erro

branches.then: Do not trust them as-is — they are biased post-divergence; apply density-weighting (next move) before localizing.

action: Select the localization point by weighting trajectory points by error-increase AND local posterior density, not by raw error magnitude.
discriminates: True cause-region (high-density, where health first declined) vs. post-divergence runaway artifacts (low-density, large error but irrelevant).
expected_findings: Density-weighted selection returns points in the high-density cause region; naive max-error selection returns scattered far-out points. If both agree, the geometry break is itself in a high-density region.
situations: A model has few flagged divergences and they are scattered across the posterior, so you cannot pin down WHERE in parameter space the geometry breaks — the diver
leaves: Sample a point from the trajectory with weight proportional to local error increase times local density. Rationale: energy error grows much MORE after the sampler has already diverged than before, so

branches.if_observe: Density-weighted point sits in a sensible high-density region
branches.then: Localize the cause there and address the geometry (e.g. reparameterize the funnel/hierarchical scale non-centered, tighten the scale prior).

branches.if_observe: Even density-weighted points remain scattered
branches.then: Divergences may have multiple sources or a global step-size/mass-matrix problem rather than one localized neck; revisit warmup/adaptation rather than a single region.

action: read the parameter summary to localize which quantities are mixing badly vs well

Pure software/positivity bug: would also show init rejections / log(0), which are present but secondary. Constraint-wander: would show params straying outside truncation bounds.

branches.if_observe: init rejections / log probability = log(0)
branches.then: check positivity of the Poisson rate argument and the scale parameter > 0

action: align the parameter constraint with its truncation bound
discriminates: whether part of the bad geometry / blow-ups is just parameters escaping their intended support (a correctness bug) vs genuine funnel geometry
expected_findings: If escaping support was the driver, fixing constraints removes blow-ups but the E-BFMI funnel signature persists (it does) — confirming the constraint issue is real but NOT the root cause of E-BFMI.
situations: A Poisson autoregressive PAR(1) state-space model (latent positive states y[t], AR transition y[t]=c+phi*(y[t-1]-c)+noise, Poisson observation) raises an E-BFMI
leaves: Truncation T[0,100] on a prior does NOT constrain the variable; declare real<lower=0,upper=100> phi and real<lower=0,upper=1000> c so the parameter cannot wander into undefined-behavior territory.

branches.if_observe: still low E-BFMI after fixing constraints
branches.then: root cause is geometric (funnel), not the constraint bug; keep going

action: guarantee positivity of the Poisson rate and the scale
discriminates: init/blow-up failures caused by invalid likelihood evaluation vs. failures caused by the sampler being unable to move through a funnel
expected_findings: Fixing positivity removes the log(0) init rejections and sporadic scale==0 rejections, but does NOT by itself remove the E-BFMI/funnel warning.
situations: A Poisson autoregressive PAR(1) state-space model (latent positive states y[t], AR transition y[t]=c+phi*(y[t-1]-c)+noise, Poisson observation) raises an E-BFMI
leaves: y is unconstrained but feeds poisson(y); non-positive y is invalid. Either declare y positive, use poisson_log (log link), or reparameterize so the rate is positive by construction; ensure noise>0.

branches.if_observe: init still fails after positivity fix
branches.then: supply explicit init function (logtau=log(dt), logc=log(mean), lognoise=log(std), W=0)

action: non-center every latent state and re-run
discriminates: whether non-centering helps (weak per-state likelihood) or hurts (strong per-state likelihood) — the centered/non-centered dichotomy from hierarchical modeling applied per state
expected_findings: If states were weakly informed, non-centering would fix the funnel. Because states are strongly informed (n_eff~4000), blanket non-centering INVERTS the geometry and explodes Rhat — the key failure branch that reveals the centered/non-centered trade-off.
situations: A Poisson autoregressive PAR(1) state-space model (latent positive states y[t], AR transition y[t]=c+phi*(y[t-1]-c)+noise, Poisson observation) raises an E-BFMI
leaves: Rewrite y[i]=c+phi*(y[i-1]-c)+W[i]*noise with W~std_normal(). Result: Rhat blows up to ~1e15 — FAILED. The OP correctly reasons the failure is because the y states are 'pinned down pretty well by the

branches.then: states are strongly likelihood-informed; do NOT non-center them uniformly. A mixed centered/non-centered (state/innovation) parameterization is required when per-state likelihood strength varies.

branches.if_observe: Rhat improves after non-centering
branches.then: states were weakly informed; non-centering was the right move

action: move the global scale/decorrelation params to the log scale and supply data-informed inits
discriminates: whether residual pathology was multiplicative/heavy-tailed geometry on positive scale params (cured by log) vs. an irreducible inter-state funnel
expected_findings: Log-scale + good inits resolves the single-process E-BFMI in practice; but the deeper structural statement (sequence of inter-state funnels) still governs the harder two-process case.
situations: A Poisson autoregressive PAR(1) state-space model (latent positive states y[t], AR transition y[t]=c+phi*(y[t-1]-c)+noise, Poisson observation) raises an E-BFMI
leaves: Sample logtau, logc, lognoise (with logtau~normal(log(dt),log(N))) and reconstruct phi=exp(-dt/tau); keep W~std_normal() for innovations. Provide init dict from data moments. This form 'works' (no war

branches.if_observe: single-process now clean but two-process still diverges
branches.then: appeal to the structural diagnosis: AR state param = chain of funnels; choose centered/non-centered per state by likelihood strength (mixed parameterization)

action: name the structural geometry and the parameterization rule
discriminates: confirms funnel-between-states as the terminal explanation and gives the actionable per-state centering rule, superseding the blanket-non-centering attempt that failed
expected_findings: All observations reconcile: E-BFMI low (inter-state funnels), blanket non-centering exploded (states strongly informed), log-scale + inits helped the easy case. The two-process case needs a per-state mixed parameterization.
situations: A Poisson autoregressive PAR(1) state-space model (latent positive states y[t], AR transition y[t]=c+phi*(y[t-1]-c)+noise, Poisson observation) raises an E-BFMI

branches.if_observe: likelihood strength varies across states (e.g. mixture / partially-observed two-process model)
branches.then: use a mixed centered/non-centered parameterization, centering the well-informed states and non-centering the weakly-informed ones

action: Reframe from counting to bias-assessment: decompose tolerability into (a) false- vs true-positive, (b) how much posterior mass is missed, (c) whether the missed mass moves the quantity of interest.
discriminates: Benign vs true-positive vs target-irrelevant — replaces 'how many' with 'does it bias what I care about'.
expected_findings: Benign: missed mass negligible and target unchanged. True-positive: missed mass tied to a region and target shifts. If you discard the model on predictive checks anyway, tolerable in early workflow regardless.
situations: A hierarchical binary/logistic model produces only 1-2 divergences that are intermittent and seed-dependent (some runs clean, some not), with no obvious funnel
leaves: Vehtari's three-part frame: divergence count alone is uninformative; the decision variable is impact on the inference target, not the number.

branches.if_observe: model will be discarded on predictive checks anyway
branches.then: tolerate divergences in early workflow phase; do not spend effort eliminating them yet

branches.if_observe: model is a keeper and you lack insight into posterior shape
branches.then: must quantify the bias before trusting it (proceed to SBC-family check)

action: Run a stability / sensitivity sweep across nuisance settings and check the decision-relevant summaries plus convergence diagnostics.
discriminates: Benign/target-irrelevant (H1) vs true-positive that moves the target (H2).
expected_findings: H1: key summaries stable across seeds and tuning, Rhat/ESS fine — divergences not perturbing the target. H2: summaries drift with seed or with tighter adapt_delta — bias is real. User finds summaries stable and diagnostics satisfactory, supporting tolerable-for-this-target.
situations: A hierarchical binary/logistic model produces only 1-2 divergences that are intermittent and seed-dependent (some runs clean, some not), with no obvious funnel
leaves: Vary random seed, adapt_delta, and max_treedepth on a fixed dataset; confirm key posterior decision quantities are stable and Rhat/ESS are satisfactory.

branches.if_observe: summaries stable, Rhat/ESS good
branches.then: tentatively tolerable for these quantities; still note this does not prove zero bias

branches.if_observe: summaries shift with seed or adapt_delta
branches.then: real bias; do not tolerate, reparameterize

action: Quantify the bias directly with a simulation-based calibration tool rather than relying on stability heuristics.
discriminates: Whether residual bias from the few divergences is measurable at all (H1 confirmed) vs detectable bias (H2).
expected_findings: H1: SBC/Posterior SBC shows calibration intact; the 1-2 divergences leave no measurable footprint — confidence that they are tolerable. H2: SBC flags miscalibration tied to the divergent region.
situations: A hierarchical binary/logistic model produces only 1-2 divergences that are intermittent and seed-dependent (some runs clean, some not), with no obvious funnel
leaves: Posterior SBC (Springer 2026, doi 10.1007/s11222-026-10825-9) assesses possible bias conditional on data; at the design/simulation stage where you can simulate, full SBC works and PPC can run on simul

branches.if_observe: SBC cannot detect any effect of the few divergences
branches.then: treat as benign; tolerable for inference

branches.if_observe: SBC flags miscalibration
branches.then: bias is real; reparameterize / fix geometry before trusting decisions

action: Try a minimal heavy-tailed 1-D target and read the diagnostic triple (divergences / E-BFMI / R-hat / treedepth)
discriminates: heavy-tailed-energy hypothesis vs funnel-geometry hypothesis as the *simplest* generator of low E-BFMI
expected_findings: Under heavy-tail: E-BFMI collapses (~0.1) with few/no divergences because instability shows up as treedepth/mixing failure not curvature blowup. Under a pure funnel you'd instead expect divergences to co-occur. The heavy-tail case fires E-BFMI cleanly but FAILS the 'decent R-hat' bonus.
situations: Chains finish with low E-BFMI (energy diagnostic flags poor momentum resampling / heavy-tailed energy distribution) but NO divergent transitions are reported —
leaves: x ~ student_t(0.1, 0, 1) with adapt_delta=0.99; observe ~0 divergences, E-BFMI~0.1, but max-treedepth saturation and bad R-hat

branches.if_observe: low E-BFMI but also bad R-hat and treedepth saturation
branches.then: heavy tail reproduces E-BFMI cheaply but is not a clean isolated example; escalate to a tunable funnel to get good R-hat alongside low E-BFMI

action: Build a funnel and dial its dimension; raise adapt_delta to suppress divergences and watch which diagnostic survives
discriminates: funnel-dimension as the knob that trades divergences for E-FMI signal; isolates 'which diagnostic dominates' as a function of geometry+dimension
expected_findings: Low-dim funnel/heavy-tail: E-FMI fires. High-dim funnel: E-FMI dominates and divergences become rare — UNLESS dimension is so high the chains never reach small enough scale values, in which case E-FMI ALSO goes quiet (false clean bill).
situations: Chains finish with low E-BFMI (energy diagnostic flags poor momentum resampling / heavy-tailed energy distribution) but NO divergent transitions are reported —
leaves: Betancourt: tweak a funnel (e.g. the 9-D funnel in his Hierarchical Modeling case study) — higher funnel dimension makes the E-FMI problem dominate over divergences; in low dimension use a heavy-taile

branches.if_observe: very high funnel dimension and BOTH diagnostics quiet across some chains
branches.then: suspect under-exploration, not a healthy fit — compare chains that did vs didn't reach the neck (e.g. chains 1/2 vs 3/4); empirical E-BFMI is only as good as realized exploration

branches.if_observe: fitting heavy-tailed target with default settings
branches.then: turn OFF inverse-metric adaptation — the variances the metric estimates do not exist for a non-finite-variance target, so adaptation is estimating a nonexistent quantity

action: Hold the funnel fixed, perturb the model with an independent high-information block, and re-read the diagnostics; then sweep adapt_delta
discriminates: Is the diagnostic flip driven by a genuine change in target geometry (the y are independent, so NO) or by ADAPTATION coupling otherwise-independent parameters through a single global step size?
expected_findings: If geometry-driven: adding independent y should not touch the funnel's diagnostics — contradicted. If adaptation-driven: the std_normal block motivates a much LARGER step size, more aggressive adaptation; the bigger step limits how deep trajectories enter the funnel before going unstable -> divergences appear and exploration is too shallow to trigger E-FMI. Raising adapt_delta softens adaptation, shrinks the step, deepens funnel exploration -> divergences squashed, E-FMI returns.
situations: Chains finish with low E-BFMI (energy diagnostic flags poor momentum resampling / heavy-tailed energy distribution) but NO divergent transitions are reported —
leaves: data K=20 funnel; N=0 vs N=80 std_normal block; N=80 with adapt_delta=0.99 — observe E-FMI->divergences flip with the added block, and divergences->E-FMI flip back as adapt_delta rises

branches.if_observe: added block is probabilistically independent yet still changes the funnel's diagnostics
branches.then: attribute to adaptation, not target curvature — a single global step size / metric couples independent blocks; the high-info block dominates step-size selection

branches.if_observe: increasing adapt_delta removes divergences but reintroduces E-FMI warnings
branches.then: confirms divergences and low-E-BFMI are complementary views of the SAME funnel, mediated by step size: large step -> shallow+unstable (divergences, no E-FMI); small step -> deep+refined (E-FMI fires, no divergences)

action: Reconfigure warmup windows to drop the metric-adaptation phase
discriminates: operational fix that lets step-size adaptation proceed while disabling the metric estimation that is ill-defined for non-finite-variance targets
expected_findings: With window=0 the metric stays at its identity/default and only step size adapts; the E-FMI/heavy-tail experiment then reflects geometry rather than a metric fit to a nonexistent covariance.

action: Instrument the sampler to dump the divergence ENDPOINT (where the threshold is crossed) and overlay it against the reported starts and the clean transitions.

Under H2: 'end' points are also scattered or land in a place that doesn't match the known pathology. Under H4: the 'start' points already concentrate once overplotting is fixed, so 'end' adds little.

action: Test the competing explanation that mere overplotting/transparency, not the reported-point choice, is what hides the concentration.

expected_findings: Under H4: with transparency the existing divergent draws already show the boundary clearly, no new stream needed. Under H1: even with transparency, starts sit amid non-divergent draws along most of the boundary so the trigger region stays ambiguous, while endpoints isolate it.

action: Scope the cost of exporting full trajectory information vs a cheap two-point summary, and check it doesn't corrupt the chain.
discriminates: full-trajectory-dump (theoretically clean per H2 but heavy + plumbing-hostile) vs endpoint-only / two-point summary (cheap, model-dependent informativeness).
expected_findings: Full dump: theoretically defensible (whole energy level set) but I/O-heavy, requires threading writers through the recursive trajectory builder, risks losing compiler optimizations, and clustering analysis would be needed to pick the fast-energy-growth region. Two-point summary: near-zero overhead, runs only on divergent iterations, but informativeness is empirical/per-model.

action: Verify the reparameterized geometry is decoupled

Prior mismatch: prior predictive draws look implausible. Insufficient tuning: more warmup steadies adaptation and reduces divergences.

action: Check whether the proposed value can leave the parameter's valid range — locate the constraint that the sampler can violate.

action: Reparameterize: re-express the intended bimodal beta prior as a Mixture of Betas (so a single draw stays in [0,1]), instead of summing the two RVs.

Under tuning hypothesis (wrong): the mixture would not have mattered and divergences would persist — not what is observed.

leaves: Replace unit_vector[2] with a free vector[2] plus explicit normalization (length = sqrt(x1^2+x2^2), u = x/length) and explicit independent standard-normal priors on the components; verify it reproduce

Confirmed empirically: narrower angular distribution -> slower, higher treedepth, more divergences.

branches.then: Reconsider; look for funnel coupling to other params (e.g. precision) instead.

action: Reparameterize with a soft radial constraint ('donut' prior) so the typical set is an annulus of near-constant radius.
discriminates: Whether the diagnosed origin-curvature mechanism is the true cause (fix removes divergences) and whether it changes the constrained answer (it must not, since length is unidentified).
expected_findings: H3: divergences -> 0, treedepth drops (~3.5 -> ~2.3), runtime drops (~41s -> ~15s), and the concentration-vs-runtime dependence flattens; constrained posterior unchanged. If the mechanism were wrong, the donut would not help or would bias estimates.

branches.then: Fix validated; tune donut width and decide on Jacobian / higher-dim behavior.

branches.if_observe: Divergences persist
branches.then: Probe residual failure modes (origin still reachable; donut too wide; secondary funnels).

action: Stress-test the reparameterization across donut shape, width, Jacobian on/off, and the no-likelihood (pure-prior) case.
discriminates: Which donut shape/width is robust; whether the Jacobian helps or hurts (it raises density near the singular origin, can create a target spike and init rejections); identifies a NEW failure branch independent of the likelihood.
expected_findings: Failure branch: even with NO likelihood divergences can occur — zero-avoiding densities give crazy gradients near origin, or leapfrog overshoots the central hole. Tradeoffs: informative -> narrower donut & lognormal best, Jacobian makes it worse; uninformative -> broader donut & reversed ranking; overall shape/Jacobian choice matters little in 2D. sd~0.1*radius near-optimal; err narrower for messy real angular distributions or precision-funnel coupling.

leaves: Sweep donut family (normal / lognormal / gamma, all ~unit mean sd~0.1), width, and Jacobian r^(d-1) on/off; test highly informative (kappa~300, ~15 pts) vs no-likelihood. Note: omitting the Jacobian i

branches.if_observe: No-likelihood divergences appear or width-dependent slowdowns
branches.then: Use a density that drops fast enough at 0 (lognormal/gamma), pick narrower donut, and/or raise adapt_delta; treat origin as forbidden region.

branches.if_observe: Jacobian causes init rejections / instability (esp. high d)
branches.then: Drop the Jacobian (length not inferred) or restrict donut reparam to low dimensions only.

action: Probe the dimension scaling of both the native representation and the donut fix.
discriminates: H1 scope: is the origin problem low-dimensional only (high-d volume near origin -> 0, so native may already work) vs universal; and whether the Jacobian is needed to stabilize the radius across dimensions.
expected_findings: H1 (scoped): in high d the typical set moves away from the origin (chi distribution of length for standard normals), so native unit_vector is largely fine — pathology is a low-dimension phenomenon. Donut works to ~256 d WITH Jacobian (keeps radius stable); WITHOUT Jacobian radius grows with d. But treedepth shows complex, non-monotone dim dependence (exponential-trajectory thresholding effects), and Jacobian can blow up numerically / reject inits at very high d.

leaves: Test the gamma-donut (with Jacobian) up to high dimensions (e.g. 256 via a von Mises-Fisher likelihood, lpdf = kappa*dot(mu,x)); examine treedepth/runtime vs dimension and vector-length distribution w

branches.if_observe: Native fine in high d, problem only low-d
branches.then: Recommend donut/multiplier reparam for low-dimensional unit vectors; leave high-d as-is.

branches.if_observe: Weird non-monotone treedepth or high-d Jacobian numerics
branches.then: Flag as open; verify correctness with simulation-based calibration on the unit sphere before trusting; consider proper manifold HMC (geodesic / Byrne-Girolami) as the principled alternative.

action: Classify the warning: separate treedepth-saturation from divergences before choosing a playbook.
discriminates: divergence-framing (wrong) vs degeneracy-framing (right)
expected_findings: If the only/primary warning is 'saturated max treedepth' with no divergent transitions, the divergence checklist is the wrong tool — points to degeneracy/non-identifiability. If divergences co-occur, that is a separate, additional pathology to handle on its own track.

branches.if_observe: Pure treedepth saturation, no divergences
branches.then: Treat as degeneracy/identifiability investigation; abandon the divergence playbook

branches.if_observe: Divergences also present
branches.then: Handle divergences separately (energy/geometry, reparam); treedepth is a distinct symptom

action: Read saturation as evidence the target is highly degenerate / possibly non-identifiable, and decide to investigate the target geometry rather than just bump the cap.
discriminates: treat-symptom (raise cap) vs find-cause (investigate geometry)
expected_findings: Under a degenerate/non-identifiable target, raising the cap buys little and trajectories stay long; under a merely complex-but-identified target, a modest cap increase resolves it with acceptable cost.
situations: NUTS/dynamic-HMC trajectories saturate max_treedepth (tree depth climbs toward the cap, e.g. 14), making sampling slow and giving low ESS; symptom appears when
leaves: Long non-terminating trajectories mean HMC keeps gliding along the target without the no-U-turn criterion firing — characteristic of a long flat ridge (degeneracy) or an unbounded direction (non-ident

branches.if_observe: Raising cap removes warning at reasonable cost and ESS recovers
branches.then: Target was complex but fine; accept the higher cap

branches.if_observe: Cap increase just makes sampling slower with persistent saturation
branches.then: Confirm degeneracy/non-identifiability and continue investigating

action: Check propriety of the target and the tails of priors/parameter constraints.
discriminates: improper/unbounded-tail target vs proper-but-degenerate target
expected_findings: If a parameter lacks a proper prior or has a flat tail, the posterior is improper and trajectories run toward infinity (chronic saturation); if all priors are proper and tails decay, saturation is instead from an internal flat ridge (degeneracy).
situations: NUTS/dynamic-HMC trajectories saturate max_treedepth (tree depth climbs toward the cap, e.g. 14), making sampling slow and giving low ESS; symptom appears when
leaves: Clarify the failure mode: it is not that the density VALUE blows up, but that the density does not DECAY to zero at infinite parameter values — an improper/flat-tailed posterior the integrator tries t

branches.if_observe: A parameter has improper/flat prior or samples drift to extreme values
branches.then: Add/tighten proper priors to ensure tail decay; recheck

branches.if_observe: Priors proper, tails decay, still saturating
branches.then: Cause is internal degeneracy, not propriety — keep digging

action: Rule out a per-chain bad-adaptation artifact before blaming geometry.
discriminates: adaptation pathology (per-chain artifact) vs genuine target geometry
expected_findings: Bad-adaptation chains show anomalously small step size + inflated metric elements relative to healthy chains and stay frozen; if all chains adapt to similar, sane step sizes yet still saturate, the cause is the target geometry, not adaptation.
situations: NUTS/dynamic-HMC trajectories saturate max_treedepth (tree depth climbs toward the cap, e.g. 14), making sampling slow and giving low ESS; symptom appears when
leaves: Inspect the warmup-adapted step size and inverse metric per chain (get_adaptation_info in RStan/PyStan). A chain stuck with a tiny step size and very large inverse-metric elements has fallen into a de

branches.if_observe: Outlier chain(s) with tiny stepsize + huge metric elements
branches.then: Adaptation fell into a bad config; reinitialize/retune warmup, not the model

branches.if_observe: Adaptation looks healthy across chains but saturation persists
branches.then: Geometry is the culprit; proceed to component isolation

action: Localize the pathology to the interaction between components rather than to any single component.
discriminates: single-component defect vs cross-component interaction defect
expected_findings: If the two-stage / decoupled fit works but the fully-joint co-estimation fails, the defect is the joint estimation of shared/latent quantities (the interaction); if a component fails even alone, fix that component first.
situations: NUTS/dynamic-HMC trajectories saturate max_treedepth (tree depth climbs toward the cap, e.g. 14), making sampling slow and giving low ESS; symptom appears when
leaves: Use the asymmetry as a clue: components-good-alone but joint-bad means the problem is NOT inside a component (those are already clean) — it is in how they couple. Note the diagnostic logic: a misbehav

branches.if_observe: Two-stage (decoupled) works, full joint fails
branches.then: Interaction/co-estimation is the problem; examine what the linked latent quantities are and whether data can inform them

branches.if_observe: A component misbehaves on its own
branches.then: Fix that component before attempting the joint

action: Test whether the data can actually identify the latent dynamics, or whether the joint is over-flexible / under-informed.
discriminates: over-flexible joint that misfits vs structurally under-informed latent dynamics (too few survival times)
expected_findings: If many distinct dynamical evolutions give the same likelihood, the latent dynamics are non-identifiable from the survival data → flat ridge → persistent saturation, fixed only by adding information; if instead the joint is over-flexible relative to a rigid simpler model, tightening structure/priors recovers a well-behaved likelihood.
situations: NUTS/dynamic-HMC trajectories saturate max_treedepth (tree depth climbs toward the cap, e.g. 14), making sampling slow and giving low ESS; symptom appears when
leaves: Two structural causes to weigh: (a) the joint model adds flexibility and can contort in weird ways while a simpler/rigid model gave a nice likelihood despite misfitting; (b) the survival observations

branches.if_observe: Survival measured at only a few times; multiple latent paths fit equally
branches.then: Structural non-identifiability — add data or informative priors on the dynamics; raising treedepth will not help

branches.if_observe: Joint is over-flexible vs a rigid simpler model that fit nicely
branches.then: Add missing structure / regularizing priors to remove the spurious contortions

action: Reject the 'ignore divergences' framing on principle before any code change
discriminates: Whether to invest in fixing geometry at all (kills H4, the benign-divergence hypothesis)
expected_findings: Under H4 the user expects divergent and non-divergent coefficient draws to be indistinguishable and concludes 'safe'; expert reframes that indistinguishability as uninformative — absence of visible bias is not evidence of correct exploration. Establishes the divergences MUST be resolved, not rationalized.
