leaves: State that divergences mean the posterior has not been adequately explored, so there is NO assurance the coefficient inference approximates the true posterior; pairs plots looking fine for the diverge

branches.if_observe: User still wants to ship because residuals/test-set look good
branches.then: Reiterate that out-of-sample residual quality does not bound posterior exploration error; require squashing divergences before trusting any parameter, including the 'good-looking' coefficients

action: Localize the divergences to specific parameters and apply parameterization selectively
discriminates: H5 (strong-likelihood contexts must stay centered) and pinpoints H1 vs H2 vs H3 at the per-parameter level
expected_findings: Divergent transitions cluster around the sparse-group scale parameters and the necks where scale couples to its locations; those parameters also show low n_eff. Strong-data contexts, if forced non-centered, would themselves start to diverge — telling you to revert them to centered.
situations: A large hierarchical regression integrating sparse repeated-measures data has persistent divergences (hundreds to >1500) that occur throughout the chains (not j
leaves: Find the source of divergences by locating where the bad geometry lives (per Betancourt's Hierarchical Modeling case study): inspect pairs plots / divergent-draw scatter for the scale parameters and t

branches.if_observe: Divergences localize to sparse-group scale/covariance parameters
branches.then: Non-center those, pool them, and stop — the strong-data contexts stay centered

branches.if_observe: Population location/scale/correlation parameters are themselves degenerate (geometry stays bad despite reparameterization+pooling)
branches.then: This degeneracy is irreducible by parameterization alone — resolve only with more data or stronger priors on the POPULATION parameters (H3); accept no purely-mechanical fix exists

action: Sweep the OTHER convergence diagnostics for corroborating warnings
discriminates: Benign-low-n_eff vs an active funnel/energy pathology that just hasn't tripped the divergence counter
expected_findings: Benign hypothesis -> all other diagnostics clean (no div, no Rhat, no EBFMI). Active-pathology hypothesis -> expect at least EBFMI or divergence flags to accompany the low n_eff. Here ALL other diagnostics are clean, which points toward a MILD/sub-threshold pathology rather than either a severe one or pure noise.

branches.if_observe: all other diagnostics clean (no div/Rhat/mcse/EBFMI), only n_eff/N low
branches.then: candidate is a mild coupling that slows mixing without compromising accuracy; keep both benign and mild-funnel hypotheses live and probe further

action: Flip the global parameterization and compare which warnings appear
discriminates: Wrong-global-parameterization vs the non-centered choice being correct overall but locally mismatched for a few contexts
expected_findings: If contexts are weakly informed -> non-centered should be uniformly fine and centered would show divergences (history: OP switched TO non-centered because centered diverged). If strongly informed -> non-centered tends to produce divergences, not just low n_eff. Observed: non-centered gives only low n_eff (no divergences) AND OP recalls centered diverged earlier -> non-centered is the right GLOBAL choice; the issue is local to a subset of contexts.

branches.if_observe: centered version samples cleanly / non-centered diverges
branches.then: likelihood is strongly informative globally -> prefer centered

branches.if_observe: centered diverges (prior OP experience) and non-centered only shows low n_eff

action: Localize the pathology to specific contexts and tie it to likelihood strength
discriminates: Mild-funnel-from-unbalanced-likelihood vs uniform/benign noise (which would scatter low n_eff randomly, not concentrate it on the strongly-informed contexts)
expected_findings: Mild-funnel hypothesis -> low-n_eff indices coincide with the most strongly-informed contexts, and the depressed n_eff appears on theta_raw and on mu/sigma but NOT on theta itself (exactly the OP's pattern). Benign-noise hypothesis -> no such structured correspondence. Observed pattern matches the mild-funnel signature.

action: Apply a mixed (per-context) parameterization
discriminates: A real speedup (coupling removed for the offending contexts) vs no change (pathology was elsewhere)
expected_findings: If the diagnosis is right -> centering the strongly-informed contexts removes the autocorrelation and yields 'substantial speed ups' with n_eff recovering on those raw/mu parameters. The transformed estimands were already fine, so accuracy is unchanged. Cost: combinatorial trial-and-error over which contexts to center when there are many (hence the appeal of automatic pre-warmup reparameterization, Gorinova et al. 1906.03028).

branches.then: done — mixed parameterization resolves it

branches.if_observe: many contexts and no systematic way to pick which to center
branches.then: either accept the slow-but-correct sampler (warning is benign for the estimands of interest) or pursue automated reparameterization / build a code-generator to manage per-context choices

action: Disambiguate whether the funnel is in the sampled parameters or in the reconstructed transformed parameters

expected_findings: If transformed-param sigma is plotted: funnel is benign, no action needed, re-plot in z-space. If z-score vs log_sig_sig is plotted and still funneled: genuine pathology, proceed to parameterization diagnosis.

branches.if_observe: Corrected z-space plot still shows a funnel/correlation

action: Hypothesize a degeneracy among the systematic-trend terms and the unstructured-heterogeneity term, then propose a direct verification plot

branches.if_observe: no improvement

action: Move diagnosis to the UNCONSTRAINED scale and frame residual divergences as standard hierarchical degeneracies, not a probit-boundary artifact
discriminates: H-funnel/population-degeneracy vs H-boundary-probit-theory vs H-bernoulli-singularity vs H-accept_stat-signal
expected_findings: If genuine funnel/degeneracy: unconstrained pairs (theta_n vs tau, and mu vs tau) reveal the cramped neck; a high-GAMMA0_SD / extreme-GAMMA0 cluster is the population-scale degeneracy showing through. Boundary theory predicts a universal asymmetric-stepsize pathology for all probit models — expert says NO, the transform is in the likelihood and won't drive prior-interaction divergences. Bernoulli p=0/1 only bites with all-0 or all-1 data plus overflow, and gradients usually stay finite → not the cause. Low accept_stat on divergent draws is mechanically expected → no extra signal.
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model still throws a moderate number of divergences (~40), but none of the usual fingerprints show up: parallel-coordinates and pair

branches.if_observe: tight mu–tau ridge
branches.then: address the population-parameter degeneracy directly (tighter/structured priors, more individuals)

branches.if_observe: no clear unconstrained neck but divergences persist
branches.then: back up and rebuild incrementally (next move)

action: Restart from a simpler model and add structure incrementally until it breaks
discriminates: isolates WHICH structural layer (hierarchy vs heterogeneity vs specific subjects vs population priors) introduces the geometry, vs a global misspecification
expected_findings: Whichever layer's reintroduction first triggers divergences is the culprit; expensive full runs are replaced by fast minimal iterations. If divergences appear only at full subject count, it's a population-identification/weak-data problem; if at a specific subject, that subject needs its own parameterization.

branches.then: use per-subject mixed centering for those subjects

branches.if_observe: divergences only at large N with binary data

action: Characterize WHERE and WHEN the divergences occur — tie divergence frequency to the data regime (number of groups / strength of the likelihood) rather than to a single fixed cause.
discriminates: funnel-geometry-driven-by-weak-likelihood (H1) vs a fixed misspecification independent of N (H3)
expected_findings: H1: divergence rate is a monotone function of N (few groups -> severe funnel -> many divergences; many groups -> likelihood dominates -> funnel softens). H3 (missing beta prior): divergence rate roughly constant across N. Observed: divergences scale with small N -> favors H1.
situations: Hierarchical regression (group-level slope/intercept hyperparameters over a per-experiment effect, H ~ Normal(alpha + beta*k, sigma)) that diverges, with diverg
leaves: OP observes divergences are 'especially when N is small'; experts read this as the curvature of the posterior being higher in the weak-likelihood regime — the signature of a neck-of-the-funnel between

branches.if_observe: divergences vanish as N grows
branches.then: treat as funnel/curvature problem -> reparameterize the funnel (centered <-> non-centered), do not just add a slope prior

branches.if_observe: divergences persist at all N
branches.then: suspect identifiability / missing prior on beta or a structural misspecification

action: Recognize the centered/non-centered tradeoff as data-regime-dependent, not a bug to optimize away with a third trick — name the two distinct failure modes.
discriminates: two-sided regime-dependent geometry (H2) vs a single still-unsolved misspecification (H3/H5) the user keeps trying to patch
expected_findings: H2: each parameterization fails in exactly the opposite regime (centered diverges at small N, non-centered hits treedepth at large N / small sigma) — a clean crossover. H5: behavior would not split cleanly by regime. Observed: clean crossover -> confirms H2.
situations: Hierarchical regression (group-level slope/intercept hyperparameters over a per-experiment effect, H ~ Normal(alpha + beta*k, sigma)) that diverges, with diverg
leaves: Ben Bales: 'There's two different problems that happen.' Centered works with big data / strong likelihood, non-centered works with small data / weak likelihood; treedepth saturation is the non-centere

branches.if_observe: centered good for many groups, non-centered good for few groups
branches.then: split the analysis by regime — run centered where N is large, non-centered where N is small (OP's own proposed resolution, endorsed by the expert)

branches.if_observe: a single parameterization works everywhere
branches.then: no regime split needed

action: Audit the proposed shortcut analytically — write out the math of the transformed model and ask whether it changes the target distribution or merely rescales.
discriminates: QR-is-a-benign-reparameterization (rescaling, same posterior) vs QR-silently-changes-the-implied-prior-on-beta (H5)
expected_findings: Benign: the QR-rotated model is provably the same target up to a deterministic invertible map -> inferences on alpha unchanged by construction. H5: the transform imposes a different (often implicit, possibly improper) prior on beta -> answers could drift, and matching results are luck not correctness. Expert verdict: for the multilevel case the trick is suspect; check the math, prefer a principled alternative.
situations: Hierarchical regression (group-level slope/intercept hyperparameters over a per-experiment effect, H ~ Normal(alpha + beta*k, sigma)) that diverges, with diverg
leaves: Goodrich: QR of a vector gives Q = x / R11, i.e. just a rescaling. Bales: 'write out mathematically the difference in the two models' — verify the implied prior on beta rather than trusting that 'resu

branches.if_observe: the transform is a pure deterministic rescaling with unchanged prior
branches.then: QR is acceptable (as in standard rstanarm X-matrix QR)

branches.if_observe: the implied prior on beta is altered or unclear
branches.then: abandon QR as the fix; it is masking the geometry, not solving it

action: Reproduce on the eight-schools archetype and inspect pairs plots in the space the sampler actually works in.
discriminates: confirms the regime-dependent two-failure picture (H1+H2) by direct visualization across all four cells
expected_findings: If H1+H2 hold: centered-low diverges (funnel), centered-high clean, non-centered-low clean, non-centered-high hits treedepth — the 2x2 crossover. Any deviation would challenge the regime-split story.
situations: Hierarchical regression (group-level slope/intercept hyperparameters over a per-experiment effect, H ~ Normal(alpha + beta*k, sigma)) that diverges, with diverg

action: Classify whether the distribution to be reparameterized is a likelihood (data) or a hierarchical prior (parameter).
discriminates: H1/H2 — whether there is even an opportunity for NCP, vs the user confusing a likelihood for a hierarchical prior.
expected_findings: Under H1 the model would contain a parameter vector with a prior std that is itself a parameter. Under H2 the truncation sits only on Y in the model{} block and the parameters (beta, sigma) have no parent scale parameter.
situations: A truncated-normal regression (likelihood Y ~ normal(mu, sigma) T[0,1]) is producing many divergent transitions and biased parameter recovery on small-variance
leaves: Distinguish: NCP applies to a parameter whose prior depends on another unknown parameter (e.g. theta ~ normal(mu, tau)), NOT to the sampling distribution of observed data y. Ask the user to show the a

branches.then: Rule out NCP for the likelihood (H2): there is no opportunity for a non-centered parameterization; the request is a category error.

branches.if_observe: There is a parameter vector with a prior scale that is itself estimated.
branches.then: Proceed toward NCP on that parameter (H1).

action: Establish what the reported divergence coordinate actually represents in the NUTS implementation

expected_findings: Under 'reported point = sample, not error site' and 'last-doubling-discard biases away from problem': you find the kept state is the multinomial draw and the divergent final doubling is discarded, so the reported point is systematically displaced from the pathology. Under 'whole-trajectory property': no single point claim survives — confirmed by betanalpha/sakrejda that divergence is a property of the entire modified energy level set.
situations: Divergent transitions are scattered/spread out all over the already-sampled posterior rather than concentrated in one region, so the reported divergence locatio

action: Define a per-leapfrog-step energy-error diagnostic and pick the highest-error step as the candidate problem location
discriminates: Where in parameter space the energy error actually spikes, vs. where the harmless sample landed
expected_findings: Under the geometric-feature hypotheses: the highest-error steps cluster tightly on the pathological feature (funnel neck, a constraint edge, a numerically unstable band) even though the posterior samples are scattered. Under 'no real localization': error-step locations stay as diffuse as the samples.
situations: Divergent transitions are scattered/spread out all over the already-sampled posterior rather than concentrated in one region, so the reported divergence locatio
leaves: For every leapfrog step in every iteration (divergent or not), compute the Hamiltonian H = potential + kinetic at start and end and take the energy difference; identify the step contributing the most

branches.then: Localization works — proceed to validate on models with known geometry

branches.then: Do not take a hard argmax; multinomial-sample the red point across all steps weighted by log of energy error to down-weight the post-divergence blow-up

action: Validate the diagnostic on benchmark models with KNOWN problem geometry and compare against Stan's native divergence plot

On 8-schools the red points concentrate in the funnel neck. Stan's native plot, by contrast, scatters the same divergences more widely.

action: Reject the automatic non-terminating escalation loop; do the escalation by hand, one step at a time, investigating between steps.
discriminates: H1 (loop terminates: divergences monotonically vanish → step-size artifact, geometry was fine) vs H2 (loop fails to terminate or divergences re-appear/increase → structural pathology). Also exposes H4 (adaptation can't move much past 0.99 anyway).
expected_findings: Under H1 the divergences fall toward zero at modest adapt_delta (~0.9) and stay gone. Under H2 the divergences plateau, fail to clear, or even rise as resolution sharpens, and adapt_delta climbs without convergence — the exact symptom the OP hit after adding the extra term.

branches.if_observe: divergences vanish at modest adapt_delta and stay gone (loop would terminate)

branches.if_observe: divergences persist or grow as adapt_delta rises; loop won't terminate
branches.then: stop escalating — this is structural (H2); the loop would just burn compute recovering equivalently pathological fits. Move to fix the model.

branches.if_observe: max treedepth saturates as step size shrinks
branches.then: you must intervene on treedepth manually anyway; another sign automation is the wrong tool and the geometry is hard

action: Check whether the model belongs to a class where high adapt_delta / deep treedepth is expected and tolerated.
discriminates: H5 (legitimately hard, accept the tuning) vs H2/H3 (pathology that should be fixed structurally).
expected_findings: Under H5 the high adapt_delta/treedepth is intrinsic to the prior family and divergences are controllable; under H2/H3 the high tuning is a symptom of an avoidable funnel or weak identification.

branches.if_observe: ordinary hierarchical model that worsened after adding a term
branches.then: treat as H2/H3 and reparameterize / add priors

action: Probe adaptation sufficiency, e.g. via the leapfrog/treedepth history rather than divergence count alone.
discriminates: H4 (insufficient adaptation — divergences shrink and n_leapfrog drops with more warmup) vs H2 (true pathology — more warmup doesn't help).
expected_findings: Under H4, extending warmup flattens the metric/leapfrog history and divergences ease; under H2, the metric stabilizes but divergences remain because the geometry, not the tuning, is the problem.
situations: A model that fit cleanly at default adapt_delta=0.8 starts producing divergent transitions after warmup once an extra modelling term is added — and the other di

action: Confirm the speed asymmetry is the symptom, not a coincidence — treat the anomalously fast chain as the suspect and inspect its per-iteration sampler behavior rather than its parameter draws

expected_findings: Stuck chain: stepsize -> 0, treedepth -> 0, divergent__ -> 1, energy frozen at an enormous value. Healthy chains: stepsize O(1e-4), deep trees, energy relaxing. The contrast is stark and per-chain

branches.then: diagnose as a chain trapped in a bad region from initialization; next inspect the adapted mass matrix to confirm degeneracy

action: Characterize the geometry: separate the initialization/warmup transient from the steady-state divergences, and locate which dimensions drive the small step size.
discriminates: Initialization-transient hypothesis vs steady-state stiff-geometry hypothesis; and whether one/few coordinates or the global scale-heterogeneity is the binding constraint.
expected_findings: Stiff-geometry: lp__ keeps ringing for thousands of leapfrog steps before each refresh; a handful of tightly-identified dimensions coincide with the step-size floor. Pure-init: lp__ settles after the first handful of iterations and divergences vanish once warm. Multimodal: lp__ settles but chains disagree / get stuck at different plateaus.
situations: A high-dimensional model (thousands of params: Dirichlet/simplex micro-data, hierarchical SDE state-space, or count/RNA-seq) where some dimensions are extremely
leaves: Watch lp__ in the live CSV: note the initial overshoot/ringing as the chain falls from the deep-tail start into the typical set (the 'nitroglycerine' transient), and identify the band lp__ settles int

branches.if_observe: lp__ settles after a few iterations and post-warmup divergences are rare
branches.then: Treat as an initialization problem: short-treedepth burn-in / VB inits to reach the typical set fast, then switch to long treedepth

branches.if_observe: lp__ rings for thousands of steps and divergences persist post-warmup, tied to specific tight dimensions
branches.then: Treat as stiff mixed-scale geometry: pursue reparameterization / mass-matrix / metric remedies below

branches.if_observe: chains settle at different lp plateaus or neff is bimodal across chains
branches.then: Suspect effective multimodality / barrier; run many dispersed chains to detect it

action: Attempt reparameterization of the offending tightly-constrained coordinates.
discriminates: Whether the pathology is a fixable coordinate-system artifact (curable by reparameterization) vs an intrinsic, non-uniform stiffness that no single global reparameterization can remove.
expected_findings: If curable: divergences drop and step size recovers after reparameterization. If intrinsic: simplex constraints resist clean reparameterization, and because the tight dimension differs per data unit (different people/genes), no single global reparameterization helps; means can be rescaled to O(1) but the ~thousands of heterogeneous variances cannot all be hand-tuned.
situations: A high-dimensional model (thousands of params: Dirichlet/simplex micro-data, hierarchical SDE state-space, or count/RNA-seq) where some dimensions are extremely
leaves: Try the standard non-centered / rescaling moves and per-coordinate reparameterizations so each constrained entry is O(1)-scaled. For the simplex case the offending entries are near-zero Dirichlet comp

branches.if_observe: reparameterization removes divergences
branches.then: Done — it was a coordinate-system artifact

branches.if_observe: simplex/per-unit constraints can't be reparameterized and divergences persist
branches.then: Reparameterization is exhausted; escalate to adapt_delta and metric-based remedies

action: Decouple 'get into the typical set' from 'sample it': use cheap pre-conditioning (VB and/or short-treedepth burn-in) for inits, then reuse a good mass matrix for the production run.
discriminates: Whether the cost was dominated by the slow descent into the typical set / poor warmup mass-matrix estimate (curable by better inits + metric reuse) vs the intrinsic per-sample cost of the stiff geometry once warm.
expected_findings: VB rarely yields correct inference here (badly biased on these models) but DOES give non-explosive inits and removes the early 'nitroglycerine' transient. Short treedepth reaches the typical set far faster. But once warm, a few highly-identified dimensions still crash the step size, so production sampling can still take a day and 2 of 4 chains can still get stuck — i.e. the steady-state stiffness remains.

branches.if_observe: warm production run now samples cleanly with the reused metric
branches.then: It was largely a warmup/init/metric problem — ship the pipeline

branches.if_observe: warm run still collapses step size / sticks on a subset of chains
branches.then: Residual intrinsic stiffness; the only escapes are a better metric (Riemannian) or accepting an approximate sampler

action: Try an approximate, divergence-resistant sampler that diffuses across energy levels instead of tracking one level set (partial momentum refreshment / momentum-diffusion / thermostatted HMC with noisy SPSA gradients).
discriminates: Whether intentionally fuzzing the dynamics (adding entropy, tunneling through high-curvature regions) buys robustness without unacceptably corrupting the target — vs whether it just yields the wrong distribution and/or still fails exactly where curvature is worst.
expected_findings: On a benign multimodal toy it samples a slightly-entropy-inflated (fuzzed) version of the target and avoids divergences. BUT: per Bob/Radford-Neal, partial momentum refreshment adds little once NUTS is in play; random perturbations 'almost never help in high dimensions'; the fuzzed sampler does NOT sample the target; and it will not equilibrate the neck of a funnel (high curvature) — i.e. it fails in exactly the high-curvature region that motivated it.
situations: A high-dimensional model (thousands of params: Dirichlet/simplex micro-data, hierarchical SDE state-space, or count/RNA-seq) where some dimensions are extremely
leaves: Position update from momentum, momentum update from gradient + random perturbation + a viscous control force proportional to Hamiltonian error (mean-reverting to target energy), with a refresh trigger

branches.if_observe: approximate sampler avoids divergences and only mildly inflates entropy on benign geometry
branches.then: Consider it as a fast typical-set-finder, not a final sampler — plan a correction step

branches.if_observe: it samples the wrong distribution and chokes on the funnel neck / high curvature
branches.then: It cannot be the production sampler; a correction step is mandatory and must itself handle the curvature

action: Try to correct the approximate ensemble to the exact target with a local cleanup move (importance-subsample proportional to p(x), then diffusive Metropolis, then parallel tempering).
discriminates: Whether cheap local moves can repair the approximate sample's biases — vs whether they fail precisely at the geometry that defeated HMC (curvature, barriers, getting global mass proportions right).
expected_findings: The naive equal-temperature swap is WRONG: at equilibrium swapping one point each way changes nothing, and off-equilibrium it concentrates everything into high-density regions (self-corrected by author). Parallel tempering DOES fix the Q-Q kinks (tail proportions). But Metropolis cannot correct gross mass-proportion errors in finite time, cannot cross a narrow tail-entry barrier, and — per Bob/sakrejda — fails to sample correctly exactly where Stan diverges (high curvature). So the cleanup inherits the original geometric obstruction.
situations: A high-dimensional model (thousands of params: Dirichlet/simplex micro-data, hierarchical SDE state-space, or count/RNA-seq) where some dimensions are extremely
leaves: Importance idea: if the base sample were uniform on the epsilon typical-set band, subsample proportional to p(x). Since the fuzzed q(x) is unknown (not uniform), instead split the ensemble and run par

branches.if_observe: tempering fixes mild tail/weight biases on benign geometry
branches.then: Workable as polish only when the approximate sample already visits all masses in roughly-right proportion

branches.if_observe: barrier/curvature present so Metropolis can't traverse or correct proportions
branches.then: Cleanup fails at the same place HMC did — the approach does not solve the original divergence problem

action: decompose the E-BFMI formula and directly inspect its inputs per chain
discriminates: stuck-chain (var=0, 0/0->NaN) vs energy overflow (Inf/Inf) vs healthy edge case
expected_findings: Stuck-chain hyp: some chains return var(energy)=0 exactly. Overflow hyp: var would be Inf or NA from non-finite energy. Healthy hyp: all chains have moderate non-zero variance and warning is spurious.
situations: A subset of chains (2 of 4) throws a "NaN E-BFMI" warning after fitting a large hierarchical model; R-hat and ESS_bulk look fine for most parameters, no diverge
leaves: Recall E-BFMI = mean(squared consecutive energy__ diffs) / var(energy__) per chain; NaN needs 0/0 (Inf if only denom is 0). Directly compute per-chain energy variance: energy <- posterior::extract_var

branches.if_observe: Two chains return var(energy__)=0 exactly while others are non-zero (here: 20, 39.9, 0, 0)
branches.then: Confirm those chains are not exploring the energy distribution at all -> NaN is real, localize to the stuck chains; pivot to WHY the energy is frozen rather than dismissing the warning

branches.if_observe: Energy variance is Inf / non-finite
branches.then: Switch to the overflow hypothesis (Jacob's): hunt for a likelihood term blowing up energy at some iteration

action: cross-read the convergence summary as corroborating evidence for the energy finding

expected_findings: Shared-cause hyp: chains otherwise mix (good bulk R-hat/ESS) but a few extreme lp__/energy values live only in the two flagged chains, producing both the NA tail ESS and the energy degeneracy. Two-bug hyp: lp__ tail problem would be unrelated to which chains are energy-stuck.
situations: A subset of chains (2 of 4) throws a "NaN E-BFMI" warning after fitting a large hierarchical model; R-hat and ESS_bulk look fine for most parameters, no diverge
leaves: Note lp__ ess_tail=NA and energy ess_tail issues co-occur; Aki reads lp__ mean far from its 90% interval and sd >> mad as a strongly skewed lp__, and ties the zero-variance energy chains to a few extr

branches.if_observe: lp__ mixes in bulk but has rare extreme values isolated to the same chains flagged for energy

action: probe the sampler tuning diagnostics for a too-small step size signature

action: Test the initialization hypothesis directly by setting an explicit init for eta_tp2 and sweeping it across the threshold; observe whether divergences vanish and the chain migrates to the true value.

Under a true funnel (H1): a closer/smaller init would be SAFER, not more dangerous — the opposite of what is seen, which is the decisive discriminator.

action: Build a geometric/analytic model of how NUTS concatenates subtrees and ask when the endpoint U-turn check can fail to see a turn.

action: Compare rho-estimator definitions across implementations and check whether a shortened rho both fixes the resonance AND preserves reversibility.

Cross-gap view (terminal): shortened rho only NARROWS the looping window so the resonance is usually missed, not eliminated; dropping a boundary momentum makes the check ASYMMETRIC, breaking the requirement that checks be symmetric over all starting points -> biased invariant distribution unless extra states are retained. The two definitions agree only as eps->0.

action: Build a systematic test harness across model classes and measure ESS-per-gradient for the new checks vs nominal, then choose the right diagnostic estimand.

On x^2 (second moments): extra checks are uniformly >= nominal across all model classes, because the normal target shows strong antithetic behavior in x that masks the true sampling quality. Conclusion flips from 'inconclusive tradeoff' to 'clear win' once x^2 is the metric.

action: Flip the worst RV to model its complement (probability of the event NOT occurring) to swap the sign of its logodds
discriminates: model-geometry explanation (sign of logodds shouldn't matter) vs implementation-bug explanation (large +logodds breaks, large -logodds fine) — i.e. is the failure symmetric in the logodds sign?
expected_findings: If it's genuine geometry: flipping sign should not help (both tails equally hard). Found: divergences drop by an order of magnitude, and the residual divergences are other Beta RVs that also reach logodds ~19.0. So Beta(alpha=3.06,beta=0.25) diverges but the mirror Beta(alpha=0.25,beta=3.06) never does — the failure is ASYMMETRIC in the logodds sign. This asymmetry rules out a symmetric geometry pathology and points to an implementation bug triggered specifically at large positive logodds near 19.0.
situations: A hierarchical model with many Beta-distributed RVs (NUTS, target_accept=0.95) produces a very high divergence rate (~60% of samples) yet the posterior looks co

branches.then: this is not a modeling problem — isolate a minimal reproducer and inspect the Beta logp / sigmoid / invlogit implementation at logodds near 19

branches.if_observe: flipping made no difference (symmetric failure)
branches.then: treat as genuine boundary geometry and pursue a non-centered / heavier-tailed reparameterization instead

action: Isolate a minimal reproducer and read the sigmoid / invlogit implementation
discriminates: user model error vs a real numerical bug in the framework's sigmoid (final root-cause confirmation)
expected_findings: Found (ROOT CAUSE): the Theano sigmoid C code hardcodes `x > 19.0 ? 1.0 : ...`, snapping the sigmoid to exactly 1.0 for any logodds above 19.0 even though the true value differs from 1 up to ~36.6. This forces the Beta logp to a flat/degenerate region (effectively a derivative cliff) for one tail only, producing the asymmetric divergences. The pymc invlogit (with its eps fudge) compiles to a graph that doesn't use the stable softplus path either, compounding it.
situations: A hierarchical model with many Beta-distributed RVs (NUTS, target_accept=0.95) produces a very high divergence rate (~60% of samples) yet the posterior looks co

branches.if_observe: the framework clamps sigmoid to exactly 1.0 above a hardcoded 19.0
branches.then: fix upstream: correct the sigmoid cutoff / use the stable softplus path so log1p(-sigmoid) stays accurate, then verify divergences vanish

action: Reframe flat traces as whole-chain freezing from leapfrog/Hamiltonian divergence, not as proof of acceptance

expected_findings: H2: frozen stretches coincide with regions of high posterior curvature and near-zero acceptance; the WHOLE state is stuck there, and the 'some move' impression is the visual artifact from H3.
situations: HMC traceplots where SOME parameters freeze (flat traces, jumps too small to show in the y-axis digits) while OTHERS keep moving in the same iterations, and WHI
leaves: Flat stretches happen when stepsize is too large for the local curvature: even the first leapfrog step sends the Hamiltonian so far from its initial value that every proposal is rejected — possibly wi

branches.if_observe: freezing aligns with high-curvature regions and low acceptance
branches.then: curvature mismatch confirmed; route to reparameterization

action: Read extreme stepsize collapse as a STIFFNESS signal (large, position-varying condition number of the Hessian)
discriminates: fixable-by-better-metric/stepsize vs needs-reparameterization (H2 terminal)
expected_findings: H2: stepsize is rate-limited by the most-curved region; a static metric helps uniformly stiff posteriors but NOT ones whose conditioning changes across the space — symptom persists until the parameterization itself is changed.
situations: HMC traceplots where SOME parameters freeze (flat traces, jumps too small to show in the y-axis digits) while OTHERS keep moving in the same iterations, and WHI
leaves: If Stan must take stepsize this small to avoid divergence, one direction has far higher curvature than others (the system is stiff). When the condition number VARIES across the posterior, no single st

branches.if_observe: condition number is roughly constant across the posterior
branches.then: a denser/learned static metric may suffice

branches.if_observe: condition number varies across the posterior (the diagnosed case)
branches.then: static metric is hopeless; reparameterize the model

action: Reject the metric/stepsize-floor fix on detailed-balance and dynamical grounds
discriminates: H1-fixable-by-flooring vs fundamentally-unfixable-that-way (terminal)
expected_findings: H1-as-fix: a floor 'rescues' frozen dims. Reality: flooring stepsize -> persistent rejection / no movement; flooring/constraining M -> broken stationary distribution. Neither yields correct mixing.
situations: HMC traceplots where SOME parameters freeze (flat traces, jumps too small to show in the y-axis digits) while OTHERS keep moving in the same iterations, and WHI
leaves: Euclidean HMC requires a CONSTANT mass matrix M; making M (or the jump) depend on q[t], q[t+eps] breaks the detailed-balance/reversibility proof, so you lose the guarantee of sampling the right statio

branches.if_observe: user wants the floor only during ADAPTATION (warmup), not sampling
branches.then: milder, but still must SHOW it helps a real model and doesn't corrupt the learned metric; default is to reparameterize instead

branches.if_observe: user wants it during sampling
branches.then: rejected: violates detailed balance / won't move

action: Derive the exact numerical-freeze condition from the leapfrog position update
discriminates: true-underflow-freeze vs merely-tiny-but-nonzero-motion (closes H1 vs H3)
expected_findings: If the increment is below machine_eps*|q|, the coordinate is genuinely pinned and the right reading is 'reparameterize so the increments aren't that small', not 'floor the metric'. Otherwise the dim is moving (H3) and there is nothing to fix.
situations: HMC traceplots where SOME parameters freeze (flat traces, jumps too small to show in the y-axis digits) while OTHERS keep moving in the same iterations, and WHI
leaves: From q[t+1] = q[t] + (stepsize-scaled) momentum, the position is unchanged in finite precision when the increment is below machine precision relative to q[t] — i.e. roughly when machine_eps * |q[t]| e

branches.if_observe: increment < machine_eps*|q[t]| (true underflow)
branches.then: geometry is degenerate at machine precision -> reparameterize / rescale so the dimension carries meaningful scale

branches.if_observe: increment above that threshold
branches.then: not frozen; visual artifact (H3) — no algorithmic change warranted

action: Compare divergence behaviour across sampler backends to separate a backend bug from a geometry problem.
discriminates: H1 (backend incompatibility) vs H2/H3 (intrinsic geometry/priors).
expected_findings: Under H1: divergences are 100% and backend-specific; default sampler is clean. Under H2/H3: divergences would appear (partially) under the default sampler too, concentrated in specific parameters/regions.
situations: A model with a built-in/library composite likelihood (here pm.HurdleGamma) sampled fine under the default sampler but produces ~100% divergences under JAX-based
leaves: Note the signature: ALL draws diverge only under JAX backends while the default (C/python NUTS) sampler is fine — an all-or-nothing, backend-conditional pattern, not the partial, region-localized dive

branches.if_observe: Divergences only under JAX backends, default sampler clean (100% vs 0%)
branches.then: Treat as implementation/lowering incompatibility, not geometry; pursue a backend-compatible reimplementation of the likelihood (H1).

branches.if_observe: Default sampler also shows divergences localized to psi/scale
branches.then: Pivot to a true geometry/prior investigation (H2/H3): locate divergences, inspect the psi prior, consider reparameterization.

action: Reimplement the composite likelihood as a hand-written custom logp to bypass the broken built-in and unlock fast backends.
discriminates: Confirms H1 — if the hand-rolled equivalent samples cleanly under the same JAX backend, the fault was the library distribution, not the model's geometry.
expected_findings: Under H1 (true cause): the custom-logp model samples with 0 divergences and is fast under nutpie/JAX. If divergences had instead persisted, H1 would be wrong and a genuine geometry/prior problem (H2/H3) would be implicated.
situations: A model with a built-in/library composite likelihood (here pm.HurdleGamma) sampled fine under the default sampler but produces ~100% divergences under JAX-based
leaves: Replace pm.HurdleGamma with pm.CustomDist whose logp is pt.switch(value==0, log1p(-p), log(p) + Gamma.dist(alpha, beta=alpha/mu).logp), modeling the hurdle on the logit scale and the gamma mean on the

branches.if_observe: Custom logp samples fast with 0 divergences
branches.then: Root cause confirmed as the built-in distribution; proceed to scale and to add structure (hierarchy).

branches.if_observe: Custom logp still diverges
branches.then: Backend wasn't the whole story — investigate priors (esp. psi) and reparameterize the hurdle/gamma scales (H2/H3).

action: Diagnose the unidentified-subgroup pathology and pool group-level parameters so data-starved groups borrow strength.
discriminates: H5 (local identifiability from over-decoupling) vs a remaining global geometry issue.
expected_findings: Under H5: re-pooling the dims gives every group a population-informed gamma estimate while sampling stays clean (0 divergences). If divergences returned upon adding the hierarchy, a centered-vs-non-centered funnel (classic H2) would be the next suspect.
situations: A model with a built-in/library composite likelihood (here pm.HurdleGamma) sampled fine under the default sampler but produces ~100% divergences under JAX-based
leaves: Recognize that fully separating the parts leaves zero-success groups with an unidentified Gamma sub-model; fix by changing the dims of beta/alpha/mu from non_zero_groups back to the full groups dimens

branches.if_observe: Re-pooled dims yield estimates for all groups, still 0 divergences
branches.then: Resolved — hierarchical custom hurdle-gamma is the final model.

branches.if_observe: Divergences reappear after adding the group hierarchy
branches.then: Now a genuine funnel: locate divergences in the group scale, reparameterize the random effects non-centered.

action: isolate the offending term by ablation

action: contrast model block vs generated quantities

action: derive an exact log-space identity
discriminates: produces the stable replacement for H2 while preserving the exact model (no added constant)
expected_findings: Algebraically exact, evaluates on the log scale, no underflow because each piece is a log of a stable primitive; ordering constraint guarantees log_diff_exp argument is positive.

leaves: Rewrite the difference as (e^{-z2}-e^{-z1})/((1+e^{-z1})(1+e^{-z2})), giving log_diff_exp(-z2,-z1) - log1p_exp(-z1) - log1p_exp(-z2); wrap the per-cycle terms (with log(R), 2log(R) weights) in log_sum

branches.if_observe: ordering constraint z_1<z_2... can be argued from mu_1<mu_2...
branches.then: the log_diff_exp arguments stay valid; proceed to implement

action: diagnose the failed implementation against the actual domain of z
discriminates: wrong-primitive (log1p vs log1p_exp) and wrong-domain-assumption as the cause of the burn, not the identity itself
expected_findings: log1p(x) needs x>-1 and fails on the real z range; log1p_exp(x)=log(1+e^x) is defined everywhere and is the term that actually appears in the sigmoid denominator. Swapping it makes the identity valid over the whole z domain.
situations: Divergences (up to half of iterations) appear during prior predictive checks the moment a log() of a CDF-like quantity enters the model block; the divergences v
leaves: Implementation used log1p(-z) (and the mental model that z>0). But z = 0.07056 x^3 + 1.5976 x can be < -1 when time << mu, so log1p(-z) takes an argument < -1 -> invalid; the correct primitive is log1

branches.if_observe: log1p fed a value < -1 / NaN
branches.then: replace log1p(-z) with log1p_exp(-z); recheck the ordering-constraint sign but note it cancels

action: Trace the reverse-mode autodiff contributions to d/dsigma from the two terms (normal_lpdf and log(sigma)) and check for cancellation at tiny sigma.
discriminates: catastrophic-cancellation hypothesis vs analytic-singularity/divergent-gradient hypothesis
expected_findings: EXPECTED (Martin Modrak's diagnosis): normal_lpdf(x|0,sigma) reverse pass adds a term including -1/sigma to d/dsigma, while the log(sigma) Jacobian adds +1/sigma. Analytically these cancel, but they arrive from DIFFERENT autodiff sources, so for sigma=3e-17, 1/sigma ~ 3e16 swamps the ~O(1-10) real gradient: e.g. 10 + 1/sigma - 1/sigma can evaluate to 12 or 0 -- catastrophic cancellation. The gradient w.r.t. sigma becomes garbage (either huge -> all proposals diverge/reject, or ~0 -> cannot move), explaining the lock-in. Confirmed by the lp__ vs log(sigma) pairs plot showing a ridge slope ~ n_grp/2 * log(sigma).

In practice (Colab test): works at LOW noise and samples a tight band around the true frequency, but as noise power approaches signal power, other spectral peaks reappear and sampling becomes erratic — so windowing is not competitive with classical estimators in the noisy regime

branches.then: window-sidelobe cause confirmed; usable for clean batched data

branches.if_observe: peaks reappear near SNR~1 / realtime or time-varying-rate needs
branches.then: windowing impairs time-varying-rate and online updating; fall back to marginalization or sequential MC

action: Consider non-gradient / alternative-domain samplers (slice-based Gibbs; Lomb-Scargle periodogram; full-spectrum sampling) and sequential MC for realtime
discriminates: Sampler-limitation (H4) vs intrinsic-posterior multimodality
expected_findings: NUTS uses local gradient — efficient for unimodal, can get trapped when scaling is wrong; slice/Gibbs would not suffer the multimodality. Lomb-Scargle handles uneven spacing for the point estimate. For realtime/time-varying rate, Stan's batch HMC is the wrong tool — sequential MC (TFP, in-pipeline Stan SMC) or sampling a whole spectrum (simpler geometry, high-dim OK) is the path
situations: Fitting a noisily-measured sinusoid (estimate frequency/phase/amplitude) in Stan/HMC. The likelihood is massively multimodal along the frequency axis — not just

branches.if_observe: only a point frequency estimate needed

branches.if_observe: full Bayesian posterior + realtime + time-varying rate
branches.then: sequential MC; or reframe to sample a full spectrum rather than a 2-param sine through a complex geometry

action: Reconsider/retract: separate the GP's variance component (magnitude) from its correlation component (length-scale).
discriminates: variance-component (R2D2-eligible) vs correlation-parameter (not eligible)
expected_findings: Confirms H-magnitude-only and kills H-lengthscale-R2D2. For length-scales, recommend instead a thick-tailed, mass-near-0 prior on the inverse length-scale (favoring long length-scales / less nonlinearity); regularized horseshoe as an option.
situations: User is hand-coding a BKMR-style model in Stan: a GP (exponentiated-quadratic kernel with ARD inverse-length-scales over multiple exposures) added to a linear t
leaves: Length-scale affects prior variance only indirectly and is not a variance-partition target; at xi=xj the kernel reduces to the magnitude (sigma), so variance is governed by magnitude. R2D2 = joint Dir

action: Reframe: decouple prior specification from the selection decision; do not encode selection in the prior.
discriminates: prior-as-selection (bad) vs post-hoc decision analysis (good)
expected_findings: Under dependent covariates, marginal inclusion probabilities are diluted (dropping any one of correlated vars barely hurts) -> length-scale/posterior-prob selection unreliable. Steers user to predictive-discrepancy ranking instead.
situations: User is hand-coding a BKMR-style model in Stan: a GP (exponentiated-quadratic kernel with ARD inverse-length-scales over multiple exposures) added to a linear t
leaves: Prior should reflect prior information; variable selection is a separate decision/loss step done AFTER inference. Reading relevance off length-scale posteriors is explicitly shown to be a poor selecti

action: Substitute a PSEUDO-VARIANCE for sigma^2 in the R2D2 scale construction.
discriminates: valid R2-interpretation vs degraded-to-global-local-shrinkage
expected_findings: Pseudo-variance R2D2 works empirically (SBC-checked by others).
situations: User is hand-coding a BKMR-style model in Stan: a GP (exponentiated-quadratic kernel with ARD inverse-length-scales over multiple exposures) added to a linear t
leaves: Use pseudo-variance from Piironen & Vehtari (2017) horseshoe paper sec 3.5 / Appendix B (idea traced to Neal 1996 App A.4). Bernoulli-logit: pseudo_var = 1/(mu(1-mu)). Without including ALL signal var

action: Break the circular dependency by making the pseudo-variance independent of model parameters.
discriminates: parameter-dependent pseudo_var (circular, hard geometry) vs data-fixed pseudo_var (no cycle, easier sampling)
expected_findings: Divergences removed once pseudo_var is fixed from data; sampling easier. (Intercept-based pseudo_var also viable but can fail for zero-inflated/mixture cases.)
situations: User is hand-coding a BKMR-style model in Stan: a GP (exponentiated-quadratic kernel with ARD inverse-length-scales over multiple exposures) added to a linear t
leaves: Use the DATA-BASED approximation: pseudo_var = 1/(mu_bar(1-mu_bar)) with mu_bar = mean(y), computed ONCE in transformed data (or passed in as data). Confirmed by davkoh's simulations; same trick used

branches.if_observe: still divergences with data-based pseudo_var
branches.then: look elsewhere -- weakly-informed length-scales / linear-vs-GP non-identifiability (next move)

action: Correct the dot-product covariance usage: beta is integrated OUT analytically, not kept as parameters; scale by the prior SD.
discriminates: beta-still-explicit (double-counted/non-identified) vs beta-marginalized-correctly
expected_findings: With beta correctly marginalized into dot-product covariance, the identifiability issue and zero-avoidance disappear; remaining cost is Stan's covariance-GP autodiff (K*N^2 nodes) being slow but feasible at this small N,K.
situations: User is hand-coding a BKMR-style model in Stan: a GP (exponentiated-quadratic kernel with ARD inverse-length-scales over multiple exposures) added to a linear t

action: Reproduce on a minimal/adversarial target and vary the angular concentration of the likelihood
discriminates: whether the pathology is intrinsic to the manifold embedding vs a benign false alarm
expected_findings: Under H6/H2, a narrow angular likelihood (sharp phase) reliably triggers divergences; a uniform/diffuse target is nearly artifact-free. Under a 'false alarm' reading the divergences would not track concentration.

leaves: spinkney's sine-wave-phase model (unit_vector[2] phase_helper, amplitude, noise); betanalpha's intuition: uniform-on-circle target makes the 0=2pi exclusion negligible, concentrated target makes it bi

branches.if_observe: divergences scale with angular concentration / vanish for uniform targets
branches.then: treat as intrinsic manifold artifact, pursue reparameterization (H3) and radial-geometry analysis (H2)

branches.if_observe: divergences independent of target shape
branches.then: suspect a coding/Jacobian bug (H5) before blaming the manifold

action: Re-fit the same model in the candidate angle parameterization and check divergences + parameter recovery across DIFFERENT true phase values
discriminates: H3 (polar fixes it cleanly) vs H4/H5 (polar has its own seam artifact or Jacobian bug)
expected_findings: Under H3, divergences disappear and it runs ~2x faster. Under H5, a wrong target+=log(sin theta) and a 0..pi (instead of 0..2pi) bound silently fail for phases outside 0..pi (e.g. -pi/2). Under H4, phases on the seam (phase=0, i.e. on the theta boundary) give chain-dependent, multimodal, biased results.
situations: Sampling a constrained unit vector (a point on a sphere/circle, e.g. a phase angle or direction) in HMC/Stan via the built-in unit_vector type (normalize a late
leaves: 2D: unit_vec=(sin theta, cos theta), theta in (0,2pi); for N>2 keep ONE 0..2pi angle and the rest 0..pi. Test phase=pi/2 (passes), phase=-pi/2 (fails until bound fixed to 0..2pi), phase=0 (exposes sea

branches.if_observe: fails for phase=-pi/2 with a Jacobian term present

branches.if_observe: different chains disagree at phase=0, combined samples only 'accidentally' correct
branches.then: confirm H4: polar has a seam; demote it to a special-case, not a safe default

action: Decompose the embedding into augment-with-radius-prior + bijective scaling, and inspect which density terms survive
discriminates: H1 (origin singularity is the culprit) vs H2 (radial pinching/funnel from concentration of measure is the culprit)
expected_findings: Mapping x->y=r*x with Jacobian -n log r, plus Stan's implicit Chi(n+1) radius prior (n log r - r^2/2), makes the +-n log r terms cancel exactly, leaving log pi(y) = -||y||^2/2. A singularity remains at y=0; but for concentrated angular likelihoods the posterior over y forms a 'wedge/funnel' pinched toward small radii — and this asymmetric smearing (worse to large radii in high-d) is the deeper problem, not just overlap with 0.
situations: Sampling a constrained unit vector (a point on a sphere/circle, e.g. a phase angle or direction) in HMC/Stan via the built-in unit_vector type (normalize a late
leaves: Seth_Axen: free to choose any proper pi(r); Stan implicitly picks Chi(n+1). betanalpha: outer arc grows exponentially wider than inner arc as dim increases, so even excluding the y=0 vertex the wedge

branches.if_observe: divergences persist even when the origin is strongly excluded
branches.then: accept H2 — the funnel/pinch, not the singularity, dominates; reach for a metric/radius-prior remedy

branches.if_observe: divergences vanish once mass is pushed off zero
branches.then: H1 is sufficient for this target; a simple radius prior suffices

action: Replace the implicit Chi radius prior with an explicit concentrated radius prior on ||y||, and tie its width to the model-posterior / metric choice
discriminates: whether radial-prior tuning is enough, and how it interacts with diagonal vs dense inverse metric
expected_findings: A normal(1, 0.05) prior on r pushes mass off 0 and narrows the donut, removing many divergences with little speed cost. With a DIAGONAL metric a very narrow model-posterior makes the sphere a narrow ridge -> high treedepth, so the radius width should match the posterior width; a DENSE (or 'partially dense', per-unit-vector block) metric captures the global correlation and reduces the tuning need. Caveat (betanalpha): nonlinear curvature can't be fixed by any static metric, so general tuning stays hard.
situations: Sampling a constrained unit vector (a point on a sphere/circle, e.g. a phase angle or direction) in HMC/Stan via the built-in unit_vector type (normalize a late
leaves: Raoul-Kima: normal(1,0.05) default, normal(1,0.005) for very narrow posteriors; jacobian-on-r and exact prior shape don't matter much. spinkney's adversarial test: unit_vector[2*K] with x[1:K]~N(0,.1)

branches.if_observe: narrow radius prior + dense/partial-dense metric clears divergences and treedepth
branches.then: good enough as an embedded fix; document the metric requirement

branches.if_observe: tuning the radius prior per-model is needed and fragile
branches.then: betanalpha's caution: not a great general 'type'; prefer coding the transform directly per application

action: Answer the interpretation question, then pivot to whether the estimate is trustworthy given the design
discriminates: H1 (interpretation) vs the deeper validity questions (H2-H5)
expected_findings: cor() on an A-structured varying intercept IS the genetic correlation with no post-processing; but 'is it interpretable as genetic' depends on design and on the divergences being resolved first

action: Strip the model: drop fixed covariates, drop to one response, and swap the random term among (1||ID), (1|p|ID), (1|gr(ID,cov=A)) to see which configuration removes divergences
discriminates: fixed-effect/prior cause vs structural (random-effect / variance) cause; H4 (prior) vs H2 (structural non-identifiability)
expected_findings: Dropping covariates does NOT help; univariate is clean; but ALL of (1||ID), (1|p|ID), (1|gr(ID,cov=A)) variants in the bivariate still diverge -> points away from a single fixable term toward a structural variance issue present whenever an individual-level varying intercept is added
situations: A brms multivariate (mvbind) animal / quantitative-genetics model with a group-level term (1|p|gr(ID, cov=A)) over a relatedness/pedigree matrix A produces a co

branches.if_observe: divergences vanish when the individual-level random effect is removed entirely

branches.if_observe: divergences persist across all random-term variants
branches.then: the problem is not a specific correlation prior but the variance decomposition itself given single observations

action: Raise LKJ eta (lkj(1)->lkj(20)) and watch divergence count and whether key parameters move
discriminates: H4 (prior cures) vs H2 (prior only brute-forces divergences away without curing the underlying degeneracy)
expected_findings: Divergences decrease as eta rises but do not fully disappear; population-level effects and rescor barely change -> the prior is masking, not curing; a divergence-free fit obtained this way is not trustworthy because rejected divergences act like an extra prior on the bad region
situations: A brms multivariate (mvbind) animal / quantitative-genetics model with a group-level term (1|p|gr(ID, cov=A)) over a relatedness/pedigree matrix A produces a co

action: Give the non-genetic correlation its own home: add a second uncorrelated-with-A varying-intercept term (1|p2|ID2) (duplicate-ID workaround) and/or use rescor=TRUE under a gaussian family

situations: A brms multivariate (mvbind) animal / quantitative-genetics model with a group-level term (1|p|gr(ID, cov=A)) over a relatedness/pedigree matrix A produces a co

branches.if_observe: skew_normal family blocks rescor

action: Use the se(error, sigma=FALSE) addition term to fix within-individual (measurement) variability at a theory-derived value so only the between-individual sd is estimated; for repeated-measure traits feed the observed SE of the mean

action: Re-derive the Laplace marginal from the identity p(theta|y) = p(x,y,theta)/p(x|y,theta) evaluated at x=x*, keeping ALL terms: likelihood + latent prior MINUS the Gaussian-approximation normalizer (the log-determinant term).

Restoring poisson_log_lpmf(y|mode) + normal_lpdf(mode|0,sigma) - normal_lpdf(mode|mode,1/sqrt(prec)) (i.e. -0.5*dot_self(mode)/sigma^2 - M*log(sigma) - 0.5*sum(log(laplace_precisions))) yields the right marginal.

action: Re-initialize the inner solver from a data-driven warm start instead of xzero=0: xzero = log((sums+0.1)/number_of_samples) (log observed mean), guarding empty categories; compare against init at 0, +/-1, +/-0.1.

expected_findings: init=0 almost always fails; log-observed-mean (or +/-1 with correct sign) almost always succeeds (<=1-2 failures); +/-0.1 fails 5-10x. Also: multiplying the gradient by sigma^2 makes the Hessian small as sigma->0, hitting max_num_steps; the unscaled gradient is better-conditioned.

Actual outcome here: once the target+Hessian+warm-start are fixed, NUTS mixes cleanly (Rhat~1, healthy n_eff), so the inconsistent-Hamiltonian concern was not the operative cause for this model.
