action: Reframe sampling the GP as constructing a matrix square root L of K and drawing u = L z with z ~ N(0,I); ask whether L can be built directly at lower rank instead of via Cholesky.
discriminates: Whether the bottleneck can be attacked purely as linear algebra (cheaper sqrt) vs. needing fewer latent parameters too.
expected_findings: L need only satisfy L L' = K (square roots are non-unique); an eigen/Mercer decomposition gives an alternative L. A truncated rank-M L is NxM, built in O(NM), bypassing both O(N^2) matrix build and O(N^3) Cholesky, and shrinks the latent vector from N to M.
situations: A latent Gaussian-process model with a non-Gaussian likelihood (e.g. squared-exponential/RBF kernel feeding a bernoulli_logit / Poisson outcome) becomes prohibi
leaves: Non-centered GP parameterization u = L*z, z~std_normal; for RBF the eigenfunctions are Hermite polynomials scaled by sqrt(eigenvalue), buildable recursively (Karhunen-Loeve / Mercer eq. 4.37 in Rasmus

branches.if_observe: Latent dimension itself (not Cholesky) is the sampler bottleneck
branches.then: Low-rank M<<N expansion is the lever; choose M from the signal's frequency content (low-frequency signal -> few modes)

branches.if_observe: Kernel is stationary and you only need a prior draw / feature map
branches.then: Use Random Fourier Features (sin/cos of sampled frequencies) as the equivalent, simpler-to-code sqrt

action: Instrument the approximation: monitor reconstruction error of the kernel online and inspect where it fails.
discriminates: Whether the truncation rank M and the 'scale' hyperparameter are adequate, and whether error is uniform or localized.
expected_findings: max|cov_exp_quad(x,sigma,l) - L L'| (in generated quantities, on a subset of x to avoid an O(N^2) rebuild dominating runtime) reveals error that is NOT uniform: it blows up for points far from the origin. The eigenfunctions go to ~0 outside |x|~1.2, so the approximate kernel is non-stationary and decays to zero off-center.
situations: A latent Gaussian-process model with a non-Gaussian likelihood (e.g. squared-exponential/RBF kernel feeding a bernoulli_logit / Poisson outcome) becomes prohibi
leaves: Error map / log10-error plot; first ~10 eigenfunctions plotted scaled by sqrt(eigenvalue) show the support window; M=10 ~minutes, M=20 drives error to ~1e-14 but scales worse-than-linearly in M.

branches.if_observe: Error large near boundaries / for off-center data
branches.then: Center x to mean zero and rescale domain into the eigenfunctions' support (e.g. map 0-48 min -> -0.5..0.5); raise M if needed

branches.if_observe: Error uniformly small after centering
branches.then: Approximation is trustworthy; proceed to inference and predictions via length-M dot products

branches.if_observe: Signal is high-frequency / rough (e.g. Wiener-like, eigenvalues decay only ~1/k^2)
branches.then: Spectral truncation converges slowly (sqrt-eigenvalues ~1/k); a spectral basis is the wrong tool — consider local/wavelet or Markov (spline) representations

action: Audit the feature-map normalization against the source theorem before trusting it as a GP draw.
discriminates: Whether the basis is correctly scaled so that z z' actually approximates the target kernel k.
expected_findings: Inherited RFF scale of sqrt(2/N) (N = #data) is wrong; correct factor is 1/sqrt(K) where K = number of sampled frequencies (the paper's D = 2K, so sqrt(2/D) = 1/sqrt(K)). A wrong global scale is partly absorbed by the amplitude parameters but should be fixed; fhat is a dot product of the feature vector z(x) with coefficients = an approximate GP prior draw (Mercer feature map z z' = k).
situations: A latent Gaussian-process model with a non-Gaussian likelihood (e.g. squared-exponential/RBF kernel feeding a bernoulli_logit / Poisson outcome) becomes prohibi
leaves: Cross-check vs Rahimi-Recht 'Random Features for Large-Scale Kernel Machines' Thm 1 / Alg 1 and 'On the Error of Random Fourier Features'; non-centered omega = omega_var * bandwidth so the sampled ban

branches.if_observe: Scale uses #data instead of #frequencies
branches.then: Replace with 1/sqrt(K); amplitudes will re-accommodate but the prior is now correctly calibrated

action: Ablate the model into its named structural pieces and fit each in isolation
discriminates: Which structural component (multivariate coupling vs hierarchical AR process vs loadings) is the source of the bad geometry
expected_findings: If a reduced sub-model samples cleanly, the removed piece owns the pathology (identifiability hypothesis); if even P=1 SV won't fit, the AR/scale construction itself is broken (mis-specification hypothesis)
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Tell the user to remove the multivariate part, or remove the hierarchical part, and see which sub-model samples cleanly; build back up from a 1-component (P=1) version, then add multivariate output wi

branches.if_observe: removing hierarchical/multivariate part fixes it
branches.then: localize to that block and inspect its parameterization

branches.if_observe: even the simplest 1-component SV won't recover scale
branches.then: suspect the AR-scale construction or the likelihood derivation itself

action: Treat any behavioral difference between mathematically-equivalent programs as a red flag and demand both exact sources to diff
discriminates: Genuine statistical effect vs a coding error vs a compiler/codegen bug
expected_findings: If equivalent programs truly differ, there is a bug somewhere; a diff should reveal either a user typo or a Stan-side miscompile
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Reparameterization/IO should change cost but not the posterior; ask the user to post both .stan files verbatim so the discrepancy can be reproduced and located

branches.if_observe: diff shows only cosmetic bracketing differences
branches.then: escalate suspicion to a Stan compiler bug and reproduce across versions

branches.if_observe: diff shows a real semantic difference (e.g. wrong declared size)
branches.then: that is the bug; the correct-size variant is the one to trust

action: Reproduce the size-mismatch across Stan versions and file an upstream compiler issue; pick the variant whose declared dimension matches the math as the trusted one
discriminates: Whether the cross-variant discrepancy is a toolchain bug vs a remaining statistical issue
expected_findings: Bug isolated and reported; removes the variant discrepancy as a confound so attention returns to the still-present divergences/identifiability
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Confirmed mismatched-size assignment was silently allowed; reproduced in 2.23-rc but not rstan 2.19.3 (regression that disappeared then returned); filed stanc3 issue #523; advise using the non-mu (cor

branches.if_observe: after using the trusted variant divergences persist
branches.then: the toolchain bug was a side issue; pursue the identifiability/geometry track

action: Swap heavy-tailed priors for normals and check whether divergences drop
discriminates: Whether the divergence source is heavy-tail geometry vs deeper non-identifiability
expected_findings: Heavy-tail hypothesis predicts a clear divergence drop after going normal; if divergences barely move, the problem is structural not tail-driven
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Replace the Cauchy/half-Cauchy on tau with std_normal(); if it helps, tighten priors further and justify the tightening with prior predictive checks rather than guessing

branches.if_observe: normal prior helps but does not zero out divergences
branches.then: tighten priors via prior predictive checks, then look for non-identifiability

action: Read the constraint trick as diagnostic, not a fix; reason about why a constraint changes initialization and ask whether posterior mass is jamming the boundary
discriminates: Constraint genuinely required by the math (stationarity |scale|<1) vs a crutch hiding non-identifiability or a bad init region
expected_findings: If estimates still collapse to near-identical values across dimensions even with the constraint, the parameter is non-identified; if posterior piles at the boundary, the constraint is fighting the likelihood
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Stan inits uniformly in [-2,2] on the unconstrained scale, so a (0,1) constraint relocates inits; if the constraint 'fixes' sampling, the real issue may be initialization/identifiability, and mass pus

branches.if_observe: per-dimension scale estimates all collapse to the same value
branches.then: non-identifiability confirmed; consider removing/simplifying the scale term

branches.if_observe: posterior mass piles against the upper bound
branches.then: the constraint is discarding information; relax it

action: Count degrees of freedom against the likelihood and test whether a variance term can be dropped or shared
discriminates: Whether the model is fundamentally non-identified vs merely hard to sample
expected_findings: If sharing/removing the scale restores recovery, it was redundant DoF; if even shared/removed it still fails, the pathology is elsewhere (likelihood form)
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Sketch the generative form (x ~ N(0,Sigma); y ~ N(mu, Sigma_diag); z = x+y) and argue you cannot identify two separate covariances plus a mean from one observed sum; propose dropping the extra scale t

branches.if_observe: fixing scale equal across dimensions still gives biased loc and no identifiability
branches.then: redundancy is not the whole story; verify the likelihood/gradients next

action: Drop below the sampler and compare raw log-density gradients of the two programs at identical unconstrained parameter values
discriminates: Whether the two programs encode the same target density (bug) vs the divergences being a genuine geometry problem common to both
expected_findings: Identical gradients => no codegen bug, both encode the same posterior, so the remaining problem is statistical geometry; differing gradients => a real program difference to hunt down
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Use grad_log_prob on both models for the same unconstrained vector and compare element-wise (with abs to stop errors cancelling); a single warmup/post-warmup draw suffices since gradients depend only

branches.if_observe: gradients match except where absolute gradient magnitude is ~1e16
branches.then: differences are floating-point noise in extreme tails; conclude gradients equal and return to the identifiability track

branches.if_observe: gradients genuinely differ at moderate magnitudes
branches.then: a real semantic divergence remains; diff the programs again

action: Verify the reference likelihood against the primary source papers rather than trusting the manual
discriminates: User's model mis-specified (wrong likelihood) vs correctly specified but non-identified/poorly-conditioned
expected_findings: If the manual derivation is wrong, fixing the likelihood could resolve everything; if eps is white-noise N(0,1) the manual is fine and mis-specification is ruled out
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Re-derive the SV observation density: question the manual's jump from y_t = eps_t * exp(h_t/2) to y_t ~ N(0, exp(h_t/2)); check Kim-Shephard-Chib and Aguilar-West / Lopes-Carvalho to see what distribu

branches.if_observe: paper specifies eps_t white noise N(0,1)
branches.then: manual derivation is correct; mis-specification ruled out, refocus on identifiability/multimodality

branches.if_observe: paper uses a different innovation distribution
branches.then: correct the Stan likelihood accordingly

action: Diagnose tiny min-Neff as multimodality via per-element across-chain marginal posteriors, and classify the modes
discriminates: Benign label/sign multimodality (harmless equivalent modes) vs genuinely distinct competing solutions
expected_findings: Equivalent modes => impose an ordering/sign constraint to collapse them; distinct modes => deeper model problem, no clean fix
situations: A multivariate hierarchical / dynamic factor stochastic-volatility model keeps producing divergences even after the user maxes out every sampler knob. Structure
leaves: Look at lowest Neff per matrix element (e.g. Sigma[i,j]), plot per-chain marginal posteriors (shinystan), and decide whether modes are genuinely different vs equivalent relabelings/sign-flips inherent

branches.if_observe: modes are reorderings/sign-flips of the same numbers
branches.then: add an identifying constraint (e.g. ordering) to pick one mode

branches.if_observe: modes are genuinely different solutions
branches.then: no easy fix; reconsider model; or accept and report

branches.if_observe: a parameter just has thick tails and hasn't converged
branches.then: run longer / tighten its prior rather than treating it as multimodality

action: Compute p_loo and compare it against the actual number of parameters p and the sample size n (the p_loo-vs-p / p-vs-n/5 decision tree)
discriminates: Routes among H1-H4 — separates true misspecification from benign over-flexibility
expected_findings: H1: p_loo << p. H2/H3: p_loo > p (split by whether p<<n or p>n/5). H4: p_loo < p AND p > n/5. Counting p itself is the hard part in hierarchical/latent models — count parameters in the model's parameters block first, then refine to observations-influencing-each-parameter.
situations: A model's PSIS-LOO output (loo() / arviz.loo) shows high Pareto k values (k > 0.7 "bad"/"very bad" counts nonzero) and/or a warning that the estimated Pareto sh
leaves: Aki's quick-note tree (post 1). Refining p: for a simple hierarchical model count all params in the parameters block then refine; for GP/latent-variable models p>n because each obs has a latent. brms

branches.if_observe: p_loo << p
branches.then: Suspect H1 misspecification; go run PPC (it should reveal the problem); consider overdispersed model or added structure

branches.if_observe: p_loo > p
branches.then: H2/H3 bad misspecification; check p vs n/5 to decide whether PPC can catch it

branches.if_observe: p_loo < p and p > n/5
branches.then: H4 likely — flexible model / weak population prior; high k may be unavoidable even for the true model

action: Check the number of effective posterior draws (ESS) and whether khat is inflated by Monte Carlo variability
discriminates: H5 (sampling noise) vs a genuine model/LOO problem
expected_findings: With large n and only ~2000 draws, some khats are large purely from MC variability; more/longer chains shrink them. If high k survives many effective draws, it is a real influence/flexibility issue not noise.
situations: A model's PSIS-LOO output (loo() / arviz.loo) shows high Pareto k values (k > 0.7 "bad"/"very bad" counts nonzero) and/or a warning that the estimated Pareto sh

branches.if_observe: few draws / low ESS
branches.then: Re-run with more or longer chains and recheck khat before concluding anything (Aki to schen5, post 9)

branches.if_observe: high k persists with ample ESS
branches.then: Rule out H5; proceed to influence inspection / exact LOO

action: Inspect those specific high-khat observations to understand why they are highly influential
discriminates: Outlier/leverage point vs systematic problem; deepens model/data understanding
expected_findings: High khat = leave-one-out posterior differs greatly from full posterior so importance sampling (and waic) fail; often these are outliers or points in sparse regions/small groups. A small proportion does not by itself certify a good fit — it means that small proportion is highly influential.
situations: A model's PSIS-LOO output (loo() / arviz.loo) shows high Pareto k values (k > 0.7 "bad"/"very bad" counts nonzero) and/or a warning that the estimated Pareto sh

branches.if_observe: they are clear outliers / tiny-group members
branches.then: Consider robust/overdispersed likelihood or more informative priors; or accept and compute their contribution exactly

branches.if_observe: no obvious reason
branches.then: Treat as genuine influence; do not ignore based on count alone — use exact LOO for those points

action: Read elpd_diff against its SE and reason about the direction of PSIS-LOO bias
discriminates: Whether the model-comparison conclusion is robust to the unreliable LOO computation
expected_findings: With many high k's, elpd_loo for the flexible model is optimistic, so the true difference is even smaller. If the difference is already not significant (|elpd_diff| < ~2 SE), better computation will not flip it. schen5: elpd_diff 34.3 ± 16.1 with optimism bias → don't over-trust the partial-pooling edge.
situations: A model's PSIS-LOO output (loo() / arviz.loo) shows high Pareto k values (k > 0.7 "bad"/"very bad" counts nonzero) and/or a warning that the estimated Pareto sh

branches.if_observe: difference not significant given SE
branches.then: Conclude no decisive winner; better LOO computation unlikely to change it

branches.if_observe: difference significant but flexible model has many bad k
branches.then: Recompute the flagged points exactly (reloo / K-fold) before trusting it

action: Replace the failed importance-sampling step with exact computation, and/or reduce posterior flexibility
discriminates: Whether a trustworthy ELPD can be recovered, and whether flexibility was a prior artifact (H6) vs intrinsic (H4)
expected_findings: reloo / brute-force exact LOO on the high-k points gives reliable elpd (full brute-force is simpler to implement than reloo and fine if speed is not an issue). Setting proper informative priors collapses p_loo and k — Fatih's case: p_loo 159, 27 bad k → after proper priors p_loo 92, essentially all k good (posts 27-36). Alternative for latent models: marginalize latents analytically for a simpler equivalent model; or K-fold-CV.
situations: A model's PSIS-LOO output (loo() / arviz.loo) shows high Pareto k values (k > 0.7 "bad"/"very bad" counts nonzero) and/or a warning that the estimated Pareto sh

branches.if_observe: brms with default (improper flat) priors
branches.then: Set proper priors and re-run loo — H6; this alone can fix it (Aki: brms defaults aren't proper, post 36)

branches.if_observe: model believed correct and irreducibly flexible (H4)
branches.then: Use reloo / brute-force LOO / K-fold-CV for a trustworthy estimate; or use a simpler equivalent model with latents integrated out

branches.if_observe: the quantity of interest is next-timepoint or new-individual prediction
branches.then: LOO is the wrong target — use the matching CV scheme (leave-future / leave-group out) instead

action: inspect the rank/shape of Xnew passed to the GP prediction call
discriminates: Xnew-rank error (1D list/array) vs a deeper model/covariance misspecification
expected_findings: If Xnew is a Python list -> indexing TypeError; if a 1D np.array -> 'too many indices'. GP API requires 2D (n_points, n_features); a single 4-D query point must be np.array([[...]]) (shape (1,4)). If fixing rank clears the error, this was purely an input-shape bug.
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Convert Xnew to a 2D ndarray: one extra bracket pair so a single point is shape (1,4); list -> np.array first (fixes the int-vs-tuple indexing), then add the outer bracket (fixes 'too many indices').

branches.if_observe: error clears after making Xnew 2D
branches.then: input-shape bug confirmed; move on to model-definition / efficiency review

branches.if_observe: still errors with a dimension mismatch deeper in the graph
branches.then: suspect covariance/observed-shape misspecification -> next moves

action: review the GP modelling choice (Latent vs Marginal) and the likelihood/parallelism settings
discriminates: structural inefficiency (non-conjugate Latent, multithreaded BLAS fighting multiple chains) vs a genuine sampling-geometry problem
expected_findings: With an MvNormal/Gaussian noise likelihood the GP is conjugate -> gp.Marginal marginalizes f analytically and is far faster than gp.Latent + sampled f. Theano matrix ops are already multithreaded, so njobs>1 oversubscribes cores and slows chains (one chain fast, the next crawling).
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Switch gp.Latent -> gp.Marginal with marginal_likelihood (conjugate speed-up); if keeping Latent with diagonal cov, use Normal instead of MvNormal; set njobs=1; drop the unnecessary start=test_point.

branches.if_observe: one chain at ~28 it/s, next at ~1.7 it/s with njobs>1
branches.then: core oversubscription -> njobs=1

branches.if_observe: still slow / divergent after Marginal switch
branches.then: suspect vague priors on hyperparameters -> prior move

action: tighten hyperparameter priors using GP-specific prior guidance
discriminates: pathology driven by vague heavy-tailed priors vs an irreducible likelihood/data problem
expected_findings: HalfCauchy's heavy tail lets the lengthscale wander into flat/degenerate regions of GP likelihood; informative priors (HalfNormal, or boundary-avoiding priors per Betancourt GP part 3) regularize lengthscale/amplitude and stabilize sampling. Covariance and hyperparameter-prior choices are of equal importance in GP design.
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Replace HalfCauchy with HalfNormal on lengthscale/amplitude; follow Betancourt's robust-GP priors (betanalpha gp_part3). Keep eta (amplitude) a SCALAR -- a vector eta corrupts cov dimensions.

branches.if_observe: eta given shape=nvar then cov = eta**2 * ExpQuad fails on dim mismatch
branches.then: make eta scalar -- amplitude is one number, lengthscale is the per-dim vector

action: request and run a minimal reproducible example with sample data instead of reasoning from transcribed snippets
discriminates: real bug in the model vs transcription artifacts in the hand-copied code
expected_findings: A runnable MWE removes ambiguity; experts repeatedly could not localize the bug from inconsistent pastes ('no sigma defined in your first block'). The attached script then exposes the true single-line culprit.
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Have the user attach Multidimentional_Gaussian.py with synthetic data; run it to surface the actual error rather than guessing from prose.

branches.if_observe: MWE runs and reproduces the error
branches.then: diff observed vs target shapes at marginal_likelihood -> next move

action: diff the shape of the observed target y against the GP API contract
discriminates: observed-target shape bug (n,1) vs (n,) -- THE root cause -- vs the surface shape errors elsewhere
expected_findings: errors built as np.array([...])[:,None] -> shape (n,1); the GP marginal_likelihood expects a flat (n,) target (vector output simply because X is a vector of points). The [:,None] column-vector is the single line breaking everything downstream; this is buried in the Marginal.marginal_likelihood docstring.
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Drop the [:,None]: errors = np.array([error_fxn(p,...) for p in X]) so y is (n,). Root-cause fix; also question whether to emulate the error vs y_true directly (modelling choice, not a bug).

branches.if_observe: removing [:,None] clears the dimension-mismatch chain
branches.then: root cause confirmed; remaining issues are downstream prediction/workflow

action: review how the random-variable query point is assembled and shaped before conditional
discriminates: Theano tensor-assembly/shape error vs needing to pass a precomputed numpy Xnew
expected_findings: stacking 4 scalar Normals then reshape to (1,4) is fragile and conditional's output shape becomes ambiguous (must hand-specify shape). Cleaner: declare params_ as one multivariate Normal('params_', mu, sd, shape=(1,4)), or skip RVs and pass a numpy Xnew sampled from the desired distribution into conditional for PPC. Per-dimension different distributions are achieved column-wise in the numpy Xnew.
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Use params_ = pm.Normal('params_', params, 0.1*tt.abs_(params), shape=(1,4)) and gp.conditional('e_pred', Xnew=params_, shape=1); OR Xnew = np.random.randn(100,4)*abs(params)*.1 + params then conditio

branches.if_observe: user wants distinct distributions per parameter
branches.then: set each column of the numpy Xnew independently

branches.if_observe: still confused about predict vs conditional
branches.then: use conditional/predictt (symbolic) to keep modelling; predict returns numpy and is terminal-only

action: check whether pm.sample is being re-run on a Model that already had new nodes added (double-counted dimensions)
discriminates: a genuine remaining model bug vs a workflow error of re-sampling the same model after extending it
expected_findings: expected ndim is double the prior model's free dims (12 vs 6) -> the model object accumulated new RV nodes (params_, sigma2, e_pred) on top of an already-sampled model, so the mass matrix init sees an inconsistent dimension count. You are not supposed to sample twice from the same model after adding nodes.
situations: Building a scalar-output, multi-dimensional-input Gaussian process surrogate in PyMC3 (gp.Latent/Marginal with ExpQuad) and trying to push random-variable param
leaves: Define a fresh pm.Model for the prediction/propagation step instead of reopening and re-sampling the fitted surrogate_model; or do the propagation via numpy Xnew + sample_ppc rather than a second pm.s

branches.if_observe: expected ndim is an integer multiple of true free dims
branches.then: stale/duplicated nodes -> rebuild model fresh

action: Reproduce the fit on an independent machine/version and inspect the FULL diagnostic set, especially divergences and ESS, not just the printed Rhat table.
discriminates: Whether the absence of warnings is real and reproducible vs OP-specific; separates 'diagnostics truly silent' (H2/H5) from 'OP just didn't look' (H1).
expected_findings: H1: at least some chains show max_treedepth and/or Rhat/ESS warnings. H2/H5: zero divergences reproducibly; only treedepth + intermittent ESS/Rhat. H4: warnings appear/vanish run-to-run.
situations: A Stan model with a bounded parameter (real<lower=0,upper=1>) and an ill-posed/improper target (here beta(1,0.25) applied twice = effective beta(1,-0.5)) sample
leaves: maxbiostat reran 4-chain default sampling: got 1133 max_treedepth exceedances + bulk-ESS-too-low warning, but explicitly asked 'do you get any divergences?' -> OP: none, and a clean run after raising

branches.if_observe: no divergences, only treedepth + intermittent ESS/Rhat
branches.then: rule toward H2/H5; stop trusting the summary and move to the unconstrained scale

branches.if_observe: consistent divergences
branches.then: ordinary improper-target story (H1); no FP mystery

action: Dump the diagnostic_file and inspect the unconstrained GRADIENT vs logit(a); compare to the analytic gradient and decompose it into log-density gradient + log-Jacobian-determinant gradient.
discriminates: Pinpoints WHERE the FP failure lives: is the effective target an accidentally-proper density (gradient flips sign at the wall) and which term (density vs Jacobian) survives. Confirms H2 and kills H3/H5.
expected_findings: H2: analytic gradient should be -0.5 to infinity, but observed gradient wavers near -0.5 then JUMPS to +1 past logit(a)~37 (pushing chains back toward 0). Decompose: log-density grad=-1.5, log-Jacobian grad=+1; past the wall inv_logit overflows to 1, log1m underflows to -inf, beta_lpdf returns ONLY the +1 Jacobian gradient -> effective well-posed density falling to zero after the wall.
situations: A Stan model with a bounded parameter (real<lower=0,upper=1>) and an ill-posed/improper target (here beta(1,0.25) applied twice = effective beta(1,-0.5)) sample
leaves: Betancourt: at logit(a)~37 inv_logit overflows to 1 and log1m -> -inf; beta_lpdf then returns only the Jacobian-determinant gradient (+1), so the EFFECTIVE target looks like a proper density with a ha

Betancourt derived a=1/(1+e^-x), pi(x)=e^x/sqrt(1+e^x) (divergent); nhuurre's log(1-a) vs lp__ scatter showed the curve fracturing from numerical error on the left and a weird vertical cluster -> samp

branches.if_observe: gradient flips to +1 at the overflow wall
branches.then: TERMINAL FP-masking explanation: improperness invisible because FP turns it into an effectively proper target; no diagnostic can see it short of higher-precision arithmetic

branches.if_observe: gradient stays -0.5 and chain keeps climbing
branches.then: no FP masking; expect divergent Rhat / blowup in unconstrained values (the 10e300 overflow seen in the x+y~N(0,1) analog)

action: Stress-test the no-divergence claim by REPLACING the parameter constraint with an explicit inv_logit + raw real parameter (same model, different transform path) and compare divergence/treedepth/step-size behavior.
discriminates: Whether absence of divergences is intrinsic to the target (H5) or an artifact of the lb_constrain 1e-15 kludge masking the boundary singularity (H2/H6); tests whether beta(1,-0.5) is really 'well-defined at a=1'.
expected_findings: H5: explicit inv_logit should also give ~no divergences. H2/H6: explicit version yields THOUSANDS of divergences, MORE stable step-size adaptation, faster run, and NO max_treedepth problems -> the constraint code's 1e-15 correction was suppressing the divergences.
situations: A Stan model with a bounded parameter (real<lower=0,upper=1>) and an ill-posed/improper target (here beta(1,0.25) applied twice = effective beta(1,-0.5)) sample
leaves: nhuurre: explicit inv_logit model gets thousands of divergent transitions + stable adaptation + no treedepth issues; beta(1,-0.5) and its derivative ARE infinite at a=1 (so H5's 'well-defined at the b

branches.if_observe: explicit transform diverges where constraint version does not
branches.then: the lb_constrain 1e-15 fudge is the culprit; file a stan-dev/math issue to remove it (resolution accepted by Betancourt/Bob Carpenter)

branches.if_observe: both paths behave identically
branches.then: boundary genuinely benign; keep H5

action: Discriminate 'concentration from a sharp-but-PROPER prior' vs 'concentration from FP-masked impropriety' by checking sd>0 and by pushing the proper analog to extremes that approach the FP wall.
discriminates: Whether 'quantiles at 1' is a reliable improper-posterior signal; calibrates which boundary behaviors actually warrant unconstrained investigation.
expected_findings: Proper sharp prior (beta(9999,1)): nontrivial sd, logit(a) only reaches ~20 where FP is fine -> clean. Improper FP-masked: sd estimated as exactly 0. Push further (beta(99999999,1)): logit(a)~26, still FP-stable. Only the truly improper case combines pinned quantiles WITH sd=0.
situations: A Stan model with a bounded parameter (real<lower=0,upper=1>) and an ill-posed/improper target (here beta(1,0.25) applied twice = effective beta(1,-0.5)) sample
leaves: Betancourt: empirical quantiles all collapsing to one value WITH sd=0 is the worrying combination (beta(9999,1) has nonzero sd; even beta(99999999,1) only reaches logit(a)~26, below the ~37 FP wall).

branches.if_observe: sd exactly 0 and all quantiles equal
branches.then: treat as improper-until-proven-otherwise; go to unconstrained trace/gradient

branches.if_observe: sd > 0 even if quantiles near 1
branches.then: consistent with a legitimately sharp proper prior

action: Reason about the model's exchangeability/generative structure: ask what population process would produce an exact sum-to-zero constraint
discriminates: H1 (real model change) vs H2/H3 (equivalent model, mechanical artifact)
expected_findings: Under H1: sum-to-zero breaks conditional independence/infinite exchangeability, fixes population mean = sample mean of alpha, cannot generate predictions for new (unobserved) groups; it is an intrinsic (ICAR-like) model, not a full (CAR-like) hierarchical model. Under H2/H3 the structural objection would not arise — the models would be measure-equivalent.
situations: An additive/varying-intercept regression (alpha_k per group, plus a global intercept mu) is non-identified in the likelihood because mu and the alpha levels tra
leaves: Betancourt: zero-sum constraint fixes the population mean to exactly the average of the realized parameters, which is extremely unlikely under a conditionally-independent-normal population; breaks inf

branches.if_observe: constraint has no natural generative interpretation and blocks new-group prediction
branches.then: treat sum-to-zero as a genuinely different model (favor H1); but still test empirically whether the *intended* equivalent reparameterization reproduces the standard model

action: Run a simulation: generate data from known mu, sigma over K groups, fit both parameterizations, compare full posterior summaries (mu, sigma, each alpha_k, plus mean/sd of alpha) across seeds
discriminates: H1 vs H2 — whether the discrepancy is a real distributional difference or a fixable artifact; also checks robustness (not seed luck)
expected_findings: Under H1/H2 both: per-group alpha posteriors match closely; mu (and sigma) differ — in the sum-to-zero model mu tracks the sample mean of alpha exactly and is tight, in the standard model mu is wider and offset. Repeating with a second seed should reproduce the same qualitative pattern (rules out a one-off).
situations: An additive/varying-intercept regression (alpha_k per group, plus a global intercept mu) is non-identified in the likelihood because mu and the alpha levels tra
leaves: K=20, N=200, seeds 1234 and 123456 in cmdstanr. alpha posteriors indistinguishable; mu larger with much smaller 95% interval in the sum-to-zero model; pattern holds across both seeds. Bob's interim co

branches.if_observe: alpha posteriors match but mu/sigma differ reproducibly
branches.then: either the models are truly different (H1) OR the supposedly-equivalent reparameterization has a coding error (H2) — escalate to careful audit of the prior statement / centering

branches.if_observe: discrepancy vanishes under one of the codings
branches.then: the residual gap is an artifact, not a model difference — move to explaining the leftover hyperparameter behavior (H4)

action: Audit the sum-to-zero model's sampling statement line-by-line against the standard model — specifically the prior's location parameter
discriminates: H2 (centering bug) vs H1 (irreducible model difference)
expected_findings: Under H2: the zero-sum prior is written normal(0, scale) — centered at 0 — while the equivalent standard model puts the group effects at normal(mu, sigma); the missing +mu in the prior location mis-anchors mu. Fixing to normal(mu, inv(sqrt(1-1/K))*sigma) should collapse most of the mu/sigma discrepancy and bring the two models into agreement (equal marginals up to MCMC error).
situations: An additive/varying-intercept regression (alpha_k per group, plus a global intercept mu) is non-identified in the likelihood because mu and the alpha levels tra
leaves: Vehtari and Goodrich (at the Stan meeting) spot that the alpha prior was centered at 0 instead of mu. Revised hier-ctr.stan uses alpha ~ normal(mu, inv(sqrt(1 - inv(K))) * sigma). After the fix the ma

branches.if_observe: fixing the prior center to mu reconciles the posteriors
branches.then: confirm H2 was the dominant artifact; remaining question (wider mu / lower ESS in standard model) is genuine, not a bug -> pursue H4

branches.if_observe: discrepancy persists after centering fix
branches.then: reopen H1 — the constraint really does alter the target

action: Reason about what mu *is* in each parameterization, using a limiting/degenerate case (e.g. K=2, N->infinity so alpha posteriors are point masses) and the finite-population vs superpopulation distinction
discriminates: H4 — whether the leftover behavior is an expected feature (mu = deterministic sample mean under the constraint vs mu = free superpopulation mean) rather than any remaining defect
expected_findings: Under H4: in the sum-to-zero model mu equals the (now deterministic) finite-population mean of alpha, so as the alpha posteriors sharpen mu is forced to their average and is tight; in the standard model mu is the free superpopulation/hyper-mean, which retains genuine uncertainty even when the alpha's are pinned -> wider posterior, lower ESS. This is the correct, expected behavior, and the wider-mu model is the one that supports prediction for a new group.
situations: An additive/varying-intercept regression (alpha_k per group, plus a global intercept mu) is non-identified in the likelihood because mu and the alpha levels tra
leaves: Niko's K=2, N=inf thought experiment: alphas -> Dirac at -1,+1; sum-to-zero forces mu=0 (it's the sample mean), while in the standard model mu is the population mean and can still explore. Gelman: thi

branches.if_observe: residual mu width explained by superpopulation-vs-sample-mean
branches.then: terminate: discrepancy fully decomposed into (a) a centering bug, now fixed, and (b) an inherent, expected difference in what mu represents

action: Relax/remove priors (drop beta(2,2) on p; widen mu prior sd 0.2 -> 1) and rerun; compare divergence count and E-BFMI
discriminates: H1 (prior-induced constraint) vs H4 (intrinsic geometry independent of priors)
expected_findings: If H1: divergences drop toward 0 and E-BFMI rises above 0.3. If H4: divergences are unchanged or WORSE and E-BFMI stays ~0.27-0.29.
situations: A Gaussian mixture model with a measurement-error layer (latent per-observation true values y_true mixed over two ordered components mu, shared scale sigma; obs
leaves: Removing beta prior gave 165/16000 (1%) divergences, E-BFMI=0.29 (worse); widening mu sd to 1 gave 138/16000 (0.86%) -- still bad.

branches.if_observe: divergences and E-BFMI unchanged/worse after relaxing priors
branches.then: reject H1; the bad geometry is intrinsic to the parameterization, not the priors -> pursue reparameterization (H2/H4)

branches.if_observe: divergences vanish
branches.then: priors were the culprit; justify weaker priors and stop

action: Inspect a pairs/scatter plot of the parameters (mu1 vs mu2, and sigma vs locations) for correlation or funnel shapes
discriminates: H2 (mu1-mu2 location correlation) vs H4 (sigma-vs-location funnel)
expected_findings: If H2: a tilted ellipse between mu1 and mu2. If H4: a wedge/funnel where the spread of sigma narrows as sigma shrinks against mu/y_true.
situations: A Gaussian mixture model with a measurement-error layer (latent per-observation true values y_true mixed over two ordered components mu, shared scale sigma; obs
leaves: Pairs plot showed only a slight funnel of sigma w.r.t. mu1/mu2 (more visible with dots) -- the diagnostic tell for a centered-scale funnel; mu1-mu2 not strongly correlated.

branches.if_observe: funnel between sigma and locations
branches.then: centered-sigma funnel -> non-centered reparameterization of components (H4)

branches.if_observe: strong mu1-mu2 correlation
branches.then: reparameterize in the difference (H2)

action: Reparameterize mu as (mu1, delta) with mu2 = mu1 + delta and a prior on the difference; rerun
discriminates: H2 (location correlation is the driver) vs not
expected_findings: If H2 correct: divergences clear. If not: divergences persist.
situations: A Gaussian mixture model with a measurement-error layer (latent per-observation true values y_true mixed over two ordered components mu, shared scale sigma; obs
leaves: Difference parameterization still gave 112/16000 (0.7%) divergences, E-BFMI=0.28 -> H2 rejected (differences not the bottleneck here).

branches.if_observe: still diverging
branches.then: reject H2; the correlation is not in the locations -> the scale (sigma) funnel remains the suspect (H4)

action: Try pure tuning: bump adapt_delta toward 0.999 and increase iterations
discriminates: H3 (tuning-curable) vs H4 (geometry, tuning-incurable)
expected_findings: If H3: divergences disappear with adapt_delta=0.99-0.999. If H4: adapt_delta makes no difference (low E-BFMI funnel is not a step-size problem).
situations: A Gaussian mixture model with a measurement-error layer (latent per-observation true values y_true mixed over two ordered components mu, shared scale sigma; obs
leaves: adapt_delta=0.99 did NOT help the original model; expert note: 'if the geometry is the culprit usually no amount of adapt_delta or iteration is going to save you' (only works on small problems). After

branches.if_observe: adapt_delta has no effect
branches.then: confirm geometry is the issue (H4); reparameterize rather than tune

branches.if_observe: divergences clear with adapt_delta on a non-centered model
branches.then: tuning is a finishing touch ON TOP of the reparameterization, not a substitute

action: Non-center the mixture components: introduce z ~ std_normal() and set component value = mu + sigma*z (transformed parameters), so the shared sigma no longer multiplies into the sampled geometry
discriminates: H4 (centered-scale funnel) confirmed vs not
expected_findings: If H4: divergences and E-BFMI warnings both vanish and posteriors match the centered model on simulated data. If not H4: warnings persist.
situations: A Gaussian mixture model with a measurement-error layer (latent per-observation true values y_true mixed over two ordered components mu, shared scale sigma; obs
leaves: Non-centered (y_mix = mu + sigma*z, z~std_normal) ran with NO warnings; posteriors matched the original on multiple simulated datasets (including 5x larger measurement uncertainties). Cost ~6x slower

branches.if_observe: no warnings AND posteriors match centered fit on simulated data
branches.then: accept non-centered parameterization as the resolution

branches.if_observe: no warnings but posteriors DIFFER from centered
branches.then: the rewrite changed the model -- audit for indexing/likelihood errors before trusting it

action: Cross-check the reparameterized fit against the centered fit (and against simulated ground-truth parameters) on multiple datasets; read the new code for indexing/likelihood errors
discriminates: genuine reparameterization (same model, better geometry) vs H5 (a rewrite that silently changed the model)
expected_findings: Correct rewrite: matches original + recovers true simulated values. Subtly-wrong rewrite: implausibly small spreads, or disagreement with simulated truth.
situations: A Gaussian mixture model with a measurement-error layer (latent per-observation true values y_true mixed over two ordered components mu, shared scale sigma; obs
leaves: Two H5 traps caught by exactly this cross-check: (1) missing [n] indexing on y_mix/uncertainties produced absurdly tiny ~0.01 spreads -> fixed by indexing y_mix1[n], uncertainties[n]; (2) the 'vectori

branches.if_observe: tiny/implausible spreads vs original
branches.then: indexing bug -> add per-observation [n] indices

branches.if_observe: good R_hat but different posterior, or bad R_hat/N_eff with strange pairs geometry
branches.then: the vectorization collapsed observation-level into dataset-level mixing -> revert to the per-observation loop (or use the contrib-vector form of vectorized log_mix)

action: Quantify what the proposed prior actually implies (tail probability + simulate draws) and sanity-check against domain plausibility
discriminates: whether the scale-bound heuristic produces a genuinely informative prior vs. one still admitting absurd effects; whether the user is anchoring on response-range instead of predictor-units
expected_findings: For 'N(0,4) is informative' hypothesis: pnorm(5,0,4,lower.tail=F) ≈ 0.106, so ~10% prior mass on a one-unit predictor change moving the response by its WHOLE range — not actually informative for a regression coefficient. Confirms the heuristic is too weak.
situations: A novice (brms) wants to move beyond vague/flat priors like N(0,10000). The response is a bounded Likert/ordinal scale (e.g. 0–4); they reason a regression coef
leaves: In R: pnorm(5,0,4,lower.tail=F)=0.1056; hist(rnorm(1000,0,4)); ask 'would I be surprised to see this number in the output table?'

branches.if_observe: tail mass on implausible effect sizes is large
branches.then: tighten scale; emphasize the coefficient scale is tied to the PREDICTOR's units, not the response range

branches.if_observe: user worried about being judged 'subjective'
branches.then: reframe: vague priors are not objective, they assert absurd effects are plausible

action: Examine the JOINT prior on the linear predictor by adding effects and tracking variance growth
discriminates: whether independent, individually-reasonable priors stay reasonable when summed (low-dim) vs. compound into an over-diffuse joint prior (high-dim)
expected_findings: For 'independence is fine' hypothesis: variance of log-intensity grows ~sqrt(#effects) (e.g. N(0,0.1)+N(0,0.1) → N(0,0.1·sqrt2)); with many effects the joint prior admits large intensities even though each margin is tight. Confirms joint behavior must be checked.
situations: A novice (brms) wants to move beyond vague/flat priors like N(0,10000). The response is a bounded Likert/ordinal scale (e.g. 0–4); they reason a regression coef
leaves: log λ = Σ α_k; Var grows with sqrt(K); 10% effect → scale log(1.1)≈0.1, far tighter than typical 'informative' advice; reference principled_bayesian_workflow / 1708.07487

branches.if_observe: many additive effects
branches.then: tighten per-effect scales or use hierarchical shrinkage; do not set marginals independently

action: Reclassify the parameters before reasoning about priors — identify which are cut points / simplex / coefficients
discriminates: ordinary-intercept intuition vs. ordinal-specific structure (cut points are latent-scale thresholds; monotonic effect carries a Dirichlet simplex)
expected_findings: In cumulative models the 'intercept' is a set of cut points mapping the latent variable to categories — standard intercept-prior reasoning does NOT transfer. Monotonic predictor adds a simplex (class=simo) with Dirichlet prior; ordinal-outcome vs ordinal-predictor are separate issues.
situations: A novice (brms) wants to move beyond vague/flat priors like N(0,10000). The response is a bounded Likert/ordinal scale (e.g. 0–4); they reason a regression coef
leaves: brms classes: intercept=thresholds (student_t(3,0,10)), b=regression coeffs (flat default), simo=Dirichlet(α=1) simplex for mo(z); see buerkner monotonic-effects vignette

branches.if_observe: predictor is monotonic mo(z)
branches.then: address the Dirichlet simplex prior separately from the slope prior

action: Simulate Dirichlet draws and visualize the implied category-step distribution at varying concentration α
discriminates: what α encodes: equal-spacing belief vs. its strength — and whether α=1 (flat) is the intended assumption
expected_findings: For 'α=1 means equal steps' hypothesis: α=1 is actually uniform over simplices (NOT a strong equal-step belief); larger α ({5,5,5,5,5}) concentrates draws toward equal probabilities — the larger the equal values, the STRONGER the equal-step prior. Corrects the user's reading.
situations: A novice (brms) wants to move beyond vague/flat priors like N(0,10000). The response is a bounded Likert/ordinal scale (e.g. 0–4); they reason a regression coef
leaves: R: gtools::rdirichlet(10, alpha=rep(k,5)) for k=1,3,5 and plot rows; Ternary::TernaryPlot + rdirichlet(5000,c(1,1,1)*k) for k=.1,1,10 to see concentration

branches.if_observe: want stronger equal-spacing belief
branches.then: raise α uniformly; if want to allow uneven steps, keep α≈1

action: Map the coefficient through the probit link to implied category probabilities across the predictor's range
discriminates: judging the prior by its density on the latent z-scale vs. by the category probabilities it implies over the actual predictor range (0–4) — the only domain-meaningful check
expected_findings: For 'N(0,1) is weakly informative' hypothesis: with β=0.15 over x=0..4 category probs shift modestly (plausible); with β=2 the probabilities saturate to ~1 by x=1–2 (absurd). So β scale matters a lot; N(0,1) is weakly informative and could even be tighter. Confirms judge-by-implied-probabilities.
situations: A novice (brms) wants to move beyond vague/flat priors like N(0,10000). The response is a bounded Likert/ordinal scale (e.g. 0–4); they reason a regression coef
leaves: R toy: pp(z,cuts)=normal mass in cut buckets; loop pp(β*x,cuts) for x=0:4 with β=0.15 vs β=2; coefficient is a z-score shift on the latent (probit) scale; betancourt ordinal-regression case study for

branches.if_observe: implied probs saturate over the predictor range
branches.then: β scale too large — tighten below N(0,1)

branches.if_observe: continuous x vs monotonic mo(z)
branches.then: slope prior can be the same scale; the monotonic-specific structure lives in the Dirichlet simplex, not the slope

action: Establish the data-generating context of the estimate before diagnosing the model (ask: prior-only or full fit?)
discriminates: a genuinely over-diffuse prior/misfit vs. an artifact of sample_prior='only' (estimates reflect prior, not data)
expected_findings: For 'priors too broad' hypothesis would suggest tightening; but the estimates came from sample_prior='only', so they correctly reflect the half-student_t(3,0,10) prior ignoring the data — not a fitting bug. Resolution: run full fit to see data-informed posterior; reduce the min scale (default ≥10) toward the response SD for a tighter prior if desired.
situations: A novice (brms) wants to move beyond vague/flat priors like N(0,10000). The response is a bounded Likert/ordinal scale (e.g. 0–4); they reason a regression coef
leaves: brms default SD prior half-student_t(3,0,10) with min scale 10; with sample_prior='only' posterior=prior; user must run prior-only AND full model and compare; can lower scale to the response SD post-l

branches.if_observe: absurd estimates only under sample_prior='only'
branches.then: expected — rerun full model; not a misfit

branches.if_observe: absurd estimates persist in full fit
branches.then: then genuinely revisit SD-prior scale / model

action: Run a scale sweep: fit the soft model across several orders of magnitude of the constraint SD and tabulate total time, n_eff, Rhat, divergences for each
discriminates: whether the soft constraint is merely a tuning problem (a stable optimum exists) vs a mis-specified/geometry problem (optimum is fragile, scale-dependent, or dimension-dependent)
expected_findings: If only-tuning: smooth U-shaped cost with a robust optimum. If mis-specified-scale: optimum shifts when N changes and tiny scales diverge / huge scales lose efficiency. If geometry: low n_eff and high treedepth even near the optimum, sensitive to parameter scale.
situations: A regression/spatial model needs a sum-to-zero (centered / identifiability) constraint on a parameter vector. Comparing a hard constraint (drop one component, s
leaves: Observed: scale 0.0001*ncat slow, 0.001*ncat fast (sweet spot), 0.01 ok, 0.1 very slow with Rhat>1; soft posterior systematically offset from hard. Sweet spot ~0.001*ncat with no a-priori reason.

branches.if_observe: best scale moves with N and small scales diverge
branches.then: suspect the scale must carry an explicit N factor (Hypothesis 1); inspect the constraint statement

branches.if_observe: soft and hard converge but to different means/SDs
branches.then: suspect wrong prior object / variance inflation (Hypothesis 2); compare implied marginals

action: Audit the constraint statement and prior placement: is the prior on sum(x) (should be scaled by N to act on the mean), and is the same prior applied to ALL N components or only the N-1 free ones?
discriminates: Hypothesis 1 (missing *N on the soft scale) vs Hypothesis 2 (prior on wrong object -> reference level has wrong variance) vs a genuinely shared target
expected_findings: If sum(x)~normal(0, s) lacks the *N factor, the soft constraint strength is wrong by a factor of N. If the prior sits only on raw[1:N-1] (or on the redundant full vector), the implied last/reference component has a different (inflated) marginal variance than the rest.
situations: A regression/spatial model needs a sum-to-zero (centered / identifiability) constraint on a parameter vector. Comparing a hard constraint (drop one component, s
leaves: Mitzi: 'sum(alpha) ~ normal(0,0.01) is missing a factor - multiply scale by length of alpha because you are constraining the MEAN.' After adding *F the soft results line up far better. Separately, pri

branches.if_observe: adding the *N factor fixes scale-sensitivity and aligns soft with hard
branches.then: Hypothesis 1 confirmed as the dominant scale bug; re-sweep to confirm robustness

branches.if_observe: marginals still differ after the *N fix
branches.then: escalate to Hypothesis 2: derive the implied marginal of the reference component

action: Derive the implied marginal distribution of the sum-to-zero vector under the naive prior (fit a tiny standalone Stan model: append_row(prefix, -sum(prefix)) ~ normal(0,1)) and read off mean/SD per component
discriminates: whether putting normal(0,1) on a sum-to-zero vector yields standard-normal marginals (no) and quantifies the variance distortion of the reference component
expected_findings: Components are NOT marginally N(0,1): constraining the full vector shrinks each to SD~0.86; putting the prior only on the N-1 free params blows the dropped component up to SD ~ sqrt(N)-ish. So hard-with-raw-prior and soft are different models.
situations: A regression/spatial model needs a sum-to-zero (centered / identifiability) constraint on a parameter vector. Comparing a hard constraint (drop one component, s
leaves: Bob's standalone experiment: full-vector prior gives sd~0.86 each; free-only prior gives mu[4] sd~1.75 (N=4). For N=100+ the reference-level prior scale is >100x the others -> 'clearly nonsensical ref

branches.if_observe: reference component variance inflated, scaling with N
branches.then: need a scale correction to recover standard-normal marginals (Hypothesis 4)

branches.if_observe: all components already symmetric and correct
branches.then: the discrepancy is geometric, not marginal -> go to unconstrained-space inspection

action: Inspect the sampler's view of the unconstrained space: dump diagnostic_file (unconstrained params, momenta, gradients), make pairs plots and param-vs-gradient correlations, and look at treedepth and step size; use shinystan
discriminates: Hypothesis 3 (geometry): hard constraint's nonlinear ridges/corners vs soft constraint's degenerate near-lower-dimensional manifold (hard to set a single step size)
expected_findings: Soft: posterior is narrow in one direction, mostly on a lower-dim manifold -> step size hard to set, low n_eff. Hard: difficult corners where data inform unconstrained params nonlinearly -> divergences localized in pairs/gradient plots.
situations: A regression/spatial model needs a sum-to-zero (centered / identifiability) constraint on a parameter vector. Comparing a hard constraint (drop one component, s
leaves: Vehtari: soft lies mostly in a lower-dimensional manifold so step size is hard to tune; hard has difficult corners. Betanalpha: it 'all comes down to geometry'; soft must fight concentration of measur

branches.if_observe: soft is degenerate/manifold-like, sensitive to scale
branches.then: prefer an exact reparameterization that produces iid-like raw geometry

branches.if_observe: hard has localized corners/divergences
branches.then: consider a smoother basis (QR) that linearizes the constraint

action: Adopt an exact linear map from N-1 iid raw params to N sum-to-zero params (QR/Cholesky of the singular covariance, Fraser 1951), and set the raw-parameter scale to the correction inv_sqrt(1 - 1/N)
discriminates: whether a principled reparameterization removes both the marginal distortion (H2/H4) and the bad geometry (H3) simultaneously, vs the cheap append_row trick
expected_findings: QR map x = Q * x_raw with x_raw ~ normal(0, 1/sqrt(1-1/N)) yields all x[i] marginally ~N(0,1), exact sum=0, and near-iid raw geometry (n_eff ~ full). The same scale correction also fixes Bob's direct append_row version. QR is O(N) via the cumulative-sum trick but adds Jacobian/autodiff overhead; the direct version is cheaper per-leapfrog but mixes slightly worse.
situations: A regression/spatial model needs a sum-to-zero (centered / identifiability) constraint on a parameter vector. Comparing a hard constraint (drop one component, s
leaves: aaronjg/andre QR sum_to_zero_QR with x_raw_sigma=inv_sqrt(1-inv(N)); Bob applies the same inv(sqrt(1-inv(N))) to the append_row version and recovers SD~1 marginals. martinmodrak confirms x_raw ~ N(0,

branches.if_observe: QR map gives correct marginals and good n_eff
branches.then: terminal: use QR (or scale-corrected direct constraint) instead of soft constraint

branches.if_observe: need cheaper per-iteration cost and can tolerate slightly worse mixing
branches.then: use direct append_row with the inv_sqrt(1-1/N) scale correction

action: Reject single-fit diagnosis; demand calibration over many prior-draw replications (SBC / Cook-Gelman-Rubin) instead of inspecting one posterior
discriminates: true systematic bias / mis-specified likelihood vs sampling noise in a single dataset vs a mis-read direction of the bias
expected_findings: If the likelihood is correct, the rank of the true rho among posterior draws is uniform across replications (flat SBC histogram). If mis-specified (wrong variance / extra division / wrong moment), ranks pile up at one end. Bob also notes the plotted point was actually below the true value, so even the claimed DIRECTION of bias is suspect.
situations: A Bayesian/Stan Heckman sample-selection model (two equations: probit participation/selection + continuous outcome) fit to simulated data shows the error-correl
leaves: Run SBC: simulate from the prior repeatedly, refit, check rank uniformity of each parameter's true value in its posterior; a single fit cannot distinguish bias from noise.

branches.if_observe: SBC ranks for rho pile up near the boundary across replications
branches.then: systematic likelihood/parameterization error -> audit the coupling term and the variance

branches.if_observe: SBC ranks roughly uniform
branches.then: the 'bias' was single-draw noise / a mis-read direction; model is calibrated

action: Question whether the two-step inverse-Mills correction belongs in a likelihood-based MCMC at all; replace it with the full Heckman bivariate-normal likelihood (selection contributes Phi(-mu) for unselected; observed units contribute the joint normal x conditional-selection factor)
discriminates: ad-hoc plug-in correction (rho fit to a wrong moment) vs the correct joint likelihood that supplants Heckman's two-step
expected_findings: Rewriting as the explicit likelihood (target += log(Phi(...)) for selected, log(Phi(-mu)) for unselected, plus y1 ~ normal(mu_y1, sd1)) recovers parameters far better than the inverse-Mills-in-mean version; confirms the original coupling was the core defect.
situations: A Bayesian/Stan Heckman sample-selection model (two equations: probit participation/selection + continuous outcome) fit to simulated data shows the error-correl
leaves: Code the Heckman log-likelihood directly: unselected -> log Phi(-X2 b2); selected -> y1~normal + log Phi of the rho-scaled standardized residual term; do NOT plug inverse Mills into the linear predict

action: Check whether the SD used is the marginal or the conditional SD of Y given selection
discriminates: marginal-SD bug (forces rho up to absorb unmodeled variance reduction) vs correct conditional-SD specification
expected_findings: For a selected unit the conditional variance is (1-rho^2)*sigma_u^2, so the SD must be sqrt(1-rho^2)*sigma_u, not sigma_u. Using the marginal sigma_u makes sigma_u play the role of the conditional SD, which pushes rho upward; switching to the conditional SD recovers lower rho (but samples worse and exposes rho/sigma_u trade-off).
situations: A Bayesian/Stan Heckman sample-selection model (two equations: probit participation/selection + continuous outcome) fit to simulated data shows the error-correl
leaves: Replace sigma_u with sqrt(square(sigma_u)-square(rho)*square(sigma_u)) as the outcome SD; also pass the latent index alpha_1+Z*gamma (not the probability p) into the inverse-Mills ratio.

branches.if_observe: After conditional-SD fix rho drops but sampling degrades and rho/sigma_u look coupled
branches.then: expose the non-identifiability -> reparameterize (e.g. fit a single coefficient theta on the Mills term and a free sigma) or move to the explicit-likelihood form

action: Probe identifiability of (rho, sigma_u); consider reparameterization and richer selection covariates
discriminates: structural weak-identification ridge vs a remaining coding error
expected_findings: rho and sigma_u trade off along a ridge because both enter the conditional moment; reparameterizing to theta = sigma_u*rho on the Mills term with a free residual sigma samples better. Separately, adding covariates that genuinely predict the selection probability (strong exclusion/instruments) tightens identification and reduces boundary drift.
situations: A Bayesian/Stan Heckman sample-selection model (two equations: probit participation/selection + continuous outcome) fit to simulated data shows the error-correl
leaves: Reparameterize the coupling (theta = sigma_u*rho, free sigma) and/or strengthen the selection equation with predictive covariates to break the rho/sigma_u ridge.

action: Validate the analytic likelihood against an independent reference implementation and re-derive the algebra by hand
discriminates: a subtle algebra/transcription bug in the selected-unit term vs a genuine modeling limitation
expected_findings: Cross-checking against Stata 'heckman' and the R sampleSelection vignette, then re-deriving, exposes an unnecessary EXTRA division by sqrt(1-rho^2) in the selected-unit contribution: rho/(sd1*sqrt(1-rho^2)) should be rho/sd1. The bug barely hurts calibration in the simple model (which is why it hid), but breaks the generalized/Tobit-5 extension.
situations: A Bayesian/Stan Heckman sample-selection model (two equations: probit participation/selection + continuous outcome) fit to simulated data shows the error-correl
leaves: Correct the selected-unit term to log Phi( (1/sqrt(1-rho^2)) * (mu_y2 + (rho/sd1)*(y1-mu_y1)) ); confirm via SBC on both the simple and generalized models.

branches.if_observe: Simple model still calibrates fine with the bug but the Tobit-5 generalization shows bad behavior
branches.then: trust the cross-reference + re-derivation over the calibration signal; fix the algebra

action: Audit the data-generating process itself: confirm it actually induces selection bias
discriminates: a genuinely informative selection test vs a degenerate DGP where there is no selection bias to correct
expected_findings: If the selection-equation regressors and outcome regressors are independent and there is no intercept, selection does not bias OLS, so 'OLS does fine' is an artifact of the DGP, not evidence about the model. Introducing correlation across the X's or an intercept makes selection bite; the corrected Heckman model stays calibrated there.

action: Diagnose the init failure as a non-finite log density at the default starting region

Either way Stan can't find a finite starting point.

action: Reparameterize the likelihood via sufficient statistics: collapse bernoulli trials to binomial counts (successes | trials).
discriminates: row-count-bound runtime (huge win available) vs posterior-geometry-bound runtime (collapse won't fix divergences)

action: Check whether the proposed point estimate (MAP of the full joint over hyperparameters AND random effects) is even well-defined.

action: Write out the actual marginal log-det for the conjugate-normal case and inspect the sparsity of A^T A (the cross-product of the random-effects design matrix).
discriminates: H2 (diagonal -> trivial, no sparse infra) vs H3 (non-diagonal -> need real sparse linear algebra)
expected_findings: Under H2 A^T A would be diagonal and the log-det factorizes. In reality, with y|u ~ N(Au,sigma^2 I), u ~ N(u0,tau^2 I), the marginal needs log det(tau^-2 I + sigma^-2 A^T A); A is sparse but A^T A is NOT diagonal because A's entries depend on covariate values and Au must equal the CONDITIONAL mean (varying slopes mix individuals across the same group). Group correlation makes Q_u block-diagonal and parameter-dependent.

action: Benchmark the inner convex optimization (the Newton solve at the heart of the nested Laplace) and the autodiff mode used to get its Jacobian/sensitivities; vary solver and parametrization.
discriminates: H4: cost lives in the inner Newton solve + sensitivity autodiff mode + covariance-vs-precision parametrization (not just outer sparse log-det)
expected_findings: Inner solve dominates and scales badly with latent dim: Powell dogleg ~2000s -> general Newton ~350s -> Newton with PRECISION matrices ~127s -> specialized Newton with ANALYTICAL gradients ~43s (single solve+sensitivities, dim(theta)=500). Autodiff mode for the system Jacobian is decisive: rev-mode >40s vs fwd-mode <1s; rev-mode with the right initial cotangent should recover this in one sweep. Scaling: dim 500 ~0.4s, dim 100 ~0.005s after optimization.
situations: A large Bayesian multilevel / MRP-style model (e.g. ~300k observations, ~100k individual-level random effects, plus varying-intercept/varying-slope group terms)

branches.if_observe: Jacobian/sensitivity step dominates
branches.then: switch rev-mode -> fwd-mode (few outputs/many params favors adjoint; many outputs/few params favors forward) and seed the right initial cotangent for a one-sweep rev-mode

action: Separate strict (technical) non-identifiability from data-limited degeneracy before anything else
discriminates: structural-flatness (would persist with infinite data) vs data-driven flatness (resolves as N grows)
expected_findings: Strict: a likelihood symmetry/redundancy provable from the model form regardless of data. Degeneracy: flatness that shrinks as you add informative data. Betancourt: 'unidentifiable models lead to degeneracies, but not all degeneracies are due to unidentifiable models.'

action: Locate the likelihood symmetry/redundancy generating the flat manifold
discriminates: which invariance is at play: label-switching, sign/scale/rotation, additive offset, or parameter redundancy
expected_findings: Mixtures: permutation (label-switching) symmetry. Latent-factor/SEM: sign of loadings (interdependent with latent ordering), scale of latent (interdependent with loading magnitude → fix z_scale=1), location (mu vs beta → fix z_mu=0). Single-factor MVN: must constrain one loading >0. Measurement-error x_obs~N(x_true,tau): degenerate likelihood from unanchored latent location/scale. Case-control logistic: intercept not identified by design.
situations: Need a simple, compactly-stated model + data where the posterior is non-trivially unidentifiable (not the obvious product-of-two-params case), for teaching earl
leaves: SEM remedies (Mike Lawrence): fix one beta sign +, fix z_scale=1, fix z_mu=0. Parameter-redundancy literature: Cole's book (Kent) for symbolic methods to detect redundant params.

branches.if_observe: mixture / multimodal
branches.then: label-switching → ordering constraint or repulsive+attractive priors (betanalpha mixture case study); note near-overlapping components are a SEPARATE data-limited problem

branches.if_observe: latent variable / factor model
branches.then: impose sign + scale + location constraints

action: Check whether the data span the region that informs the weakly-constrained parameters
discriminates: which parameter pair is unconstrained, and which missing data range would constrain it
expected_findings: Sigmoid y0+A/(1+exp(-b(x-x0))): b and x0 unidentified when no data near the inflection. Emax E0+Emax*d^h/(ED50^h+d^h): trouble with too few doses, all doses >>ED50, or no plateau observed. Logistic growth: max growth rate confounds with carrying capacity / return speed unless time series covers both very low AND very high density. Lotka-Volterra sparsely sampled with Gaussian noise.
situations: Need a simple, compactly-stated model + data where the posterior is non-trivially unidentifiable (not the obvious product-of-two-params case), for teaching earl

action: Run on simulated data and on prior-predictive / increasing-N data and inspect the likelihood/posterior geometry
discriminates: persistent flatness (strict) vs flatness that contracts with N (data-limited); also exposes multimodality vs ridge vs heavy tails
expected_findings: Strict: posterior stays as wide as the prior along the flat direction no matter how much data; chains explore symmetric modes. Data-limited: posterior contracts as informative data are added. Generating simulated data from known params and failing to recover them is the pedagogical payoff (Lawrence's SEM exercise, Modrák's sigmoid post).

action: Explain LKJ semantics by analogy: eta plays the role of a symmetric Beta shape on the (-1,1) correlation scale; larger eta concentrates mass near the identity matrix (zero correlation), not a hard bound.
discriminates: Whether the user's goal (bounding rho to a sub-interval) is achievable with LKJ at all, vs a misconception about what the prior does.
expected_findings: Under the bounding hypothesis, the user expects a min/max argument; expert reveals LKJ(eta) only controls concentration toward the identity (e.g. LKJ(10) ~ beta(10,10): ~zero density past +/-0.4) and there is NO generic way in Stan to restrict correlations to a sub-interval of (-1,1).
situations: A Stan/PyStan Gaussian-mixture model using multi_normal_cholesky where the user wants to bound the correlation to a sub-range and put separate priors on sigma_x
leaves: LKJ(eta) is the matrix generalization of a symmetric Beta((K+1)/2 ...) on each marginal correlation; mode of the matrix is the identity. To express a real prior belief you set eta, not a range; eta=1

action: Correct the type/role mismatch: L is the Cholesky factor of a CORRELATION matrix (unit diagonal), not a covariance; prescribe building covariance scale by pre-multiplying with standard deviations.

action: Clarify array indexing and recover the correlation matrix via the proper matrix product rather than element gymnastics.

The correlation matrix is Omega=L L' (multiply_lower_tri_self_transpose) and rho is simply its off-diagonal Omega[2,1] -- no division by sqrt of diagonals needed since Omega already has unit diagonal.

action: Diagnose whether the failure is cosmetic (init tuning) or structural (the inverse link can leave the admissible probability region).
discriminates: H1 (init tuning) vs H2 (link-function mismatch as root cause).
expected_findings: Under H1, constraining inits to a region with eta<0 would give stable runs and the issue is a nuisance. Under H2, the failure persists across init ranges because exp(eta) > 1 for any eta>0, so part of the unconstrained parameter space is structurally inadmissible — no init range fixes it. The thread lands on H2.
situations: Binomial/Bernoulli regression fit with a LOG link to directly estimate a relative risk (RR = exp(beta)). Sampler keeps hitting bad/invalid initializations becau
leaves: Key insight (Gabry, Goodrich): exp(x) is not a function from the reals to (0,1); inv_logit(eta) always is. The admissible region has a hard frontier and the posterior wants to put mass near/over it, w

branches.if_observe: Stable only with hand-picked eta<0 inits
branches.then: Treat as H2 confirmed; the constraint is masking a structural problem, not solving it — abandon the log link rather than hand-tune inits.

branches.if_observe: Prevalence is rare (small probabilities)
branches.then: exp(eta)>1 almost never occurs and log-binomial ~ log-Poisson; the log link may run, but it is still not a strictly admissible distribution (John Kern's caveat).

action: Reframe RR from a model parameter into a derived quantity of the generative model: fit with a proper inverse link, then compute RR from posterior predictive probabilities under counterfactual covariate settings.
discriminates: H3 (RR as derived contrast) vs the original framing of RR-as-parameter (and vs H4 family-swapping hacks).
expected_findings: With family=binomial(link='logit') (or probit), set the exposure/treatment to 0 and to 1 on the SAME design matrix, push both through the inverse link to get Pr_0 and Pr_1 per draw per observation, and form RR = Pr_1/Pr_0 (and ARR = Pr_1 - Pr_0). This yields a full posterior of RR with valid probabilities for ALL covariate inputs and no inadmissible region, sidestepping the log-link entirely. Note: RR is then generally nonlinear in the predictors and varies by covariate level — a single constant RR only exists if a log link were actually used in the fit.
situations: Binomial/Bernoulli regression fit with a LOG link to directly estimate a relative risk (RR = exp(beta)). Sampler keeps hitting bad/invalid initializations becau
leaves: rstanarm/brms recipe (Goodrich): stan_glm(y ~ treatment + x, family=binomial(link='logit'), QR=TRUE); nd$treatment<-0 -> Pr_0 = posterior_linpred(post, newdata=nd, transform=TRUE); nd$treatment<-1 ->

branches.if_observe: Continuous predictor — no natural 0/1 contrast
branches.then: Pick reference vs comparison values (e.g. percentiles 10th vs 30/50/70/90th) and contrast; a single 'linear' constant RR is not recoverable from a logit fit because RR depends nonlinearly on the prediction point (Goodrich, posts 33-37).

branches.if_observe: Original Mantel-Haenszel formula needed and some cell count c_i = 0 -> division by zero
branches.then: Avoid the count-ratio estimator; work with predicted probabilities (which are never exactly 0) instead of adding pseudo-counts, which epidemiologists distrust as biasing.

action: Evaluate family-swapping alternatives (Poisson+log+offset, Gaussian+log link) for generative coherence vs the binary/binomial data.
discriminates: H4 (swap family to dodge the bound) vs H3 (keep a valid generative model and derive RR).
expected_findings: Poisson with log link + offset=log(Total) runs and yields a rate/relative-RISK-RATIO (not the binomial RR), good enough when events are rare (Poisson ~ binomial); but Poisson is not appropriate for binary 0/1 outcomes. Gaussian(link='log') may numerically match WinBUGS-style RR estimates but uses a continuous likelihood for binary data — generatively incoherent and not recommended even though a paper cites it. The coherent answer remains: use a proper inverse link and derive RR via posterior_linpred.
