action: Fit the nlf-based brms model with sensible priors and adapt_delta high, then compare estimates to the MPlus/Stan reference
discriminates: validates the brms fix end-to-end vs lingering misspecification
expected_findings: success: previously-off intercept/mean params now align with the reference within MC noise and prior-induced differences.
situations: A brms non-linear multilevel model meant to latent-mean-center a time-varying predictor (urge ~ alpha + phi*(urge1-alpha) + beta*(dep-depb), with depb ~ 1+(1|pe
leaves: phi, beta, sigma and all variance components match the authors' table closely; residual small gaps in alpha/depB attributed to prior differences / chain length, not structural error

branches.if_observe: estimates now match the reference
branches.then: accept nlf-wrapped latent-mean as the general brms solution

action: Interrogate the truncation: check whether truncation occurs in the prior tail and what it does on the unconstrained scale.
discriminates: Whether divergences/boundary mass are a real model feature or an artifact of truncation interacting with HMC (which the original accept-reject sampler never exposed).
expected_findings: Truncation-artifact hypothesis: probability mass piles at the truncation boundary because the unconstrained scale has infinite volume near it; removing truncation (truncation is only in the tail) is safe and improves geometry. Genuine-conflict hypothesis: removing truncation does not resolve the prior-data inconsistency.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: charlesm93: with truncation 'a lot of the probability mass concentrates at the boundary (on the unconstrained scale you have infinite volume near the boundary)'; argues you 'shouldn't need to truncate

branches.if_observe: Boundary pile-up disappears once truncation removed
branches.then: Keep priors untruncated and re-assess geometry

branches.if_observe: Prior-data conflict persists untruncated
branches.then: Audit the forward model / units (Move on units)

action: Re-derive the ODE/forward model from first principles (mass conservation per compartment) and analyze its structure for hidden linearity and parameter correlations.
discriminates: Whether the ODE is genuinely stiff/nonlinear everywhere (needing an expensive stiff solver) vs effectively linear (admitting a closed-form/matrix-exponential solution) and whether structural parameter anticorrelations build the funnel.
expected_findings: If the only nonlinearity is the Michaelis-Menten metabolic sink in one (liver) compartment, the rest is linear in the state -> exact/matrix-exponential solution exists and a structural V_s vs P_s anticorrelation is exposed (a built-in funnel/ridge). If genuinely nonlinear throughout, no closed form and discretization precision dominates.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: Funko_Unko proves dC_s/dt = sum_k A_sk C_k + f_s is LINEAR apart from the liver Michaelis-Menten term, derives the closed-form linear solution and reformulation in C_out,s = C_s/P_s revealing 'perfect

branches.if_observe: ODE is linear except for one sink term
branches.then: Use closed-form/matrix-exponential for the linear part; handle the MM term separately

branches.if_observe: Structural anticorrelation found between two parameter families
branches.then: Expect a ridge/funnel; consider reparameterizing to the identifiable combination

action: Pin down units and magical scaling constants by matching the equation dimensionally to the figures and to table values.
discriminates: Whether the prior-data conflict / wrong recovery is a units/specification error vs a genuine inference pathology.
expected_findings: If units were wrong (e.g. Vmax vs VMI*bodyWeight^0.7 scaling, KMI as mg vs mg/l), fixing them shifts recovered parameters toward the true/expected values; if units were right, the conflict survives and points back to geometry.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: Funko_Unko finds Vmax = VMI*bodyWeight^0.7 (VMI in mg/min/kg^-0.7), F_alveolar = 0.7*F_pulmonary, compartment volumes sum to a 'magically precise' 0.837/0.873 of lean body volume, and flags KMI units

branches.if_observe: Fixing a scaling makes recovery snap to truth
branches.then: Lock that constant; continue auditing remaining ambiguous units

branches.if_observe: Units verified correct but recovery still off
branches.then: Return to geometry/bug hypotheses

action: Treat unexpected multimodality as a code-bug suspect (not a real feature); audit the exact parameter constraints/transforms in the non-centering reparameterization.
discriminates: Whether the multimodality is genuine posterior structure vs a sign/inversion symmetry introduced by a wrong constraint bound.
expected_findings: Bug hypothesis: a constraint allows an inversion symmetry making two parameter values likelihood-equivalent -> exactly the observed mode-flipping; fixing the bound collapses the modes. Genuine-multimodality hypothesis: modes survive a correct constraint and reflect real non-identifiability of population spread under sparse data.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: The geometric-SD population_eS was constrained >0 instead of >1. Because person_params = population_eM .* pow(population_eS, unit_log_person_params) and pow(eS, x) == pow(1/eS, -x), values eS and 1/eS

branches.if_observe: Multimodality vanishes and leapfrog count drops after the constraint fix
branches.then: It was the bug; proceed to add back the nonlinear MM term

branches.if_observe: Modes persist after correct constraint
branches.then: Investigate genuine sparse-data non-identifiability of population spreads

action: Replace the black-box stiff solver with an operator-splitting scheme: exact flow for the linear part (matrix exponential) plus a cheap explicit approximation (one implicit-Euler step) for the MM part, with a tunable number of sub-steps.
discriminates: Whether the cost is intrinsic or an artifact of solving the (mostly-linear) system with a general stiff integrator; and how much discretization precision the geometry actually requires.
expected_findings: If the posterior concentrates in the near-linear region, few sub-steps suffice and fits drop to seconds-minutes; the wide prior (with nonlinear configs) needs many sub-steps. Larger VMI/KMI and smaller measurement noise demand more sub-steps.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: Funko_Unko uses a Strang-style splitting: exact linear flow + single implicit-Euler MM step (closed form), avoiding floating-point overflow of a pure exact splitting. Equidistant timepoints let the ma

branches.if_observe: Posterior mass sits in near-linear region
branches.then: Use a modest fixed sub-step count for cheap qualitative exploration

branches.if_observe: Prior support includes strongly nonlinear configs (for SBC)
branches.then: Expect much higher per-draw cost; adapt sub-steps per draw

action: Monitor discretization convergence: recompute lp__ at higher precision (more sub-steps) over the existing draws (e.g. via log_prob/generate_quantities) and use PSIS/importance-sampling diagnostics + ESS to decide if precision is sufficient.
discriminates: Whether residual sampling problems are statistical (geometry) or numerical (under-resolved ODE) — separates the discretization parameter dt/no_sub_steps from model parameters.
expected_findings: If under-resolved: recomputed lp__ at finer steps shifts the geometry / low PSIS-k or low ESS flags it -> increase sub-steps until stable. If adequately resolved: diagnostics stable as sub-steps increase, confirming faithful sampling.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: Funko_Unko recomputes lp__ with more sub-steps to estimate neff and decide if more steps are needed. Niko tunes dt and no_fit_sub_steps during warm-up until PSIS diagnostics show reliable+efficient sa

branches.if_observe: PSIS-k / lp__ recomputation unstable as sub-steps grow
branches.then: Increase precision (more sub-steps / smaller dt) and refit

branches.if_observe: Diagnostics stable across sub-step counts
branches.then: Precision is adequate; trust the posterior and report ESS

action: Tune warm-up to the precision requirements (precision-adaptive, incremental, parallel) rather than relying on default warm-up.
discriminates: Whether the hours-long runtime is fundamental or a warm-up/precision-scheduling inefficiency.
expected_findings: Precision-adaptive warm-up reaches higher minimal ESS in far less wall-time than default warm-up on the same oracle config; per-prior-draw cost still dominated by the few highly-nonlinear configs.
situations: A hierarchical (population-level) ODE model — e.g. a pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic compartment model with per-patient latent parameters and population means/spr
leaves: Niko: custom warm-up fit the Monster model in ~1 hour for minimal ESS~700; Stan's regular warm-up (given the oracle config) took ~4 hours for minimal ESS~83. 6 persons x 2 measurement rounds, 15 per-p

branches.if_observe: Adaptive warm-up gives large ESS-per-hour gain
branches.then: Adopt it as the workflow; treat precision tuning as part of warm-up

action: Triage magnitude + timing of the violation: is it tiny (machine-epsilon scale) and does it appear only on re-read rather than during sampling?
discriminates: H1 (precision round-trip) vs H2 (broken transform) vs H3 (tooling bug)
expected_findings: H1: violation is ~1e-6, matches default 6-sig-fig CSV truncation, and the live in-memory vector during the fit summed to ~0 exactly -- error only on re-read. H2: violation would be large/structural and present during sampling too. H3: violation tiny but framed as the validator being wrong, not the data.
situations: Using Stan's new sum_to_zero_vector[N] (CmdStan 2.36+) with a normal prior on a hierarchical model's varying intercepts for identification. The main fit runs fi
leaves: Sum reported as -2e-06, consistent with ~6 significant figures of CSV output; error fires only in loo moment-match recompile and standalone GQ (both re-read saved draws), never in the main NUTS run. T

branches.if_observe: violation ~1e-6 and only on re-read
branches.then: Adopt H1: round-trip precision loss; fix by increasing output precision rather than touching the model or filing a transform bug

branches.if_observe: violation large or present during the live fit
branches.then: Escalate to H2: investigate the constraint transform itself / file a stan-math issue

action: Increase saved-output numeric precision so the round-tripped zero-sum vector re-validates
discriminates: H1 (fixable by more sig_figs) vs H3 (would persist regardless of precision -> a real tooling tolerance bug)
expected_findings: Raising sig_figs makes the re-read sum close enough to 0 that the validator passes; if it still failed, the validator tolerance (H3) would be the true culprit.
situations: Using Stan's new sum_to_zero_vector[N] (CmdStan 2.36+) with a normal prior on a hierarchical model's varying intercepts for identification. The main fit runs fi
leaves: Set sig_figs=9 on the cmdstanR output (Brian Ward's suggestion; alternatively a compiler flag to loosen the tolerance). User confirmed sig_figs=9 cleared both the loo moment-match and standalone-GQ er

branches.if_observe: error clears
branches.then: Done -- it was output-precision truncation (H1); no model or transform change needed

branches.if_observe: error persists at high sig_figs
branches.then: Reclassify as H3 and pursue the validator tolerance / loosen-tolerance compiler flag, or report to loo maintainers

action: Audit constraints: separate hard (physical, e.g. positivity) from soft (plausible-range) constraints; replace soft interval bounds with priors, and guarantee transformed-param bounds hold by construction (log-scale)
discriminates: whether warmup slowness is caused by the sampler repeatedly hitting bounds and shrinking step size, vs a deeper geometry problem
expected_findings: Hard-bound hypothesis predicts the constraint-violation warnings vanish and step size / treedepth recover once soft bounds become priors and transforms are on the log scale. Stan only CHECKS (does not enforce) transformed-param bounds, so pAm_i = pAm+sd*z routinely violates its declared <lower,upper> -> rejection -> veeery small steps.
situations: A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje
leaves: Soft constraints -> priors (e.g. real<lower=0> pAm; pAm ~ lognormal(4.9,0.15)); reserve bounds for true hard constraints. For positive params use log-scale parametrization and drop interval bounds. Re

branches.if_observe: warnings gone, speed up ~4-5x with parallelism, but still hours not minutes
branches.then: remaining slowness is parametrization-funnel + discontinuity -> next moves

branches.if_observe: no change
branches.then: bounds were not the bottleneck; move to discontinuity / parametrization

action: Smooth the discontinuity, but check that the smoothing is actually mild (large-slope tanh is numerically still a discontinuity); consider eliminating the switch entirely by restructuring states or solving the ODE piecewise around the threshold-crossing event
discriminates: whether ODE-solver cost / gradient pathology comes from the non-smooth RHS vs elsewhere
expected_findings: A very steep tanh behaves numerically almost like the step (no relief); a milder slope should reduce solver work and stabilize gradients. Better still: collapse states {E,V,H,UR} to {E,V,H+UR} (recover H=min(Hp,H+UR)) so the switch is no longer in the integrated dynamics, or solve fecund/non-fecund segments separately if puberty time can be solved for.
situations: A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje

branches.if_observe: milder slope / restructured states integrate faster and sample cleaner
branches.then: keep; the step was a real cost

branches.if_observe: interpretability demands the sharp threshold
branches.then: move threshold handling out of the ODE RHS into an event/likelihood term

action: Move the expensive computation (the per-subject ODE solve) INSIDE the partial_sum function so the parallelized work is the ODE, not just the cheap likelihood reduction
discriminates: whether the lack of parallel speedup is a wiring problem vs a fundamental limit
expected_findings: With the ODE inside partial_sum, speedup approaches the number of cores (~#fish), because ODE evaluation dominates cost; user then observed ~4-5x, matching wds15's prediction (cmdstan >2.24 + variadic ODE interface required).
situations: A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje
leaves: reduce_sum only parallelizes what is computed inside partial_sum_lpdf; grainsize=1 is fine here since each ODE solve is heavy. Pack the ode_rk45 call into the partial-sum slice keyed by fish.

branches.if_observe: speedup ~ Ncores after moving ODE inside
branches.then: parallelism fixed; remaining gap is warmup/geometry

action: Treat the zeros as a model-specification issue, not a numeric nuisance: match the likelihood to the data-generating process (discrete count, censoring, or a maturity-event/threshold likelihood) instead of fudging zeros into a lognormal
discriminates: whether the flat/pathological likelihood contribution from the zero-fudge is a geometry drag and a correctness bug, vs harmless
expected_findings: Adding 1e-20 makes a locally-flat likelihood contribution in the immature regime (no gradient pulling the fish out of/into maturity), which both hurts geometry and is statistically wrong: if there are exact zeros the error is not lognormal. A negative-binomial on egg counts handles zeros seamlessly; or model the maturity event directly e.g. 0~bernoulli_logit(H+UR-Hp) to push immature fish away from observed-egg contradictions; censoring is an option if zeros mean below-detection.
situations: A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje
leaves: Lognormal cannot host structural zeros. Options: negative binomial on egg counts (log-mean proportional to latent), censored likelihood (Stan user guide), or a bernoulli_logit threshold-event term on

branches.if_observe: data are egg counts
branches.then: negative binomial -> zeros handled, no fudge

branches.if_observe: zeros mean immature (true structural)
branches.then: threshold-event term / restructure so maturity is a modeled latent event

action: Adapt the centeredness of the hierarchical parametrization continuously during warmup (rather than committing to fully centered or fully non-centered), and consider that adding more subjects can stabilize the between-subject variance estimate
discriminates: whether the residual slowness is a static-parametrization funnel that a per-parameter, continuously-adapted centeredness resolves
expected_findings: Continuously adapting centeredness during a custom warmup is the dominant lever: an expert reproduced the fit in ~5 min for 10 fish and ~12 min for 32 fish (vs 20+ hours) with good diagnostics, attributing the gain chiefly to adaptive centeredness plus a better warmup. More fish actually helped pin down between-fish variance, easing the funnel.
situations: A hierarchical Bayesian ODE model (coupled multi-state ODE, latent states observed only through noisy derived quantities) is correct and fast for a single subje
leaves: Stan's default warmup + a single fixed centeredness is the bottleneck for variable-centeredness funnels; a custom warmup that adapts centeredness per parameter (VI/partial pooling continuum) gives nea

branches.if_observe: adaptive-centeredness warmup available
branches.then: minutes-scale fit achievable -> resolution

branches.if_observe: only stock Stan warmup available
branches.then: settle for parallelism+bounds+smoothing gains (hours), keep separate-per-subject fallback

action: inspect the parameterization for invariances - check whether the map from unconstrained params to the target-relevant quantity is one-to-one
discriminates: H1 (non-identifiability) vs H2/H4 (formula/numerics)
expected_findings: If H1: a clear translation symmetry x->x+c leaving the likelihood unchanged, explaining the flat target; baseline native simplex model samples fine. If H2/H4: no such symmetry, problem would persist even after identifying.
situations: Want to give a prior (e.g. Dirichlet) to a simplex via an unconstrained vector mapped through softmax, adding a Jacobian correction by hand (target += log_deter
leaves: Note that softmax(prop) is invariant to adding a constant to all elements of prop (exp(alpha)/exp(alpha) cancels); since the target depends only on prop_soft, there is a flat ridge -> non-identified,

branches.if_observe: translation invariance present (full K free vector)
branches.then: remove the extra degree of freedom: fix one element (e.g. 0) or impose sum-to-zero, reducing to K-1 free params, then re-test

branches.if_observe: already K-1 params yet still failing
branches.then: shift suspicion to the Jacobian expression / numerics (H2/H3/H4)

action: Triage severity: judge whether the plotted posterior correlation is large enough to actually impede HMC, and separate the correlation question from the divergence question
discriminates: benign correlation (cosmetic) vs harmful geometry (funnel/strong curvature) that drives divergences
expected_findings: Modest plotted correlation alone is usually NOT enough to give the numerics trouble; divergences and thin tails are the signals that matter and must be chased separately
situations: After non-centered reparameterization of correlated parameters in a latent AR(1) state-space model with sigmoid (inv_logit / soft-clipping) link components (inp
leaves: Expert: 'I do not think the correlation in the posteriors you plotted would be enough to give the numerics difficulty.' Decouples the correlation worry from the divergence/tail worry.

branches.if_observe: correlation is mild and parameters of interest have R-hat~1, high n_eff
branches.then: deprioritize the correlation; focus on divergences, tails, and runtime

branches.if_observe: correlation is extreme / funnel-shaped
branches.then: suspect mass-matrix mismatch or non-identifiability; pursue dense metric or model reparam

action: Do NOT band-aid the tails with hard bounds; investigate WHY the sampler visits them via posterior predictive exploration
discriminates: numerical artifact vs genuine alternative solutions the data support (weak identifiability)
expected_findings: Tails are sampled frequently because they are reasonable-enough solutions; plotting model predictions for tail vs non-tail draws reveals what the parameter is trading off; hard constraints just pile density at the boundary instead of fixing the cause
situations: After non-centered reparameterization of correlated parameters in a latent AR(1) state-space model with sigmoid (inv_logit / soft-clipping) link components (inp
leaves: Expert: 'They must be reasonable-enough solutions that you're sampling them frequently. Make plots of predictions when this parameter is in the tail regions vs not.' User had already found hard constr

branches.if_observe: tail draws give plausible predictions
branches.then: this is a weak-identifiability symptom -> fix via model (priors/reparam/more data), not constraints

branches.if_observe: tail draws are clearly degenerate/non-predictive
branches.then: reconsider likelihood/parameterization of that component

action: Probe identifiability by shifting the true generating value and watching whether the estimate tracks the data or stays anchored to the prior mean
discriminates: data-identified parameter (estimate follows true value) vs weakly/non-identified (estimate pulled to prior mean regardless of truth)
expected_findings: If the estimate sits between truth and prior mean and barely moves with the data, the parameter is weakly identified by the likelihood; the sigmoid (inv_logit) nonlinearity and the all-ones intercept column alias the intercept p[1] against the slope p[3], inflating their correlation and variance
situations: After non-centered reparameterization of correlated parameters in a latent AR(1) state-space model with sigmoid (inv_logit / soft-clipping) link components (inp
leaves: User: hard-coding intercept p[1] gives clean p[2:3]; freeing all of p[1:3] produces very strong p[1]-p[3] correlation. User tests by moving the true p[3] to 0.68 to see if estimate lands between -> id

branches.if_observe: estimate ignores truth, anchored to prior
branches.then: non-identifiability -> reparameterize / fix intercept / stronger justified prior / more data

branches.if_observe: estimate tracks the shifted truth
branches.then: parameter is identified; inaccuracy is just finite-sample noise

action: Diagnose warmup empirically (save_warmup=1, plot traceplots and the step-size trajectory across warmup) before guessing buffer sizes; choose adapt_delta as the smallest value that avoids divergences
discriminates: adaptation starting before reaching the typical set (then larger init_buffer helps) vs step-size collapse / curvature problems (buffers won't fix)
expected_findings: If chains haven't reached a common region by ~150-200 draws, enlarge init_buffer; watch for step-size plummeting (e.g. to 1e-10) in warmup; (window,term_buffer) ~ (35,150) helped marginally; crucially, unnecessarily high adapt_delta itself causes max_treedepth hits and long runtime -> tune adapt_delta DOWN to the divergence-free minimum
situations: After non-centered reparameterization of correlated parameters in a latent AR(1) state-space model with sigmoid (inv_logit / soft-clipping) link components (inp
leaves: User's empirical sweep: diag_e at adapt_delta 0.82 beats dense at 0.982 on speed; default buffers near-optimal; key lesson 'finding the suitable adapt_delta, not the higher the better.' Save_warmup pl

branches.if_observe: chains converge to common region late in warmup
branches.then: increase init_buffer / window; keep step-size adaptation at default

branches.if_observe: step-size collapses or treedepth saturates during warmup
branches.then: lower adapt_delta to the divergence-free minimum and/or fix the model geometry

action: Ablate model components: start from intercept-only (y~1), add predictors, then add the group-level + covariance term one at a time, re-running pp_check / dispersion ppc_stat at each step
discriminates: which component (covariates vs varying intercepts vs covariance matrix) introduces the dispersion inflation
expected_findings: Intercept-only gives dispersion ~1 (clean Poisson); covariates bring it to the observed ~1.1-1.2; the (1|gr(species,cov=A)) term is what jumps dispersion to ~2 — isolates the covariance/group term as the culprit, not the response family alone
situations: A Poisson GLMM with a phylogenetic group-level term (1|gr(species, cov=A)) on zero-heavy, slightly-overdispersed counts (78% zeroes, observed dispersion ~1.1).

branches.if_observe: dispersion jumps only when the covariance term is added
branches.then: drop the covariance matrix (fit plain 1|species) to test whether the covariance vs the bare grouping factor is responsible

branches.if_observe: dispersion already off with covariates only
branches.then: suspect response distribution / prior, not the covariance

action: Compare 1|species (no matrix) against 1|gr(species, cov=A) via pp_check
discriminates: whether it is the correlation structure specifically, or any species-level random effect, that inflates variance
expected_findings: Plain 1|species predicts the marginal distribution well; adding the covariance matrix widens the predictive distribution (more high counts) — points at the covariance structure, and at the structural coupling of inflation to the intercept SD
situations: A Poisson GLMM with a phylogenetic group-level term (1|gr(species, cov=A)) on zero-heavy, slightly-overdispersed counts (78% zeroes, observed dispersion ~1.1).

branches.if_observe: only the cov-matrix version is bad
branches.then: interrogate the covariance/phylogeny: data integrity, or signal strength vs assumed strength

action: Quantify phylogenetic signal (Moran's I) on the data and on the posterior-mean varying intercepts (with vs without the matrix); simulate datasets with and without signal and refit both models
discriminates: covariance-data-corruption / weak-signal hypothesis vs a genuine model-data incompatibility; also checks direction of the effect
expected_findings: Data has real, moderate signal (I~0.31). The matrix inflates posterior autocorrelation BEYOND the data's (e.g. 0.14 data -> 0.26 posterior with matrix). Variance inflation appears only in the PRESENCE of signal — the opposite of a 'matrix is broken / signal too weak' story; the matrix is over-imposing correlation, raising variance and pushing predictions off the data
situations: A Poisson GLMM with a phylogenetic group-level term (1|gr(species, cov=A)) on zero-heavy, slightly-overdispersed counts (78% zeroes, observed dispersion ~1.1).

branches.if_observe: inflation only when signal present, matrix increases autocorrelation past the data
branches.then: reframe: the covariance model is genuinely mis-fitting (over-correlating), not corrupted — but note this may still help out-of-sample

branches.if_observe: no-signal data also over-correlates yet pp_check stays fine
branches.then: the marginal pp_check is insensitive to this — escalate to a pointwise / out-of-sample diagnostic

action: Switch from the marginal-distribution view to pointwise accuracy: histogram of (predicted - observed) per observation, then formalize as a ppc_stat over many draws (e.g. % correctly predicted), and bring in LOO/WAIC for out-of-sample predictive comparison
discriminates: in-sample marginal-shape fit vs pointwise / out-of-sample predictive performance — separates 'systematic error / miscalibrated uncertainty' from 'large but non-systematic error'
expected_findings: The covariance model is MORE accurate pointwise (e.g. ~71% vs ~57% correct) and clearly preferred by LOO (large elpd_diff, acceptable Pareto-k). Without the matrix the error is large but non-systematic, so marginal pp_check passes while LOO fails — diagnostics measure different things and disagree by design
situations: A Poisson GLMM with a phylogenetic group-level term (1|gr(species, cov=A)) on zero-heavy, slightly-overdispersed counts (78% zeroes, observed dispersion ~1.1).

branches.if_observe: LOO favors cov model but marginal pp_check favors no-cov
branches.then: trust LOO for predictive ranking; treat marginal pp_check as a qualitative calibration check, not a model-selection oracle

branches.if_observe: still ambiguous
branches.then: do grouped pp_checks (by taxonomic order) to localize WHERE the response distribution fails

action: Run grouped PP checks (pp_check by taxonomic order / family) and reason about the response distribution's variance-mean coupling against the observed 0/1-with-sharp-cutoff shape
discriminates: global response-family inadequacy vs a localized subgroup where mean~1 forces the Poisson into an impossible underdispersed shape
expected_findings: Most groups are near-zero-mean and fit acceptably; a subgroup (Galliformes, with mean near 1) fits terribly because Poisson/NB cannot give high prob to 1-2 while near-zero prob to 3-4 (variance is locked to the mean). Confirms the core issue is the response distribution's inability to represent underdispersed small counts — covariance was a secondary amplifier
situations: A Poisson GLMM with a phylogenetic group-level term (1|gr(species, cov=A)) on zero-heavy, slightly-overdispersed counts (78% zeroes, observed dispersion ~1.1).

branches.if_observe: a mean~1 subgroup drives the misfit
branches.then: the response distribution is the real problem; try distributions that decouple zeroes/tail or model counts as ordered thresholds

action: Try alternative response families that decouple the zero/low-count structure from the tail: hurdle Poisson, zero-inflated Poisson, COM-Poisson, and an ordinal model (cratio on trait+1)
discriminates: which family can produce high prob on 1-2 with near-zero prob on >3 — i.e. handle underdispersion / a hard upper cutoff
expected_findings: ZIP ~ identical to Poisson (zi~0.01, can't reduce zeroes below Poisson); hurdle fixes zeroes but the tail blows up (predicts counts in the 1000s); COM-Poisson fails to converge; cratio (continuation-ratio ordinal) fits excellently overall and per-order, with fixed-effect directions matching the Poisson model and good Rhat/ESS
situations: A Poisson GLMM with a phylogenetic group-level term (1|gr(species, cov=A)) on zero-heavy, slightly-overdispersed counts (78% zeroes, observed dispersion ~1.1).

branches.if_observe: cratio fits cleanly and preserves the sign of effects
branches.then: adopt the ordinal model; justify it as a flexible count model (sequential hurdles 0->1->2->...) and as spur-count being the rank of a latent continuous total-spur-mass

branches.if_observe: worry that ordinal is 'cheating' (extra intercept params)
branches.then: note cratio has max_observed-2 extra intercepts so greater flexibility is expected; defend interpretability rather than treating accuracy as suspicious

action: Reduce the BNN to the simplest model that exhibits the same pathology and recover known truth from simulated data
discriminates: whether the pathology is intrinsic to flexible exchangeable models (so it reproduces in a 3-component Gaussian mixture) vs specific to deep nets
expected_findings: A mixture of Gaussians with means and SDs free reproduces the long curving valleys; importantly the nasty continuous non-identifiabilities only appear with MORE THAN TWO components, so a 2-component test would falsely look benign.

action: Characterize the local gradient/curvature geometry of the non-identified valley and connect it to step-size limits
discriminates: a step-size/curvature problem (controllable but slow) vs a global mixing problem (HMC follows one connected region but cannot bridge to disconnected probability mass)
expected_findings: Along the valley the gradient is ~zero (no force to move you); across it the gradient is zero at the bottom but changes rapidly (high curvature) — same geometry as the neck of a funnel, forcing a tiny step size. Separately, if probability is diffuse with complex geometry, HMC must build very long trajectories and crawls.

action: Stress-test each candidate against the high-dimensional, multimodal, full-posterior requirement
discriminates: remedies that only find/explore around one mode or only work in low dimension vs ones that actually quantify all modes/paths in high dimension
expected_findings: An ensemble of optimizers tuned to one objective (varying inits) does not yield draws distributed as the target; perturbed-energy (Gumbel/A*) sampling scales terribly with dimension and needs many solves; SGLD/stochastic-gradient methods don't scale to high dimension and uncorrected Langevin (no Metropolis correction) introduces asymptotic bias/error drift. Optimization only needs ONE path/optimum; Bayesian QUANTIFICATION needs all of them.

action: Probe whether this filter-vs-smoother resemblance is superficial or a real structural property of full-joint Bayesian inference.

action: Benchmark the two formulations head-to-head on speed, ESS, and parameter estimates to decide whether the filter is worth its complexity.
discriminates: explicit-GRW (smoother) vs Kalman-marginalized formulation — and re-tests H3 by exposing whether the filter-vs-smoother distinction produces materially different inferences

The GRW/explicit-state form leaks future info (smoother) and explores the variance space poorly; the Kalman marginalization exploits conjugacy to make the parameter geometry easy. Confirms the filter-vs-smoother choice changes parameter estimates and downstream missing-data interpolation.

action: Decompose the chain's behavior into the directions where it random-walks vs the directions where it moves coherently
discriminates: Why NUTS is efficient where RWMH is not — locates the residual random walk and counts how many dimensions still suffer from it
expected_findings: RWMH random-walks in all d position dimensions (diffusive, ~d² cost-to-explore). HMC/NUTS augments to 2d variables (position+momentum); it retains only a 1-D random walk in energy (level sets of the Hamiltonian) and moves coherently/efficiently in the remaining 2d-1 directions. NUTS additionally tries to fling the trajectory as far as possible from the start (vs HMC's fixed travel-time), maximizing the coherent step.
situations: Want a coherent heuristic for WHY NUTS/HMC beats random-walk Metropolis as dimension d grows, and where the break-even is. Have a candidate framework from Neal'
leaves: Random-walk-in-energy picture: RWMH = d-dimensional random walk; NUTS = 1-D random walk in energy + efficient coherent exploration in 2d-1 dims; energy is a function of all 2d variables, so no single

action: Step back to the gradient vs gradient-free dichotomy and the type of gradient dynamics
discriminates: H4: it's not really about the specific exponent at all — it's derivative-free (guess-and-check, exponentially hard in d) vs derivative-based, and within derivative-based, diffusive (Langevin/RW-flavored) vs coherent (HMC)
expected_findings: Guess-and-check / derivative-free becomes exponentially hard as d grows → forced toward gradient methods. Among gradient methods, diffusive (Langevin) loses to coherent (HMC) essentially always. So the high-D winner is structurally HMC regardless of fine exponent bookkeeping.
situations: Want a coherent heuristic for WHY NUTS/HMC beats random-walk Metropolis as dimension d grows, and where the break-even is. Have a candidate framework from Neal'

action: Reintroduce the prefactor/constant and ask what controls it; check it against concrete high-D demonstrations
discriminates: H1 (exponent decides) vs H2 (constant prefactor dominates in practice, driven by posterior correlation/conditioning)
expected_findings: The O(d^{5/4}) vs O(d²) statement omits the constant factor F/G, which can be enormous for Gibbs/Metropolis when parameters are strongly correlated (ill-conditioned posterior). Concrete: original NUTS paper's 250-D correlated multivariate normal — optimally tuned RW-Metropolis and Gibbs fail to converge after 1M iterations; NUTS nails it in ~1K. So the practical gap is dominated by the constant, not the exponent.

action: Add a safety/validity check: ask whether the cheaper sampler's ESS / R-hat are even trustworthy

situations: A Bayesian latent class / finite-mixture model (Bernoulli/categorical indicators) samples cleanly for 2 classes but produces bimodal / multimodal marginal poste
leaves: spinkney: ordered[C] pi[I] removes bimodality but is a strictly more restrictive model (assumes logits ordered across classes for EVERY item) -> explains divergence from poLCA. Brauer's correction: th

branches.if_observe: Bimodality gone but disagrees with poLCA and theory
branches.then: Reject as too-strong; relax to ordering one anchor item only

branches.if_observe: Ordering one item still inconsistent run-to-run
branches.then: Treat as starting-value sensitivity; constraint alone insufficient

action: Sanity-check any identifying reparameterization against the model's substantive meaning before adopting
discriminates: valid identification vs an assumption that silently redefines the data-generating model
expected_findings: simplex[C] pi[I] runs and matches poLCA after rebasing, but it forces item probabilities across classes to sum to 1 (e.g. P(correct|class1)=0.9 forces P(correct|class2)=0.1), which is not implied by LCA theory -> invalid as a general identification device.
situations: A Bayesian latent class / finite-mixture model (Bernoulli/categorical indicators) samples cleanly for 2 classes but produces bimodal / multimodal marginal poste
leaves: Brauer's correction to spinkney: the simplex constraint is 'another identifying assumption that substantially changes the meaning of the model'; flipping indices doesn't help. Lesson: distinguish cons

branches.if_observe: Constraint changes substantive interpretation
branches.then: Discard regardless of how clean the sampling looks

action: Replace the model-estimated (shared hyperprior) on item parameters with a fixed, sufficiently wide prior and re-examine modality
discriminates: H5 (prior-induced mode-merging) vs constraint-only explanations
expected_findings: Switching from an estimated prior on pi (mu_pi, sigma_pi learned) to a fixed wide prior (e.g. pi ~ normal(0,5)) yields unimodal marginals for this dataset -- evidence the shared prior was squeezing classes together -- but does NOT fully solve identification (still run-dependent).
situations: A Bayesian latent class / finite-mixture model (Bernoulli/categorical indicators) samples cleanly for 2 classes but produces bimodal / multimodal marginal poste
leaves: Brauer: dropping the estimated prior in favor of a preset wide prior gives unimodal pi; 'best guess why your specific example is multimodal' but 'doubt it fully solves' identification. Author confirms

branches.if_observe: Unimodal after fixing prior
branches.then: Keep wide fixed prior; but verify across multiple seeds

branches.if_observe: Still flips between runs
branches.then: Prior alone insufficient; problem is sampler trapping / intrinsic multimodality

action: Reparameterize the likelihood from raw per-class logits to a structured intercept + main-effect (DINA-style) parameterization, with a constraint difference between C=2 and C>2
discriminates: H6 (parameterization pathology) vs irreducible difficulty of the likelihood
expected_findings: The DINA-adapted parameterization (mean intercept + item deviations + class main effect) samples well for increasing C, where the plain bernoulli_lpmf form fails -- but the specific exponent line pi=master^(c-1)*nonmaster^(1-(c-1)) is only valid for C<=2 and imposes a nonsensical structure (e.g. P1^2/P2) for C>2.
situations: A Bayesian latent class / finite-mixture model (Bernoulli/categorical indicators) samples cleanly for 2 classes but produces bimodal / multimodal marginal poste
leaves: Author imports DINA model code; works for larger C with a small constraint change. Brauer flags the master/nonmaster exponent line as invalid for C>2. spinkney rewrites it: drop the exponent trick, us

branches.if_observe: Recovers poLCA, no divergences, but slow
branches.then: Accept; optionally simplify to reduce runtime

branches.if_observe: Need polytomous indicators
branches.then: Dummy-code each level into binaries and reuse the same binary model (works), or move to a per-variable simplex over levels

action: Adopt poLCA's native multinomial likelihood: per-variable, per-class simplex over response levels, marginalized via log_sum_exp
discriminates: fastest/cleanest identified parameterization vs the workaround of dummy-coding levels into binaries
expected_findings: array[J,C] simplex[K] prob with llik incremented only at the observed level reproduces poLCA up to relabeling; MLE optimizer matches poLCA exactly; full HMC runs in ~8s with no divergences or treedepth issues for moderate C -- but for larger C still needs an ordering constraint (e.g. positive_ordered lambda for nu) and remains sensitive to init and to the Dirichlet/gamma alpha on class proportions.
situations: A Bayesian latent class / finite-mixture model (Bernoulli/categorical indicators) samples cleanly for 2 classes but produces bimodal / multimodal marginal poste
leaves: spinkney's final per-level-simplex model matches poLCA's log L = sum_i ln sum_r p_r prod_j prod_k pi_{jrk}^{Y_ijk}; runs in ~8s. Author then adds positive_ordered lambda -> nu ordering: converges 'som

branches.if_observe: Converges without divergences but only on some seeds
branches.then: Add an ordering anchor (on nu via positive_ordered lambda, or on class_effect_non); accept residual init-sensitivity

branches.if_observe: Still mode-trapping across seeds
branches.then: Try better inits (VI/MLE-based) rather than random; multimodality is intrinsic, manage rather than eliminate

action: Look at the raw varying-intercept estimates (ranef) histograms in both models
discriminates: normal-scale shrinkage (H3 controlled) vs runaway/heavy-tailed random intercepts (H3/H4 active)
expected_findings: H3/H4: Model B respondent offsets are 'wild', wide and heavy-tailed; Model A offsets look like a sane bell shape
situations: Two nested brms cumulative-probit ordinal models on Likert data with crossed (1|respondent)+(1|question) random intercepts; the only difference is one model add
leaves: Model B random-intercept histogram is wacky/over-dispersed vs Model A's reasonable shape; this first raises 'maybe I just need a stronger prior on the random effects' and points the finger at the prio

branches.if_observe: Model B offsets pathologically wide vs Model A sane
branches.then: suspect weak identification of the upper-level variance (H3); test prior sensitivity directly

action: Prior sensitivity test #2: keep default SD prior but add weakly-informative threshold priors derived from an equiprobable-category null via qnorm(cumulative proportions)
discriminates: whether the upper-level variance OR the flat threshold prior is the binding source of non-identification (both can express H3)
expected_findings: if thresholds were the weak link: informative threshold priors alone tame the model even with the default SD prior
situations: Two nested brms cumulative-probit ordinal models on Likert data with crossed (1|respondent)+(1|question) random intercepts; the only difference is one model add
leaves: normal(0,1) on sd makes ALL parameters (respondent sd 11.32->1.13, question sd 2.52->0.49, thresholds) match Model A; pp_check no worse / slightly better; loo flips from elpd_diff~170 (se 17) favoring

Adding qnorm-derived normal(., 1) threshold priors (and leaving SD prior default) is by itself enough to make the model behave -- shows the generous flat threshold prior is an independent contributor;

branches.if_observe: threshold priors alone fix it
branches.then: confirms H3 generally: brms defaults are too generous for upper-level variance AND ordinal thresholds in this regime

action: Diagnostic battery on the data: per-respondent/per-question response distributions, count of always-extreme responders, and pp_check(type='bars_grouped', group=group) plus outcome overlap by group
discriminates: H4 (extreme responders drive inflation) and 'group-distributions differ' vs H3 (it's the prior, data is benign)
expected_findings: H4: many always-extreme respondents and/or strongly divergent group distributions; H3: only a few extreme responders, near-uniform respondent means, groups overlap heavily
situations: Two nested brms cumulative-probit ordinal models on Likert data with crossed (1|respondent)+(1|question) random intercepts; the only difference is one model add
leaves: Only 3 respondents always pick 1, none always 7; respondent means ~uniform; question means narrow (3.1-4.2); grouped bar pp_check shows the two group distributions are NOT drastically different -> sep

branches.if_observe: few extreme responders and overlapping groups
branches.then: down-weight H4 and group-difference; the inflation is identification/prior, not data pathology

action: Swap probit link for logit, keep ALL priors at default, refit
discriminates: H5 (link-function fragility) vs H3 (purely prior-driven regardless of link)
expected_findings: H5: logit gives sensible estimates with default priors; H3: link wouldn't matter
situations: Two nested brms cumulative-probit ordinal models on Likert data with crossed (1|respondent)+(1|question) random intercepts; the only difference is one model add
leaves: Logit link with default priors yields much more sensible estimates with no prior changes; matti notes logit samples faster and coefficients scale roughly by x/1.6 to the probit/normal scale -> link ch

branches.if_observe: logit-default behaves where probit-default did not
branches.then: treat probit+flat-default as a fragile combination; prefer logit or add weak priors

action: Reason from outcome support+shape: enumerate candidate families by matching the data's support (bounded integer, both sides) and shape (bimodal + boundary mass) instead of trying families ad hoc
discriminates: count-on-unbounded-support families vs bounded/ordinal families
expected_findings: Gaussian: continuous, ignores bounds, smears density and misses the boundary spike. Poisson/negbinomial: unbounded above, leak mass past 10. Bounded/ordinal families should respect 1..10 exactly.
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: Confirms any unbounded count family (Poisson, negbinomial, ZI/ZINB) will mis-fit at the upper bound regardless of dispersion tuning.

branches.if_observe: predictions exceed the hard upper bound
branches.then: drop unbounded count families; move to truncated or ordinal/binomial families that respect both bounds

branches.if_observe: only the mean matches but density shape is wrong
branches.then: this is a shape problem, not a location problem -> need a family whose support/shape matches, not a link/dispersion tweak

action: Read out the failure signatures of each attempted family to rule out the count/zero-inflation/mixture branches
discriminates: whether dispersion/zero-inflation/mixture flexibility can rescue a count framing vs a fundamentally wrong family class
expected_findings: negbinomial/ZINB: not meaningfully better than Gaussian and harder to interpret. truncated Poisson: best density but bad stat_2d, divergences + invalid-prediction (ntrys) warning. hurdle/binomial: density horrible-to-ok, stat_2d never good. Gaussian mixture: thousands of divergences, hours to run -> identifiability/sampling failure.
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: ntrys/invalid-prediction warning is the truncation machinery rejecting out-of-range draws; mixture divergences flag a poorly identified latent-class model. Both are symptoms of forcing the wrong gener

branches.if_observe: truncated Poisson density good but moments (stat_2d) bad
branches.then: truncation patches the support but not the mechanism; keep searching for a family matching the data-generating process

branches.if_observe: mixture gives thousands of divergences
branches.then: abandon the unconstrained mixture; the bimodality likely reflects a structured process (sequential stopping), not two free latent Gaussians

action: Switch pp_check to discrete-appropriate views: type='bars' and type='bars_grouped'
discriminates: per-category fit and subgroup fit for an integer outcome vs a smoothed density that hides category-level and boundary behavior
expected_findings: bars/bars_grouped should expose exactly where each family mis-counts categories (especially the upper-bound pile-up and per-group means).
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: For the truncated Poisson, bars_grouped shows shape captured reasonably but mean/sd reproduced better by Gaussian; neither is fully satisfactory -> reinforces that no count framing wins cleanly.

branches.if_observe: bars reveal systematic miss at the boundary category / within subgroups
branches.then: the family does not match the mechanism; prefer an ordinal family that fits each category directly

action: Propose an ORDINAL model, specifically the continuation-ratio / sequential family (brms cratio), as matching the data-generating process; point to Burkner's ordinal-models tutorial (the 'Sequential models' section)
discriminates: ordinal/sequential generative match vs count/continuous framings
expected_findings: cratio pp_checks fit the overall distribution almost perfectly ('too good to be true') because ordinal families carry N-1 thresholds for N categories; the sequential link mirrors the experiment's draw-by-draw stopping.
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: cratio = continuation-ratio = sequential model: each category's probability is a conditional stop-vs-continue probability, directly mirroring 'after each draw, decide or draw again'. Naively swapping

branches.if_observe: cratio pp_check looks 'too good to be true'
branches.then: expected from N-1 parameters; shift scrutiny to subgroup fits and interpretability, not overall density

branches.if_observe: user unsure how to interpret cratio coefficients
branches.then: read the sequential-models section of the ordinal tutorial; verify the sequential story actually matches the experiment before trusting it

action: Reframe selection: warn that ordinal flexibility (N-1 thresholds) makes a good overall fit unsurprising, so judge on SUBGROUP fit and interpretability, and guard overfitting with cross-validation (kfold; grouped folds per subject)
discriminates: overall-distribution fit (uninformative here) vs subgroup fit + genuine predictive generalization
expected_findings: overall density fit is nearly guaranteed for ordinal; meaningful discrimination comes from how well subgroups (groups x scenarios) are reproduced and from out-of-sample CV that respects the hierarchical/repeated-measures structure.
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: kfold with folds generated per subject (e.g. 10% of each subject's trials per fold) avoids leakage from the (1|chiffre) repeated-measures structure; plain random folds would be optimistic.

branches.if_observe: subgroup pp_checks also fit well under CV
branches.then: trust cratio as the model that matches both data and mechanism

branches.if_observe: good overall fit but poor subgroup/CV behavior
branches.then: treat the good density as overfitting; reconsider structure rather than family

action: Caution against using loo as the primary selector here, for one technical and one conceptual reason
discriminates: predictive-density ranking vs question-driven model choice
expected_findings: technical: loo cannot directly compare CONTINUOUS-family fits against DISCRETE-family fits (different likelihood normalization; see Vehtari's CV-FAQ). conceptual: loo optimizes out-of-sample prediction, which can favor models that are worse for the actual inferential question (e.g. confounder/collider analogies); a more interpretable family may be preferable even if loo disfavors it.
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: loo should be second-order; the primary input is the question being asked. Gaussian-vs-cratio is exactly the continuous-vs-discrete case loo cannot compare directly.

branches.if_observe: models share the same outcome scale/family class
branches.then: loo_compare is usable as a secondary check

branches.if_observe: comparing continuous vs discrete (Gaussian vs ordinal)
branches.then: loo is invalid as a direct comparator; decide on mechanism/interpretability instead

action: Apply the all-models-are-wrong pragmatic test (Box's 'tigers vs mice'): judge adequacy against the specific inferences needed, not against a perfect pp_check
discriminates: harmful misfit for the target inference vs cosmetically-imperfect-but-adequate fit
expected_findings: if only subgroup means/sds are of interest and the Gaussian reproduces those well in the relevant subgroups, the density misfit may be tolerable; but the sequential cratio is the safer, more mechanism-faithful and interpretable default.
situations: Bounded integer outcome (draws-to-decision, ranges 1-10) that is bimodal with a middle peak and a large pile-up at the upper bound. Gaussian/student/skew-normal
leaves: Box: 'Since all models are wrong the scientist must be alert to what is importantly wrong... be concerned about tigers, not mice.' Adequacy is inference-relative.

branches.if_observe: inference is only about means/sds and Gaussian matches them in relevant subgroups
branches.then: Gaussian can be defensible (no worse than the standard frequentist choice)

branches.if_observe: inference touches the distribution shape, boundary behavior, or per-category probabilities
branches.then: the Gaussian misfit IS importantly wrong -> use the ordinal/sequential model

action: Operationalize the error signal: plot per-group residuals (point estimate vs raw rate) against the amount of data per group and check for a trend
discriminates: real systematic bias (trend in residuals) vs benign shrinkage (unbiased residuals as a function of sample size)
expected_findings: H1/H2/H3: residuals biased — more low-data groups fall on one side of the fitted band (here more points below the band in the 0-100 at-bat region = abilities pulled too high). H5: residuals scatter symmetrically vs sample size
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) model with a normal latent population for per-group ability parameters: groups with little data get over-shrunk toward the popu
leaves: In the post-1 graph more black dots sit below the orange (normal) band for <100 at-bats; Teddy defines 'wrong' as 'a good model would have residuals unbiased as a function of at-bats'

branches.if_observe: residual bias vanishes once an at-bats covariate is added
branches.then: omitted-covariate / selection-proxy (H3/H2) is sufficient to remove the location bias

branches.if_observe: bias persists with covariate
branches.then: population-shape misspecification (H1) is implicated independently

action: Reason about the population-model-as-prior mechanism: a hierarchical density is a prior on group params; if its shape is wrong it contorts to fit, and selection makes high-data groups dominate the population fit
discriminates: H1 (shape wrong) vs H2 (selection-driven) vs H5 (no problem) — and shows they share a mechanism
expected_findings: If population is skewed toward better performers, the normal location is dragged into the upper tail; because selected (better) groups have the narrowest likelihoods they pin the population location high, dragging all weakly-informed groups up. Under H5 there'd be no such asymmetric pull
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) model with a normal latent population for per-group ability parameters: groups with little data get over-shrunk toward the popu
leaves: Betancourt: normal is right only if there's a bulk of average players with as many poor as good; under skew+selection the normal 'contorts', concentrates around high-at-bat players, and pulls everyone

action: Plot the marginal posteriors of all individual/group parameters together (against each other and the population location) and inspect for outliers / clustering / skew
discriminates: normal population adequate vs needs heavier tails or mixture/clustered population (separates H1 from H5; can reveal multimodality vs single heavy tail)
expected_findings: H1: outliers / a heavy upper tail / asymmetry inconsistent with a single normal. H5: marginals look like a coherent normal cloud. Clustering would instead point to a mixture population
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) model with a normal latent population for per-group ability parameters: groups with little data get over-shrunk toward the popu
leaves: Betancourt's Hierarchical Modeling case study: 'any outliers and clustering in the marginal posteriors would suggest a more sophisticated latent model'; Teddy adopts this as the go-to diagnostic

branches.if_observe: heavy/skewed upper tail
branches.then: adopt skewed or power-law (generalised Pareto / horseshoe-style heavy-tailed) population model

branches.if_observe: distinct clusters
branches.then: use a mixture / clustered population model instead

action: Choose modeling target: (a) directly model the SELECTED population with a non-normal (heavy-tailed/Pareto) hierarchical prior, or (b) keep a normal latent UNSELECTED population and explicitly model the selection mechanism
discriminates: whether to absorb selection into the population-prior shape (a) vs represent it as an explicit missingness/selection process (b)
expected_findings: Both valid: (a) is modeling the marginal of (b). Choose (b) when the selection process is known/modelable (e.g. at-bats as a selection proxy), (a) when it isn't and a heuristic skewed population suffices
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) model with a normal latent population for per-group ability parameters: groups with little data get over-shrunk toward the popu
leaves: Betancourt: 'these aren't actually different — the question is what population the hierarchy models'; adding the at-bats effect (b-flavored) removed the bias, while the Pareto model (a) handled the sk

branches.if_observe: you know the selection mechanism
branches.then: model selection explicitly atop a normal latent population (route b)

branches.if_observe: selection unknown / only want a fit to the observed sample
branches.then: use a skewed/heavy-tailed (generalised Pareto) population model (route a)

action: Add a domain-informed prior to the heavy-tailed population to concentrate group estimates within plausible bounds
discriminates: H4 (under-regularization fixable by prior) vs the heavy-tailed model being fundamentally wrong
expected_findings: With a prior putting mass on plausible rates (here logit-scale, mass between ~.100 and ~.400, the Mendoza-to-Ted-Williams band) the absurd-ability compatibility disappears and intervals become reasonable; residual bias separately handled by the at-bats effect
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) model with a normal latent population for per-group ability parameters: groups with little data get over-shrunk toward the popu
leaves: Teddy adds a normal prior on logit-scale ability params (most mass for batting avg between .1 and .4); dgerth5 noted max plausible ~.4. Result: gpareto no longer compatible with crazy averages, though

branches.if_observe: intervals still too narrow for low-data groups
branches.then: ideal model is between normal and unconstrained-Pareto; consider that a season may end early (truncation) widening low-data uncertainty

action: Specify a predictive evaluation: hold out future observations for the same individuals and score out-of-sample (e.g. next 100-200 at-bats / next seasons)
discriminates: H5 (heavy regularization predicts better) vs H1/H2 (selection-aware/heavy-tailed population predicts better)
expected_findings: Resolvable only with future/hold-out data, which this single-season dataset lacks; the thread cannot adjudicate predictive superiority and flags it as the needed next step
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) model with a normal latent population for per-group ability parameters: groups with little data get over-shrunk toward the popu
leaves: jd_c: to be convinced normal+heavy-regularization isn't best, wants models compared on a decent out-of-sample future-at-bats set; Teddy lists 'find richer data to predict future at-bats' as a to-do; j

action: Reframe the problem: distinguish composability of the posterior distribution from composability of posterior computation
discriminates: Whether exact plug-in is even available (H1) vs whether any chosen method is an approximation that loses information (H2-H5)
expected_findings: H1: composable w.r.t. independent/exchangeable observations as a distribution, but the MCMC computation is NOT composable — there is no analytic posterior object to reuse; only draws. SMC is the sole partial exception and is not yet a general tool. Any intermediate-density construction 'hemorrhages information,' worst in high dimensions.
situations: Have posterior draws (a sample matrix) from a fitted Stan model and want to use that posterior as the prior for a second/next model — i.e. sequential Bayesian u
leaves: betanalpha #9 / #15: posteriors composable wrt independent observations but posterior computation typically is not; only SMC is exempt. Intermediate densities leak information, especially high-dim. Th

branches.if_observe: full refit is computationally feasible
branches.then: prefer refitting pooled data — exact, no approximation; stop here

branches.if_observe: refit infeasible / streaming or historical data must be summarized
branches.then: accept an approximate prior and choose among H2-H4, knowing it loses information

action: Pick a density-approximation family matched to the parameter geometry
discriminates: Which approximation is admissible given dimensionality and constraints (univariate vs unconstrained-multivariate vs simplex/constrained)
expected_findings: Univariate: quantile-parameterized distributions (posterior quantiles become next prior's hyperparameters). Unconstrained multivariate: couple marginals with a multivariate-normal copula density (metalog). Constrained (simplex): no good general solution known. Naive moment-match to normal/Student-t works only if the marginal is well captured by that family.
situations: Have posterior draws (a sample matrix) from a fitted Stan model and want to use that posterior as the prior for a second/next model — i.e. sequential Bayesian u
leaves: bgoodri #2: quantile-parameterized / metalog dists for univariate; MVN copula for unconstrained multivariate; simplexes have no great solution. JAQuent #22 / ckoenig #23: moment-matched normal/t is po

branches.if_observe: univariate / independent marginals
branches.then: quantile-parameterized prior; posterior quantiles -> prior hyperparameters

branches.if_observe: correlated unconstrained multivariate
branches.then: fit marginals + MVN copula to preserve dependence

branches.if_observe: constrained (simplex / positive) params
branches.then: no clean parametric prior; lean toward power-prior or refit instead

action: Add the historical-data likelihood raised to power a_0 as a power prior in the new Stan model
discriminates: Power-prior route (H3) vs density-approximation route (H2): power prior keeps the raw historical data + likelihood instead of summarizing the posterior
expected_findings: Works directly in Stan: target += a_0 * loglik(historical | theta) + base_prior. With a_0 fixed this is a clean drop-in (maxbiostat's NIG regression example). Power priors also leak information; a_0 controls the discounting and for some models can be set for an 'information-optimality.'
situations: Have posterior draws (a sample matrix) from a fitted Stan model and want to use that posterior as the prior for a second/next model — i.e. sequential Bayesian u
leaves: maxbiostat #5/#8/#15: power-prior code (Normal-inverse-Gamma regression), prior.stan adds target += a_0 * normal_lpdf(y0|X0*beta,...) + multi_normal + inv_gamma; posterior.stan adds the new-data likel

branches.if_observe: a_0 fixed (typical)
branches.then: drop-in target += a_0 * historical_loglik; no normalizing-constant issue

branches.if_observe: a_0 to be estimated
branches.then: must handle the a_0-dependent normalizing constant c(a_0) — go to next move

action: Expose the normalized-power-prior pitfall: the normalizing constant c(a_0) is a function of a_0, not a constant
discriminates: Correct normalized power prior vs the broken constant-divisor attempt — the bug is treating c(a_0) as data
expected_findings: c(a_0) = ∫ L(D|theta)^{a_0} π(theta) dtheta depends on a_0, so `real<upper=0> C; ... / C` is wrong. Need a closed form when available, else approximate c(a_0) via bridge sampling and embed that function in the model.
situations: Have posterior draws (a sample matrix) from a fitted Stan model and want to use that posterior as the prior for a second/next model — i.e. sequential Bayesian u
leaves: maxbiostat #14/#20 + ckoenig #17/#19: c(a_0)=∫ L^{a_0} π; dividing by a fixed C fails because C varies with a_0; use bridgesampling to estimate the marginal likelihood / approximate c(a_0), or closed

branches.if_observe: model admits closed-form c(a_0)
branches.then: plug the closed form in directly

branches.if_observe: no closed form
branches.then: approximate c(a_0) with bridge sampling and incorporate the fitted function

branches.if_observe: hierarchical / mixed-effects historical model
branches.then: power prior = integral of historical likelihood times random-effects density over the random effects — open/harder problem, spin off separate thread

action: Pattern-match the model structure to a known non-identifiability class rather than chasing the sampler symptom: an unstructured observation-level random effect on the logit scale with a free variance.
discriminates: non-identified latent-variance pathology vs ordinary prior/tuning problem
expected_findings: If classic obs-level RE non-identifiability: dropping sigma and the normal sampling statement yields a plain, well-behaved logistic regression; the symptom is structural, not tuning. If merely tuning, the funnel would respond to reparameterization alone.
situations: A Gaussian/linear (or GP) regression is converted to a binary outcome by inserting a latent continuous predictor: ystar ~ normal(linear_predictor, sigma) follow
leaves: Traditional logistic regression removes sigma and the ystar~normal statement entirely. Binary data carry too little information to pin down the variance of an unstructured observation-level random eff

branches.if_observe: User truly needs only the binary fit
branches.then: Collapse to plain bernoulli_logit(alpha + x*beta); done.

branches.if_observe: User insists the latent normal layer is scientifically required
branches.then: Either supply a strongly informative prior on sigma + non-centered parameterization, or move to the structured-residual route (next moves).

action: Reduce to the canonical case: use a standard GP term as the linear predictor of a logistic regression and observe behavior; build up from there.
discriminates: GP-structured residual (potentially identifiable) vs unstructured residual (not identifiable) as the source of the pathology
expected_findings: A GP-only logistic model (e.g. brms bf(y ~ gp(x1,x2), family=bernoulli)) fits with few/no divergences, whereas the ystar~gp + separate unstructured-residual formulation does not. This is the rare case where naive build-up-from-simple fails because the GP pools information across space.
situations: A Gaussian/linear (or GP) regression is converted to a binary outcome by inserting a latent continuous predictor: ystar ~ normal(linear_predictor, sigma) follow
leaves: 'Get rid of the e — you don't need it.' Since only G/V (and beta) are of interest and e was introduced only during the decomposition, removing it gives the intended identifiable model: bernoulli_logit
brms gp() term gives a clean comparator; the working brms code emits bernoulli_logit_glm with the GP folded directly into the linear predictor and NO separate unstructured logit-scale residual.

branches.if_observe: Custom covariance function needed (brms can't express it)
branches.then: Keep the hand-written Stan but mirror brms' structure: GP enters the linear predictor directly, no extra residual.

action: Decompose the latent linear predictor conceptually into ystar = mu + G + e and classify each residual term by whether the binary data can inform its variance.
discriminates: structured residual G (variance controls observed-response COVARIANCE -> identifiable) vs unstructured residual e (variance only inflates per-obs noise -> NOT identifiable from binary data)
expected_findings: Under mu~0, an unstructured logit residual: any residual variance is consistent with ~half 0s/half 1s (probs all .5 vs all near 0/1 look identical in aggregate) -> non-identified. A GP residual: large variance forces strongly-covarying pairs to share values, so data like [0,1] vs [1,1] discriminate the covariance -> identified.
situations: A Gaussian/linear (or GP) regression is converted to a binary outcome by inserting a latent continuous predictor: ystar ~ normal(linear_predictor, sigma) follow
leaves: The key intuition: e is an observation-level normal RE whose variance binary data cannot resolve; G is an obs-level RE with covariance structure whose variances ARE constrained because they control th

branches.if_observe: Posterior check: push ystar draws through inv-logit
branches.then: Diagnostic confirmation — per-draw probabilities collapse to 0/1 while the across-draw mean recovers P(y); signature of the unidentified residual variance.

action: Reparameterize: declare G as a parameter with a multi_normal_cholesky(0, ...) prior and form ystar = X*beta + G (+ e) in transformed parameters; e ~ std_normal fixes its variance to 1.
discriminates: whether the residual variance is being ESTIMATED (pathological) vs FIXED by construction (tractable)
expected_findings: With sigma on G estimated and e's variance fixed to 1: divergences/treedepth warnings disappear, but EBFMI/energy and ESS/mixing warnings appear — residual marginal-likelihood geometry is still hard. Fixing e's variance removes the worst non-identifiability but the term is still unnecessary.
situations: A Gaussian/linear (or GP) regression is converted to a binary outcome by inserting a latent continuous predictor: ystar ~ normal(linear_predictor, sigma) follow
leaves: G cannot be drawn via _rng in transformed parameters; it must be a parameter sampled from the MVN prior. e ~ std_normal() pins its scale, sidestepping the variance non-identifiability of posts #1/#8.

branches.if_observe: EBFMI / mixing warnings remain after fixing e's variance
branches.then: Question whether e is needed at all (next move).

branches.if_observe: Any prior on e other than std_normal tried
branches.then: It 'poisons' the fit — reintroduces the unidentified-variance funnel.

action: Distinguish multimodality from continuous non-identifiability via the Rhat-doubling test plus per-chain-vs-pooled Rhat and ESS
discriminates: well-separated-modes multimodality (incl. sign reflection) vs continuous non-identifiability vs adequate-but-slow mixing
expected_findings: Reflection/multimodality: huge pooled Rhat that barely moves with more iters; per-single-chain Rhat near 1 (each chain happily samples one mode); ESS <= number of chains. Continuous non-identifiability: per-chain Rhat also not near 1 and parameters wander. Just slow: Rhat shrinks meaningfully when iters double.
situations: A Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis / SEM (latent-variable measurement model) in Stan fails to converge: Rhat stays far from 1 (e.g. 6-15) for the freely es
leaves: Cited Vehtari et al. rank-normalized R-hat paper (1903.08008); recommend watching ESS as iters grow (ESS<=#chains is the multimodality tell) rather than trusting raw Rhat alone.

branches.if_observe: per-single-chain Rhat ~1 but pooled Rhat huge, ESS<=#chains
branches.then: treat as well-separated modes -> go to the sign-indeterminacy check

branches.if_observe: per-single-chain Rhat also far from 1
branches.then: treat as genuine identifiability problem -> tighten priors / parameterization

action: Visually confirm reflection invariance via loading histograms and traceplots
discriminates: sign-flip (reflection) indeterminacy vs other multimodality vs ordinary noise
expected_findings: Sign indeterminacy: a loading's marginal histogram is bimodal with two modes of equal magnitude and opposite sign; traceplots show chains 'sign-switching' (jumping between +/- of the same absolute value).
situations: A Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis / SEM (latent-variable measurement model) in Stan fails to converge: Rhat stays far from 1 (e.g. 6-15) for the freely es

branches.if_observe: bimodal +/- loading histograms / sign-switching traceplots
branches.then: confirmed reflection invariance -> apply sign-correction remedy

branches.if_observe: bimodal but not symmetric / not a clean sign flip
branches.then: look for other modes or a model/index bug

action: Audit the generative spec for mis-fixed means/intercepts and indexing bugs
discriminates: model mis-specification / software bug vs a genuine identifiability pathology
expected_findings: Spurious fixes: factor means computed but unused; item intercepts hard-fixed at 0 inside multi_normal mean; off-by-one in the loading-assembly loop. These can masquerade as or compound non-convergence.
situations: A Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis / SEM (latent-variable measurement model) in Stan fails to converge: Rhat stays far from 1 (e.g. 6-15) for the freely es
leaves: Reviewer flagged unused zero factor-means and intercepts fixed at 0 in Y ~ multi_normal(rep_vector(0,ind), Sigma).

branches.then: proceed treating it as identifiability

action: Estimate loadings unconstrained, then post-hoc sign-correct in generated quantities against a marker loading
discriminates: the recommended Stan remedy vs the cruder hard-constraint remedies
expected_findings: Sampling unconstrained then, per factor, if the chosen marker loading is negative, multiplying that factor's loadings AND factor scores AND the relevant factor-correlation rows/columns by -1 collapses the two reflection modes into one consistent orientation -> Rhat ~1, unimodal, without truncating the prior.
situations: A Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis / SEM (latent-variable measurement model) in Stan fails to converge: Rhat stays far from 1 (e.g. 6-15) for the freely es
leaves: Done in generated quantities (blavaan-style; target=stan/stanclassic both sign-correct there). Choose the indicator with the strongest absolute loading as the marker. CRITICAL: also flip the off-diago

action: Constrain the first loading per factor positive (lower=0) in parameters
discriminates: whether a hard in-model positivity constraint removes the modes
expected_findings: Constraining all loadings (or the marker) positive yields immediate Rhat~1 unimodal sampling, BUT it forbids genuinely negative loadings a priori (too strict, theoretically problematic). Constraining only lam_first positive while lam_remain is free still left multimodal posteriors for the unconstrained loadings -> partial constraint is insufficient.
situations: A Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis / SEM (latent-variable measurement model) in Stan fails to converge: Rhat stays far from 1 (e.g. 6-15) for the freely es

branches.if_observe: all-positive constraint, converges
branches.then: acceptable only if you can defend non-negative loadings (e.g. reverse-score reversed items, add method effects)

branches.if_observe: only marker constrained, still multimodal
branches.then: fall back to full post-hoc sign correction in generated quantities

action: Combine sign correction with sampler-geometry tuning (raise adapt_delta) and a correctly chosen marker
discriminates: residual stepsize/geometry problem vs leftover sign issue vs wrong marker choice
expected_findings: Sign-correcting on a weak diagonal loading can fail to converge; using the strongest-loading marker plus raising adapt_delta (e.g. to 0.9) finally yields good mixing. Indicates a geometry component on top of the identifiability fix.
situations: A Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis / SEM (latent-variable measurement model) in Stan fails to converge: Rhat stays far from 1 (e.g. 6-15) for the freely es
leaves: User reports convergence after adapt_delta=0.9; sign-correction on the diagonal element alone was insufficient until marker/geometry adjusted. EFA needs its own identification (e.g. zero upper-triangl

branches.if_observe: converges after adapt_delta bump

branches.if_observe: still failing
