action: Audit the model/data structure (count params vs data) and remove crutches: drop fixed seeds, sanity-check that nuisance structure (the N/n split) actually does something
discriminates: over-parameterization & seed-fixation artifacts vs a genuine identifiability problem
expected_findings: One pi per observation means N*n latent weights for N*n data => no clean large-N limit; under H-overparam, optimizer returns wildly different answers (c(17,17), c(6,37)) across seeds; under a clean model the answer would stabilize
situations: A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar
leaves: bbbales2: 'you have N*n parameters to go with N*n data, I don't think you get nice limiting behavior'; advises NOT setting seeds during model development; for N=6000 optimizing gives c(17,17) or c(6,3

branches.if_observe: answer flips between seeds / collapses to one mean
branches.then: treat as over-parameterized + weakly identified; reduce weights (one pi per row) and probe separability

action: Probe component separability directly: plot hist(data) and try to split the two groups BY EYE across a sweep of phi; also fit each true group separately by hand (MASS::fitdistr)
discriminates: non-identifiability from overlap (H-separability) vs a coding/estimation bug

situations: A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar
leaves: bbbales2: 'no real chance of separating the groups until phi >= 100'; LucC: a mixed NB can be approximated by a single NB unless shapes differ wildly; user confirms by-hand fitdistr on the true split

action: Stress the mixing-weight degree of freedom: fix pi to the data-generating value (hard-code log(0.25)/log(0.75)) and, separately, try a 50/50 split; compare to estimating pi freely
discriminates: whether the per-row pi flexibility is injecting the bias vs the component-mean prior being the culprit

situations: A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar

branches.if_observe: fixing pi helps but free pi diverges

action: Derive the membership posterior as a one-step Gibbs full-conditional and implement it correctly in generated quantities (integrating over theta=(mu,phi,pi))
discriminates: post-processing bug (H-membership-bug) vs model-fit problem; also clarifies that group membership is NOT the sampled per-row pi
expected_findings: p(g_n | y, mu, pi) ∝ p(y_n | mu, g_n) p(g_n | pi); normalize over the two groups. Key bug: neg_binomial_2_lpmf returns a LOG pmf -> must exp() it before forming the ratio; NaNs vanish once exp is applied
situations: A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar
leaves: bbbales2 derives the Gibbs full-conditional; fix is to wrap each neg_binomial_2_lpmf/normal_lpdf in exp() before the responsibility ratio; user's corrected generated-quantities block confirmed correct

branches.if_observe: pi_post is NaN / undefined
branches.then: you forgot exp() on the log-pmf; this is independent of the fit pathology

action: Run a controlled ladder: swap NB for Normal likelihood, start with small data + well-separated components, then crank up data, then drop NB back in on small data
discriminates: likelihood-family numerics (H-numerics) vs identifiability (H-separability) vs sampler-tuning (H-tuning)
expected_findings: Normals with well-separated means fit fast and recover mu and sd; Normals sample MUCH faster than NB. When normal means are made close (mu1=20,mu2=19), the upper ordered mean blows up to 1e+153 -> exposes the prior problem. NB diverges even at phi=100 with one pi -> exposes NB-specific numerics. adapt_delta=0.99 does NOT fix it -> rules out pure tuning
situations: A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar
leaves: bbbales2: 'when the sampler fails it's usually not for no reason... try smaller data and fit normals, then crank up data, then drop in negative binomials'; user finds normals fast & fine when separate

branches.then: improper prior on ordered mu is letting the redundant component escape -> add a proper prior

branches.if_observe: NB still diverges where normal/poisson succeed
branches.then: suspect NB lgamma numerics; substitute Poisson

action: Simplify the count likelihood: replace NB with Poisson (and separately hard-code phi=100 in the NB) and compare sampling behavior
discriminates: NB lgamma(phi) numerical fragility (H-numerics) vs a structural model error
expected_findings: Poisson mixture samples cleanly with good n_eff/Rhat and reasonable rate estimates; the NB-with-phi=100 (which should approximate Poisson) STILL diverges -> implicates the NB log-density's lgamma-in-phi as numerically finicky in the HMC geometry, not a structural bug. Caveat (LucC): a Poisson mixture will mis-estimate membership probabilities and means if the true DGP is NB, because the tails differ
situations: A 2-component negative-binomial (or normal/Poisson) finite mixture fit in Stan recovers the component means badly even on simulated data with known truth and ar

branches.then: confirm NB numerics; Poisson is not a valid substitute if true DGP is NB (tails drive membership) -> reparameterize instead

action: Reframe the construct: recognize that a prior on lambda is a second prior model for lambda layered on the one already implied by p(mu,beta), and decide whether to keep, merge, or reverse-engineer
discriminates: H1 (valid extra prior) vs H2 (redundant/over-restrictive) — i.e. whether two prior models for the same quantity is a problem
expected_findings: Under H1 the joint factorizes as p(Y|lambda)p(lambda)p(lambda|mu,beta)p(mu,beta) and remains a valid prior; under H2 layering risks an accidentally too-tight effective prior. Decision menu: (a) keep as-is, (b) pool/hierarchically merge the two lambda models, (c) reverse-engineer a consistent prior on (mu,beta) from the outcome-space knowledge (prior-predictive / history-matching / Bayesian-opt style)
situations: A GLM / structural time-series count model (Poisson or neg-binomial, log link, local-level latent state plus intervention covariates) where the analyst wants to
leaves: hhau enumerates the three options and lists objections: model no longer generative / no DAG (prior predictive now needs MCMC, not ancestral sampling); many (mu,beta) map to the same lambda so the inve

branches.if_observe: outcome-space knowledge is rich and parameter-space knowledge is thin/high-dimensional
branches.then: favor keeping the outcome-scale prior (option a) — it's the natural place to put the information; reverse-engineering (option c) is hard with many predictors

branches.if_observe: you need a generative model / cheap prior predictive checks
branches.then: prefer reverse-engineering a parameter prior (option c)

action: Apply the product-of-densities argument to quantify how stacking priors shrinks the effective prior
discriminates: H2 confirmation — distinguishes 'two priors = same coverage' (naive) from 'two priors multiply -> narrower'
expected_findings: Product of two densities on the same quantity is proportional to a narrower density (limiting case: product of two standard normals ∝ N(0, 1/2)), so realized prior variance halves; layering is NOT free
situations: A GLM / structural time-series count model (Poisson or neg-binomial, log link, local-level latent state plus intervention covariates) where the analyst wants to

branches.if_observe: the two lambda priors are intended to encode the SAME information
branches.then: do not stack them; use one, or widen each to compensate

branches.if_observe: they encode genuinely different information sources
branches.then: stacking is defensible but inspect the implied product

action: Check whether the outcome-mean prior actually regularizes the coefficients, contrasting with a derived joint (R2D2-type) prior
discriminates: H3 — narrow-prior-on-mean regularizes coefficients vs it fails under collinearity
expected_findings: Under collinearity a tight prior on the mean does NOT regularize: opposing large +/- coefficients on correlated predictors cancel in the mean while individually exploding. R2D2 instead derives the joint coefficient prior from an outcome (R^2) prior and regularizes the sum of magnitudes, which a raw prior-on-the-sum does not
situations: A GLM / structural time-series count model (Poisson or neg-binomial, log link, local-level latent state plus intervention covariates) where the analyst wants to

branches.if_observe: predictors are correlated
branches.then: also put (tight) priors directly on coefficients, or adopt an R2D2-style joint prior that regularizes magnitudes — don't rely on the outcome-mean prior alone

branches.if_observe: predictors near-orthogonal
branches.then: outcome-mean prior is less dangerous

action: Plot/compare prior vs posterior of the counterfactual (and the directly-priored lambda) to see if the prior overwhelms the likelihood
discriminates: H4 — prior dominates the counterfactual posterior vs likelihood still informs it
expected_findings: If prior dictates posterior, prior and posterior of the counterfactual overlap tightly and the data adds nothing; if not, posterior is shifted/narrower than prior. Empirically here: posterior did NOT match the (long-tailed, exponentiated) prior summaries, so the prior was not dictating the counterfactual
situations: A GLM / structural time-series count model (Poisson or neg-binomial, log link, local-level latent state plus intervention covariates) where the analyst wants to

branches.if_observe: prior ≈ posterior for the counterfactual
branches.then: the prior is too strong / inference is prior-driven — widen it or reconsider

branches.if_observe: posterior departs from prior
branches.then: prior is masking implausible regions without dictating the answer — acceptable; (note: prior-predictive sampling itself threw NaNs, a separate diagnostic snag)

action: Formalize the masking prior as multiplying base priors by bounded non-negative 'distortion' functions q(f(theta,x)) over hypothetical covariate points, and check normalizability + sampling geometry
discriminates: H1 (valid generalized-dependency prior) and H6 (geometry helps vs hurts sampling)
expected_findings: p(a)p(b)...·∏ q(f(theta,x_i)) is a valid prior whenever the whole expression is normalizable; q need not be a probability, just bounded/non-negative; the product encodes a generalized dependency structure. Geometry can be benign or pathological; here adding the mask made the model run FASTER with BETTER diagnostics
situations: A GLM / structural time-series count model (Poisson or neg-binomial, log link, local-level latent state plus intervention covariates) where the analyst wants to

branches.then: the induced manifold is problematic — soften the mask or reparameterize

branches.if_observe: sampler improves / diagnostics improve

action: Ask for the explicit block-covariance derivation; distinguish numerical vs structural singularity
discriminates: whether the rank deficiency is a floating-point artifact (jitter is a legitimate fix) or an inherent rank<=M property of the truncated approximation (kriging formulation is the wrong frame)
expected_findings: If numerical: tiny jitter restores invertibility and predictions are stable -> benign. If structural: Phi_1 Lambda Phi_1^T is exactly rank M, so for M<n_1 it can never be inverted, and jitter only masks the problem by lifting all eigenvalues positive.
situations: Using a Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP) reduced-rank prior with M basis functions for a non-Gaussian / no-nugget model (e.g. Poisson GLM or spatially-varyin
leaves: Request the derivation of Sigma_joint = [[Sigma, Sigma_0^T],[Sigma_0, Omega]] with Sigma=Phi_1 Lambda Phi_1^T; have user test whether adding small diagonal jitter alone resolves the failure.

branches.if_observe: jitter alone fixes it and predictions look sane
branches.then: suspect numerical-only; but check whether a hidden n_1<M assumption is making it look fine

branches.if_observe: matrix is exactly rank-M and inversion is meaningless without jitter
branches.then: abandon the kriging frame; the conditional-Gaussian route is structurally wrong for a rank-M prior

action: Split by outcome type: latent-variable (sampling) formulation vs analytic-joint (conditioning) formulation
discriminates: non-Gaussian / no-nugget case (use latent f sampled via HSGP weights, no inversion) vs noise-free-Gaussian case (where one is tempted to use the analytic joint and conditioning)
expected_findings: For Poisson/logistic the latent f = Phi beta is already a model parameter, so prediction = evaluate Phi at new x times the SAME beta draws; no Sigma inversion ever arises. The inversion only appears if one (wrongly) re-imposes a kriging conditioning step the HSGP does not require.
situations: Using a Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP) reduced-rank prior with M basis functions for a non-Gaussian / no-nugget model (e.g. Poisson GLM or spatially-varyin
leaves: Point to the latent-variable GP formulation where f is built from HSGP basis functions instead of a Cholesky of full K; recommend it for non-Gaussian outcomes.

branches.then: show the joint N(0, Phi Lambda Phi^T) is rank-M, so conditioning is degenerate -- pivot to the parameter-space view

action: Audit the working demo for a hidden assumption that contradicts the failure regime
discriminates: whether the demo genuinely refutes the singularity claim or silently used n_1 < M (or the jitter) so the matrix happened to be full rank
expected_findings: Demo accidentally set n_obs (50) < M (512), i.e. n_1 < M, so Phi_1 Lambda Phi_1^T was full rank n_1 and inverted fine -- it does NOT cover the M < n_1 regime the user actually cares about.
situations: Using a Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP) reduced-rank prior with M basis functions for a non-Gaussian / no-nugget model (e.g. Poisson GLM or spatially-varyin

action: Reason about what a low-rank truncation drops and whether a nugget is the right absorber
discriminates: nugget-as-noise (well-identified when the model genuinely has observation noise) vs nugget-as-approximation-patch (poorly identified, conflates truncation error with iid noise)
expected_findings: Because the rank-M approximation under-estimates the true PD covariance, the missing eigenvalues are small if M captures the important features; a small diagonal add gives a reasonable covariance approximation, but a 'learned' nugget in a no-noise model is weakly identified and changes the fit character (wigglier for rough kernels like Matern-1/2 vs RBF).
situations: Using a Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP) reduced-rank prior with M basis functions for a non-Gaussian / no-nugget model (e.g. Poisson GLM or spatially-varyin
leaves: Weigh adding sigma^2 I (kernel additivity K_n = K + sigma^2 I, equivalent to integrating out x ~ GP, y ~ N(x,sigma)) against its identifiability cost in a nugget-free model.

branches.if_observe: model naturally has observation noise
branches.then: use the nugget; conditional prediction via Woodbury (invert M x M) is fine

branches.if_observe: no natural noise (GLM/SVC)
branches.then: do NOT bolt on a nugget just to invert; use the linear-basis prediction instead

action: Re-frame HSGP prediction as a linear-in-parameters model rather than a Gaussian-conditioning problem
discriminates: kriging frame (cost driven by inverting an n_1 or M sized covariance) vs basis-regression frame (cost is just evaluating Phi_new and multiplying by posterior beta draws -- no inversion at all)
expected_findings: Out-of-sample prediction with HSGP is exactly like any linear model: f_pred = Phi(x_pred) %*% beta_draws, propagating posterior uncertainty in beta. No M x M or n x n inversion is required; the whole singularity dilemma dissolves. (If a backward-compat inversion is done it lives in generated quantities, off the autodiff path, so cheap.)
situations: Using a Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP) reduced-rank prior with M basis functions for a non-Gaussian / no-nugget model (e.g. Poisson GLM or spatially-varyin
leaves: State the resolution: HSGP gives basis functions; prediction is a linear-model evaluation at new locations, carrying the posterior weight samples; eq.37-style inversion is not needed.

branches.if_observe: user asks whether the linear-model prediction captures full predictive uncertainty
branches.then: yes -- uncertainty comes through the posterior over beta (and the likelihood for the outcome), no separate conditioning step needed

branches.if_observe: user still sees an inversion in the 2020 paper (eq.37)
branches.then: note that for the noisy-Gaussian analytic case an M x M inversion can appear, but it is unnecessary in the parameter-space formulation and can sit in generated quantities

leaves: Reviewer spots: drawing gamma_Z_raw = MvNormal(chol=chol, ...) and then gamma_Z = dot(chol, gamma_Z_raw.T) double-correlates. Correct is EITHER centered MvNormal(chol=chol) alone, OR non-centered Norm

action: Per-MCMC-draw, compute the correlation across subjects of the (nsubject x 2) random-effect array (rho_a) and compare its distribution to the cov-matrix rho (rho_b)
discriminates: Sample-derived population correlation (rho_a) vs model-parameter correlation (rho_b) — tests whether they coincide
expected_findings: If H3 holds, rho_a (per-draw across-subject correlation of the realized random effects) will NOT match rho_b well; the model parameter rho is the population correlation, while the realized-effects correlation is a noisy finite-sample shrinkage-affected statistic
situations: Varying-intercept/varying-slope mixed model (y ~ x1 + (1 + x2 | subject)). User parameterizes the random effects two ways — (a) independent Normals per effect,
leaves: rho_a does not agree with rho_b; rho_b agrees with brms. Confirms the population correlation is a model parameter, not something to be read off realized random-effect draws or per-subject posterior sc

branches.then: Conclude the literature is right: an explicit covariance matrix is needed to estimate the random-effect correlation; sample-based recovery would need far more data/samples

action: Reframe as a latent time-varying state model: pick observation model + explicit transition model

action: Define the two quantities operationally / mechanically (how each is computed per posterior draw)
discriminates: Whether epred = per-draw conditional mean (posterior over means) vs per-draw RNG sample from likelihood (predict)

So epred draws = means of the ppd; predict draws = the ppd itself.

Under the conditional reading, each MCMC draw has its own conditional ppd whose expectation is well-defined, giving a posterior DISTRIBUTION of expectations. expected_findings: Under H1, pooling N chains reduces error like 1/sqrt(total draws) with no floor — linear speedup. Under H2, each chain carries an O(1/ESS) bias toward its initial point; the CLT is only asymptotic, so for finite per-chain ESS the bias dominates and does NOT shrink by averaging more chains (biases don't average out, only variance does)

H4: stacking/reweighting is valid only when each chain is approximately convergent to the stationary distribution (niche metastable/multimodal case); for generic non-converged short chains it does not recover the truth, though the Riemann-sum/grid view shows reweighting many 1-sample 'chains' is a different, deliberate construction

action: Stress-test the early-stopping/discarding heuristic against a known pathological target (the funnel) and against SMC as an alternative rescue

expected_findings: H5: slow chains are often slow precisely because they sit in genuinely-part-of-the-typical-set regions (funnel neck, tau~0, high curvature); discarding them biases inference toward the easy regions. Poor adaptation usually signals pathological sampler-target interaction, where ESS-scaling arguments break down entirely. H6: SMC resampling favors the slow, high-density, high-curvature stuck chains and adds particle collapse — SMC solves a different problem (move to a new distribution) and inherits MCMC pathologies plus new ones
situations: Planning to speed up MCMC by running many short parallel chains (or by weeding out slow ones) and pooling their estimates, on the assumption that MCMC is "embar

action: Trace how each metric interacts with the parallelization conclusion and with non-amortizable warmup overhead
discriminates: H1's implied metric (ESS/sec, embarrassingly parallel) vs H7 (time-to-ESS-target)
expected_findings: ESS/second post-convergence is itself embarrassingly parallelizable, so optimizing only it rewards the wrong thing (it hides per-chain warmup overhead and the O(10)-ESS/chain floor). Time-to-ESS-target captures warmup overhead and the bias-imposed minimum per-chain ESS; ESS/sec remains useful as a local equilibrium quantity feeding the total. Even when ESS=1000 is overkill, that very fact means you care about time-to-target, not steady-state ESS/sec

action: Identify what information is safe to share across chains during adaptation and connect cross-chain consistency to existing diagnostics
discriminates: naive per-iteration synchronization vs window-boundary pooling of adaptation state; and whether cross-chain disagreement is signal or noise
expected_findings: Pooling per-chain inverse-metric/covariance estimates at adaptation-window boundaries (deterministic, e.g. via Intel TBB dependency graph) gives more precise metric estimates earlier and can pull a stray chain out of a vicious cycle (bad corner -> tiny step size -> poor exploration -> worse covariance). Per-iteration sync is infeasible. Cross-chain inconsistency is exactly Rhat logic: a 'weird' chain doesn't mean that chain is broken — it means the others haven't yet encountered the pathology; chains must run long enough to surface a problem but not so long they re-converge and mask it (why Rhat has a sensitivity sweet spot)

action: Stop tuning the prior to the diagnostic; shift the target of the sensitivity check from individual coefficients to the quantity of interest (predictions)

action: Diagnose for funnel-type mean/variance dependence (joint non-identifiability), not just bad priors.

action: Treat max_treedepth as a sampler-budget cap, not a model verdict: raise the cap and check whether trees stabilize at a finite depth.

branches.then: Prefer the better-sampling parameterization; the speed gap is itself diagnostic of fit (folk theorem)

action: Restate the problem in probabilistic notation and probe the user's intent; flag that posterior-as-prior has no closed form
discriminates: sequential-updating reading vs inverse-prediction reading; whether x is a covariate to condition on or a quantity to infer
expected_findings: Under updating-hypothesis: user wants p(theta|y1,y2)=p(y2|theta)p(theta|y1), no analytic prior exists. Under inverse-prediction: user actually wants p(x|y,theta) for new y with x unknown.

action: Enumerate solution families keyed on dimensionality of the unknown and whether to reuse draws or refit

expected_findings: Univariate x => inverse-CDF sampling in generated quantities per posterior draw is viable; multivariate x => write a second Stan model with x as parameter; or just include x in the original joint model.

action: Reconsider feasibility/efficiency of doing the 1-D inverse problem inside Stan generated quantities; propose better-suited tools

expected_findings: In-GQ requires computing the normalizing constant via integrate_1d and a binary-search inverse-CDF: cumbersome and repeated. Slice sampling against BridgeStan log-density evaluations is far simpler and faster.

situations: Have a multivariate model y ~ MVN(mu(x), Sigma(x)) (power-law mean, heteroskedastic noise, LKJ-Cholesky correlation) already fit with the scalar predictor x obs

action: Implement adaptive Gauss-Hermite quadrature (aghq) on the BridgeStan log-density of the x-as-parameter model; work in unconstrained space then map back
discriminates: whether moments/CDF of x can be computed cheaply per parameter-set without sampling; quadrature on a smooth 1-D unconstrained posterior

situations: Have a multivariate model y ~ MVN(mu(x), Sigma(x)) (power-law mean, heteroskedastic noise, LKJ-Cholesky correlation) already fit with the scalar predictor x obs

action: Slice-sample x against BridgeStan log-density, looping over ~100-1000 posterior draws of theta; profile and optimize how draws are passed

expected_findings: Draws-as-data: ~375s (and ~60min on Windows due to slow I/O during repeated instantiation). Draws-as-parameters: ~4.5s for 100 draws / ~38s for 1000 draws -> ~500x faster; parallelizable over observations.
situations: Have a multivariate model y ~ MVN(mu(x), Sigma(x)) (power-law mean, heteroskedastic noise, LKJ-Cholesky correlation) already fit with the scalar predictor x obs

discriminates: DGP/harness bug & likelihood-coding error (target is wrong) vs identification/geometry hypotheses (target right, posterior legitimately wide)
expected_findings: If a clean sampler co-exists with strong undercoverage, weight shifts toward a mis-specified target (DGP bug or likelihood algebra) over a sampler/geometry problem, since geometry problems usually leave SOME signal

branches.if_observe: longer chains (10k iters) DO eventually surface max_treedepth violations
branches.then: geometry is not perfectly clean after all -> partial-identification hypothesis gains support; pursue reparameterization + boundary priors in parallel

action: Read the transformed-parameters + likelihood for redundant offset cancellation (does y1[n]-mu_y1[n] reduce to sd1*eta_y1, i.e. is the X1*b1 mean being subtracted right back off?)
discriminates: Reparameterization-algebra-error hypothesis vs the rest

situations: An extended/hierarchical model passes every sampler health check — no divergent transitions, no treedepth saturation, traceplots and pairs plots look fine — yet
leaves: stevebronder flags y1=mu_y1+sd1*eta_y1 then (y1-mu_y1) in target; author concludes it is the intended reparam, not the bug — discounted as red herring but it usefully sharpens intuition about the meas

branches.if_observe: the subtraction is the genuinely-intended conditional-mean correction

action: Restructure the latent variable: sample y1 directly as a parameter (vs as a non-centered transform), and swap Phi for normal_lcdf for numerical stability
discriminates: Latent-geometry hypothesis (does parameterization cause the bias?) vs target-mis-specification hypotheses

situations: An extended/hierarchical model passes every sampler health check — no divergent transitions, no treedepth saturation, traceplots and pairs plots look fine — yet

branches.if_observe: bias remains after the reparam fixes speed
branches.then: rule out parameterization as root cause; escalate to DGP audit

action: Integrate out the latent y1: replace per-unit latent with inflated observation SD sqrt(y1_hat_se^2 + sd1^2) and fit y1_hat directly
discriminates: Whether the latent layer per se introduces the bias vs the bias being upstream in the data

branches.if_observe: marginalized model still undercovers identically

action: Audit the data-generating R code line-by-line against the model's generative assumptions; check the rnorm/rng call signatures (arg order: n, mean, sd)
discriminates: DGP/harness-bug hypothesis vs genuine-model-undercoverage hypotheses

branches.if_observe: fixing the rng call restores coverage for most params but rho still undercovers
branches.then: the remaining miss is a real boundary/identification effect, not a bug -> move to the identification-corner analysis

action: Model the coverage indicator against the true generating parameters: regress per-dataset 95%-coverage on abs(rho) and sd via logistic GLM to find WHERE it fails
discriminates: Genuine-non-identification-corner hypothesis vs remaining-bug hypothesis
expected_findings: Expect coverage to drop where |rho| is large and/or sd1 is small — the region where selection correlation and outcome scale become weakly identified; coefficients should be significant (abs(rho) negative, sd positive)
situations: An extended/hierarchical model passes every sampler health check — no divergent transitions, no treedepth saturation, traceplots and pairs plots look fine — yet
leaves: glm(cover95 ~ sds + abs(rhos), binomial): abs(rho) coef -4.33 (p=1.6e-4), sds +1.34 (p=0.034) — undercoverage concentrated at |rho|->1 and sd1->0, exactly the model's non-identified corner; also bound

branches.if_observe: undercoverage tracks the non-identified boundary
branches.then: this is expected, not a defect; constrain the prior away from the boundary instead of treating it as a sampler bug

action: Tighten/shape priors away from the degenerate boundary: Beta(3,3) on rho_raw (rho in [0,1]) and Gamma(2,2) on sd1; also match the prior to the DGP's actual rho support
discriminates: Whether the residual undercoverage is curable by realistic priors vs a structural model defect

situations: An extended/hierarchical model passes every sampler health check — no divergent transitions, no treedepth saturation, traceplots and pairs plots look fine — yet

branches.if_observe: coverage recovers across the board with the boundary-shaping priors
branches.then: accept as resolution: regularize away from the non-identified corner rather than expecting calibration there

action: Sanity-check the verbal summary against the posterior geometry: does the prose's stated confidence track the actual posterior mass relative to the ROPE?

branches.if_observe: prose validated by experts

action: Pin down which interval the plotting function actually computes, then label it explicitly rather than by an interpretive name.

situations: Three brms difference-in-differences regressions fit; user has posterior-density plots of one interaction coefficient (time x disclosure) per outcome, annotated

action: Question whether the interaction alone supports the substantive claim about disclosure; consider surfacing main effects and other parameters.

situations: Three brms difference-in-differences regressions fit; user has posterior-density plots of one interaction coefficient (time x disclosure) per outcome, annotated

action: Audit the plot's visual encoding for consistency and avoid implying false precision.
discriminates: statistical-substance problems vs presentation friction that is fixable without changing the analysis
expected_findings: Under H4, the shaded band (89% interval) is a different quantity than the annotated P(effect>ROPE), so a reader can conflate them; mixed more-is-better/worse axes add cognitive load; one-decimal percentages overstate precision; 89% is a deliberate McElreath-style choice to avoid 95% fetishism.
situations: Three brms difference-in-differences regressions fit; user has posterior-density plots of one interaction coefficient (time x disclosure) per outcome, annotated

action: Inspect the constraint/prior pair on the GP range parameter and reason about the implied transform's effect on HMC geometry
discriminates: whether sampling pathology is risked by the prior's hard bounds/flatness vs a benign well-chosen prior

leaves: Replace real<lower=ap,upper=bp> phi + uniform with real<lower=0> phi and a soft decreasing prior; cite Stan manual GP chapter (Sec 18.3) and Betancourt 'Robust GPs in Stan' Part 3 for range/marginal-s

branches.then: constraint is fine; keep it

branches.if_observe: bounds are arbitrary (just to make uniform proper)
branches.then: drop upper bound, use lower=0 + decreasing prior to avoid transform-induced sampling problems

situations: A scalable Gaussian-process model (NNGP / Vecchia approximation) is being implemented in Stan for spatial data, and the modeler wants to (a) set sensible priors
leaves: Recommend cov_exp_quad for the basic GP for speed, but note the all-in-Stan GP is still the fair comparator to the all-in-Stan NNGP; flag that NNGP itself could likely be sped up with a built-in/analy

action: Frame the candidate parameterization and name it (ARD: pointwise distances with separate spatial vs temporal lengthscale parameters), then put it up for expert sanity-check rather than implementing blindly
discriminates: ARD-on-joint-3D-input vs a structured spatiotemporal/Kronecker covariance
expected_findings: ARD hypothesis: implies collapsing (x,y,t) into one 3D Euclidean input and learning per-axis lengthscales — mathematically a 3D anisotropic space model. Spatiotemporal/Kronecker hypotheses: would instead keep space and time as factored structures, not a single distance metric.

action: Solicit/accept a pointer to a known multi-output / spatio-temporal GP codebase and inspect which covariance structure it encodes
discriminates: Generic multi-output GP vs the specific isotropic-in-space / anisotropic-space-vs-time structure the OP wants
expected_findings: A repo model (gp_3dim_ncp.stan) that implements isotropy within space and anisotropy between space and time, structurally different from the ARD single-distance idea
situations: Have hierarchical space-time data (2D spatial coords + 1D time) with multiple trials nested in multiple individuals; want a GP that smooths the average surface

action: Restate as distribution-selection: explicitly enumerate what is observed, what is known-but-fixed, and what is to be inferred, instead of patching the multinomial
discriminates: whether the constraint is a likelihood-truncation problem vs a fundamentally different sampling scheme (without-replacement)
expected_findings: Truncated-multinomial hypothesis: workable but requires computing the precluded-region mass per time-step (Ben Goodrich: subtract log1m(sum_of_precluded_probabilities)). Without-replacement hypothesis: capacity-bounding IS the defining feature of a hypergeometric, so the multinomial is the wrong base object.
situations: Want a multinomial-like count model where each category's drawn count cannot exceed a known per-category supply (capacity), and the per-category preference/prob
leaves: Ben suggests multinomial_lpmf(y|theta) - log1m(sum_of_precluded_probabilities); Luc reframes 'bound by available stock' as draws-without-replacement -> multivariate hypergeometric (Wikipedia link)

branches.if_observe: draws are with-replacement / counts only soft-bounded
branches.then: stay with truncated/renormalized multinomial

branches.if_observe: counts hard-bounded by a finite urn of known composition
branches.then: switch to the hypergeometric family

action: Sanity-check the candidate distribution against the inference target: what are its free parameters, and do they match what is known vs unknown
discriminates: whether the distribution's parameters are the unknowns you want to learn, or instead force you to estimate quantities you already know
expected_findings: Central multivariate hypergeometric has NO preference parameter; its parameters are the urn counts N_k — which here are known data. So fitting it would (perversely) try to learn the N_k and cannot express preference. Mismatch on both ends.
situations: Want a multinomial-like count model where each category's drawn count cannot exceed a known per-category supply (capacity), and the per-category preference/prob
leaves: User (post 8): hypergeometric would attempt to learn N_k which are already known, and offers no preference; dirichlet-multinomial conjugacy noted but judged not what is wanted

branches.if_observe: distribution's params = your knowns and it lacks a preference knob
branches.then: reject; look for a weighted/biased generalization

action: Search for a generalization of the chosen family that adds the missing structural feature (weights/bias)
discriminates: central vs noncentral hypergeometric — i.e., purely-random draw vs preference-weighted draw
expected_findings: Noncentral hypergeometric distributions add per-category weights (omega) -> exactly the preference knob needed; need the MULTIVARIATE noncentral version. Dirichlet simplex can supply the weights to be inferred.
situations: Want a multinomial-like count model where each category's drawn count cannot exceed a known per-category supply (capacity), and the per-category preference/prob
leaves: Luc (post 12): noncentral hypergeometric (Wikipedia) allows weighted sampling; multivariate version needed; weights can come from a Dirichlet simplex

action: Match the variant to the physical/temporal draw mechanism (sequential one-at-a-time vs single simultaneous draw)
discriminates: Wallenius (draws taken one at a time, weights change conditional state) vs Fisher (all items drawn at once / independent binomials conditioned on total)
expected_findings: Wallenius fits many customers buying piece-by-piece through the day (consumer behavior). Fisher fits a single bulk purchase decided in one go. Aggregating a day with only start/end inventory sits between the two.
situations: Want a multinomial-like count model where each category's drawn count cannot exceed a known per-category supply (capacity), and the per-category preference/prob
leaves: Posts 15-18: Luc flags Wallenius assumes drawing individual pieces; consumer behavior ~ Wallenius, single bulk stock-buy ~ Fisher; aggregate daily rollup is in-between. User picks Wallenius for the pe

branches.then: Fisher noncentral

branches.if_observe: sequential individual draws over time
branches.then: Wallenius noncentral

action: Implement a custom _lpmf; the pmf contains a definite integral, so pick an integration approach and confront its numerical instability
discriminates: built-in integrate_1d vs external C++ (Agner Fog library) vs hand-rolled quadrature, and which is numerically stable near the integrand's singularity at t=1
expected_findings: integrate_1d (Boost) is the recommended first try for hard analytic integrals (Ben). External C++ linking is possible (rstan external.html vignette) but the Fog code is awkward to call. Naive attempts return 0 likelihood / -inf log-likelihood. The integrand has a singularity at t=1.
situations: Want a multinomial-like count model where each category's drawn count cannot exceed a known per-category supply (capacity), and the per-category preference/prob
leaves: Posts 13-27: Ben points to integrate_1d example and rstan external-C++ vignette; user's first integrate_1d/Fog attempts give log-lik = -inf for most inputs

action: Re-derive the integral in the log domain and approximate it with a stable quadrature that avoids the t=1 singularity
discriminates: whether instability is fixable by log-domain accumulation (log_sum_exp over a left-hand grid) vs requiring the full adaptive integrator
expected_findings: Working solution: compute each grid term as a log, accumulate with log_sum_exp, use a LEFT-hand rule so the t=1 singularity is excluded; wrap in reduce_sum for speed; infer omega as a Dirichlet simplex. Trades adaptivity/accuracy (hardcoded points, fixed r) for numerical stability.
situations: Want a multinomial-like count model where each category's drawn count cannot exceed a known per-category supply (capacity), and the per-category preference/prob
leaves: Post 29: approximate_wallenus_integral builds vector of log-terms (r*d-1)*log(i*dt) + sum(y .* log1m_exp(r*omega*log(i*dt))), returns log(r)+log(d)+log_sum_exp(...); MWNCHG_lpmf adds lgamma normalizer

branches.if_observe: log_sum_exp left-hand-rule pmf gives finite log-lik
branches.then: fit converges; refine later with trapezoidal rule, variable point count, optimal r (Fog eqn 19)

branches.if_observe: want cleaner/analytic integral
branches.then: use integrate_1d with the xc complement arg to handle the singularity (linked follow-up thread)

action: Enumerate and trial practical heuristics: (a) keep wide prior and ignore minor divergences, (b) use an unrealistically narrow prior that removes the pathology, (c) rejection-sample 'bad' datasets via a binary a-priori condition, (d) drop non-converging runs from diagnostics
discriminates: which intervention buys a valid-enough calibration at acceptable cost; surfaces the worry that (c)/(d) silently modify the prior (H3)
expected_findings: Narrowing (b) assumes good computation under narrow prior transfers to wide prior — usually OK since many bugs/bad parametrizations manifest even narrow, and passing narrow-prior SBC beats no SBC. Rejection (c) is reinterpretable as adding a weird joint prior on theta; tracking rejection rate is itself a diagnostic. Practitioner initially believes (c)/(d) should make SBC fail given enough runs (H3).

branches.if_observe: fear that rejection invalidates the uniformity proof

action: Derive the effect of a rejection function f(y) on each factor: the induced prior on theta, the induced generating distribution y|theta, and their product the posterior
discriminates: H3 (rejection breaks SBC) vs H4 (rejection on y is harmless) — the decisive correction of the thread
expected_findings: Define L(theta)=∫f(y)π(y|theta)dy and R=∫L(theta)π(theta)dtheta. Prior distorts to (L(theta)/R)π(theta); generating dist distorts to (f(y)/L(theta))π(y|theta); but in the posterior the L(theta) factors CANCEL, leaving π(θ|y) → (f(y)/R)π(y|θ)π(θ), and f(y)/R is a CONSTANT in θ. So the posterior is unchanged up to normalization → ranks still uniform. Refutes H3, establishes H4.

action: Reason from the variational FAMILY geometry: ask what a product-of-independent-Gaussians can possibly represent vs a correlated/narrow ridge.
discriminates: H2 (mean-field structural limit) vs H3 (optimizer instability) -- a family that provably cannot contain the target explains failure without invoking the optimizer.
expected_findings: Mean-field = product of axis-aligned Gaussians; cannot capture a Gaussian concentrated near y=x. So even the BEST mean-field fit is wrong by construction. Predicts mean-field marginals should match true CONDITIONALS, not marginals.

action: Reason about the autodiff cost model rather than the linear-algebra cost model: count the operations a hand-built NxN covariance records on the reverse-mode tape.
discriminates: Distinguishes the autodiff-memory hypothesis from the user's O(N^3)-flops mental model and from the missing-jitter / numerical-PD hypothesis.
expected_findings: A directly-built covariance creates ~N^2 autodiff nodes; at N~728 this plausibly exceeds 16GB, explaining stalls and non-mixing as memory pressure, not a flop-count issue. Missing jitter would instead surface as Cholesky rejections, not slow grinding.

leaves: Autodiff in Stan records one node per elementary operation; an NxN covariance assembled element-by-element in Stan code makes the expression graph O(N^2) and memory grows with the graph, not just with

branches.if_observe: Memory is the binding constraint
branches.then: Reduce autodiff-tree size: use a builtin covariance function, or a basis-function (Hilbert-space) approximation for <=3D problems

branches.if_observe: Problem is >3D (here 8D)
branches.then: Hilbert-space basis approximation does not apply; keep looking for a cheaper covariance construction

action: Profile/compare warmup+sample timings of the multi-call cov_exp_quad version against the original, and attribute the regression.
discriminates: Tests whether 'use the builtin' is sufficient, or whether the number of separate autograd-tracked matrix operations (multiple cov_exp_quad calls + multiple pointwise multiplies) is itself the cost driver.
expected_findings: Multiple cov_exp_quad calls plus elementwise products inflate the autodiff tree again, making it slower than the single hand-built loop. Confirms cost is the size of the expression graph, not which function is used.
situations: A hand-coded Stan GP emulator for Bayesian calibration (Kennedy-O'Hagan / Higdon-style: field y and simulator eta stacked into one z, covariance matrix built by
leaves: cov_exp_quad lacks a vector (ARD) length-scale, so emulating ARD via one call per dimension and .* products multiplies the number of tracked O(N^2) operations; ~7 such matrices is ~7x the autodiff cos

branches.if_observe: Multi-call builtin is slower
branches.then: Collapse to a single covariance evaluation: pre-scale the inputs by the inverse length-scales, then compute distances once

action: Fold the per-dimension length-scales into the inputs by scaling columns of X (diag_post_multiply by inverse length-scale), then compute the kernel once via squared_distance inside a single loop.
discriminates: Whether ARD can be achieved with one O(N^2) autodiff pass instead of D separate passes; also exposes a unit/algebra bug in the user's transcription.
expected_findings: Pre-scaling moves the D-fold cost inside one squared_distance call, shrinking the tape; result is faster than both the hand-built loop and the multi-call builtin. A transcription slip is likely (e.g. forgetting exp of the squared distance, or scaling by beta vs inv(beta)/square).
situations: A hand-coded Stan GP emulator for Bayesian calibration (Kennedy-O'Hagan / Higdon-style: field y and simulator eta stacked into one z, covariance matrix built by
leaves: scaled_X = diag_post_multiply(X, inv(rho)); Sigma[i,j] = exp(-0.5*squared_distance(scaled_X[i,], scaled_X[j,]))/lambda. Keeping the D-vector scaling inside squared_distance uses less tape than expandi

branches.if_observe: Faster and correct after fixing the exp/scaling
branches.then: Adopt; for still-larger N consider Kronecker/eigen structure on the simulator grid, but expect autodiff to again dominate

branches.if_observe: Output looks wrong (e.g. no decay)

action: Separate the two distinct roles of a diagonal addition: numerical jitter (tiny fixed constant for PD-ness) vs estimated observation/emulator noise (a parameter).
discriminates: Whether the diagonal term is a numerical fudge or a modeling quantity; determines fixed-vs-estimated and whether using it to suppress overfitting is legitimate.
expected_findings: If the matrix is only numerically borderline, a fixed ~1e-10 jitter suffices and should NOT be a parameter; if the emulated simulator truly has irreducible noise (here: simulator subsampled on a sensitivity-selected subset, so identical inputs can give different outputs), then it is real noise and SHOULD be estimated, optionally with a lower bound from known measurement accuracy.
situations: A hand-coded Stan GP emulator for Bayesian calibration (Kennedy-O'Hagan / Higdon-style: field y and simulator eta stacked into one z, covariance matrix built by
leaves: Jitter = smallest constant making the mathematically-PD covariance numerically PD under Cholesky; if the model runs without it, omit it. A genuine noise term is a modeled parameter, not jitter; a phys

branches.if_observe: Matrix only numerically borderline
branches.then: Use a tiny fixed jitter (~1e-10), not a parameter

branches.if_observe: Real residual noise exists (subsampled simulator)
branches.then: Estimate the nugget; optionally bound it below using measurement-accuracy prior info

action: Probe the fitted GP with marginal predictions: hold 7 of 8 inputs at their means, sweep the 8th, overlay training data, and inspect interpolation behavior.
discriminates: Confirms the tiny-length-scale -> revert-to-mean-between-points mechanism versus other causes of poor validation; identifies which inputs are pathological.
expected_findings: On pathological dimensions the GP is accurate at training points but snaps back toward the prior mean between them (high-frequency, physically implausible wiggles), visually confirming overfitting from too-short length-scales.
situations: A hand-coded Stan GP emulator for Bayesian calibration (Kennedy-O'Hagan / Higdon-style: field y and simulator eta stacked into one z, covariance matrix built by
leaves: Per-input marginal sweeps (other inputs fixed at average) with training data overlaid reveal accurate-at-points / poor-between-points behavior on dimensions whose length-scale collapsed; this localize

branches.then: Constrain the function class / length-scales: boundary-avoiding (inverse-gamma) length-scale priors and, since most relationships are expected monotonic, use monotonic functions which are less prone to overfitting

branches.if_observe: Predictions leave the training envelope
branches.then: Check extrapolation / coverage of the training design

action: Refute the artifact hypothesis: re-run under varied priors and on simulated (fake) data with known parameters
discriminates: H3 (prior/data artifact) vs H1/H2 (likelihood-geometry property)
expected_findings: H3: correlation changes/disappears with prior or data. H1/H2: identical correlation pattern persists regardless of prior or fake-data realization
situations: Fitting a Gamma (or similar 2-parameter positive distribution) in Stan via shape & rate (e.g. log-shape, log-rate); the posterior estimates of shape and rate ar
leaves: OP reports same dependence across different priors and a fake-data simulation -> rules out prior/data artifact, points at the likelihood/parameterization itself

branches.if_observe: correlation persists identically
branches.then: treat as a geometry problem; compute the Fisher information matrix

branches.if_observe: correlation tracks the prior
branches.then: it is prior-induced; specify f(shape)*f(rate|shape) instead

action: Write the Fisher information matrix in the current (shape alpha, rate beta) coordinates and inspect the off-diagonal
discriminates: H1 (intrinsic, no obvious diagonalizing substitution) vs H2 (removable by reparameterization)
expected_findings: Off-diagonal is -1/beta (entries trigamma(alpha), -1/beta, alpha/beta^2); no obvious substitution diagonalizes it in these coordinates -> suggests but does not prove non-orthogonalizability
situations: Fitting a Gamma (or similar 2-parameter positive distribution) in Stan via shape & rate (e.g. log-shape, log-rate); the posterior estimates of shape and rate ar
leaves: Goodrich: 'I don't think orthogonal is possible... I don't see a substitution that would make that diagonal' -> initial (later overturned) read toward H1

branches.if_observe: off-diagonal identically 0
branches.then: already orthogonal, done

branches.if_observe: off-diagonal nonzero with no obvious diagonalizer
branches.then: search candidate reparameterizations and test each for a zero off-diagonal

action: Adopt the operational criterion: for a candidate parameterization, compute the Fisher/Hessian off-diagonal expectation and check whether it is identically zero
discriminates: which specific transform achieves orthogonality (separates the many failed common transforms from the working ones)
expected_findings: Common transforms (log shape/rate, scale, mean, mode, std) -> nonzero off-diagonal. A construction where one coordinate equals E[log y] (the geometric-mean coordinate) -> off-diagonal evaluates to zero
situations: Fitting a Gamma (or similar 2-parameter positive distribution) in Stan via shape & rate (e.g. log-shape, log-rate); the posterior estimates of shape and rate ar
leaves: OP computes off-diagonal for (log geometric mean gamma = E[log Y] = psi(rho)+log theta, log scale tau = log theta) and finds it = 0 -> orthogonal parameterization exists, overturning the pessimistic H

branches.if_observe: candidate gives off-diagonal 0
branches.then: orthogonal; verify with explicit derivation and empirical sampling

branches.if_observe: candidate gives nonzero
branches.then: discard, try another (e.g. mean + untransformed shape)

action: Derive the cross second-derivative of the log-density and take its expectation, exploiting that E[log Y]=gamma by construction
discriminates: genuine orthogonality vs algebra error / coincidence
expected_findings: E[d2 log f/d(tau)d(gamma)] = omega''(gamma-tau)(gamma - E[log y]) = omega''(.)(gamma-gamma) = 0, where omega is inverse-digamma; the zero comes from the coordinate being defined as the mean of log Y
situations: Fitting a Gamma (or similar 2-parameter positive distribution) in Stan via shape & rate (e.g. log-shape, log-rate); the posterior estimates of shape and rate ar
leaves: OP supplies the derivation; the off-diagonal vanishes because gamma := E[log Y] makes (gamma - E[log y]) = 0 -> confirms orthogonality structurally, not numerically

branches.if_observe: expectation reduces to 0 cleanly
branches.then: orthogonality confirmed analytically

branches.if_observe: residual nonzero term
branches.then: parameterization not orthogonal; revisit

action: Try mean + untransformed shape (mu = alpha*beta, tau = alpha): re-derive log-density and its off-diagonal Hessian
discriminates: whether a closed-form, easy-to-code orthogonal parameterization exists (mean+shape) vs the inverse-digamma geometric-mean one
expected_findings: Off-diagonal Hessian = (x - mu)/mu^2, whose expectation is 0 since E[x]=mu -> orthogonal, and no inverse-digamma needed; contrast: mean+scale does NOT orthogonalize (off-diagonals nonzero)
situations: Fitting a Gamma (or similar 2-parameter positive distribution) in Stan via shape & rate (e.g. log-shape, log-rate); the posterior estimates of shape and rate ar
leaves: JohnnyZoom4H: (mu, tau)=(mean, shape) gives off-diag (x-mu)/mu^2 -> E=0; mean+scale failed. Simple closed-form orthogonal parameterization

branches.then: code it as a custom lpdf and benchmark sampling

branches.if_observe: nonzero

action: Declare the constrained linear combination as a bounded transformed-parameter vector (vector<lower=0>[N] dydx) and rely on Stan's reject-on-violation behavior.
discriminates: Whether boundary-rejection on a derived/transformed quantity is a workable way to enforce coupled inequality constraints.
expected_findings: If rejection-on-transformed-param were viable -> sampler proceeds normally. Observed: sampler hangs or random-walks, needs carefully chosen inits to even start, and yields a huge divergence fraction (>80%) and low ESS -> rejection on a transformed parameter does NOT properly constrain the underlying betas.

branches.then: Reject the bound-on-transformed-parameter approach; look for either a smooth penalty or a valid constraining transform.

action: Check applicability of the closed-form D-inversion reparametrization to this problem's dimensions.
discriminates: Whether the standard 'invert D' trick (for square/full-rank constraint systems) transfers to a tall over-determined per-observation constraint system.
expected_findings: If D were square/invertible -> reuse directly (route=recall). Observed: D is 2N x K with 2N >> K (many more rows than columns), non-invertible -> the prior-thread method does not apply; must reason a new approach.
situations: Fitting a (quadratic) regression in Stan where linear combinations of coefficients — the partial derivatives dy/dx = beta1 + beta11*x1 + beta12*x2 — must be non
leaves: 2N inequality rows vs K=5 columns means D cannot be inverted; the convenient quadratic-D reparametrization is unavailable for per-observation derivative constraints.

branches.if_observe: D tall / non-square
branches.then: Abandon recall of the D-inversion case; pursue penalty or ordered-transform constructions.

action: Place an informative prior on the transformed derivatives (lognormal, then gamma with varying shape/rate) and watch the divergence-vs-bias tradeoff.
discriminates: Whether the divergence problem is curable by a prior, and whether such a prior corrupts the estimates (prior-data conflict).
expected_findings: Spiky prior (lognormal(log0.8,1)) -> divergences vanish but estimates pulled to prior mode, far from truth. Flat-ish prior (gamma(1.05,0.125)) -> estimates recover truth but divergences return en masse. Intermediate gamma is only a compromise -> a prior is the wrong instrument; it trades positivity-enforcement against bias.
situations: Fitting a (quadratic) regression in Stan where linear combinations of coefficients — the partial derivatives dy/dx = beta1 + beta11*x1 + beta12*x2 — must be non
leaves: Diagnostic that confirms prior-data conflict: at the prior mode the density is ~5x that at the 0.05 quantile, so the prior 'signal' overwhelms the data 'signal'; estimated derivatives collapse to the

branches.if_observe: no divergences but estimates biased to prior mode
branches.then: Prior too informative; this is conflict, not a fix.

branches.if_observe: estimates good but divergences return
branches.then: Prior too weak to regularize the boundary; need a different mechanism than a generative prior.

action: Add an explicit smooth penalty to target only when a derivative falls below the threshold (soft constraint), adding a matching constant when above it for smoothness.
discriminates: Whether leaving the interior likelihood untouched and only penalizing boundary violations smoothly fixes the HMC geometry without biasing estimates.
expected_findings: Expected (and observed): no divergent transitions, estimates (mean/median of betas and of all derivatives) very close to truth, all fitted derivatives positive, only a handful of slightly-negative derivative draws at a few obs/iterations. Soft constraint works where both hard-rejection and prior-on-derivative failed.
situations: Fitting a (quadratic) regression in Stan where linear combinations of coefficients — the partial derivatives dy/dx = beta1 + beta11*x1 + beta12*x2 — must be non
leaves: for i in 1:N: if dydx[i]<lt: target += normal_lpdf(dydx[i]-lt | 0, rs) else target += normal_lpdf(0|0,rs); regularization_sigma rs controls strength; it need not be a valid distribution since we are p

branches.if_observe: no divergences, estimates recover truth
branches.then: Adopt soft constraint as the easy, fast, working solution.

branches.if_observe: rare slightly-negative draws
branches.then: Acceptable; tighten rs if strict positivity matters.

action: Build a valid ORDERED constraining transform: declare the betas the bound depends on (b11,b12) before b1, and bound b1 by -min(b11*x1+b12*x2).
discriminates: Whether mutually-dependent (data-and-parameter-dependent) bounds are legitimate in Stan, and whether the by-construction-feasible transform samples well.
expected_findings: Corrects the misconception: Stan samples on the UNCONSTRAINED scale and the constraining transform maps to feasible values for any unconstrained input (like simplex/ordered/sum-to-zero) — declaration order = transform order (b11=unc, b12=unc, b1=exp(b1_unc)-min(b11*x1+b12*x2)). Result: zero infeasible draws ever, estimates basically identical to soft constraint, but slower (slowest chain 124s vs 45s) at adapt_delta=0.999.
situations: Fitting a (quadratic) regression in Stan where linear combinations of coefficients — the partial derivatives dy/dx = beta1 + beta11*x1 + beta12*x2 — must be non
leaves: parameters{ real b11; real b12; real b22; real<lower=-min(b11*x1+b12*x2)> b1; real<lower=-min(b12*x1+b22*x2)> b2; }. The min over data x makes the per-obs constraints simultaneously satisfied. Guarant

branches.if_observe: samples cleanly, identical estimates, slower
branches.then: Valid alternative; choose between hard/soft on cost vs strictness.

branches.if_observe: still slow/sticky
branches.then: Tune the parametrization geometry (scale/rotate so leading coeffs align with the long axis of the polygon-like feasible region; pick b1<b2 vs b1>b2 ordering).

action: Reframe the comparison metric to effective-sample-size-per-second, not total runtime.
discriminates: Which method is actually more efficient once precision-per-time is accounted for, and whether extra iterations can be traded off.
expected_findings: Methods may take similar time but differ in ESS; ESS/sec is the right yardstick. Observed: soft 45s vs hard 124s slowest chain, ESS roughly comparable (some params favor soft, some hard) -> soft is cheaper here; if precision is excess, cut sampling/warmup iterations.
situations: Fitting a (quadratic) regression in Stan where linear combinations of coefficients — the partial derivatives dy/dx = beta1 + beta11*x1 + beta12*x2 — must be non
leaves: Compare ESS/second across methods; a faster constraining transform (e.g. built-in sum_to_zero_vector style transforms for the analogous sum-to-zero family) lets you buy more iterations for higher ESS

branches.if_observe: similar ESS, soft faster
branches.then: Prefer soft unless strict zero-violation is required.

branches.if_observe: need guaranteed feasibility every draw
branches.then: Prefer the ordered hard transform despite higher cost.

action: Add the ordering assumption between b1 and b2 (e.g. b1<b2) and bound b2 above by a data-and-parameter expression to fix identifiability of the feasible region.
discriminates: Whether resolving the b1-vs-b2 ordering tightens the posterior enough to sample at default settings without bespoke inits.
expected_findings: With b1<b2 assumed and b2 given lower=b1, upper=max(b12*(x2-x1)+b11*x1-b22*x2+b1), the posterior is pinned down: adapt_delta can drop to 0.8 and inits can be dropped — everything samples fine.
situations: Fitting a (quadratic) regression in Stan where linear combinations of coefficients — the partial derivatives dy/dx = beta1 + beta11*x1 + beta12*x2 — must be non
leaves: real<lower=b1, upper=max(b12*(x2-x1)+b11*x1-b22*x2+b1)> b2; supply the b1<b2 (or b1>b2) ordering as the extra identifying information; different bound choices give nicer or nastier posteriors.

branches.if_observe: samples fine at adapt_delta=0.8 without inits
branches.then: The ordering+two-sided bound is the most robust hard parametrization.

action: Classify the specific warning as critical (statistically actionable) vs stylistic/conservative, using best-practice priors as the yardstick.

H2: ignore or hardcode. H3: ignore, report bug. H4: pedantic mode is an aggregation of statistical + stylistic advice never community-ratified as critical, so it is too conservative to be a sensible default.

action: Recall the theory of the probability integral transform for non-continuous margins and check whether poisson_cdf(y) gives a valid Uniform(0,1) input to the copula
discriminates: valid-PIT-for-discrete vs PIT-only-valid-for-continuous (requires latent augmentation)
expected_findings: For a continuous margin F(Y)~Uniform(0,1) exactly. For a discrete margin F(Y) lands on a discrete grid and is NOT Uniform(0,1); the inverse map uniform->discrete is non-unique, so plugging poisson_cdf(y) into the copula systematically distorts the implied dependence and biases rho.
situations: A Gaussian-copula model coupling a continuous (Gaussian) and a count (Poisson) regression in Stan consistently recovers the WRONG copula correlation rho. The sa
leaves: Accepted answer (andrjohns, #2): 'You can't directly use discrete outcomes in copulas since there isn't a unique mapping from the uniform -> discrete space; you need to use a data-augmentation approac

branches.then: replace plug-in PIT with latent data-augmentation (Smith & Khaled 2012)

action: Apply the Smith & Khaled (2012) variable-augmentation construction: introduce a per-observation latent uniform for each discrete margin bounded by the CDF jump, with the corresponding Jacobian, and apply the standard-normal quantile
discriminates: where the discrete margin enters the likelihood -- via an explicit ~poisson(...) statement vs ONLY through the latent u's
expected_findings: Correct model: for each discrete observation declare a nuisance latent U_j ~ Uniform(F_j(y-1), F_j(y)) (the CDF interval bracketing the observed count), add Jacobian log(F(y)-F(y-1)) = log pmf, then set q_j = inv_Phi(U_j) and feed q into the Gaussian copula. The discrete marginal contributes to the likelihood THROUGH the latent u's, not through a separate sampling statement.

Under H4: both means drift toward the data centroid and sit there (one fat component swallows both clusters).

action: Ask for the posterior standard deviation of the cluster means, per component.
discriminates: H4 (scale collapse: one real component + one empty/unconstrained component) vs H2 (a genuine single central optimum).

action: Compare the implied marginal SD of each model component against the actual cluster width and inter-cluster distance in the data.
discriminates: H4 confirmation: the model has NO free scale parameter (L is a correlation Cholesky factor -> marginal SD pinned to 1), so if real clusters are narrower than 1 and their separation is ~1.5, a single unit-SD component can straddle both.
expected_findings: Inter-cluster distance ~1.5, cluster width much smaller; model component SD fixed at 1 => model expects clusters far wider than they are, so two narrow real clusters comfortably fit inside one model component.
situations: A multivariate (2D+) Gaussian mixture fit in Stan collapses onto a single component: posterior cluster means converge to roughly the average of the two true mea
leaves: nhuurre: 'L is a correlation matrix Cholesky factor so marginal SD is 1 ... clusters are much smaller than the model expects and close enough to fit inside a single component.'

action: Confirm the MAP=MLE identity at the math level, then qualify it: posterior ∝ likelihood × prior, so a constant prior gives argmax(posterior)=argmax(likelihood) — but only on the scale where the prior is actually constant.

action: Name the estimand: REML marginalizes out the fixed effects and optimizes only over the variance components — not full ML.

leaves: REML = restricted ML, fixed effects integrated out; equals marginal-posterior maximization of variance params with flat prior on fixed effects. Ref: Hodges, 'Richly Parametrized Linear Models', sec 1.

action: Reject 'max draw from a sample = MLE' — invoke concentration of measure / typical set: the highest-density draw in high dim is nowhere near the mode.
discriminates: H5 vs valid optimization routes.
expected_findings: In N dims a standard-normal draw sits at squared-distance ~ chiSq(N) from the mode (e.g. dim 100 -> central interval (55,161)), never at the mode. Some densities (e.g. beta(0.5,0.5)) have no mode at all though sampling is fine. Use a real optimizer (or annealing that shrinks the target), not the argmax over draws.

action: Disambiguate 'maximum likelihood for hierarchical models' into three estimands and show the joint mode is pathological.
discriminates: H4 — separates (a) literal max p(y|alpha) [ignores hierarchy], (b) joint mode of (alpha,phi) [UNDEFINED], (c) marginal mode of hyperparams phi [defined].
expected_findings: Joint mode (b) does NOT exist: the joint likelihood has a pole as group-level variance -> 0 (it blows up, local integral diverges). Any optimizer that 'converges' has found a LOCAL mode; matching numbers across algorithms just means they fell into the same local basin, not that an MLE exists. The reasonable target is (c) marginal mode, or better full Bayes.
situations: User wants a maximum-likelihood (point) estimate for a Rubin-style random-effects / hierarchical meta-analysis model (theta ~ normal(beta, sigma_theta); y ~ nor
leaves: Gelman (a/b/c): (a) max p(y|alpha) doesn't use hierarchy; (b) joint mode undefined — pole at sigma->0; (c) marginal posterior mode of phi (= REML-like), can hit sigma=0 boundary. Recommend full Bayesi

action: Pin down INLA's true scaling axis: separate the count of latent Gaussian variables from the count of hyperparameters, because INLA treats them with different machinery.
discriminates: H1 vs H2 — whether INLA's approximation actually applies, which hinges on the hyperparameter count not the latent-field size.
expected_findings: Under H1 (naive): 'GP is the prototypic LGF, so INLA fits.' Under H2 (correct): INLA software handles thousands of latent Gaussian variables fine, but integrates hyperparameters by CCD (a deterministic grid), which is only practical up to ~15 hyperparameters. The large intermediate matrix can be a function of just a few hyperparameters — that's the regime INLA is built for.

action: Ask whether replacing CCD with MCMC over the hyperparameters rescues the approach, and check what that actually costs.

User's own insight: sampling the hyperparameters partly defeats the speed purpose of INLA.

action: Frame fixed effects with Gaussian priors so they can be marginalized analytically in a Laplace block; note this is the direction Stan is building with the INLA developers.

action: Contrast fixed-point simulation against drawing theta from the prior; test whether a point-estimate framing is even the right object in Bayes.

expected_findings: H1: a single-point prior gives bias that is an artifact of the prior mean and tells you nothing general — dead end. H2: drawing from the prior makes the check well-posed and prior-consistent. H3: clarifies that the posterior, not a point, is the inferential object.
situations: Analyst has a working frequentist evaluation recipe (simulate N datasets at a fixed known theta; compute bias / variance / MSE / coverage of the estimator) and
leaves: Reframe: SBC draws theta ~ prior each replicate (lambda ~ rnorm(...) then rpois), so simulations are tied to the same prior the posterior uses; the data-averaged posterior / rank-uniformity self-consi

branches.if_observe: analyst's measured bias is just (prior_mean - theta), flipping with the prior
branches.then: diagnose this as the single-point-prior artifact; switch to prior-averaged SBC / decision-theoretic utility

action: Confirm the rank-uniformity target, but separate 'computation is correct' from 'estimator is good'.
discriminates: H2 (SBC validates computation) vs the analyst's lingering H1 goal (rank estimators by bias).
expected_findings: H2: uniform ranks => no gross computational/implementation error. H3: SBC cannot tell you which of two models is 'better' on real data — only real data can.
situations: Analyst has a working frequentist evaluation recipe (simulate N datasets at a fixed known theta; compute bias / variance / MSE / coverage of the estimator) and
leaves: Yes — aim for ranks ~ Uniform(0, n_iter); but clarify SBC certifies the posterior is computed correctly given the model, not that the model is the best estimator of theta.

branches.if_observe: ranks uniform
branches.then: conclude not-strongly-miscalibrated; quantify residual miscalibration with empirical_coverage / plot_coverage rather than declaring perfect calibration

branches.if_observe: ranks non-uniform (U-shaped / sloped / spiked)
branches.then: treat as a computation or model-implementation bug (if DGP matches model) and debug there

action: Calibrate the strength of the conclusion against the number of simulations and quantify residual miscalibration.
discriminates: H2 strong-pass vs weak-pass; how many replicates S are enough (precision hyperparameter).
expected_findings: H2: 'not strongly miscalibrated' rather than 'proven calibrated'; coverage plots bound residual error. H5: if data simulated exactly as model assumes, any leftover miscalibration is a computation bug.
situations: Analyst has a working frequentist evaluation recipe (simulate N datasets at a fixed known theta; compute bias / variance / MSE / coverage of the estimator) and
leaves: ~100 sims reliably catches *major* problems but minor miscalibration can hide; use empirical_coverage and plot_coverage / plot_coverage_diff to bound how large a miscalibration is still consistent wit

branches.if_observe: coverage-diff bands comfortably contain zero
branches.then: accept as well-calibrated within simulation resolution; increase S only if tighter bounds needed

branches.if_observe: coverage materially off nominal
branches.then: if DGP==model assume computation/implementation bug; if DGP!=model interpret as a robustness finding

action: Distinguish 'model' (probabilistic data description) from 'estimator', and state the limits of simulation-based comparison.
discriminates: H3 (estimator/bias-MSE comparison framing) vs correct Bayesian model-checking scope.
expected_findings: H3: bias/MSE-on-simulated-data model ranking is circular; the frequentist case differs because one statistical model admits many estimators (MLE vs Firth) worth ranking, whereas one Bayesian model admits one posterior.
situations: Analyst has a working frequentist evaluation recipe (simulate N datasets at a fixed known theta; compute bias / variance / MSE / coverage of the estimator) and
leaves: In Bayes the model uniquely defines the posterior — there is no family of estimators to rank by bias; SBC checks computation. To compare against frequentist estimators or assert 'use this at N=50', yo
No — SBC validates that A and B are each implemented correctly and pose no sampler problem; it cannot say which is better for a real dataset. Generating data under A's assumptions trivially favors A.

branches.if_observe: goal is genuine model comparison for a real problem
branches.then: use real-data tools (e.g. cross-validation / predictive performance), not SBC

action: Localize the NA: identify which specific parameters carry NA R-hat/ESS/MCSE and confirm their draws are all identical (zero variance)
discriminates: separates a single benign constant (points to H1) from NA spread across genuinely-sampled parameters (points to H2)
expected_findings: H1: NA confined to the deliberately-fixed parameter, all its draws equal; H2: NA on a parameter that should vary; H3: NA on a transformed/derived quantity whose pre-transform source does vary
situations: An MCMC fit reports "largest R-hat is NA, indicating chains have not mixed" plus low Bulk/Tail-ESS warnings, while the printed per-parameter summary shows R-hat
leaves: Inspect draws for the flagged parameter; the global 'largest R-hat is NA' message is just max() over per-parameter values including the constant — it does not by itself mean the real parameters failed

branches.if_observe: NA only on the by-design-constant parameter
branches.then: treat as H1 benign for that parameter, but do NOT assume the rest are fine — re-check real parameters' R-hat/ESS independently

branches.if_observe: NA on a parameter expected to vary
branches.then: escalate to H2: investigate stuck chain / non-identifiability

action: Apply the identifiability principle: the diagnostic cannot distinguish a logically-constant parameter from a stuck chain using only finite equal-valued draws; bring external knowledge of whether the variable is *meant* to be constant
discriminates: H1 (logically constant -> ignore for that var) vs H2 (logically non-constant -> real convergence problem) vs H3 (fp collapse of a varying quantity)
expected_findings: H1: user knows the parameter is fixed by construction -> NA is expected and uninformative; H2: parameter should be free -> NA signals a real problem requiring different diagnostics; H3: e.g. indicator p(theta>0) near 0/1 or inv_logit(alpha) at fp limits -> check the underlying continuous theta/alpha instead
situations: An MCMC fit reports "largest R-hat is NA, indicating chains have not mixed" plus low Bulk/Tail-ESS warnings, while the printed per-parameter summary shows R-hat
leaves: Examples: int theta_gt_zero = theta>0 yields constant 0/1 even when p is in (0,1); inv_logit(alpha) can collapse to constant despite distinct alpha draws. Convergence/efficiency must then be checked o

branches.if_observe: variable is logically constant by design
branches.then: NA is correct/expected; do not chase it — verify the genuinely-estimated parameters separately

branches.if_observe: variable should vary but draws are constant
branches.then: diagnose as stuck/non-identified; check the underlying parameter and re-run/re-parameterize

action: Enumerate the candidate workarounds and test each against the constraint 'keep the parameter, keep real warnings'
discriminates: which mitigation actually works vs which is a hack vs which is the principled fix
expected_findings: suppressWarnings() hides real warnings too (rejected); moving the constant to data/model block works but drops it from the reported parameters (user rejected); generated-quantities jitter (uniform_rng tiny value) is a hack and FAILS when the name is already bound in transformed parameters (can't reassign); the principled fix is updated diagnostic code that reports single-value variables correctly
situations: An MCMC fit reports "largest R-hat is NA, indicating chains have not mixed" plus low Bulk/Tail-ESS warnings, while the printed per-parameter summary shows R-hat
leaves: Tested jitter `mu[1]=uniform_rng(1e-15,1e-16)` in generated quantities: rejected because a name set in transformed parameters cannot be reassigned in generated quantities; also semantically wrong (mak

branches.if_observe: jitter blocked by name binding / produces meaningless posterior
branches.then: abandon jitter; go to the updated-diagnostic-code fix

branches.if_observe: willing to drop var from reported params
branches.then: move constraint to model block (simplest, but loses the param in output)

action: Switch to the current diagnostic implementation (posterior package / CmdStanR) instead of old RStan summary
discriminates: H4 (old-code artifact) vs a substantive numerical problem; also confirms which tool/version produced the scary message
expected_findings: H4: posterior's as_draws_df + summarise_draws (or RStan develop branch) handles single-value variables and returns correct values without the misleading global message; substantive issue: even with new code a should-vary parameter still shows NA/poor diagnostics
situations: An MCMC fit reports "largest R-hat is NA, indicating chains have not mixed" plus low Bulk/Tail-ESS warnings, while the printed per-parameter summary shows R-hat
leaves: Fix paths: install RStan develop (remotes::install_github 'stan-dev/rstan' ref='develop'); or install.packages('posterior'); as_draws_df(your_fit); summarise_draws(draws_df). CmdStanR already uses pos

branches.if_observe: new code clears the false alarm for the constant
branches.then: resolved — the warning was the old summary path; trust posterior output

branches.if_observe: new code still flags a should-vary parameter
branches.then: genuine convergence/identifiability problem (H2) — not a tooling artifact

branches.then: transition to the separation/shrinkage sub-investigation below

action: Run a concrete simulation to show the fitted hierarchical model behaves, separating sample proportions from fitted probabilities

expected_findings: Model fits fine, sd(Intercept) ~ 1, no infinities; the only genuinely pathological case is COMPLETE separation, where neither Bayesian-without-strong-prior nor ordinary logistic regression works — so it isn't special to the hierarchical trick.
