action: Replace the two-stage mental model with joint estimation and a target-function (penalty vs likelihood) argument
discriminates: two-stage/marginal frequentist mental model vs joint Bayesian target maximization
expected_findings: Resolves 'shrink from infinity' as a non-problem: the joint target never benefits from sending coefficients to infinity; rapidly diminishing likelihood returns vs steep prior penalty pin the estimate at an interior point.
situations: Want to set scale-by-number-of-predictors priors (the R^2-based Regression and Other Stories rule: coefficient SD = sqrt(R^2_*/p)*sd(y), and sigma ~ Exponential
leaves: All parameters (random-effect mean, random-effect SD, the random-effect vector, everything else) are estimated JOINTLY — there is no first-stage MLE. Pulling an element to -inf while pulling the SD to

branches.if_observe: user asks 'what exactly is the penalty?'
branches.then: give the tall-skinny-vs-short-wide normal density argument

branches.if_observe: user cites frequentist 'two-stage GLMM' sources
branches.then: advise mentally discarding them — they conflate the statistical model with the fitting/inference method (like thinking the F-distribution lives inside the ANOVA generative model)

action: Locate the source of shrinkage in the LIKELIHOOD geometry, not the SD prior
discriminates: shrinkage-driven-by-SD-prior hypothesis vs shrinkage-intrinsic-to-the-hierarchical-likelihood hypothesis
expected_findings: Confirms the latter: even a virtually flat SD prior still yields strong shrinkage, because the hierarchical normal density (the 'random effect contribution to the target') penalizes over-dispersed SDs; the observation-level random effect behaves like an added residual-SD parameter that ML/Bayes keeps as small as the data allow.
situations: Want to set scale-by-number-of-predictors priors (the R^2-based Regression and Other Stories rule: coefficient SD = sqrt(R^2_*/p)*sd(y), and sigma ~ Exponential
leaves: The shrinkage is NOT coming from the SD prior's tail; it comes from the normal random-effect density itself: a short-wide normal places less probability density over its quantiles than a tall-skinny o

branches.if_observe: user reconciles via marginal-vs-conditional likelihood framing
branches.then: accept their corrected synthesis: frequentist marginal-likelihood view (concentrate the RE distribution) and Bayesian joint-conditional view both push the SD small; no two-stage step needed

action: Diagnose the centered-coefficient confusion as (a) a tooling artifact and (b) a conditional-interpretation + symmetric-data artifact, and verify by plotting predictions
discriminates: brms-double-centering artifact vs genuine conditional-interpretation change vs symmetric-toy-data coincidence
expected_findings: Predictions coincide across parameterizations; the 'opacity' dissolves once you recognize centered main effects are conditional estimates and the symmetric example happens to zero out the conditional SexF effect — unlikely in real data.
situations: Want to set scale-by-number-of-predictors priors (the R^2-based Regression and Other Stories rule: coefficient SD = sqrt(R^2_*/p)*sd(y), and sigma ~ Exponential

action: Refuse to write code until the intended index/dimensional structure is pinned down; restate the proposal as a concrete declaration and ask the user to confirm what it means

branches.if_observe: User wants only 3 total parameters or j folded into simplex length

action: Inspect the hyperparameter's declared Stan type and its actual R-supplied shape/length against what dirichlet and the array broadcast require
discriminates: H2 (hyperparameter type/shape) vs H1 (array rank): isolates whether the error is the beta argument rather than theta's structure

situations: Hierarchical Dirichlet model in Stan where each unit needs a simplex parameter replicated over two indices (player i x repetition j). User wants theta indexed b

branches.if_observe: Error clears after fixing beta length

action: Compare the loop's index depth to the declared array rank; check what type `theta[i,j]` actually resolves to
discriminates: H3 (over-indexing past the simplex to a real) vs H1 (declared rank doesn't match intended rank): double-indexing a 1-D array of vectors yields a real scalar, which dirichlet rejects
expected_findings: Under H3/H1: `theta_1[i,j]` on a 1-D array of 3-vectors indexes element i (a vector) then element j (a real) — dirichlet gets a real, not a simplex. The fix is to declare theta as a 2-D array `simplex[m] theta[M,K]` so `theta[i,j]` is a vector
situations: Hierarchical Dirichlet model in Stan where each unit needs a simplex parameter replicated over two indices (player i x repetition j). User wants theta indexed b

branches.if_observe: User keeps 1-D theta but stops double-indexing (`theta ~ dirichlet(beta)` vectorized)
branches.then: Also valid when only M simplexes intended — but contradicts the confirmed M x K spec

action: Trace the custom `_lpdf` call's argument types against the function's declared parameter types; identify the rank mismatch introduced when theta gained a dimension

action: Give the concrete Stan signature for array-of-vector arguments and the element-access rule, pointing to the reference manual

action: Score square error in estimating expectations of theta, theta^2, and theta'theta against reference draws/moments, rather than estimating ESS directly
discriminates: genuine-efficiency vs noisy-ESS-artifact; also surfaces per-parameter worst case
expected_findings: Efficient-and-correct sampler: square error drops at the expected rate and reaches ESS>100 on all params in fewer grad evals than NUTS. Biased sampler: low error early but the worst-parameter cost is hidden if you only look at mean ESS.

action: Run the O(1/sqrt(n)) scaling check: on a known distribution, confirm standard error drops as 1/sqrt(n) in number of draws
discriminates: unbiased vs biased; magnitude of the bias floor
expected_findings: Unbiased: monotone 1/sqrt(n) decay. Biased: decay then plateau at a non-zero error level whose height = the bias.

branches.if_observe: error plateaus above reference
branches.then: the method is biased; ESS/square-error metrics overstate quality past the plateau — report the bias floor explicitly rather than only short-horizon accuracy

action: Check the inequality boundary: distinguish strict (>) from inclusive (>=) tail probabilities by the single category mass at y

action: Before reaching for the discrete sampler, test whether the discrete structure can be marginalized out and the model recast as a smooth continuous target.
discriminates: H1 (marginalizable/Rao-Blackwell) vs the need for any genuine discrete sampler (H2-H5).

If it marginalizes, the discrete sampler question is moot.

action: Separate asymptotic consistency from finite-sample practical performance; do not treat a non-crashing run as a correct posterior.
discriminates: H2 (consistent but catastrophically slow/unreliable mixing) vs the assumption that 'it ran => it worked'.
expected_findings: Building an asymptotically-consistent MCMC chain over a discrete/generic space is easy; engineering one that actually mixes and gives useful estimates in finite time on a specific discrete geometry is very hard. Expect chains that look fine but are stuck or biased; the desire for these methods to work makes failures easy to overlook.

action: Reason from the generative (Poisson-process) ideal down to the ICAR discretization
discriminates: Whether the ICAR sum-of-intensities likelihood is a legitimate approximation vs a fundamentally unimplementable object
expected_findings: The exact object is a Poisson process whose latent field is integrated over each observed region; correlations across overlapping regions arise from integrating the SAME field over shared intersections. That can't be implemented because random fields can't be analytically integrated over arbitrary regions. ICAR is the practical discretization: per-observation intensity = sum of the node intensities it covers (lambda_obs = sum exp(alpha_n) on log scale).

action: Code review of the Poisson link and area handling
discriminates: Genuine numerical overflow / mis-link bug vs purely statistical degeneracy
expected_findings: y ~ poisson(A*exp(log_lambda)) exponentiates before summing and risks overflow. Correct form is poisson_log(log_lambda + log(A)) with log_A precomputed in transformed data. But because A is a matrix mapping cells->observations, the aggregation is a log-sum-exp of cell intensities, not a simple offset -> needs explicit per-observation logsumexp over intersecting cells.

action: Pin down the ICAR-node interpretation, tied to the research question
discriminates: Integrated-intensity interpretation (node = integral over its area, no extra area weight, no partial overlaps allowed) vs constant-differential-intensity (node = density, must weight by intersection area, allows partial overlap)
expected_findings: Integrated interpretation is more consistent with the ICAR derivation and is preferred IF a fine-enough grid is achievable (observations fully cover whole nodes). Differential interpretation lets partial overlaps be modeled via intersection areas but the ICAR correlation is invariant to inter-node distance, which is awkward. The research question (population density from land-cover ancillary data over a known 1km grid) implies area/population terms are essential to the inference, so weighting is not a free choice.

branches.if_observe: partial overlaps unavoidable / density is the estimand

action: Make the prior's role explicit: the ICAR encodes the cross-scale extrapolation assumption

expected_findings: Observations are informative JOINTLY, not individually: a strong trend at the observed scale, under the ICAR prior, is extrapolated to the unobserved finer scale (smooth continuation rather than interruption). That joint smoothing is exactly the background information enabling disaggregation; it is what fills the likelihood-flat directions.

expected_findings: If sub-observation trends are assumed ~uniform, the ICAR must be tuned so pairwise correlations are strong among ALL nodes within that scale; otherwise any shorter-range correlation is consistent with every observation and must be carried in the posterior (the degeneracy). Suppressing short-range correlation IS possible with ICAR but requires a subtle configuration not shown in standard demos.

leaves: Convergence fix is not 'just a more informative prior' generically but a deliberate ICAR configuration that strongly correlates within-observation nodes and suppresses unidentified short-range contras

action: Map the spline onto the random-effect / penalized-spline duality and read sds() as a wiggliness-controlling variance
discriminates: whether sds() is a directly-interpretable effect size vs. a hyperparameter governing how much the spline departs from a single smooth function
expected_findings: Under H1: sds() behaves like a group-level SD — large => wiggly, ~0 => near-complete-pooling (one basis function / linear fit suffices). Its raw magnitude (e.g. 302, 0.23) is NOT on the data scale and is not practically interpretable as a quantity.
situations: A brms/mgcv GAM with an s() smooth term has fit cleanly, but the user cannot map the printed summary to anything interpretable: a population-level coefficient l

action: Identify it as the unpenalized null-space basis-function weight, separate from the penalized (random-effect) part

action: Test the naive 'one sds per factor level' hypothesis against the actual count

action: Decompose the fs basis into its constituent penalties (expert structural analysis of the smoother)

action: Establish that the variance is in the marginal-likelihood ESTIMATOR, not in the model fit — re-run the estimator many times on the fixed posterior and summarize the spread.
discriminates: Stochastic estimator noise (H1/H2/H3) vs a deterministic-but-wrong value. Locates the problem at the bridge-sampling layer rather than the posterior/likelihood/prior spec.
expected_findings: Under H1/H2/H3: a wide distribution of BF across reruns (here Min 0.057 to Max 0.85, ~15x), confirming estimator-level noise. Under H4/H5 you would more likely see a stable-but-implausible value, not run-to-run jitter.

branches.if_observe: wide run-to-run spread on FIXED posterior
branches.then: estimator-level problem -> pursue H1/H2/H3 (sample count and bridge convergence), not the model spec

branches.if_observe: stable value that is just surprising/implausible

action: Increase the number of posterior draws by ~an order of magnitude and recompute the Bayes factor; check whether run-to-run spread collapses.
discriminates: H1 (too few posterior samples) vs H3 (irreducible second-level noise that more draws won't fix). This is the cheapest first intervention and the canonical bridgesampling guidance (testing needs >> samples than estimation).
expected_findings: Under H1: spread shrinks dramatically and BF stabilizes to a sensible value (this is what happened). Under H3 (or H2/H5): warnings persist or spread stays large even after 10x–20x samples.

branches.then: H1 confirmed; terminal for the simple case — just use far more draws

branches.if_observe: still unstable / warnings persist even at 20k draws
branches.then: escalate to H2/H3/H5 (this is exactly the tiflo follow-up branch)

action: Quantify bridge-sampler uncertainty at two levels: (a) within a fixed posterior set, use the bridge sampler's built-in error measures (relative MSE, coefficient of variation, percentage error via error_measures()/summary()); (b) across the second level, obtain MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT posterior sample sets and compute a marginal-likelihood estimate from each.
discriminates: First-level (bridge-given-fixed-draws) noise vs second-level (posterior-draw) noise — i.e. H2 vs H3. A single converged estimate with tiny error_measures can still be unstable across independent posterior sets (that is H3).
expected_findings: error_measures() gives relative-MSE / CV / % error for one fixed posterior set; bridge_sampler(..., repetitions=k) gives the spread within one fixed posterior set. The DECISIVE check is multiple independent Stan runs: if marginal-likelihood estimates across independent posterior sets agree relative to the between-model gap, the BF is trustworthy; if not, more posterior draws are needed.
situations: A fitted Bayesian hierarchical model (brms/Stan crossed-effects GLMM) where repeated calls to bayes_factor()/bridge_sampler() on the SAME fitted posterior retur
leaves: Henrik_Singmann: bridgesampling already reports approximate relative MSE (Fruhwirth-Schnatter 2004) via summary()/error_measures(); repetitions= gives within-set spread; but the only real convergence

branches.if_observe: tiny error_measures AND agreement across independent posterior sets (relative to between-model gap)
branches.then: BF is kosher / 'kosher' — report it

branches.if_observe: tiny error_measures within a set BUT disagreement across independent sets
branches.then: H3 — single-run convergence is illusory; increase posterior draws, do not average

action: Test the tail-exploration intuition (H6) and the pooling/prior/identifiability factors before adding more compute: check whether chains are pooled vs counted, whether priors are appropriate for comparison, and whether the model is over-parameterized.
discriminates: Separates a genuine sampling pathology (H6 tails / H5 unidentified maximal model / H4 prior) from plain H1 'need even more draws'. Determines whether throwing samples at it will ever help.
expected_findings: Bridge sampler pools all posterior draws (chain identity does not matter — answers tiflo's combine_models() worry). On priors: marginal likelihood IS prior-sensitive, so weakly-regularizing/default priors that are fine for estimation can be poor for comparison (H4 plausible). On the maximal model under sparse data: variance components revert to their priors -> instability (H5). The tail-specific intuition (H6) is acknowledged as a shared guess by experts but unbacked by data/theory in-thread.
situations: A fitted Bayesian hierarchical model (brms/Stan crossed-effects GLMM) where repeated calls to bayes_factor()/bridge_sampler() on the SAME fitted posterior retur

action: Switch the inferential tool entirely: use LOO cross-validation (ELPD difference + SE) and/or stacking instead of a marginal-likelihood Bayes factor.
discriminates: H7 — whether the BF was even the right instrument. Reframes 'support for the null' as predictive-accuracy comparison, which is far less prior-sensitive and far cheaper than a marginal likelihood.
expected_findings: andrewgelman recommends LOO + stacking over BF outright. For a nested null, compare ELPD between models: a small difference (e.g. < ~2 SE) indicates the null model has essentially the same predictive accuracy as the larger model. BUT LOO has its OWN diagnostics/pathologies — Pareto-k.

action: Recognize the diagonal special case: at i==j the coordinate differences are exactly 0, so the kernel evaluates sqrt(0); rewrite the loop to skip i==j and set the diagonal analytically (sigma + jitter)

action: Build a minimal reprex and inspect the autodiff: print(sqrt(x - x)) in the model block vs same in generated quantities / with literal 0
discriminates: VALUE-finiteness vs GRADIENT-finiteness, and whether merely creating an autodiff node (even unused, e.g. only printed) is enough to break init

Replacing x-x with literal 0 or moving to generated quantities makes it run. Confirms: Stan differentiates every autodiff expression created in the model block regardless of whether it contributes to target, and sqrt has an infinite slope at 0.

But Stan's T[a,] truncation does not accept vector (per-customer) bounds; naive tau ~ Exp(mu) T[tx,] yields gamma_lccdf negative-argument errors because the global bound lets tau < tx. Confirms the explicit-tau route is both wrong and awkward in Stan.

action: Swap the heterogeneity family on the rate parameters from Gamma to lognormal (Gamma chosen only for conjugacy/closed-form, which Stan does not need); keep the same purchase/death likelihood

expected_findings: With lognormal heterogeneity (optionally a multivariate/correlated lognormal via Cholesky factor on log-lambda, log-mu, and log-k for regularity), the model converges where Gamma did not. The likelihood is unchanged; only the heterogeneity distribution changes. This is the resolving move.

action: Reframe the user's intuition: separate (a) density-at-mode from probability MASS over intervals, and (b) the mode from the posterior MEAN, noting left-truncation redistributes mass rightward.
discriminates: H2 (user is reasoning from the wrong quantity) vs H3 (there is a genuine need for a positive-mode/boundary-avoiding prior)
expected_findings: H2: the 'too much weight at zero' worry dissolves — for half-normal, Pr[sigma<1] ~ Pr[sigma>1], the relevant comparison is interval mass not modal density; and the half-normal MEAN is already near 1 because truncation shifts the mean right, so the prior does NOT actually favor zero. H3: even after correctly comparing interval masses, the application demands the bulk of mass strictly away from zero (strong prior knowledge sigma is non-negligible), which a half-normal cannot give.

branches.then: no positive-mode prior needed; keep half-normal/half-t, just scale it — collapse H3

action: Interrogate the provenance/role of boundary-avoiding priors — were they designed as Bayesian priors or as penalties for penalized maximum likelihood?
discriminates: H3 (boundary-avoiding prior is a legitimate Bayesian fix) vs H4 (it is a penalty for pMLE mode-pathology, inappropriate as a default Bayesian prior)
expected_findings: H3: gamma keeps the mode off zero yet (per the wiki) still lets the posterior get arbitrarily close to the data, so density->0 as sigma->0 means it's still 'consistent with zero' and harmless. H4: Chung et al. priors were introduced precisely for pMLE because the posterior MODE under uniform/log-uniform priors lands on the boundary (sharp zero) and under-fits — a mode problem that does NOT arise in full-Bayes; as priors they encode unwarranted strong knowledge that sigma can't be near 0, hurting shrinkage/prediction when effects are truly small.
situations: Using the Stan-recommended default half-normal(0,1) / half-t(4,0,1) prior on the group-level scale (sigma) of a hierarchical (e.g. logistic regression) model, t
leaves: anon75146577 (Daniel Simpson) #15/#17/#18: 'These are bad priors, good penalties; Andrew was sloppy not distinguishing. They say you have strong knowledge SD can't be near 0.' Reason boundary-avoidanc

branches.then: boundary-avoiding penalty is legitimate

branches.if_observe: goal is full-Bayes posterior
branches.then: reject boundary-avoiding prior; prefer a zero-consistent prior with a calibrated upper tail (H4 resolution)

action: Specify the prior by a calibrated tail bound on the standard deviation rather than by its mode, choosing scale relative to the data.
discriminates: principled tail-calibrated prior (H4 resolution) vs ad-hoc mode/center placement (H3)
expected_findings: H4: put the prior on the SD, pick an upper bound U (often 1 if the model is scaled) and calibrate so Pr(SD > U) = 0.05; this stays robust if U is too small (posterior only concentrates above U with strong data evidence). Preferred families: half-t_7 or exponential over half-normal. H3: any fix that just relocates the mode/center leaves you over-influencing the posterior from the prior.

branches.if_observe: cannot rescale

action: Trace the computational/geometry consequence of the positive-constraint log transform under HMC, and ask whether the issue is density at the boundary or probability MASS at the boundary.
discriminates: H5 (the resistance is geometric/computational: unconstrained-space tails + step-size/curvature failure) vs H3 (lognormal/positive-mode prior is fine and the objection is purely statistical)
expected_findings: H5: constraining sigma>0 via log puts the boundary at -inf on the unconstrained scale; a prior whose density SPIKES at sigma=0 puts heavy mass / heavy tails in unconstrained space, and HMC can't find a single step size that works in the tails (slow, and overshoots near zero since gradients only approximate curvature). BUT the log-transform Jacobian actually keeps tails from stretching too far for densities that stay FINITE at the boundary — so a half-normal is fine and only boundary-spiking densities are dangerous; dynamic HMC can even handle genuine heavy tails (Cauchy case study) at higher cost. H3: would predict no special computational penalty, contradicted by the step-size/curvature failure mode.
situations: Using the Stan-recommended default half-normal(0,1) / half-t(4,0,1) prior on the group-level scale (sigma) of a hierarchical (e.g. logistic regression) model, t
leaves: Bob #21/#23 + Betancourt #24: log transform -> boundary at -inf -> numerical trouble for spiking priors; 'can't find a step size that works in the tails, samplers get slow, overshoot near zero.' Betan

branches.if_observe: prior density finite at sigma=0 (half-normal/half-t/exponential)
branches.then: transform is well-behaved; safe

branches.if_observe: prior density spikes at sigma=0 (inv-gamma(eps,eps), some boundary-avoiders)
branches.then: expect heavy unconstrained tails + step-size/curvature failure; rescale instead

action: Diagnose likelihood-data mismatch via posterior predictive check on distribution SHAPE, not just moments

Ad-hoc 'alpha_adhd/alpha_control = right-skewness' ratio from posterior-median alpha/beta is NOT mathematically meaningful and discards uncertainty.

action: inspect the SHAPE and SEMANTICS of the pointwise log_lik object before trusting any loo estimate; read the loo header dimension as a diagnostic
discriminates: coding error in log_lik construction vs genuine model misspecification vs method-inapplicability
expected_findings: If header is N-by-1: log_lik was summed across observations (leave-all-out) -> H1, pure coding bug. If N-by-T but p_loo>>N: either cumulative/double-counted terms (H2) or true misspecification (H3).

action: cross-check the model-block likelihood against the generated-quantities log_lik: do the per-observation terms SUM to the same total log density the model increments, and is each term a single-observation contribution?
discriminates: H2 (cumulative forward-prob terms / model-vs-GQ mismatch, double counting) vs H3 (real misspecification)
expected_findings: Under H2: model block uses only logalpha[T] once (the full marginal likelihood), but GQ sets log_lik[t]=log_sum_exp(logalpha[t,]) for every t; since logalpha[t,] = log p(y_{1:t}, z_t) is cumulative, summing log_lik over t counts y_1 ~T times, y_2 ~T-1 times, etc. -> the implied 'total' is hugely larger than target -> inflated p_loo and degenerate importance ratios -> all Pareto-k > 1. Clean Rhat/ESS rules out a sampling problem and points squarely at the log_lik definition.
situations: A Bayesian HMM / Markov-switching VAR fit in Stan converges fine (good Rhat/ESS, posterior means match the simulated truth), yet loo() returns nonsense: p_loo i
leaves: Aki (final): 'in the model you include only the last logalpha; in generated quantities you include all elements of logalpha, so there is a mismatch and the resulting loo computation is wrong... why is

branches.if_observe: terms are cumulative forward probs (logalpha) -> per-t differences needed
branches.then: the correct per-observation conditional density is the INCREMENT log p(y_t | y_{1:t-1}) = log_sum_exp(logalpha[t,]) - log_sum_exp(logalpha[t-1,]), not log_sum_exp(logalpha[t,]) itself

branches.if_observe: p_loo plausible (< N) and only a few high Pareto-k

action: separate 'is loo applicable here' from 'is the log_lik coded correctly'; check the actual requirement loo imposes and the provenance of the cited claim
discriminates: H4 (method-inapplicability) vs H1/H2 (user-side coding), and reliable-source vs LLM-hallucination
expected_findings: loo is agnostic to where log-liks come from; it needs CONDITIONAL independence of observations given parameters at the chosen factorization level, not the package being HMM-aware. The marginalized HMM CAN be cross-validated, but the unit of factorization must be chosen deliberately (e.g. whole-series, or independent units). The ChatGPT Pareto-k explanation is unreliable: thin training material + post-cutoff guideline changes -> read loo docs / PSIS paper instead.

action: compare the user's factorization unit against the level at which observations are conditionally independent
discriminates: correct CV unit (independent units) vs incorrect CV unit (within-series time steps of one dependent series)
expected_findings: In ecological HMMs there are MANY independent capture-histories -> forward algorithm run per individual, log_lik incremented once at the final occasion per individual; observations factorize across individuals. The user's economic series is ONE individual over T correlated times -> incrementing a separate log_lik at every t treats correlated time points as if independent, which is the wrong factorization (analogous to a per-survey-occasion log_lik in mark-recapture).

action: audit whether the chosen information criterion is even reliable here, and whether 'richer model fits better' is a red flag or expected
discriminates: DIC trustworthy vs unreliable; genuine long-range dynamics vs overfitting/instability vs label-switching artifacts
expected_findings: If WAIC/PSIS-LOO warn (high Pareto-k), DIC is equally unreliable and typically ships WITHOUT a warning, so it gives false confidence. 'Longer lag lower AIC/BIC' is the same instability signature the user saw in plain VAR. Richer models legitimately can fit better given enough data; priors that work in both low- and high-data regimes are the hard part. Genuine (non-label-switching) multimodality of the regime-independent VAR coefs tracks how informative/short the data is.

action: Reconcile the conditional-given-w model against the marginal (w-integrated) model and check whether the same covariance is entering twice
discriminates: DOUBLE-COUNTING the random-effect covariance (sampling w AND adding Sigma_w into the noise variance) vs a correctly specified single-source variance
expected_findings: If you SAMPLE w, the likelihood for X given w must use only the residual variance sigma^2 -> normal(Mu, sig). The full Sig (sig1^2 + log(t)(2*rho*sig1*sig2 + sig2^2*log(t)) + sig^2) is the MARGINAL variance after integrating w OUT -- valid only if you do NOT also sample w. Using Sig while also sampling w injects Sigma_w twice, inflating variance; the data compensate by driving sig toward ~sig^2 and rho to nonsense. Original working code used 'sig' (conditional); the 'fix' to 'Sig' introduced the double count.
situations: A hierarchical mixed-effects + survival model (Weibull failure-time block plus a Gaussian covariate-process block with unit-level random effects w_i ~ N(0, Sigm
leaves: bbbales2: 'Is the covariance of w being included twice? Either sample w with N(eta+w0+w1*log t, sigma^2), OR integrate w out and use N(eta, something+sigma^2) -- not both.' OP: original code used norm

branches.if_observe: conditional model (Mu, sig) recovers truth
branches.then: RESOLVED -- double-counting confirmed; keep w sampled with residual-only variance

branches.if_observe: want to drop the per-unit w
branches.then: use the marginal form N(eta, full Sig) and do NOT sample w

action: Marginalize (integrate out) the group-specific intercepts from the likelihood instead of sampling them

For non-Gaussian GLMs no closed form — must use integrate_1d numerically. brms cannot do this automatically -> requires hand-written Stan.

action: Separate basis-construction cost from sampling cost and pick the basis type accordingly
discriminates: setup-bound (basis build/eigendecomp) vs sampling-bound (coefficient count + geometry) — they call for different fixes
expected_findings: Default low-rank TPRS (bs='tp') builds the full basis and partially eigen-decomposes it (first k eigenvectors; capped at 2000 unique knots when n_u>2000), which is COSTLY to set up at large n. Switching to bs='cr' (cubic regression spline) makes SETUP far cheaper with near-equivalent fit. But SAMPLING time is roughly the SAME for tp vs cr once the basis exists; sampling time is driven by k (coefficient count), not basis type.

expected_findings: H1/H2: per-individual log-lik and total target print as FINITE numbers, yet gradient is non-finite -> isolates problem to autodiff through log(0) terms. H4: a print reveals -inf/NaN for some individual -> a data/structure bug instead.
situations: A discrete-state HMM / multi-state capture-recapture (multi-event) model in Stan, with the forward algorithm coded on the log scale (log_sum_exp over gamma[t-1,
leaves: set diagnostic_file argument in stan(); add `print(...)` for log_sum_exp(gamma[T]) and for theta/phi just before `target += log_sum_exp(gamma[T]);` to check each individual contributes finite mass

branches.if_observe: value finite everywhere but gradient still non-finite
branches.then: commit to the log(0)-gradient hypothesis (H1/H2); the value path is fine, the derivative path is poisoned

branches.if_observe: some individual prints -inf/NaN
branches.then: value-level structural bug: check simulated capture histories / impossible observation under phi

action: Attribute the infinite gradient to differentiating through log(0) / 0*log(0) terms (cross-check against known Stan-math behavior)
discriminates: whether the issue is the generic 'derivative of log(p) unbounded as p->0' (H1) vs the specific multiply_log(0,0)/0*log(0) infinite-gradient bug (H2)
expected_findings: matches Stan-math issue #2494 (multiply_log(0,0) / poisson_lpmf(0|lambda=0) has infinite gradient): Stan evaluates 0*log(0) value correctly but the gradient is infinite. Confirms the symptom is the derivative, not the value.
situations: A discrete-state HMM / multi-state capture-recapture (multi-event) model in Stan, with the forward algorithm coded on the log scale (log_sum_exp over gamma[t-1,
leaves: reference stan-dev/math #2494 'multiply_log(0,0) has infinite gradient'; note derivative of log(p) is unbounded as p->0+, so any -inf log-prob feeding autodiff contaminates the gradient

branches.if_observe: structural zeros are the only source of log(0)
branches.then: fix = keep those zeros OUT of the differentiated log path (scale change, delta-regularization, or skip them entirely)

action: Regularize the structural zeros away (replace 0 entries in theta/phi with a tiny positive delta, renormalizing rows) and re-run
discriminates: confirms zeros are causal (if it now samples) vs some other gradient source
expected_findings: H1/H2: model now initializes and samples; recovers simulated parameters -> confirms structural zeros caused the infinite gradient. Inelegant but diagnostic.
situations: A discrete-state HMM / multi-state capture-recapture (multi-event) model in Stan, with the forward algorithm coded on the log scale (log_sum_exp over gamma[t-1,
leaves: delta = 1e-15; theta[2]=[delta,(1-delta)*psi2,(1-delta)*(1-psi2)]'; etc. (Reuben's fix); fixes symptom in cmdstan-2.28.1

branches.if_observe: samples cleanly after delta-regularization
branches.then: structural zeros confirmed as cause; choose a cleaner permanent fix (probability scale or skip-zeros)

branches.if_observe: still non-finite gradient
branches.then: look for OTHER log(0): a boundary parameter (H3) or an impossible observation given the data

action: Move the forward recursion to the PROBABILITY scale (store marginal probs in gamma, multiply, take log only once at the final target increment)
discriminates: whether log-scale was actually necessary; trades underflow-robustness for avoiding log(0) in the autodiff path
expected_findings: model samples cleanly and recovers parameters; no log(0) ever enters the differentiated path because logs are taken only at the very end. Valid when (factor)^T stays above ~1e-308, i.e. small T and not-tiny probabilities (rough rule: factors >=~0.04 and T<=~100).
situations: A discrete-state HMM / multi-state capture-recapture (multi-event) model in Stan, with the forward algorithm coded on the log scale (log_sum_exp over gamma[t-1,
leaves: gamma holds marginal probabilities; acc[j]=gamma[t-1,j]*theta[j,k]*phi[k,y]; gamma[t,k]=sum(acc); finally target += log(sum(gamma[T])). See BPA js_ms.stan; Fujiwara & Caswell (2002) matrix-multiply fo

branches.then: cannot stay on probability scale; need a log-scale method that skips the zero terms

branches.if_observe: T small, no underflow
branches.then: probability-scale forward algorithm is the simplest correct fix (accepted answer)

action: Keep log scale but exclude the impossible (log(0)) entries from the computation entirely via sparse log-domain matmul or control-flow special-casing

expected_findings: H1/H2: with zero-probability transitions/states never stored or summed, Stan samples with no infinite-gradient complaint and recovers parameters; code is far less readable than the dense version.

action: Add boundary-avoiding priors and/or narrow the [0,1] constraints away from the endpoints

expected_findings: H3: beta(2,2)-type priors (density vanishing at 0 and 1) or real<lower=0.01,upper=0.99> pulls params off the boundary and removes the infinite gradient. If it persists, the boundary was not the cause -> structural zeros (H1/H2).

branches.then: not a boundary problem; return to structural-zero fixes (probability scale / skip-zeros)

action: Reframe: separate the deterministic map from the observational model; identify where stochasticity actually enters
discriminates: whether the problem is 'no likelihood exists' vs 'observational model is just unspecified'
expected_findings: Root-finding is deterministic given the current model configuration theta; it gains a connection to data only once an observational model is declared. The root being 'random' is just an artifact of theta being uncertain — nothing is random once theta is fixed.

branches.then: pi(y|root)=delta(y-root): singular, HMC cannot handle — must reparameterize (H3) or smooth

action: Diagnose the singularity geometry of the delta likelihood for HMC
discriminates: H1 (truly hopeless) vs H2/H3 (smooth or reparameterize)
expected_findings: Posterior density is non-smooth: gradients are zero on the vast inconsistent region (no information to find the consistent sliver) and infinite transverse to the sliver — this breaks HMC. Adding tiny noise regularizes gradients but forces tiny step sizes and long trajectories (costly).
situations: A generative model defines the observed data implicitly: an equation F(t; params)=const is solved (via bisection / Newton root-finder) for a quantity t that is
leaves: delta(y-f(x;theta)) is zero wherever y != f(x;theta); a thin normal smooths it but at high sampling cost. Sets up reparameterization as the productive route.

branches.then: reparameterize onto the consistent set (H3)

branches.if_observe: accept cost / need genuine measurement model

action: Evaluate the narrow-noise hack and look for a cheaper exact alternative by exploiting invertibility structure
discriminates: H2 (works but slow / needs forward solve every leapfrog) vs H3 (exact, no forward solve)
expected_findings: Narrow-sigma target samples very slowly. KEY structural observation: although solving for t needs iteration, the equation 1 = A_3·t·f(t,A_1,A_2) is trivially invertible for A_3 given an observed t: A_3* = 1/(t·f(t,A_1,A_2)). So treat the OBSERVED root as fixing the easy parameter and apply change-of-variables.
situations: A generative model defines the observed data implicitly: an equation F(t; params)=const is solved (via bisection / Newton root-finder) for a quantity t that is
leaves: nhuurre: pick the parameter the equation solves for in closed form (A_3); the per-observation A_3 is then a transformed parameter computed directly from observed rt, not sampled with a forward root-fi

branches.if_observe: equation invertible for some parameter given t
branches.then: use change-of-variables / Jacobian likelihood (H3)

branches.if_observe: not invertible for any parameter
branches.then: fall back to narrow measurement noise (H2)

action: Treat failure as a Jacobian/derivative bug; recompute |dA_3/dt| carefully and re-test recovery
discriminates: H4 (chain-rule error in the Jacobian) vs a flaw in the change-of-variables method itself
expected_findings: The method is sound; the derivative was wrong — multi-step chain rule dropped terms. Common concrete error: substituting inv_logit(-x) as the derivative of inv_logit(x) when the correct derivative is inv_logit(x)·inv_logit(-x). Fixing the Jacobian makes recovery work.
situations: A generative model defines the observed data implicitly: an equation F(t; params)=const is solved (via bisection / Newton root-finder) for a quantity t that is
leaves: Recompute via dA_3/dt = (f - t·df/dt)/(t²f²), simplify with t·A_3·f=1; df/dt by chain+product rule with h'(x)=h(x)h(-x). Wrong vs right Jacobian numerically differ and only the corrected one recovers

branches.if_observe: corrected Jacobian -> recovery succeeds
branches.then: terminal: working integrated/transformed-parameter likelihood

branches.if_observe: still fails
branches.then: check sign/abs of Jacobian and the parameter chosen for inversion

action: Formalize: rewrite the singular delta observation as a delta on the consistent parameter set with the inverse-Jacobian weight
discriminates: whether nhuurre's method is ad hoc vs the canonical reparameterization of a deterministic observation
expected_findings: delta(y - f(x;theta)) = delta(theta - f^{-1}(y;x)) · |∂f/∂theta|^{-1}; by the inverse-function rule |∂f^{-1}/∂y| = |∂f/∂theta|^{-1}, so this is exactly density(t)=p(implied param)·|Jacobian|. It is just Bayesian inference / standard change of variables — 'directly parameterizing the set theta=f^{-1}(y;x) instead of hoping HMC finds it.' ABC is the same object with a finite tolerance (approximate model).

action: Re-derive the change of variables treating the simplex as an (N-1)-dimensional manifold embedded in N-space, not as a square map
discriminates: User's square N×N diagonal Jacobian (wrong, ignores the sum-to-one constraint) vs. the correct rectangular N×(N-1) map requiring a Gram-determinant volume adjustment
expected_findings: Because only N-1 coordinates are free (x_N = 1 - sum of the rest), the Jacobian is N×(N-1); the volume factor is the square-root Gram determinant sqrt(det(J^T J)) = sqrt(N)*prod x_j. Expanding the Dirichlet density in z and combining the (alpha_N - 1)*log(x_N) term with the volume term produces alpha_N*log x_N - log x_N, i.e. the dot_product(alpha, log_theta) absorbs alpha_N*log_theta[N] and leaves exactly the residual -log_theta[N].
situations: A custom log-simplex transform (append-0-then-log-softmax: pin the last/reference element to 0, then subtract log_sum_exp to normalize on the log scale) is used

branches.then: point out the missing sum-to-one constraint; redo with N×(N-1) Gram determinant

action: Distinguish the two separate change-of-variables steps by reasoning about base measures and offer a CDF-based intuition
discriminates: One combined adjustment vs. two distinct adjustments (unconstrained->constrained, and simplex-density->log-simplex-density), each relative to a different base measure
expected_findings: Stan samples on unconstrained Euclidean (Lebesgue) space; one Jacobian maps unconstrained->constrained simplex, a separate one converts the simplex distribution to its log-simplex form. The terms cancel only because the same log_theta[N] appears in both with opposite sign. The CDF view (Modrak: differentiate F_Y = F_X(f^{-1}) via chain rule) shows the Jacobian is just the derivative, not magic; symmetry of which element is pinned is irrelevant to whether the constraint term appears.

action: Inspect what the likelihood is actually conditioning on vs what the generative/selection process produces
discriminates: H1 (conditional-likelihood bug) vs H2 (prior) vs H4 (unidentifiable)
expected_findings: Under H1: the Stan model only ever sees published studies and only adds log(delta) — it never sees or penalizes the suppressed Z=0 studies, so the likelihood is monotone increasing in delta and is maximized at delta=1 regardless of prior. Under H2: changing prior would move delta, but it does not. Under H4: even a correct likelihood would be weakly identified.

action: Reduce to the minimal analogue — estimate a Bernoulli rate p after discarding all the zeros
discriminates: H1 (structural conditional-likelihood error) vs H2/H3 (fixable by prior/selection-function form)
expected_findings: If you fit y~bernoulli(p) but first throw out every y=0 and show the model only the y=1 cases, p is driven to ~1 by construction. This is isomorphic to the publication-bias model: discarding Z=0 (non-published) and showing only Z=1 forces delta->1. The unconditional likelihood (including the y=0 / Z=0 cases) is required to recover the true rate; as data->infinity the conditional fit goes to 1, not the truth.

action: Switch to a selection-model formulation: divide the per-study likelihood by the selection probability P(Z=1) (the normalizing constant of the truncated/weighted distribution)
discriminates: H1 resolution path — proper normalized selection likelihood vs the naive log(delta) increment
expected_findings: The correct conditional density is f(TE|theta,se)*P(Z=1|t)/P(Z=1). P(Z=1) integrates the publication weights over the predictive distribution of the t-stat for that study — computed via normal_lcdf over the (-1.96,1.96) and tail regions. With the normalizer present, over-stating delta now costs likelihood, so delta becomes identifiable.

action: Audit how the normalization constant is computed and applied across observations
discriminates: H5 — global/double-counted normalizer vs correct per-study normalizer
expected_findings: Two failure modes: (a) computing a per-obs normalizer but then summing into ONE global constant and subtracting it in every term -> applies the correction N times (over-correction); (b) using a single global normalizer at all. Correct: the normalizer is STUDY-SPECIFIC because P(Z=1) depends on that study's se (a very precise study with non-zero true effect is almost surely significant -> negligible selection; an imprecise study spans all three significance regions). Subtract log_normalization_individual[i] per observation i.

action: Characterize the model output: ask for a plot/run of the ODE solution and the dynamics it exhibits (wiggly? flat? range?), plus the noise scale in the data
discriminates: Smooth well-behaved dynamics (-> geometry/prior problem) vs sharp abrupt features tracking a forcing function (-> integrator/discontinuity problem)
expected_findings: Solution shows big abrupt wiggles/bumps driven by a cyclic step-like voltage; noise is small relative to signal -> flags forcing discontinuities, not a tail/prior issue
situations: A Stan/HMC (NUTS) fit of an ODE model driven by a step-like or cyclically-varying data-supplied forcing function (e.g. applied voltage, dosing, intake events) s
leaves: bbbales2 asks 'what dynamics does the ODE exhibit'; Ben sends fake data + a plot of the driving current showing 'quite abrupt changes'

branches.if_observe: smooth, slowly varying solution
branches.then: pivot to geometry/prior hypotheses (sigma, conditioning, reparameterization)

branches.if_observe: sharp jumps/bumps from a step-like forcing input
branches.then: suspect the integrator is stepping over discontinuities; go to event-resolution checks

action: Inspect and stress the observation-noise prior (rescale toward the true residual scale; replace a sigma->0 piling prior with one that barriers away from zero, e.g. gamma)
discriminates: Whether stuck chains are a sigma-driven local-optimum / scale-mismatch artifact vs something deeper in the forward model
expected_findings: If sigma were the cause, correcting the prior frees the chains; here it does NOT -> sigma misspecification is real but not the blocker
situations: A Stan/HMC (NUTS) fit of an ODE model driven by a step-like or cyclically-varying data-supplied forcing function (e.g. applied voltage, dosing, intake events) s
leaves: dlakelan flags normal(1,0.1) (likely meant normal(0,0.1)) and suggests gamma(3,3/0.1) to barrier sigma away from 0; Ben tries the variants -> 'didn't make much difference, chains still not mixing'

branches.if_observe: chains start mixing after sigma fix
branches.then: resolved: scale/local-optimum artifact

branches.if_observe: still stuck
branches.then: eliminate sigma; move to likelihood-code and forward-model checks

action: Audit the likelihood code for a vectorization/summation bug (does normal_lpdf over vectors sum all data points or drop them?)
discriminates: A silent code bug producing a degenerate/constant target vs a correct likelihood with a pathological surface
expected_findings: Confirm _lpdf returns a scalar summed internally over all components -> the likelihood code is correct, so the pathology is in the forward solve, not the scoring
situations: A Stan/HMC (NUTS) fit of an ODE model driven by a step-like or cyclically-varying data-supplied forcing function (e.g. applied voltage, dosing, intake events) s
leaves: dlakelan questions normal_lpdf(I|I_int,sigma) on vectors; betanalpha/Bob confirm lpdf always returns a scalar with summation internal -> code is fine; Bob also advises 'test those functions to make su

branches.if_observe: lpdf only scored one element
branches.then: fix vectorization, re-run

branches.if_observe: lpdf correctly sums
branches.then: rule out code bug; look at sampler diagnostics and forward-model fidelity

action: Run standard HMC adaptation diagnostics and a cross-sampler check: inspect diagonal mass matrix + stepsize consistency across chains, do pairs plots for funnels/banana/high correlation, AND fit the same model with a gradient-free sampler (Metropolis / adaptive covariance)
discriminates: Ill-conditioned geometry that needs reparameterization (HMC-specific) vs a non-smooth target that breaks gradients specifically (HMC fails where gradient-free MH succeeds)
expected_findings: If geometry: pairs plots show strong correlations/funnel and reparameterizing to unit scale helps. Here MH mixes and converges on the SAME model while NUTS stays stuck -> the problem is gradient/smoothness-specific, pointing at a bumpy likelihood from the forward model, not mere conditioning
situations: A Stan/HMC (NUTS) fit of an ODE model driven by a step-like or cyclically-varying data-supplied forcing function (e.g. applied voltage, dosing, intake events) s
leaves: Bob lists adapt-longer / mass-matrix-and-stepsize consistency / pairs-plot / reparameterize-to-unit-scale; Ben reports MH with adaptive covariance DOES mix (with strong posterior covariances) but NUTS

branches.if_observe: both samplers stuck and pairs show funnel/correlation
branches.then: reparameterize / non-centered, tighten conditioning

branches.if_observe: MH mixes but NUTS frozen
branches.then: the target is non-smooth for HMC's gradients -> investigate the forward ODE solve for discontinuities

action: Diagnose the integrator's interaction with the discontinuities: log the times at which the RHS/derivatives are actually evaluated and compare to the forcing-event times; sanity-check by adding a known integral (e.g. integral of the forcing) to the output
discriminates: Integrator silently interpolating over jumps (events missed -> bumpy likelihood) vs integrator correctly resolving every event
expected_findings: Derivative evaluations are widely spaced in smooth regions; some forcing events fall between evaluations and are skipped, so bumps appear/disappear intermittently; whether a bump is captured flips with tiny parameter changes -> jumps in MSE/likelihood at nearby parameter values (a bumpy surface)
situations: A Stan/HMC (NUTS) fit of an ODE model driven by a step-like or cyclically-varying data-supplied forcing function (e.g. applied voltage, dosing, intake events) s
leaves: wds15: 'discontinuities of your forcing function can be a problem for the ode integrator... I have seen cases where the integrator would step over those jumps'; Robert_Dodier later confirms by printin

branches.if_observe: all events evaluated, solution stable
branches.then: discontinuity is not the cause; revisit conditioning/reparameterization

branches.if_observe: events skipped / bumps intermittently missing
branches.then: force event resolution: restart integration at each discontinuity (preferred) or include before/at/after-event time points in the output / smear events with tight tolerances

action: Make the integrator 'see' every event: restart the ODE solve at each discontinuity (call the solver once per inter-event interval, chaining the state as the new initial condition), or as a cheaper alternative emit output around each event / widen the event into a smooth delta with tight tolerances
discriminates: Restart-per-interval (guaranteed but possible perf cost, larger when t0 state is data not a parameter) vs single big solve with extra event-bracketing output (cheaper but not guaranteed unless RHS truly evaluated there)
expected_findings: With the integrator restarted at each event, every forcing event is captured, the likelihood becomes smooth in the parameters, gradients are well-defined, and the NUTS chains move and mix
situations: A Stan/HMC (NUTS) fit of an ODE model driven by a step-like or cyclically-varying data-supplied forcing function (e.g. applied voltage, dosing, intake events) s
leaves: wds15: 'integrate step-wise through the system... preferred solution for step-function inputs'; Robert_Dodier switches from one solve over 1152 intervals to 1152 single-step solves restarting at each

branches.if_observe: chains now mix after restart-per-event
branches.then: resolved; consider cvodes restart or ode_ckrk to cut the per-interval performance penalty

branches.if_observe: still stuck after bracketing-only output
branches.then: bracketing did not force RHS evaluation; switch to true restart-at-discontinuity

action: Reach for the existing tool that concatenates posterior draws across fitted models
discriminates: whether a software path even exists (H1 feasibility) vs needing a bespoke joint model
expected_findings: combine_models() merges posterior samples of multiple brmsfit objects; brm_multiple already does this pattern for multiple imputation — so the mechanical path exists under an equal-likelihood assumption
situations: Have a posterior sample of N phylogenetic trees (e.g. 100 from BEAST/revBayes) and want to carry the phylogenetic uncertainty into a downstream Bayesian multile
leaves: combine_models() to pool chains; brm_multiple is the analogous multiple-imputation merge; cov_ranef must be set per-fit (cannot pass a list of trees to brm_multiple)

branches.if_observe: trees enter via cov_ranef (covariance), not via data=
branches.then: brm_multiple won't help; use an explicit for-loop fitting one model per tree then combine_models

action: State the equality to be checked precisely as a probability identity and pressure-test it
discriminates: H1 (pooling == marginalization) vs H2 (pooling != marginalization in general)
expected_findings: Under the assumption all trees are EQUALLY LIKELY, pooling the per-tree chains is a Monte Carlo estimate of the tree-marginalized posterior (this is exactly the multiple-imputation justification); identity holds only conditional on that equal-weight assumption, so H2's worry collapses into a weighting question, not a structural impossibility
situations: Have a posterior sample of N phylogenetic trees (e.g. 100 from BEAST/revBayes) and want to carry the phylogenetic uncertainty into a downstream Bayesian multile
leaves: p(params|data) = sum_i w_i p(params|data,tree_i); with equal w_i and trees sampled proportional to probability, concatenation approximates the mixture; accuracy degrades if per-tree posteriors differ

branches.if_observe: trees NOT equally likely
branches.then: move to explicit weighting — H4

branches.if_observe: per-tree posteriors carry very different information / mix differently
branches.then: pooled draws are biased (H3); no formal analysis available

action: Ask where weights would come from and whether the upstream sampler already supplies them
discriminates: H1/H4-benign (BEAST sampling makes equal weights correct) vs H4-problematic (need explicit weights) vs H5 (need marginal-likelihood weights)
expected_findings: Because BEAST already samples trees in proportion to their probability, equal-weighting the drawn trees is approximately the right importance for THAT sample; posterior_average can take explicit weights if needed; but with only ~100 trees the sample poorly reflects the true tree posterior
situations: Have a posterior sample of N phylogenetic trees (e.g. 100 from BEAST/revBayes) and want to carry the phylogenetic uncertainty into a downstream Bayesian multile
leaves: posterior_average supports weights; combine_models does not; equal weights are the implicit-importance interpretation of a probability-proportional tree sample

branches.if_observe: user subsamples a larger tree posterior
branches.then: no obvious way to reweight the subsample sensibly

action: Identify the exact quantity that makes the marginal posterior consistent and assess its computability
discriminates: H4 (any reasonable weights suffice) vs H5 (only marginal-likelihood-per-topology weights are consistent, and they are intractable)
expected_findings: Consistency requires weighting each topology by the marginal likelihood conditioned on that topology; these marginal likelihoods are extremely hard to compute from MCMC samples alone — effectively no current technology computes them accurately; so the clean Bayesian answer is presently unattainable
situations: Have a posterior sample of N phylogenetic trees (e.g. 100 from BEAST/revBayes) and want to carry the phylogenetic uncertainty into a downstream Bayesian multile
leaves: marginal likelihood per topology is the correct weight; not recoverable from within-topology MCMC draws; the equal-weight pooling is therefore an approximation whose error is uncharacterized

branches.if_observe: user only needs phylogenetic-uncertainty propagation for a manuscript, not a provably consistent posterior
branches.then: accept equal-weight pooling as 'reflects the uncertainty in this tree sample' and document the caveat — terminal pragmatic path

action: Question representativeness of the upstream topology sample and its sensitivity to initialization
discriminates: H6 (tree posterior is biased by sampler getting stuck) vs assumption that BEAST/revBayes give a representative topology posterior
expected_findings: Between-topology mixing is the hard part; samplers tend to stay near the initialized topology, making the initial clades look representative when they are not; effective priors over topologies are often more diffuse than the random-tree initializations, so multiple random-start chains can all collapse into the same basin and falsely agree
situations: Have a posterior sample of N phylogenetic trees (e.g. 100 from BEAST/revBayes) and want to carry the phylogenetic uncertainty into a downstream Bayesian multile
leaves: within-topology sampling is straightforward; between-topology moves obstruct everything; multi-start convergence checks are only as good as how diffuse the initial trees are relative to the topology p

branches.if_observe: biologist's question is clade-level ('is this split in most trees?')
branches.then: the full-distribution problem is partly sidestepped; but comparative VCV models need the whole tree, so this escape doesn't apply here

branches.if_observe: want full-distribution uncertainty quantification
branches.then: needs accurate Frechet means in tree space — theory too young/inapplicable today

action: Frame as importance sampling: old posterior = proposal, new posterior = target; check tail/coverage condition
discriminates: whether IS is in-regime (proposal covers target) vs out-of-regime (target has mass where proposal is thin)
expected_findings: H(covers): adding data usually TIGHTENS posterior, so old (broader) posterior has heavier tails and concentrates into a high-old-mass region where draws are plentiful -> IS valid. H(informative new data): if new data pulls mass to a poorly-sampled region, proposal undercovers -> IS unreliable.
situations: Have a fitted Bayesian posterior (MCMC draws from old data) and a modest batch of NEW data; want the updated posterior without refitting the whole model from sc
leaves: Old posterior as proposal has more tail mass than the tightened new-data target; the operative quantity is movement of posterior mass, not the data-size ratio.

branches.if_observe: new data only mildly informative / posterior barely moves
branches.then: IS works well; but note if it barely moves you may not even need to update

branches.if_observe: new data strongly shifts/concentrates mass into a thinly-sampled region
branches.then: expect IS failure; refit or fall back to MCMC

action: Resample draws with replacement using the importance weights; choose a low-variance resampling algorithm
discriminates: naive multinomial resampling (extra variance) vs Kitagawa-1996 stratified resampling (variance-reduced)
expected_findings: Multinomial inflates Monte Carlo variance of the resampled posterior; stratified resampling preserves more information. Resampling unavoidably loses info vs weighted draws but is needed for standard tooling.
situations: Have a fitted Bayesian posterior (MCMC draws from old data) and a modest batch of NEW data; want the updated posterior without refitting the whole model from sc
leaves: Use posterior::resample_draws() implementing Kitagawa (1996) stratified resampling; weighted-draws support in posterior is an alternative that avoids the resampling info loss.

branches.if_observe: weighted-draw tooling available
branches.then: prefer weighting to avoid resampling information loss

branches.if_observe: must use standard brms summary/post-processing
branches.then: resample, but stratified not multinomial

action: Resample independently within each chain so Rhat remains a valid post-update diagnostic
discriminates: resampling that destroys chain structure (Rhat uninformative) vs per-chain resampling (Rhat still diagnoses failure)
expected_findings: Per-chain resampling keeps chains separable: if reweighted chains disagree, Rhat/standard convergence warnings fire. ESS becomes slightly wrong (repeated draws partly break autocorrelation), but when IS fails the Rhat component inside ESS dominates anyway, so ESS still flags it.
situations: Have a fitted Bayesian posterior (MCMC draws from old data) and a modest batch of NEW data; want the updated posterior without refitting the whole model from sc
leaves: Stratify/resample per chain; Rhat retained as an independent breakdown check; ESS slightly biased but Rhat-driven failure dominates.

branches.if_observe: per-chain resampled chains disagree (high Rhat)
branches.then: treat update as failed; refit

branches.if_observe: chains agree
branches.then: update plausibly OK, cross-check against Pareto-k

action: Cross-check the PSIS Pareto-k diagnostic against the MCMC convergence (Rhat) warnings
discriminates: whether IS-validity and convergence diagnostics give consistent vs conflicting verdicts on the update
expected_findings: Empirically (small experimentation) convergence warnings begin at roughly the same point as Pareto-k warnings — the two signals are concordant and partially redundant; the Rhat-inside-ESS term dominates once IS starts failing.
situations: Have a fitted Bayesian posterior (MCMC draws from old data) and a modest batch of NEW data; want the updated posterior without refitting the whole model from sc
leaves: Pareto-k and Rhat warnings co-onset; concordance gives confidence the breakdown point is real rather than a diagnostic artifact.

branches.if_observe: both clean
branches.then: accept update

branches.if_observe: either warns
branches.then: distrust update / refit

action: Audit the draw extract/resample/reinsert plumbing against the original fit
discriminates: correct reinsertion of resampled post-warmup draws vs a recombination bug when warmup draws are present
expected_findings: A bug existed in splitting/recombining warmup and resampled post-warmup draws; after the fix the resampled fit object behaves like the original.
situations: Have a fitted Bayesian posterior (MCMC draws from old data) and a modest batch of NEW data; want the updated posterior without refitting the whole model from sc
leaves: Bug was in separating then re-combining warmup and resampled post-warmup draws when the stanfit retains warmup; fixed so the rebuilt fit is manipulable like the original.

branches.if_observe: fit object misbehaves / values mismatched
branches.then: check warmup/post-warmup split-and-recombine logic

action: Decompose the design space by WHAT each varying component is allowed to change in the latent ordinal model (offset of latent distribution vs. shape/spacing of the cut points)
discriminates: H1 (vary offset only) vs H2/H3 (vary full cutpoint structure) — i.e. coherent global probability shift vs richer per-category probability variation
expected_findings: Varying phi/anchor (or beta*x) only shifts the latent logistic/normal location, so all category probabilities move together coherently; varying the cut points themselves gives different latent distributions and a richer family of probability variation. Choice depends on whether per-category heterogeneity is needed.
situations: Hierarchical ordered/ordinal (multinomial probit or logit) regression where the interior cut points must vary by group, but a naive group-varying cut point vect
leaves: phi = beta*x is the general offset; 'anchor point set to zero' in the case study is just an identification choice, not a free hierarchical knob for richer variation

branches.if_observe: only a coherent global shift of probabilities is needed
branches.then: vary the offset/anchor phi_j ~ N(0, sigma_phi) per group (H1) — simplest

branches.if_observe: need different per-category probability spacing across groups
branches.then: must vary the cut point vector itself -> go to constraint-respecting reparameterization (H2/H3)

action: Reparameterize the constrained ordered vector to an unconstrained space and put the population model there
discriminates: H2 (unconstrained leftmost+log-diff deltas with independent normals) vs H3 (induced-Dirichlet population model)
expected_findings: Map c -> delta = (leftmost point, log differences to next points); this is exactly how Stan's ordered vector works internally. Independent normal population models on each delta keep ordering automatically but impose a subtle asymmetry across cut points (the leftmost point and the log-spacings are modeled differently, so the model is not symmetric in the cut points).
situations: Hierarchical ordered/ordinal (multinomial probit or logit) regression where the interior cut points must vary by group, but a naive group-varying cut point vect
leaves: delta_{n,k} ~ normal(mu_n, tau_n) independently on the unconstrained log-difference space; ordering guaranteed by construction; asymmetry is the known cost

branches.if_observe: the cross-cutpoint asymmetry is acceptable and informative priors are not critical
branches.then: use H2 (log-difference reparameterization + independent normals)

branches.if_observe: want a symmetric, self-consistent prior that is easy to make informative on the probability scale
branches.then: use H3 induced-Dirichlet as the population model with alpha promoted to parameters

action: Promote the induced-Dirichlet prior from fixed-hyperparameter to a population model by making its Dirichlet concentration alpha a parameter with its own hyperprior
discriminates: fixed-alpha induced-Dirichlet (single shared prior, complete pooling) vs hierarchical induced-Dirichlet (alpha as hyperparameter -> partial pooling across groups)
expected_findings: Replace induced-Dirichlet(c_n | alpha) with constants by induced-Dirichlet(c_n | alpha)·pi(alpha); per-group cutpoints C[s] ~ induced_dirichlet(exp(log_alpha), anchor) with log_alpha ~ std_normal() (or log alpha ~ MVN). Gives a self-consistent constrained population model with partial pooling.
situations: Hierarchical ordered/ordinal (multinomial probit or logit) regression where the interior cut points must vary by group, but a naive group-varying cut point vect
leaves: parameters: vector[n_thresh+1] log_alpha; vector[n_thresh] C[n_studies]; model: log_alpha ~ std_normal(); for s: C[s] ~ induced_dirichlet(exp(log_alpha), 0)

branches.if_observe: sampler runs cleanly
branches.then: proceed; note there is NO single population-mean cutpoint (see summary move)

branches.if_observe: 'Dirichlet_lpdf: probabilities is not a valid simplex' / probabilities > 0 errors throughout sampling
branches.then: go to the simplex-error triage move

action: Localize the fault: confirm the induced_dirichlet snippet is well-formed in isolation, then attribute the invalid simplex to numerical issues or bugs elsewhere in the program (the part transforming params to the induced category probabilities), not to the prior call itself
discriminates: snippet-level mis-specification of induced_dirichlet vs program-level numerical/bug issue producing an invalid simplex feeding the Dirichlet
expected_findings: The posted snippet (log_alpha ~ std_normal; C[s] ~ induced_dirichlet(exp(log_alpha),0)) looks fine; an invalid simplex means the induced probabilities computed from the cutpoints/linear predictor go non-positive or fail to sum to 1, i.e. ordering of C[s] is violated, the anchor/linear-predictor handling is off, or initialization/numerical underflow. The reviewer asks for the full program because the snippet alone is insufficient to diagnose.
situations: Hierarchical ordered/ordinal (multinomial probit or logit) regression where the interior cut points must vary by group, but a naive group-varying cut point vect
leaves: snippet alone insufficient; need full program — check that C[s] is a proper ordered vector and that the induced category probabilities stay a valid simplex throughout, suspect numerical issues or a bu

branches.if_observe: C[s] not declared/constrained as ordered or anchor mismatched
branches.then: fix the ordering/anchor so induced probabilities are a valid simplex

branches.if_observe: snippet fine but error persists
branches.then: fault is elsewhere in the program (numerical underflow / bug); provide full model for review

action: Reframe the request: test whether a 'population mean' summary even exists for this constrained, multidimensional population model
discriminates: H5 (population-average cutpoint exists and is directly estimable, normal-hierarchical intuition) vs the correct view (no single mean-like summary exists for Dirichlet/induced cutpoint populations)
expected_findings: In a normal hierarchical model theta_k ~ N(mu,tau) = mu + eta_k decomposes into baseline mu (a valid counterfactual 'no heterogeneity' value) plus heterogeneity. Constrained/multidimensional population models (Dirichlet, induced cutpoints) do NOT admit this baseline+heterogeneity split: population parameters only characterize the distribution of individual parameters, not an 'average individual'. Component-wise averages need not respect the ordering/simplex constraint and need not resemble any real group.
situations: Hierarchical ordered/ordinal (multinomial probit or logit) regression where the interior cut points must vary by group, but a naive group-varying cut point vect
leaves: alpha (or log_alpha MVN params) are pure population parameters with no 'average cutpoint' interpretation; cf. betanalpha hierarchical_modeling case study 2.3.1 (Inferring Individuals vs the Latent Pop

branches.if_observe: modeler insists on a single reportable number for a journal
branches.then: acknowledge any single summary is a compromise; choose the one readers misinterpret least and always show group-specific cutpoints + heterogeneity alongside

branches.if_observe: an approximate 'average cutpoint' is acceptable with caveats
branches.then: use the a-posteriori construction (next move) rather than reading a hyperparameter

action: Construct the summary a posteriori by simulating from the fitted population model and converting on the probability scale, instead of treating a hyperparameter as the mean
discriminates: directly-estimated location parameter (impossible here) vs a-posteriori-derived average computed in generated quantities
expected_findings: In generated quantities, sample new cutpoints from the population posterior (alpha -> induced category probabilities -> invert the case-study relations between latent cutpoints and ordinal probabilities to recover cutpoints), then average. This recovers per-cutpoint averages but discards the cross-cutpoint correlation and within-cutpoint variation; alternatively (H2 route) average the population mean of the log-differences and rebuild cutpoints via cumulative exp().
situations: Hierarchical ordered/ordinal (multinomial probit or logit) regression where the interior cut points must vary by group, but a naive group-varying cut point vect
leaves: use the latent-cutpoint <-> ordinal-probability relations from the ordinal_regression case study to convert population-level posterior probabilities back to summary cutpoints; or rebuild from mean log

branches.if_observe: summary respects ordering and is reported with heterogeneity plots
branches.then: acceptable compromise summary

branches.if_observe: component-wise average violates ordering/constraints
branches.then: constraint-respecting averages have dubious statistical interpretation — report distributions, not a point

action: Explain the placement effect and tighten obvious code-level inefficiencies
discriminates: whether the surprising speedup is I/O (CSV writes) vs autodiff-typing vs trajectory copies; and whether cheap code cleanups exist before structural changes
expected_findings: model-block locals are not written to the CSV and are not retained across the HMC trajectory the way transformed parameters are, so f drops out of per-draw write cost and in-memory copy cost; std_normal() over normal(0,1) and add_diag() over the jitter loop are free micro-wins
situations: A latent Bernoulli/logit Gaussian process (2D spatial, species-distribution, presence + random pseudo-absence, ~1200-2000 points) using the exact-covariance non
leaves: Mike Lawrence: model-block vars not written to csv (I/O save) and TPs persist in memory with multiple trajectory copies while model-block locals/GQ compute once; andrjohns: replace diag loop with add_

branches.if_observe: user on PyStan 2.19 so add_diag/_glm/GPU unavailable
branches.then: recommend migrating to cmdstanPy (latest Stan) to unlock add_diag, bernoulli_logit_glm and GPU offload

action: Refactor the latent-GP likelihood as a GLM
discriminates: whether fusing the matrix-multiply + logit-likelihood into one optimized primitive (and a GPU-eligible one) beats the explicit two-step
expected_findings: y ~ bernoulli_logit(a + L_K*eta) is algebraically a logistic GLM with design matrix L_K, intercept a, coefficients eta, so y ~ bernoulli_logit_glm(L_K, a, eta); the fused form has more efficient value+gradient and (Stan >=2.25) can run on GPU even when the design matrix is a parameter
situations: A latent Bernoulli/logit Gaussian process (2D spatial, species-distribution, presence + random pseudo-absence, ~1200-2000 points) using the exact-covariance non
leaves: andrjohns: bernoulli_logit_glm(L_K, a, eta); generalizes to any latent GP (e.g. normal_id_glm for Gaussian noise); Tadej's work in 2.25 lifted the data-only restriction so parameter design matrices ar

action: Standardize predictors and re-test
discriminates: whether the slowness/poor scaling was a prior-scale / geometry problem rather than a compute problem
expected_findings: with predictors on a sensible (N(0,1)) scale the inv_gamma(5,5) length-scale prior and the GP covariance are on the right scale, adaptation improves and reduce_sum now gives a real speedup (60 min -> 12 min); standardization, not parallelism per se, was the unlock
situations: A latent Bernoulli/logit Gaussian process (2D spatial, species-distribution, presence + random pseudo-absence, ~1200-2000 points) using the exact-covariance non
leaves: Nathan_Lemoine: standardizing both predictors to N(0,1) made reduce_sum give ~5x; reduce_sum 'helped only when predictors had been standardized (priors were too far off for non-standardized variables)

action: Step back and attack the GP itself rather than the likelihood/parallelism
discriminates: whether the right fix is an algorithmic approximation of the O(N^3) exact GP vs continued micro-optimization
expected_findings: the exact dense cholesky is the real bottleneck; the durable speedup comes from a GP approximation - Hilbert-space reduced-rank basis-function GP (Riutort-Mayol et al.), or binning/coarser representation - plus the glm sampling statement; reduce_sum is at best a minor add-on
situations: A latent Bernoulli/logit Gaussian process (2D spatial, species-distribution, presence + random pseudo-absence, ~1200-2000 points) using the exact-covariance non
leaves: wds15 priority order: (1) switch to glm sampling statement even without GPU, (2) simplify the GP via binning/approximation, (3) maybe add reduce_sum; avehtari: use Hilbert space approximate Bayesian G

branches.if_observe: a discrete GPU were available
branches.then: enable STAN_OPENCL to offload cholesky_decompose and the glm matrix-multiply - higher parallelism than reduce_sum; but integrated graphics only here, so not an option

action: Audit how the hierarchical priors are applied across subjects; replace ad-hoc index-gating with direct ID-indexed (or vectorized over N) prior statements
discriminates: H1 (loop/indexing bug malforming the density) vs deeper geometry/numerics causes
expected_findings: If H1: removing the previous_id branch and indexing log_theta[ID[i]] (or looping 1:N once) reduces warning magnitudes but does NOT eliminate them. Other hypotheses: no change.
situations: A nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) longitudinal model in Stan with unbalanced per-subject data and subject-specific random effects on log-scale parameters (a sigm
leaves: previous_id-gated `if (i!=previous_id)` only places a prior on a subject the first time its row appears; cleaner is `log_thetaA[ID[i]] ~ normal(...)` indexed by data ID, or a single loop `for n in 1:N

branches.if_observe: Numbers shrink but divergences/R-hat persist
branches.then: Indexing was a real but partial bug; move to prior-boundary and parameterization checks (H2/H3)

branches.if_observe: Warnings unchanged
branches.then: Indexing not the driver; go straight to numerics/geometry

action: Remove hard interval priors on variance components and effects; check whether posterior mass bunches against the declared boundaries
discriminates: H2 (boundary-bunching driving unconstrained values to +/-inf) vs intrinsic funnel/numerics
expected_findings: If H2: probability piling up at an interval edge correlates with divergences; relaxing/removing bounds (or using soft priors) removes the boundary-driven divergences. Use interval constraints ONLY for genuinely physical bounds.
situations: A nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) longitudinal model in Stan with unbalanced per-subject data and subject-specific random effects on log-scale parameters (a sigm
leaves: Hard <lower,upper> bounds map the boundary to +/-inf on the unconstrained scale; mass near the edge blows up the Jacobian-transformed geometry. Diagnose by inspecting marginal histograms near bounds.

branches.if_observe: Mass bunched at a bound
branches.then: Replace with soft prior; expect divergence count to drop

branches.if_observe: No bunching
branches.then: Bounds not the main culprit; proceed to parameterization

action: Switch to non-centered parameterization via offset/multiplier on the random-effect vector
discriminates: H3 (funnel geometry / varying curvature) vs misspecification
expected_findings: If H3: declaring `vector<offset=log_bar_theta, multiplier=omega>[N] log_theta;` (still with the normal prior statement) decouples location/scale from the raw draws and sharply reduces funnel-induced divergences. Reported effect: divergences dropped to ~31/10000.

branches.if_observe: Divergences fall dramatically
branches.then: Funnel confirmed; tune remaining residual divergences via numerics + priors

branches.if_observe: Still many divergences
branches.then: Suspect numerics (H4) or misspecification/multimodality (H6)

action: Check the nonlinear predictor for overflow/underflow; instrument with a guard print when |exponent| is large; simplify the algebra (drop the redundant *1)
discriminates: H4 (numerical overflow in exp(a)^exp(b)) vs genuine posterior pathology
expected_findings: If H4: exp(a)^exp(b) overflows/underflows whenever |b|>6 unless exp(a)~1; warnings appear when log_thetaC strays large. Confirmed by the user's guard `if (abs(log_thetaC[i])>6) print(...)` firing mostly in warmup, rarely in sampling.
