leaves: Warmup-only numerical warnings are usually benign (tails explored before adaptation settles); persistent sampling-time ones are a specific divergence. Multiplying by 1 is harmless (only extra chain-ru

branches.if_observe: Warnings only in warmup
branches.then: Treat as benign; do not chase

branches.if_observe: Warnings persist in sampling
branches.then: Bound/reparameterize log_thetaC or rescale to keep exponents in range

action: Add proper truncation T[0,] to normalize the parameter-dependent positive-outcome density; weigh against switching to a natively-positive residual model
discriminates: H5 (improper normalization) vs H7 (wrong residual family)
expected_findings: If the RHS scale involves parameters, T[0,] is required for a correctly normalized truncated normal; dropping it gives an unnormalized (wrong) target even if it superficially has fewer warnings. For multiplicative positive errors, lognormal is the natural choice (additive errors exponentiated); normal(y,sqrt(y)) is the Poisson normal-approx form.
situations: A nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) longitudinal model in Stan with unbalanced per-subject data and subject-specific random effects on log-scale parameters (a sigm
leaves: Fewer warnings without T[0,] is a red herring — it changes the target, not the correctness. Correct options: keep T[0,] for the truncated-normal assumption, OR reparameterize residual as lognormal for

branches.then: Keep T[0,] for correctness; accept it as the specified model

branches.if_observe: Free to change residual family
branches.then: Use lognormal (multiplicative) — smoother, naturally positive

action: Diagnose multimodality/identifiability: inspect traceplots and pairs plots of posterior draws; do NOT trust a fit that only works for lucky seeds
discriminates: H6 (multimodality / poor identifiability / prior-likelihood mismatch) vs a fully resolved model
expected_findings: If H6: stuck chains at distinct values = multiple modes or non-identifiability; a seed-sensitive 'good' run is biased because it under-explores the posterior. Pairs plots reveal strong correlations (poor identifiability) or separated clusters (multimodality).
situations: A nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) longitudinal model in Stan with unbalanced per-subject data and subject-specific random effects on log-scale parameters (a sigm
leaves: Two root causes of divergence per Bob: (1) misspecification — prior/likelihood/data mismatch induces bad geometry; (2) sampling difficulty — HMC struggles with high correlation (poor identifiability)

branches.if_observe: Pairs show separated clusters
branches.then: Genuine multimodality — rethink model/identifiability, tighter priors

branches.if_observe: Pairs show ridge/strong correlation
branches.then: Poor identifiability — reparameterize, tighten priors

action: Tighten hierarchical priors using prior-predictive reasoning; replace hard bounds with soft priors set at ~95% central intervals; validate with synthetic-data parameter recovery and (gold standard) SBC
discriminates: Whether remaining warnings reflect tolerable sampling noise vs real bias
expected_findings: Hierarchical priors are hard to reason about; prior samples often reveal the subject-level prior must be MUCH tighter than naive guesses — tightening eliminated divergences and improved recovery (jerlich's experience). Soft priors (e.g. log_bar~normal(4,5.5); omega~lognormal(log(0.2),1)) give smoother computation than hard bounds. SBC: out of N params in 50% intervals ~ Binomial(0.5,N); a few divergences may be dwarfed by sampling variance — SBC certifies whether bias is real.
situations: A nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) longitudinal model in Stan with unbalanced per-subject data and subject-specific random effects on log-scale parameters (a sigm
leaves: Recommended pipeline: (1) forward-simulate from prior with RNGs (no HMC -> no divergences unless under/overflow); (2) fit synthetic data generated FROM the model and check posterior interval coverage;

branches.if_observe: SBC coverage ~ binomial(0.5,N) and synthetic recovery good
branches.then: Trust the fit; residual divergences are tolerable

branches.if_observe: Coverage off / cannot recover generative params
branches.then: Bias is real — tighten priors further, reparameterize, or revisit specification/data

action: Locate where the NaN enters by instrumenting the integrand: print the input node x alongside the returned value (retval) for every evaluation, including the gradient pass.
discriminates: whether the NaN is produced by the integrand at a specific x (integrand-domain problem) vs injected by Stan's quadrature/autodiff machinery itself
expected_findings: H1/H3: a specific extreme node (x=+DBL_MAX) returns NaN while interior nodes are finite -> integrand-domain problem. Stan-bug hypothesis: NaN would appear with finite integrand values -> not seen.
situations: A Stan model uses integrate_1d over (-inf, +inf) to marginalize a latent person parameter out of a partial-credit / categorical-logit (ordinal IRT) likelihood t
leaves: Add `real retval = exp(sum(log_lik)+normal_lpdf(x|pars[mu_index],1)); print("x=",x,", retval=",retval); return retval;` (bbbales2's suggested print).

branches.if_observe: the very first gradient-pass node x=+1.79769e+308 gives retval=-nan while x=-1.79769e+308 gives 0 and all interior nodes are finite
branches.then: confirm the failure is an at-infinity evaluation specific to the gradient integral (which samples DBL_MAX); focus on what the integrand does at x=DBL_MAX, not on the quadrature code

action: Read the integrate_1d implementation to map the two-phase evaluation (value integral, then one extra integral per parameter for the gradient).
discriminates: whether DBL_MAX is reached because of the gradient integral's node placement (so the value pass never exercised it) vs a genuine logic error
expected_findings: The gradient is a separate integral, explaining why x=DBL_MAX appears only on the gradient pass; the existing guard cannot save a case where fx.val() is itself NaN (not 0).
situations: A Stan model uses integrate_1d over (-inf, +inf) to marginalize a latent person parameter out of a partial-credit / categorical-logit (ordinal IRT) likelihood t
leaves: stan-dev/math integrate_1d.hpp: forward integral at L133, per-parameter gradient integral at L146; the NaN-gradient guard at L50 sets gradient=0 only when fx.val()==0.

branches.if_observe: gradient is integrated separately per parameter and guarded only against fx.val()==0
branches.then: a NaN VALUE at the tail (not a clean 0) defeats the guard -> the integrand must be made to return exactly 0 (not NaN) far in the tail

action: Replace the tail return with an is_nan/zero short-circuit on the computed value.
discriminates: whether masking the NaN value is sufficient to satisfy the gradient guard vs whether NaNs that actually matter could be silently dropped
expected_findings: Masking NaN->0 lets sampling complete; but it is a blunt instrument and could hide meaningful NaNs, motivating a more principled cutoff and a deeper diagnosis of WHERE the NaN is born.

leaves: nhuurre proposed `if (retval==0.0) return 0.0; else return retval;`; user found `==0.0` did not catch it and used `if (is_nan(retval)) return 0.0;`, which made the model finish clean.

branches.if_observe: is_nan(retval)->0 fixes it but raises the question of +nan vs -nan and false masking
branches.then: guard the mask with a magnitude condition (e.g. only when |x-mu|>1e20) and continue decomposing the integrand to find the true source

action: Decompose the integrand: print sum_log_lik, normal_lpdf_val, and the per-class log_lik vector separately at the failing node.
discriminates: H2 (exp/normal_lpdf is the culprit) vs H3 (softmax/cumsum overflow is the culprit)
expected_findings: H2: normal_lpdf would be NaN -> NOT observed (it is finite -inf). H3: log_softmax(append_row(0, cumulative_sum(...))) is NaN because cumulative_sum overflows DBL_MAX+DBL_MAX -> +inf, and log_softmax of an inf vector is all-NaN -> CONFIRMED.
situations: A Stan model uses integrate_1d over (-inf, +inf) to marginalize a latent person parameter out of a partial-credit / categorical-logit (ordinal IRT) likelihood t
leaves: At x=+DBL_MAX: normal_lpdf_val=-inf, and log_lik=[-inf,-nan] then [-nan,-nan]; the NaN is in sum(log_lik) (from log_softmax/cumulative_sum), not in normal_lpdf (merely -inf).

branches.if_observe: sum_log_lik=-nan while normal_lpdf_val=-inf (finite)
branches.then: root cause is overflow in cumulative_sum/log_softmax at DBL_MAX, not exp underflow; the integrand only needs to recognize the tail and short-circuit before computing softmax

action: Insert a mathematically-justified early return at the START of the integrand when x is far enough into the normal tail that the result is provably ~0.

expected_findings: Early cutoff removes the exception and is correct because the softmax factor is bounded by 1 so the tail contributes negligibly; works in the full model (I=1000, K=10, J=4, mixture).

discriminates: whether the slowness is the quadrature itself vs redundant integrand recomputation across the 1000-factor likelihood

discriminates: whether a cheaper (lower-refinement) integrator is acceptable vs whether precision loss biases the posterior
expected_findings: Posterior means invariant for >=5 levels even though pointwise error ~1e-6/0.006; sampler is insensitive to errors far below the data/noise scale, so the faster low-refinement integrator is safe.

action: Audit the diagnostic itself before trusting any pattern in its output; recognize the chosen uniformity test as a known-flawed instrument
discriminates: diagnostic artifact (flawed chi-squared on ranks) vs genuine sampler/parameterization pathology
expected_findings: If the metric is unsound, its erratic, direction-flipping, sample-count-sensitive behavior is expected and uninterpretable; under a sound rank-uniformity assessment the centered funnel would show a coherent, stable deviation. The Cook-Gelman-Rubin chi-squared has fundamental mathematical flaws; the robust SBC rank-histogram construction (Talts et al, 1804.06788) fixes them.
situations: Running SBC on 8-schools (centered vs non-centered) to validate/compare samplers. Using a chi-squared test on per-dimension rank statistics (Cook-Gelman-Rubin 2
leaves: Betancourt (post 2): 'The chi-squared test in the Cook, Gelman, Rubin paper has some fundamental mathematical flaws.' Redirect to the SBC paper arXiv:1804.06788 which introduces the robust rank-based

branches.if_observe: a stable deviation persists under the robust method
branches.then: now attribute it to parameterization geometry (e.g. centered funnel) and investigate sampler

action: Re-derive what SBC actually requires of the sampler and isolate the single failure mode
discriminates: unbiasedness requirement vs exactness requirement; autocorrelation as the operative threat vs raw sample count
expected_findings: SBC needs the rank statistic to be drawn correctly, i.e. it needs UNBIASED (exchangeable) draws, NOT exact/high-precision estimates. Therefore the only real implementation challenge for MCMC is removing autocorrelation (hence thinning). Increasing sample count does not 'fix' SBC; it only makes the assessment more sensitive, so it can surface existing problems more strongly (explaining why more samples looked 'worse').
situations: Running SBC on 8-schools (centered vs non-centered) to validate/compare samplers. Using a chi-squared test on per-dimension rank statistics (Cook-Gelman-Rubin 2
leaves: Betancourt (post 6): 'SBC doesn't require that the estimators are exact, just that they're unbiased. The only challenge for MCMC is removing the autocorrelations as much as possible. Increasing the nu

branches.if_observe: rank histograms have boundary spikes when thinning is applied
branches.then: treat as autocorrelation signature, not a thinning bug; thin more aggressively until ranks are exchangeable

branches.if_observe: more samples degrades the apparent calibration
branches.then: do not interpret as worse sampling; it is increased sensitivity exposing a pre-existing autocorrelation/parameterization issue

action: Reject the search for a universal scalar uniformity summary; characterize the multiplicity of deviation modes instead
discriminates: one interpretable scalar test vs many distinct, information-bearing deviation shapes
expected_findings: There is no single 'deviation from uniformity' - the rank histogram can deviate in many ways (smiles=over/under-dispersion, frowns, tilts=bias), each meaning something different. Any scalar test (e.g. KS) collapses these and corresponds to no interpretable deviation, so it is poor for automated diagnosis. The rank histogram is the most information-dense presentation; KS-type statistics are not useful here.
situations: Running SBC on 8-schools (centered vs non-centered) to validate/compare samplers. Using a chi-squared test on per-dimension rank statistics (Cook-Gelman-Rubin 2
leaves: Betancourt (post 6): 'there is no single deviation from uniformity... each distinct deviation says something different.' KS and similar 'don't correspond to any particularly interpretable deviation in

branches.if_observe: model is high-dimensional
branches.then: do not test every parameter; prioritize the few parameters/summaries of actual inferential interest and inspect their histograms

branches.if_observe: a scalar is required for automation
branches.then: accept it only as a coarse flag; expect it to miss deviation modes orthogonal to the chosen statistic

action: Separate three distinct distance/validation concepts and check which one SBC actually delivers
discriminates: (a) integral probability metric distance between one approx and one exact posterior; (b) MCMC-CLT stochastic, function-specific convergence bounds; (c) SBC ensemble-level rank consistency
expected_findings: These are not interchangeable. IPMs/Wasserstein give deterministic bounds between a single approximate and exact posterior; CLT bounds are stochastic and vary function-to-function. SBC does NOT define a distance between an approximate and exact posterior - it compares an ENSEMBLE of posteriors against an ensemble of exact ones from a common prior; rank uniformity does not tie back to any individual paired-comparison bound. So SBC cannot be repurposed as a quantitative per-method IPM-style distance; explicit IPM bounds are unavailable outside simple cases and not numerically computable in general.
situations: Running SBC on 8-schools (centered vs non-centered) to validate/compare samplers. Using a chi-squared test on per-dimension rank statistics (Cook-Gelman-Rubin 2
leaves: Betancourt (post 8): SBC 'compares an ensemble of approximate posteriors and an ensemble of exact posteriors... rank uniformity doesn't immediately tie into bounds on any of the individual paired comp

branches.if_observe: you need a quantitative per-method error
branches.then: you need known/estimable exact expectation values (stat_comp_benchmarks); generally unrealistic, so SBC's qualitative ensemble consistency is the practical fallback

branches.if_observe: you only need to know an algorithm is broadly calibrated over a model family
branches.then: SBC suffices, but only within the prior-predictive scope - no guarantee for a target y' not drawn from the prior

action: Bound the scope of the validation claim - empirical (fixed model family) vs theoretical (target-adaptive) vs empirical-extension-of-theory
discriminates: validation that holds only over the prior-predictive ensemble vs validation that transfers to a specific real-data posterior
expected_findings: SBC is empirical validation: it holds for the fixed family of posteriors generated from the prior predictive, and does not adapt to a given target. Passing SBC for all y'~prior gives NO guarantee for a y' outside that ensemble. A good workflow builds the model to be consistent with the observed data, so the observed posterior sits near the SBC scope - then SBC is as close a validation of the real posterior as one can get, but still contingent on that proximity assumption.
situations: Running SBC on 8-schools (centered vs non-centered) to validate/compare samplers. Using a chi-squared test on per-dimension rank statistics (Cook-Gelman-Rubin 2
leaves: Betancourt (post 10): classifies SBC as empirical validation; theoretical = explicit IPM/CLT bounds (low-dim or log-concave only); empirical-extension = turning CLT-obstruction conditions into diagnos

branches.if_observe: observed data is far from the prior predictive scope
branches.then: SBC pass does not transfer; revisit model so observed posterior lies within the validated family

branches.if_observe: comparing algorithms theoretically
branches.then: explicit IPM/CLT bounds exist only in simple/log-concave cases; otherwise fall back to empirical/SBC-style evidence

discriminates: checking every parameter vs checking a sufficient subset
expected_findings: Hypothesis (community, not author-confirmed): good recovery of hyperparameters is a necessary condition for good recovery of lower-level parameters; if all lower-level theta pass SBC, the hyperparameters are implicitly exercised. So for whole-model validation it may be economical to test theta only - consistent with the earlier guidance to prioritize the few summaries of interest rather than testing all parameters.

action: Provenance / consistency check: confront the empirical column-mean subtraction against the mathematical construction of the basis (eigenfunctions of the Laplace operator with zero Dirichlet boundary conditions).
discriminates: H1-as-valid vs H3 — whether the decorrelation trick respects the basis-expansion derivation or is an ad hoc addition that has no guarantee of preserving the approximation.
expected_findings: H3: subtracting empirical, data-dependent column means is not part of the eigenfunction expansion; it perturbs the basis in a way with no controlled effect on the implied covariance, so distortion (the observed center-variance shrinkage) is expected and uncontrolled.

leaves: Contrast with the cleaner alternative of dropping the intercept term, whose effect on the model is mathematically transparent; de-meaning's effect is not. The author concedes the centering was empiric

branches.if_observe: de-meaning is ad hoc / outside derivation
branches.then: do NOT de-mean basis functions; the prior-fidelity cost is real and the math guarantees are lost — treat it as an error to be removed from the case studies

branches.if_observe: centering corresponds to a principled basis modification
branches.then: could be retained, but only if marginal variance is verified to stay correct

action: Compare candidate decorrelation fixes by their completeness and side effects rather than convenience.
discriminates: H4 — whether dropping the intercept fully solves the correlation, and what implicit prior it imposes.
expected_findings: H4: removing the intercept eliminates only one correlating variable; about half the basis functions still have nonzero column means and remain correlated with any intercept-like quantity, so it is a partial fix. It also implicitly puts a point-mass-at-zero prior on the intercept, which is only appropriate when data are empirically centered and is problematic when the GP models a scale parameter or when the global scale is unknown (e.g. multi-component Birthdays).
situations: In a Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP / reduced-rank basis-function expansion), the columns of the basis matrix PHI are de-meaned (subtract each column's empi
leaves: Motorcycle/Birthdays: when GP feeds a scale parameter or several components share an unknown global scale, you cannot simply center the data or drop the intercept; hence the appeal of zero-mean basis

branches.if_observe: intercept removal acceptable for the specific model (data empirically centered, intercept truly ~0)
branches.then: drop the intercept as a transparent fix

branches.if_observe: intercept must be retained (scale-parameter GP, unknown global scale, multi-component)
branches.then: neither de-meaning nor intercept-removal is right; seek a reparameterization that keeps intercept + undistorted basis but decorrelates them

action: Localize where the transform IS vs ISN'T applied across the two code paths (inference/log_prob vs the .random draw)
discriminates: predictive-sampling-bug vs transform-is-one-directional-by-design
expected_findings: Transform is applied only to the latent free variable used in log_prob/inference; the .random method is assumed to already produce in-domain samples, so the transform is never applied during predictive draws. This is the intended PyMC3 contract, not a bug.
situations: User passed a custom transform (e.g. sum_to_1) to a distribution's transform= kwarg, then drew prior/posterior predictive samples and found the returned values

action: Examine whether the supplied transform unbounds an already-constrained parameter vs changes the distribution's domain
discriminates: legitimate-bug vs misapplied-transform (the real fault)
expected_findings: sum_to_1 on a HalfNormal is not a valid unconstraining transform — HalfNormal does not emit vectors summing to 1. The transform is being asked to convert one distribution into another (domain change), which the transform= kwarg was never designed to do. For default transforms (HalfNormal log, Uniform interval, etc.) the .random output IS already correct, so it is not a predictive-sampling bug.
situations: User passed a custom transform (e.g. sum_to_1) to a distribution's transform= kwarg, then drew prior/posterior predictive samples and found the returned values
leaves: lucianopaz: 'transform is at fault here ... returns correct samples for halfnormal/halfcauchy/uniform'. junpenglao: two operations are conflated — [1] unbounding a constrained parameter (what transfor

branches.if_observe: transform only unbounds (default)
branches.then: .random already correct; no action

action: Print/inspect the Theano computational graph of the transformed variable to expose the FreeRV/TransformedRV/Deterministic structure
discriminates: which node owns the .random method and why predictive draws skip the transform
expected_findings: PyMC3 puts an unconstrained FreeRV (weights_sumto1__) higher in the hierarchy; the named var (weights) becomes a TransformedRV computed as a Deterministic of it. In pm.sample the FreeRV is sampled and weights is a deterministic function (correct). In prior predictive, drawing the FreeRV raises MissingInputError, so PyMC3 falls back to calling weights' .random directly and returns it untransformed.
situations: User passed a custom transform (e.g. sum_to_1) to a distribution's transform= kwarg, then drew prior/posterior predictive samples and found the returned values
leaves: lucianopaz debugprint of weights shows ViewOp -> Join -> weights_sumto1__ ... ; 'in sample prior predictive we first try to draw weights_sumto1__, that raises MissingInputError ..., then we simply cal

branches.if_observe: graph confirms FreeRV-on-top + Deterministic structure
branches.then: explain the MissingInputError fallback as the precise reason predictive ignores the transform

action: Trace the Markov-blanket / subgraph value flow for log_prob vs forward sampling
discriminates: downstream-corruption-via-internal-transformed-RV vs corruption-confined-to-the-directly-queried-leaf
expected_findings: For default transforms the constrained value passed to children is correct: in log_prob computation the parent A is replaced by user/programmatic input in BOTH the distribution and the value, so as long as it sits in the proper constrained space the child sees a valid value. Only the user's domain-changing transform (case [2]) breaks; default unconstraining transforms propagate correctly.
situations: User passed a custom transform (e.g. sum_to_1) to a distribution's transform= kwarg, then drew prior/posterior predictive samples and found the returned values
leaves: junpenglao Markov-blanket sketch: ... -> A ~ d1 -> B ~ d3(A,C) ...; for log_prob you get d1.log_prob(input_A) + d3(input_A,input_C).log_prob(...); A is replaced by input in both distribution and value

action: Prescribe an explicit model reparameterization instead of relying on transform=

action: Count unique unordered pairs vs. supplied rows; check whether mm() is order-sensitive
discriminates: genuine information vs. duplicated/pseudoreplicated rows; whether the directionality worry is even real
expected_findings: C(43,2)=903 unique pairs << 4200 rows -> heavy duplication. mm(site_1,site_2) is symmetric, so swapping site_1/site_2 changes nothing -> there is NO directionality to protect against, and the duplication is pure upsampling.

leaves: amynang: 4200 from 903 = overdrawing; mm() ignores which site is first/second, so use one row per lower-triangle entry. Duplicating rows is the same illegitimate move as upsampling an ordinary regress

action: Probe what the response physically IS and whether it inherits a constrained dependency structure (triangle inequality / shared latent)
discriminates: whether residual non-independence is benign vs. a structural mis-specification that no additive random effect can fix
expected_findings: Response is CV of two sites' functioning = (1/2)|a-b|/mean(a,b); equivalently division-by-larger gives 1-b/a, and on the log scale log(b/a)=beta-alpha, a genuine distance that obeys the triangle inequality. So the response is a derived comparison with a non-trivial dependency structure, exactly the dissimilarity-matrix problem.
situations: A brms regression on pairwise / distance-matrix data: the response is a between-site dissimilarity (CV of two sites' ecosystem functioning, or a distance/Fst ma
leaves: jsocolar: what matters is the dependency structure of the RESPONSE, not the covariates. CV(2 vals) reduces to abs-diff/sum ~ log distance => triangle-inequality-constrained; pairwise rows are logicall

action: Construct adversarial thought-experiments (all-identical sites; two perfectly-distinct sets; 37-vs-3 split) and trace what additive intercepts can encode
discriminates: additive multi-membership model vs. true dependency structure (overconfidence test)
expected_findings: Additive intercepts: when all sites identical, 45 'zero' dissimilarities carry only 9 independent bits -> model over-constrains (overconfident). A high intercept needed to make a site dissimilar from others ALSO forces it to look dissimilar from members of its own set -> cannot represent 'A far from B, C near A => C far from B'. Multi-membership intercepts thus fail to capture the constraint and yield over-precise fixed effects.
situations: A brms regression on pairwise / distance-matrix data: the response is a between-site dissimilarity (CV of two sites' ecosystem functioning, or a distance/Fst ma

(b) mu_ij = beta0 + beta*x + (alpha_i - alpha_j)^2 (White et al.) literally is a 1-D ordination and respects the structure the additive form cannot.

action: Trace what a spatial prior on ADDITIVE intercepts actually implies for predicted dissimilarities, and where the residuals truly live
discriminates: spatially-structured additive intercepts vs. a coherent dependency model; also double-use of geographic distance
expected_findings: On additive intercepts, CAR implies nearby sites share alpha sign: two positive alphas -> very HIGH dissimilarity, two negative -> very high similarity -- spatially incoherent. Residuals are properties of PAIRS, so their spatial structure lives in 4-D (both sites' positions, symmetry-constrained), not in single-site space. On DIFFERENCED intercepts a CAR prior instead sensibly makes distant sites more dissimilar.

action: Reinterpret the diagnostic signature: treedepth-saturation-on-everything WITH no divergences is the fingerprint of an unbounded/flat posterior direction (sampler drifts along a ridge toward +/-inf), NOT a step-size or local-curvature problem
discriminates: unbounded-ridge non-identifiability vs a benign efficiency-only treedepth warning
expected_findings: If non-identified: chains never mix, stepsize collapses, n_eff stays ~minimal. Treedepth being maxed everywhere (not just occasionally) points away from 'just slow' and toward a posterior with no proper mode along some direction.
situations: Ordered-logistic / ordinal-IRT model where EVERY post-warmup transition hits max treedepth (12), R-hat is huge and n_eff is about 2, traceplots show no mixing a
leaves: Max treedepth is NOT merely a computational-efficiency issue: when it fires on every transition alongside R-hat>>1 and n_eff~2, it signals the trajectory cannot turn around because the target is impro

action: Test the additive intercept-vs-cutpoint degeneracy: ask whether raising alpha by a constant and lowering all cutpoints by the same constant leaves the likelihood invariant
discriminates: alpha-cutpoint additive non-identifiability (H_alpha_cut, the ROOT cause) vs cutpoints merely under a bad prior (H_cutprior)
expected_findings: Under H_alpha_cut: the shift alpha -> alpha+d, c -> c-d gives an EXACTLY equal ordered_logistic likelihood, so the posterior is flat along that ridge and only the priors bound it -> exactly the unbounded direction producing maxed treedepth. This is the by-construction overparameterization (alpha = 'one too many cutpoints').

action: Evaluate the fixed-uniform-grid reparameterization as a MODELING decision, not just an identifiability hack: check what constraints uniform cutpoint spacing imposes on the implied category probabilities
discriminates: principled-identification (flexible/parametric cutpoints with a sensible prior) vs a cheaper but model-distorting fix (frozen uniform grid)
expected_findings: Fixing cutpoints to a uniform grid makes a DIFFERENT, more restrictive model: the latent logistic density integrated between equally spaced cutpoints yields very NON-uniform, counterintuitive category probabilities the model can no longer adjust. Spacing s also rescales beta's meaning (beta/s ~ expected y-change per unit theta) and caps achievable category probability at (e^s-1)/(e^s+1). Cheap but wrong unless those constraints are intended.

expected_findings: H4: reparam + better inits cut warmup cost; pure-Bernoulli proportion compresses exactly to a binomial; BUT compressing the hierarchical likelihood by row-aggregation is flagged as not obviously valid -> leads to H5.
situations: Stan model with a per-unit discrete latent state (infection status ~ Bernoulli(Pinf)) that cannot be sampled as an integer parameter; user wants to estimate bot
leaves: Standardize priors with offset/multiplier (real<offset=25,multiplier=0.5> mu_Vp) so default inits land near the prior; non-centered hierarchy; try Nutpie (faster warmup) and Pathfinder draws as MCMC i

branches.if_observe: user aggregates identical singleton rows via target+=weight*log_sum_exp
branches.then: check validity of weighting under per-id random effects (H5)

action: Diagnose whether the sufficient-statistic weighting is statistically valid given per-individual random effects; verify by A/B against the unaggregated model on simulated data.
discriminates: H5: invalid conditional-likelihood aggregation vs (rejected) priors-on-eta / lupdf / numerical issues
expected_findings: H5: weighting is correct only for the marginal (random-effect-integrated) likelihood; weighting the conditional likelihood is wrong and is the root cause of non-mixing + sigma->0. Validation: aggregated model must reproduce the unaggregated model's posterior on simulated data.
situations: Stan model with a per-unit discrete latent state (infection status ~ Bernoulli(Pinf)) that cannot be sampled as an integer parameter; user wants to estimate bot
leaves: Key insight (jsocolar/Bob): individuals with identical observed (time, Ct) do NOT have identical likelihood contributions CONDITIONAL on their random effects -- each formerly had its own eta. Weight*l

branches.if_observe: random effect can be analytically integrated out for the singleton group
branches.then: aggregate the marginal likelihood and weight that -> valid compression, restores mixing

branches.if_observe: random effect cannot be integrated out
branches.then: aggregation across distinct-ranef individuals is not valid; keep them separate or accept changed model

action: Retrieve the package convention for how brms parameterizes likelihood families
discriminates: mu/phi (mean-precision) vs a/b (shape) parameterization
expected_findings: brms parameterizes families so the main linear predictor maps to the mean: it uses mu/phi. The base R dbeta(x,a,b) uses shape (a,b), so to recover the density you convert a=mu*phi, b=(1-mu)*phi (or use extraDistr's dprop, which is natively mu/phi).
situations: A (zero-inflated) beta regression fitted in brms runs fine, but the user is confused about: which beta parameterization brms uses (mu/phi vs a/b shape), whether
leaves: Confirmed mu = mean (logit link), phi = precision/'spread' (log link). dbeta needs converted shapes; extraDistr::dprop/rprop takes mu,phi directly. phi behaves like an inverse-dispersion (higher phi =

branches.if_observe: Need to verify the convention rather than trust memory
branches.then: Inspect generated Stan code via make_stancode to read off exactly how mu/phi enter the beta_lpdf

action: Retrieve the link-scale convention for brms coefficient output
discriminates: estimates reported pre-inverse-link (link scale) vs post-inverse-link (response scale)
expected_findings: Coefficients are reported BEFORE applying inverse-link, i.e. on logit scale for mu and log scale for phi. Default links are listed on the brmsfamily help page. So back-transform Intercept with plogis/inv-logit and phi with exp to interpret.
situations: A (zero-inflated) beta regression fitted in brms runs fine, but the user is confused about: which beta parameterization brms uses (mu/phi vs a/b shape), whether
leaves: Intercept (mu) -> inv_logit; phi -> exp. Default links inspectable at ?brmsfamily / link_XX args.

action: Move interpretation from link scale to the response (outcome) scale
discriminates: sign/uncertainty of effect (readable on logit) vs magnitude of effect (only meaningful on outcome scale)
expected_findings: On the logit scale you can read direction (positive/negative/uncertain) but not magnitude, since the nonlinearity makes a coefficient's outcome effect depend on the rest of the model / baseline. conditional_effects() shows the effect on the outcome scale for an average case; pp_check() visualizes the posterior predictive on the outcome scale.
situations: A (zero-inflated) beta regression fitted in brms runs fine, but the user is confused about: which beta parameterization brms uses (mu/phi vs a/b shape), whether
leaves: Use conditional_effects() for average-case outcome-scale effects, pp_check() for posterior-predictive visual check. Caveat: outcome-scale effect varies with distance from the mean.

action: Read the energy diagnostic correctly: treat energy__<->scale-parameter correlation as the primary signal and ask which parameter the Hamiltonian trajectories cannot explore
discriminates: localizes the problem to a specific scale parameter (sigma_r) and reframes 'reparameterize' as 'find the degenerate geometry', vs the user's diffuse correlation hunch
expected_findings: Funnel hypothesis: energy correlates with the population scale because trajectories stall as that scale shrinks. Location-scale-correlation hypothesis: NUTS handles smooth correlations fine, so mu_a/sigma_a correlation alone would NOT produce low E-BFMI.
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: arviz.plot_energy + pairs plot of energy__ vs each parameter; the energy-vs-sigma_r correlation is the tell, mu_a-sigma_a correlation is a red herring because Stan's NUTS handles posterior correlation

branches.if_observe: energy correlated with a scale parameter
branches.then: pursue funnel/hierarchical-geometry hypothesis

branches.if_observe: only smooth location-scale correlation, energy flat
branches.then: correlation is benign; look elsewhere (latent identifiability)

action: Probe the latent per-response parameter by plotting random individual E[i] entries against energy and the scale parameters
discriminates: per-response-latent identifiability/funnel vs a purely population-level scale issue
expected_findings: Latent-non-identifiability hypothesis: individual E[i] show nothing dramatic on their own but the energy-vs-sigma_r relationship reappears across random smatterings of E -> consistent with a global funnel feeding through the latents, not an isolated bad parameter.
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: add a few randomly-sampled E[i] to the pairs plot; here individual E looked unremarkable but energy<->lsig_react persisted -> points away from a single pathological E and toward the population-scale f

branches.if_observe: a specific E[i] blows up / pins a boundary
branches.then: local identifiability problem at that observation / the constraint boundary

branches.if_observe: nothing per-E but energy<->scale persists
branches.then: global hierarchical funnel; marginalize the latent if its value is not needed

action: Check whether the sub-model is actually an unconstrained normal population model before applying the centered<->non-centered swap
discriminates: genuine normal-hierarchical (NCP applicable) vs interval-censored normal under the additivity/ordering constraint (NCP does NOT apply)
expected_findings: NCP-fix hypothesis fails: because E+d_rt must equal rt and E<rt, log_d is interval-censored, so the standard reparameterization identities don't hold; the funnel cannot be removed by a coordinate change the way it can in an unconstrained normal hierarchy.
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: OP self-corrects in-thread: cannot just write drt=exp(raw*sigma+mu) because E[i]+d_rt[i] is pinned to rt[i]; betanalpha confirms 'interval censored normal model does not admit the same reparameterizat

branches.if_observe: no constraint coupling the latent and the scale
branches.then: try non-centered (and centered) parameterizations per Betancourt hierarchical case study

branches.if_observe: hard constraint couples them
branches.then: reparameterization options are limited; consider marginalizing the latent instead

action: Run prior/posterior predictive checks comparing simulated data from the true generative process against the candidate shifted-lognormal
discriminates: whether the theory-driven generative structure is needed vs an empirically-adequate simpler distribution
expected_findings: Collapse hypothesis fails: simulated anticipatory RT distributions (sum of normal-CDF-event time + lognormal delay) do not look like a shifted lognormal, so adopting the simpler model would mean rejecting the posited data-generating mechanism.
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: generate RT distributions over draws of mus/sigmas (and at fixed x) -> visibly not shifted-lognormal; mixture-with-single-shift would shove genuine anticipation-buildup variability into the lognormal

branches.if_observe: simulated data matches shifted lognormal
branches.then: switch to the simpler likelihood, geometry problem dissolves

branches.if_observe: does not match
branches.then: keep the structured model; must address geometry via marginalization

action: Marginalize out the latent E numerically and re-run parameter recovery + diagnostics
discriminates: whether removing the explicit per-response latent dimension removes the divergences / improves the geometry
expected_findings: Marginalization helps with divergences and speed (~4x faster) and passes diagnose() over recovery cycles; BUT the underlying sigma_r funnel can still manifest when data are sparse or true sigma_r is small, because marginalizing inside one chain doesn't remove the population-scale degeneracy.
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: trapezoid integration over E (chosen over integrate_1d, which threw tolerance errors on ~1e-200 underflow values in the parameter tails); analytic integration impossible due to the constraint

branches.if_observe: clean diagnostics across recovery cycles
branches.then: accept marginalized model

branches.if_observe: divergences/low-EBFMI return at small true sigma_r or small N
branches.then: residual funnel; the scale degeneracy survives marginalization

action: Distinguish 'fixed the geometry' from 'masked the geometry' by asking what a zero-avoiding prior actually does to the funnel and to inference
discriminates: principled domain-informed lower bound on heterogeneity vs cosmetic suppression of warnings that biases sigma_r upward
expected_findings: Zero-avoiding-prior-as-fix hypothesis is only conditionally valid: the Weibull works by cutting off the bottom/neck of the funnel, but it suppresses model configurations near zero that the data (via the likelihood) actually found consistent; unless a principled lower scale is elicited AND it is large enough to cover the funnel neck, it forces larger heterogeneity than the data support.
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: jsocolar: the sampler visiting small sigma_r under half-normal means the data don't rule that region out; betanalpha: the danger is suppressing configurations *near* zero, not zero itself; valid only

branches.if_observe: a principled lower bound on sigma_r exists and is large enough to cover the funnel neck
branches.then: a containment prior away from zero is legitimate

branches.if_observe: no elicited lower bound, prior chosen to silence warnings
branches.then: reject; inference now conflicts with what the data support

action: Attribute the residual pathology to the population-scale funnel exposed by low information / concentration at small sigma_r, not to the (now-integrated) per-response latent
discriminates: which degeneracy remains (mu_r-sigma_r vs log_d-sigma_r) and why data size / true scale modulate it
expected_findings: Funnel hypothesis confirmed as terminal explanation: less data -> more diffuse sigma_r posterior -> more of the log_d-sigma_r funnel exposed (the neck at small sigma_r); smaller true sigma_r -> posterior concentrates exactly where the funnel is worst. Stan must confront all parameter interactions jointly, so you cannot 'integrate out' to dodge it unless done before MCMC (rarely analytic; INLA-style approximate for limited classes).
situations: HMC fit that recovers parameters, has zero divergences and good R-hat, but very low E-BFMI (<0.1) that barely improves with more warmup; energy pair plot shows
leaves: betanalpha: residual is a mu_r-sigma_r or log_d-sigma_r degeneracy; the interval-censored constraint blocks both analytic integration and the usual non-centered escape; OP later finds an earlier 'dive

branches.if_observe: good recovery at realistic N and realistic sigma_r
branches.then: accept marginalized + half-normal model within its validated regime

branches.if_observe: target regime has small sigma_r / sparse data
branches.then: funnel is intrinsic; options are elicited containment prior covering the neck, or accept limited computability

action: Prescribe the standard out-of-sample idiom: wrap inputs in a mutable data container and overwrite them before re-running PPC.
discriminates: Whether the blocker is conceptual (how PPC obtains new inputs) vs mechanical (which API call). It is conceptual: the static arrays give PPC no swap point.
expected_findings: Under H1 the data-container route works once X is a pm.Data node. Under H2 (simple model) re-instantiating the model with new X and the same posterior also works, which is why set_data isn't strictly required.
situations: Bayesian multivariate linear regression fit in PyMC; sample_posterior_predictive regenerates Y only for the original training X because predictors are hard-code
leaves: Wrap predictors as pm.Data('x', ...) named nodes at build time; for prediction, inside the model context call pm.set_data({'x': new_X}) then pm.sample_posterior_predictive(trace). Posterior over a, B,

branches.if_observe: model is simple / one-off diagnostic prediction
branches.then: skip the container; re-instantiate the model with new X and feed posterior samples

branches.if_observe: model is complex or reused across many prediction scenarios
branches.then: make it data-aware with pm.Data so the same model can be re-run via set_data

action: Restore visible progress and reduce the iteration budget: add refresh=1 to force per-iteration printing; reason from the printed gradient-eval timing to an honest expected wall-clock

situations: A brms/rstan multilevel model that "used to run" now compiles the Stan program but then hangs: it prints gradient-evaluation timing and "Iteration: 1/N (Warmup)

action: Collapse to a minimal reproducible model: warmup=1, iter=1, chains=1, cores=1, AND strip the formula to a single (continuous) predictor; ask for str()/dim() of the data frame
discriminates: Data-specific fault (hypothesis 5: bad column type/dimensions) vs anything intrinsic to the formula/sampler (hypotheses 3,4) vs compile/link issue
expected_findings: If a single-predictor 1-iteration model ALSO stalls/blows up, the formula and sampler are exonerated and the fault is in the data frame itself; str() should expose a column with the wrong type/cardinality (e.g. a supposedly continuous predictor stored as a 896-level character/factor -> a giant dummy-encoded design matrix).

action: Reframe the question from within-table relative comparison to an absolute reliability threshold
discriminates: whether 'low' n_eff is genuinely problematic vs a non-issue created by comparing to very-high-n_eff parameters
expected_findings: Under H1 the low-n_eff set stays near or below the reliability floor and concentrates in the hierarchical scale/group terms; under H2 even the minimum n_eff (~2015) sits well above the ~100/chain floor, so it is acceptable.
situations: A model runs without divergences but some parameters have markedly lower n_eff (~2000-5000 out of 16000 draws) and Rhat ~1.001-1.002, while others have n_eff >1
leaves: Old rule of thumb (andre.pfeuffer): be concerned when n_eff < ~10% of sample size AND Rhat is more than ~1% away from 1.0. Modern guidance (avehtari, rank-norm Rhat paper 1903.08008): require a minimu

branches.if_observe: lowest n_eff per chain < ~100
branches.then: treat as genuinely unreliable; investigate geometry (H1)

branches.if_observe: n_eff comfortably above floor (the actual case)
branches.then: stop ranking n_eff; switch to a precision-based acceptance criterion (H2)

action: Replace the n_eff ranking with a precision (MCSE) criterion tied to the reporting need
discriminates: whether the chain is precise *enough for the intended use* vs chasing ever-higher n_eff for its own sake
expected_findings: mcse for the reported parameters is far below the 2-sig-fig threshold given se_mean ~0.001 in the table, so the run is acceptable for reporting (favours H2).
situations: A model runs without divergences but some parameters have markedly lower n_eff (~2000-5000 out of 16000 draws) and Rhat ~1.001-1.002, while others have n_eff >1
leaves: Decide the accuracy needed for the quantity of interest (for reporting usually 2 significant digits), then check that the Monte Carlo standard error (mcse, which itself uses n_eff) is small enough for

branches.then: run longer or fix sampling geometry

branches.if_observe: mcse already below target precision

action: Calibrate how much Rhat departure matters and which Rhat to trust
discriminates: real non-convergence vs benign Rhat noise; classic vs rank-normalized Rhat
expected_findings: All Rhat <= 1.002 << 1.01, so no convergence alarm under either heuristic; favours H2 over H3.

leaves: andre's heuristic: only worry if Rhat is >~1% from 1.0 (i.e. >~1.01); 1.002 is within tolerance. avehtari: use the rank-normalized, folded Rhat (1903.08008) rather than classic Rhat, since classic Rha

branches.if_observe: rank-normalized Rhat > ~1.01
branches.then: real convergence problem; inspect chains/geometry (H1)

branches.if_observe: Rhat <= ~1.01 (the actual case)
branches.then: convergence acceptable; do not over-read 1.001-1.002

action: Inspect chain-mixing and pairwise geometry with the right plots
discriminates: whether low n_eff stems from a fixable geometry (funnel/correlation between scales and group terms, H1) vs being already-adequate sampling (H2)
expected_findings: Under H1, pairs plots reveal funnel/banana shapes (scale vs group-level) and rank histograms look non-uniform; under H2, rank histograms are roughly uniform and pairs plots show nothing critical.
situations: A model runs without divergences but some parameters have markedly lower n_eff (~2000-5000 out of 16000 draws) and Rhat ~1.001-1.002, while others have n_eff >1
leaves: Use rank histogram plots (avehtari) instead of traceplots for mixing — traceplots get fuzzy with long chains and fail for long-tailed distributions; the rank-norm Rhat paper's appendix shows the compa

branches.then: reparameterize (non-centered) and/or tighten priors on scale terms (H1)

action: Localize the distortion to the model's parameterization rather than the data or the sampler: ask which structural feature could break category symmetry while leaving the likelihood intact.
discriminates: prior/parameterization-induced asymmetry vs. data-side coincidence vs. sampler pathology
expected_findings: Under H1/H2 the asymmetry tracks exactly the reference-category choice and the use of any non-flat prior (incl. the hierarchical one). Under the 'nothing to model' read, the issue would persist regardless of parameterization.

branches.then: the hierarchical structure acts as a non-symmetric prior on the reference parameterization -> confirm H1/H2; pursue a reorder-invariant parameterization

branches.if_observe: asymmetry persists even with flat priors and no hierarchy
branches.then: reconsider whether the contrived data leaves anything to estimate (H4)

action: Switch to a parameterization that estimates all K category logits and rely on regularization (a proper prior) for identification.
discriminates: whether removing the reference category restores symmetry, and whether a mild prior is enough to identify the over-parameterized model
expected_findings: With refcat=NA + a modest prior, posterior epred recovers p('2')=p('3')=0.25 across all groups and restores the '1' vs '4' mirror (matches the symmetric data).
situations: Hierarchical (categorical/multinomial) logistic regression `resp | trials(n) ~ 1 + (1|group)` in brms produces posterior predictions that violate symmetries cle
leaves: In brms set `family = multinomial(link=logit, refcat=NA)`; this drops the pinned reference row so all K linear predictors are free. Model is then formally unidentified under a flat prior, so a (modera

branches.if_observe: symmetric, sensible epred recovered with a moderate prior
branches.then: adopt refcat=NA; flag that fully-flat priors are inadvisable because the model is unidentified without regularization

branches.if_observe: posterior wanders / poor mixing under near-flat prior
branches.then: tighten the prior; treat the random intercepts themselves as the identifying regularization (analogy to glmnet's all-K + penalty)

action: Compare the dummy-reference construction against refcat=NA at the generated-Stan-code level to see whether they are genuinely different models.

action: Reparameterize the K-1 linear predictors with a reorder-invariant simplex transform instead of a pinned reference.
discriminates: cosmetic symmetry (works on this data) vs. structural reorder-invariance (works for any data/prior)
expected_findings: Sum-to-zero / log-ratio parameterization is exchangeable in the K categories for any prior, so it never imprints an arbitrary reference asymmetry; the refcat=NA+prior fix achieves the same effect when the prior is symmetric.

branches.then: refcat=NA + symmetric prior is the practical stand-in; full invariance needs a custom family
