leaves: Recommended formulas: non-hierarchical `resp | trials(n) ~ 0 + group`; hierarchical `resp | trials(n) ~ 1 + (1|group)`; both with `family = multinomial(link=logit, refcat=NA)` and a moderately informa

action: Sampler-tuning triage first: raise adapt_delta, shrink initial stepsize, lengthen warmup
discriminates: curable step-size/geometry artifact (H1) vs a deeper identifiability/parameterization problem (H2/H3)
expected_findings: If H1: divergences vanish at adapt_delta 0.95-0.99 and the fit is trustworthy. If H2/H3: divergences shrink but some remain regardless of adapt_delta, signaling the funnel is intrinsic to the parameterization/data, not just step size.
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) Dawid-Skene annotation / measurement-error model in which each coder's misclassification simplex matrix theta[j,k] is drawn fro
leaves: adapt_delta 0.95 then 0.99; smaller stepsize; longer warmup to improve mass-matrix adaptation; a handful of divergences often clears this way

branches.if_observe: divergences fully clear
branches.then: accept fit; treat as solved geometry artifact

branches.if_observe: some divergences remain at adapt_delta=0.99 (the observed case)
branches.then: escalate to priors / reparameterization (next moves)

action: Reparameterize misclassification rows onto the unconstrained log-odds scale: draw log-odds from a (multivariate) normal, then softmax to the simplex
discriminates: whether the bottleneck is the Dirichlet simplex parameterization (H3) vs the data/prior (H2/H4)
expected_findings: A correctly-built logistic-normal model samples cleanly and removes divergences; a naively-built one (next move) regresses badly, exposing reparameterization pitfalls.
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) Dawid-Skene annotation / measurement-error model in which each coder's misclassification simplex matrix theta[j,k] is drawn fro
leaves: log_odds ~ multi_normal(_cholesky); theta = softmax(log_odds); log-odds scale is additively symmetric about 0 and lets you model category covariance; convert back to simplexes to communicate priors to

branches.if_observe: naive softmax(multi_normal) version is built
branches.then: expect convergence failure; go to the reparameterization-debugging move

action: Diagnose the three classic logistic-normal/softmax reparameterization defects
discriminates: which of {missing covariance prior, softmax non-identifiability, scale/log-sum-exp bug} drives the non-convergence
expected_findings: If softmax non-identifiability dominant: chains wander along the unidentified ridge -> high Rhat, low n_eff independent of data size (matches 'large data didn't help'). If missing covariance prior: scale of theta_raw drifts. If log vs log-sum-exp bug: posterior is systematically wrong, not just slow.
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) Dawid-Skene annotation / measurement-error model in which each coder's misclassification simplex matrix theta[j,k] is drawn fro
leaves: (1) no prior on the covariance matrix -> put a prior via scaled Cholesky factor of a correlation matrix (regression chapter), better statistically and computationally; (2) softmax of K free parameters

branches.if_observe: non-convergence independent of data size
branches.then: prime suspect is softmax over-parameterization -> move to K-1 / anchored parameterization and add Cholesky-correlation prior

branches.if_observe: scale of log-odds drifts
branches.then: add scaled-Cholesky correlation prior on the covariance

action: Reject the constant-add pattern; pin the latent true label instead, or model gold sources as high-accuracy annotators
discriminates: incorrect (no-op constant) vs correct (pinned latent / high-accuracy-annotator) handling of known labels
expected_findings: The constant-add appears to 'work' in tests (softmax_lqz matches the gold with zero-width intervals) but does not actually condition the model; pinning z[i] or the high-accuracy-annotator encoding correctly tightens theta estimates and is robust to imperfect gold.
situations: A hierarchical (partial-pooling) Dawid-Skene annotation / measurement-error model in which each coder's misclassification simplex matrix theta[j,k] is drawn fro
leaves: Adding the log-density of a constant is a no-op and does NOT pin z[i]. To use a known label, fix z[item] in the categorical likelihood: y[k] ~ categorical(theta[annotator[k], z[item[k]]]). Robust alte

branches.if_observe: gold sources trusted absolutely
branches.then: fix z[i] (or take gold as truth with no uncertainty)

branches.if_observe: gold sources fallible (real-world)
branches.then: encode as high-fixed-accuracy annotators in a separate non-pooled coder group

action: Separate 'same target distribution' from 'same sampling geometry'; check whether the transform's Jacobian is constant.
discriminates: naive-equivalence hypothesis vs. the geometry-matters reality
expected_findings: Because mu and sigma are themselves parameters, the affine map x = mu + sigma*x_z has a NON-constant Jacobian (depends on sigma). So the posterior geometry the sampler traverses differs between forms even though the implied distribution of x is identical — equivalence hypothesis is false.
situations: A hierarchical/scaled Gaussian parameter `x ~ normal(mu, sigma)` with `sigma` weakly informed. A practitioner asks whether the three codings — centered, manual
leaves: Key correction (post 13): an affine transform with parameter-valued coefficients is NOT a measure-preserving relabeling; the funnel is created/removed by how sigma multiplies the latent.

branches.if_observe: sigma were a fixed constant
branches.then: Jacobian constant -> forms truly equivalent, no geometry change; reparam buys nothing

branches.if_observe: sigma is a free parameter
branches.then: geometry differs -> parameterization choice is load-bearing; proceed to choose by informativeness

action: Assess informativeness of sigma and of the individual x values (data per group); locate whether the funnel lives in the prior or the likelihood.
discriminates: centered-preferred vs non-centered-preferred (the conditional decision rule)
expected_findings: sigma weakly informed is the precondition for trouble. Then: if x are WEAKLY informed (little data per group) -> non-centered wins (prior funnel dominates). If at least some x are STRONGLY informed (lots of data per group) -> centered wins (likelihood funnel; non-centering re-introduces a funnel). Neither parameterization is universally correct.
situations: A hierarchical/scaled Gaussian parameter `x ~ normal(mu, sigma)` with `sigma` weakly informed. A practitioner asks whether the three codings — centered, manual
leaves: post 12 rule: trouble requires sigma weakly informed; the x-informativeness then flips which parameterization funnels. The funnel-in-likelihood case is subtler to recognize than the funnel-in-prior (8

branches.if_observe: sigma weakly informed AND x weakly informed
branches.then: use non-centered (manual or offset/multiplier)

branches.if_observe: sigma weakly informed AND some x strongly informed
branches.then: keep/switch to centered; non-centering would create a likelihood funnel

branches.if_observe: mixed groups (some data-rich, some data-poor)
branches.then: consider per-element/partial non-centering rather than a global choice

action: Compare the two non-centered implementations on correctness-equivalence vs. practical cost (runtime, stored output size, numerical robustness).
discriminates: manual transformed-parameter form vs offset/multiplier form
expected_findings: Both are the SAME non-centered reparameterization and (absent numerical issues) give the same posterior; but they are NOT bit-identical under the hood. Manual form stores both x and x_z (2x storage for long x, fixable by moving x into model block); runtimes can differ when x is long and the rest of the model is cheap; offset/multiplier had an open 'sticky boundary' numerical bug.
situations: A hierarchical/scaled Gaussian parameter `x ~ normal(mu, sigma)` with `sigma` weakly informed. A practitioner asks whether the three codings — centered, manual
leaves: post 12 + 6/8: offset/multiplier is cleaner syntactically (f^{-1}(y)=offset+multiplier*y) but at the time carries the sticky-boundary caveat -> prefer manual non-centered if that bug could bite.

branches.if_observe: x is a long vector and rest of model is cheap
branches.then: watch runtime/storage; move x computation to model block to avoid saving both

branches.if_observe: boundary/numerical pathology suspected
branches.then: avoid offset/multiplier (sticky boundary) until fixed; use manual transformed-parameter form

action: Re-attribute the mechanism: the operative transform is the SCALING by sigma, not the centering shift by mu.
discriminates: 'centering is what matters' vs. 'scaling by sigma is what matters'
expected_findings: The mu-shift is a constant-Jacobian (harmless) affine offset; the sigma-multiply is what couples scale and location and creates/removes the funnel. So 'centered/non-centered' is a misnomer for what is really a scaling choice — useful for explaining WHY to a confused practitioner.
situations: A hierarchical/scaled Gaussian parameter `x ~ normal(mu, sigma)` with `sigma` weakly informed. A practitioner asks whether the three codings — centered, manual
leaves: posts 8/12/14: terminology aside; the decision is fundamentally about the sigma multiplier's non-constant Jacobian, which is also the resolution of the original confusion.

action: Clarify intended link/scale BEFORE debugging the syntax
discriminates: pure-syntax bug vs conceptual prior-scale mismatch
expected_findings: If the user wants logit reporting but writes Beta priors, the priors are mis-scaled (Beta lives on [0,1], log-odds params are unbounded), so the diagnosis is scale mismatch, not a typo; if they want identity link, Beta priors are directly valid
situations: brms binomial regression with cell-means parameterization (y | trials(N) ~ 0 + grp) where the user wants informative Beta(a,b) priors on the per-group success p
leaves: Ask 'logit link or identity link?'. User answers 'ideally both' (risk difference from identity, odds ratio per cardiology convention) — confirms the link choice is unresolved and is the root of the co

action: State the rule: prior scale must match the parameter scale; choose one consistent design
discriminates: keep-logit-with-unbounded-priors vs switch-to-identity-to-keep-Beta
expected_findings: Under logit link, replace Beta with an unbounded continuous prior (Normal); under identity link, keep the Beta priors. Either is internally consistent; mixing them is the bug.
situations: brms binomial regression with cell-means parameterization (y | trials(N) ~ 0 + grp) where the user wants informative Beta(a,b) priors on the per-group success p
leaves: Recommend: keep log-odds model -> use Normal priors; OR if you insist on Beta priors -> switch to identity link. This reframes the user's errors as a scale-consistency problem.

action: Resolve the coefficient-naming mismatch with get_prior() on the exact formula+family
discriminates: wrong coef label vs deeper non-applicability of the prior
expected_findings: get_prior() reveals the actual parameter names the model exposes (class b, coef = factor-level names like grp20/grp21); the user's failures came from guessing coef names (e.g. plain 'grp2') and from a transient/stale session
situations: brms binomial regression with cell-means parameterization (y | trials(N) ~ 0 + grp) where the user wants informative Beta(a,b) priors on the per-group success p
leaves: Run get_prior(formula=f, data, family=binomial('identity')); use the listed coef values verbatim. With correct coef names the Beta-on-identity model fits (grp20~0.17, grp21~0.28, Rhat 1.0). Note the e

branches.if_observe: same code now runs but emits many warnings
branches.then: warnings are about missing lower/upper bounds on the Beta priors (parameters can stray outside [0,1] during sampling), not convergence failures — proceed to bound them

action: Add lb/ub bounds to bounded priors, working around the lb/ub-incompatible-with-coef restriction
discriminates: warnings safe to ignore vs need explicit [0,1] truncation
expected_findings: In brms you cannot pass lb/ub inside prior() together with coef; the workaround is a GLOBAL class='b' prior carrying lb=0, ub=1 (e.g. beta(1,1)) plus the per-coef Beta priors. The global prior's distribution is unused but its bounds propagate to all class b coefficients (verify via model$prior).
situations: brms binomial regression with cell-means parameterization (y | trials(N) ~ 0 + grp) where the user wants informative Beta(a,b) priors on the per-group success p
leaves: prior = c(prior(beta(1,1), class=b, lb=0, ub=1), prior(beta(25,122), class=b, coef='grp20'), prior(beta(44,94), class=b, coef='grp21')). Confirms warnings vanish; m3$prior shows lb=0/ub=1 on all three

action: Translate a Beta(a,b) probability prior into a log-odds-scale prior
discriminates: approximate Normal-match vs exact Jacobian-adjusted Beta vs avoid-the-problem-entirely
expected_findings: Approximate route: simulate Normal(mu,sd) draws, push through plogis()/inv_logit, compare sample mean+variance to the Beta's analytic mean a/(a+b) and variance ab/((a+b)^2(a+b+1)), iterate on mu/sd. No Normal exactly reproduces a Beta pushforward, so it stays approximate.
situations: brms binomial regression with cell-means parameterization (y | trials(N) ~ 0 + grp) where the user wants informative Beta(a,b) priors on the per-group success p
leaves: Iterative recipe (Solomon): plot Beta target, rnorm(1e6), p=plogis(n), compare descriptive stats, tune mean/sd. For Beta(25,122): target mean 0.170, var 9.5e-4; Normal(-1.5,0.25) gives 0.185, 1.4e-3 —

branches.if_observe: user wants the EXACT Beta, not a Normal approximation
branches.then: use the Jacobian-adjustment trick (next move)

branches.if_observe: goal is real peer-reviewed inference, not learning brms
branches.then: do NOT fit two models; fit only the prior you believe (identity link), then transform posterior draws to odds ratios post hoc: or = (p1/(1-p1))/(p0/(1-p0)) via as_draws_df() — no second model needed

action: Put Beta on inv_logit(parameter) and add the Jacobian adjustment
discriminates: raw Beta-on-log-odds (wrong, no Jacobian) vs correctly change-of-variables-adjusted Beta
expected_findings: To make inv_logit(b) carry a Beta(a,b) density, increment the target with the Beta log-density of inv_logit(b); the Jacobian of the logit<->probability transform is exactly the logistic(0,1) density, so passing prior = logistic(0,1) on class b supplies the adjustment, and stanvars adds the Beta term. Result: an exact Beta pushforward on the probability scale, not a Normal approximation.
situations: brms binomial regression with cell-means parameterization (y | trials(N) ~ 0 + grp) where the user wants informative Beta(a,b) priors on the per-group success p
leaves: set_prior('logistic(0,1)', class='b') + stanvars = stanvar(scode='target += beta_lpdf(inv_logit(b[1]) | 25, 122);', block='model') + stanvar(... b[2] | 44, 94 ...). Confirmed by Solomon to run and rep

branches.if_observe: logistic(0,1) Jacobian term omitted
branches.then: the implied prior on the probability scale is distorted — the beta_lpdf alone on inv_logit(b) is not a proper Beta pushforward without the change-of-variables term

action: Point to randomized quantile (PIT) residuals as the conceptually-correct censoring-robust residual
discriminates: global distributional misfit detectable via PIT uniformity vs structural/covariate misfit — and whether a residual approach suffices vs needing full PPC
expected_findings: If H2: randomized quantile residuals should be ~Uniform/Normal under a correct model; deviations flag misfit. Works under censoring because the PIT is taken on the censored likelihood contribution.
situations: A Bayesian Weibull survival model (likelihood built from weibull_lccdf for right-censored obs and a CDF-difference for interval-censored obs) fits fine, but the
leaves: tidybayes 'Extracting and visualizing tidy residuals' vignette, 'Randomized quantile residuals' section; caveat surfaced later — the helper functions are not compatible with raw rstan/cmdstanr fit obj

branches.if_observe: analyst can compute PIT residuals
branches.then: check uniformity / QQ for global misfit

branches.if_observe: tooling incompatible with cmdstanr objects
branches.then: fall back to a hand-rolled PPC (H3/H4/H5)

action: Reconstruct the censoring/observation process and apply it to uncensored posterior predictive draws so simulated data are directly comparable to observed
discriminates: whether misfit is in the latent event-time model vs an artifact of ignoring the observation schedule — forces predictions onto the same support as the data
expected_findings: If H3: simulate surv_raw ~ weibull_rng, then floor it to the last inspection time it exceeds (loop over full inspection schedule obs_times). Predicted censored data then live on the same grid as observed -> can compare directly.
situations: A Bayesian Weibull survival model (likelihood built from weibull_lccdf for right-censored obs and a CDF-difference for interval-censored obs) fits fine, but the
leaves: surv_raw[i]=weibull_rng(k,lambda[i]); surv_censored=0; for t_i in 1:N_obs_times: if surv_raw>obs_times[t_i] surv_censored=obs_times[t_i]; requires knowing ALL inspection times, not just the y1/y2 brac

branches.if_observe: full inspection schedule known
branches.then: generate censored replicates and compare distributions of censored times / status-at-inspection

branches.if_observe: analyst only has bracketing y1,y2 (schedule irregular/unknown)
branches.then: this exact loop doesn't apply; need a coarser summary (H4) or the partition approach (H5)

action: Collapse interval-censored obs to a point (midpoint of y1,y2) to get pseudo-event-times, fit a Kaplan-Meier survival curve, overlay posterior-predicted survival curves
discriminates: agreement vs disagreement between the model-implied survival function and the empirical (KM) survival function across time
expected_findings: If H4: KM curve from y_obs=(y1+y2)/2 with right-censoring flag, overlaid with 1 - ECDF of weibull_rng posterior draws; visual concordance indicates adequate marginal survival fit. Caveat: midpoint imputation discards interval-width info and can bias.
situations: A Bayesian Weibull survival model (likelihood built from weibull_lccdf for right-censored obs and a CDF-difference for interval-censored obs) fits fine, but the
leaves: y_obs=(y1+y2)/2; survfit(Surv(y_obs,cens)~1); loop 100 posterior draws, plot curve(1 - ecdf(post_draws$surv[i,])(x)); reported plot 'looks good'

branches.if_observe: predicted curves bracket the KM curve
branches.then: accept as a workable marginal PPC (analyst's resolution)

branches.if_observe: systematic gap between KM and predicted survival
branches.then: investigate shape k, covariate effects, or hierarchical structure

action: Partition the outcome space into the disjoint censoring intervals, compute per-bin probabilities, and compare observed vs posterior-predicted empirical bin probabilities / empirical CDF; separately replace log_diff_exp(lcdf,lcdf) with a direct CDF difference
discriminates: where on the time axis the model mis-predicts (which interval's probability mass is off) vs global fit; and naive-log-CDF numerical error vs a genuine fit problem
expected_findings: If H5: define bins (0,x1),(x1,x2),(x2,x3),(x3,inf); p_k = integral of rho over bin = CDF differences; empirical p_k = n_k/N from data and from PP draws give scalar retrodictive checks; cumulative sums recreate an empirical CDF over the censoring grid — approaches quantile-residual checks but uses the problem's natural structure. Numerically, cdf(xL)-cdf(xR) is more stable/efficient than log_diff_exp of the lcdfs.
situations: A Bayesian Weibull survival model (likelihood built from weibull_lccdf for right-censored obs and a CDF-difference for interval-censored obs) fits fine, but the
leaves: betanalpha: observational model pi = (1 - P[I(0,x)])*rho(x); right-cens P_R = integral_xR^inf rho; interval P_I = integral_xR^xL rho; categorical draw over (p1..p4) to simulate observation type; compa

branches.if_observe: empirical bin probabilities (and their cumulative CDF) match between data and PP draws
branches.then: model adequate over the censoring grid; refine by subdividing the fully-observed interval into finer bins to probe survival-function shape

branches.if_observe: a particular bin's probability is off
branches.then: localizes misfit to that time region -> revisit shape/scale or covariate/hierarchical terms there

branches.if_observe: likelihood uses log_diff_exp of naive lcdfs
branches.then: swap to direct CDF difference for stability/efficiency

action: Re-express pairs/diagnostic plots on the sampler's UNCONSTRAINED scale (plot log(sigma_theta), not sigma) before interpreting geometry
discriminates: whether the apparent geometry is a constrained-space artifact vs the true space HMC explores
expected_findings: On log scale a clear funnel appears in mu_theta vs log(sigma_theta); funnel is broader/more diffuse in the NCP fit. Divergences do NOT cluster in one region.
situations: A hierarchical model with group-level means (normal/lognormal likelihood) fit in Stan: the non-centered parametrization produces divergent transitions after war
leaves: Constrained-vs-unconstrained: a lower=0 param is sampled on log scale, so diagnose the funnel in mu_theta vs log(sigma_theta), not vs sigma.

branches.if_observe: funnel visible on log scale
branches.then: treat as a real geometry/identifiability problem, not a bug; proceed to inspect raw parameters

branches.if_observe: divergences cluster in a corner
branches.then: localize the pathological region; but here they are diffuse

action: Plot the RAW sampled parameters (theta_raw, sigma, mu_theta, sigma_theta) AND swap Cauchy scale priors for Normal as an independent divergence test
discriminates: heavy-tailed-prior cause vs intrinsic posterior-geometry cause
expected_findings: Divergences PERSIST under Normal(0,5) priors -> not a prior-tail artifact. Raw-parameter pairs show a non-Gaussian blob with correlations and changing curvature.
situations: A hierarchical model with group-level means (normal/lognormal likelihood) fit in Stan: the non-centered parametrization produces divergent transitions after war
leaves: Prior-swap test: Cauchy->Normal on scales. Divergences surviving the swap rules out heavy tails and points at structural funnel geometry.

branches.if_observe: divergences vanish with Normal priors
branches.then: blame heavy-tailed prior; tighten/replace it

branches.if_observe: divergences persist (observed)
branches.then: geometry is structural; analyze identifiability between group params and population params

action: Diagnose identifiability regime: assess how narrow each group's likelihood is relative to the population spread (data informativeness per group)
discriminates: narrow-likelihood / strongly-identified-individuals regime (CP better) vs wide-likelihood / weakly-identified regime (NCP better)
expected_findings: With informative/well-separated groups the per-group likelihoods clamp theta_n and decouple them from mu,tau, so CP is well-conditioned and NCP introduces a funnel (theta_raw must move opposite to mu to hold theta fixed). Hence NCP diverges, CP does not.
situations: A hierarchical model with group-level means (normal/lognormal likelihood) fit in Stan: the non-centered parametrization produces divergent transitions after war
leaves: Identifiability rule: narrow likelihoods -> theta well-identified -> centered best; wide/weak likelihoods -> non-centered best. NCP and CP have opposite failure modes. This is the crux.

branches.if_observe: groups informative/separated
branches.then: prefer centered; expected NCP funnel

branches.if_observe: groups weakly informed (little data, large obs noise)
branches.then: prefer non-centered; predict NCP improves if you cut draws-per-group

action: Run a minimal canonical control (8-schools) toggling likelihood width: large effects/normal-scale obs error vs shrunk obs error sigma
discriminates: confirms likelihood-width as the controlling variable; isolates it from the lognormal and from this dataset
expected_findings: Shrinking obs sigma (more informative individuals) flips the advantage toward centered; this reproduces the same CP-better-than-NCP behavior in a known model.
situations: A hierarchical model with group-level means (normal/lognormal likelihood) fit in Stan: the non-centered parametrization produces divergent transitions after war
leaves: Canonical control = Betancourt's 8-schools divergence example; manipulate sigma to move between centered- and non-centered-favorable regimes.

branches.if_observe: control reproduces the flip
branches.then: generalize: parametrization choice is data/likelihood-width dependent

action: Reframe the choice as PER-PARAMETER (per-group), not model-level; account for unbalanced group sizes
discriminates: model-level single-choice framing vs per-group mixed-parametrization framing
expected_findings: With unbalanced data, some groups are narrowly informed (want centered) and others weakly informed (want non-centered); optimal implementation can mix schemes per group. Overlap heuristic is unreliable.
situations: A hierarchical model with group-level means (normal/lognormal likelihood) fit in Stan: the non-centered parametrization produces divergent transitions after war
leaves: Per-parameter parametrization (Betancourt-Girolami): decision is made per theta_n by its own likelihood width; mixed CP/NCP can be optimal though unwieldy.

action: Reframe the question: refuse to treat it as a single-answer software fix; assert that with multiple observed variables there is no unique waic/loo and the prediction task must be chosen first.
discriminates: H1 (it's a bug to patch) vs H2 (underdetermined by design — choose the predictive task)
expected_findings: Under H1 a default auto-combine would be safe; under H2 any fixed default would silently answer the wrong question for some users. Expert lands on H2: 'there is not a single way of computing waic/loo... ArviZ will probably never work via az.loo(idata) with multiple observed values.'
situations: A PyMC model has two (or more) separate observed/likelihood variables (e.g. home_goals and away_goals as two Poisson likelihoods in a soccer/rugby model). Sampl
leaves: Key reframing: identify the prediction task whose predictive accuracy you want, THEN check exchangeability of the held-out unit (per Vehtari CV-FAQ / hierarchical-model CV case study). Candidate tasks

branches.if_observe: user can articulate what they want to predict (a future match? a new team? one goal count?)
branches.then: map that to a concrete held-out exchangeable unit and proceed to the reduction recipe

branches.if_observe: user cannot say what the prediction target is
branches.then: send them to the cross-validation FAQ / hierarchical-CV case study to define the task before any code

action: Group the pointwise log-lik by team (groupby home_team / away_team), SUM within team, then add the home and away per-team contributions; call waic.
discriminates: leave-one-team-out vs leave-one-match-out — held-out unit is a team (all its matches), so all log-lik rows touching that team collapse into one column
expected_findings: log-lik matrix shape 3000 x 6 (n_teams); elpd_waic ~ -541, p_waic ~ 25. Requires team indices stored as constant_data (pm.Data) so groupby has the grouping key.
situations: A PyMC model has two (or more) separate observed/likelihood variables (e.g. home_goals and away_goals as two Poisson likelihoods in a soccer/rugby model). Sampl
leaves: Code: import xarray as xr; idata.sample_stats['log_likelihood'] = xr.concat((log_lik.home_points, log_lik.away_points), 'match').rename({'match':'observation'}).

Code: home_points_team = log_lik.home_points.groupby(const.home_team).sum().rename({'home_team':'team'}); away_points_team = log_lik.away_points.groupby(const.away_team).sum().rename({'away_team':'tea
Code: idata.sample_stats['log_likelihood'] = log_lik.home_points + log_lik.away_points; az.waic(idata). Pointwise lik = Pois(y_h)·Pois(y_a) per match; the SUM of log-liks = log of the joint per-match

branches.if_observe: home_team/away_team not in idata.constant_data
branches.then: wrap them with pm.Data before from_pymc3 (or pass via dims) so ArviZ stores them in constant_data for the groupby

action: Build the combined log-lik by list-comprehension over var_names, xr.concat along an auxiliary dim, then reduce with .sum over that aux dim; write into the log_likelihood group (not sample_stats, which is deprecated).
discriminates: naive 'initialize key then += in a loop' (fails with KeyError / wrong dims) vs the align-and-reduce xarray idiom
expected_findings: xr.concat([ll[v] for v in var_names], dim='aux_dim').sum('aux_dim') aligns the DataArrays and collapses cleanly; must end up as a Dataset member with (chain,draw,...) dims, else waic fails downstream.
situations: A PyMC model has two (or more) separate observed/likelihood variables (e.g. home_goals and away_goals as two Poisson likelihoods in a soccer/rugby model). Sampl
leaves: Code: idata.log_likelihood['combined'] = xr.concat([idata.log_likelihood[v] for v in var_names], dim='aux_dim').sum('aux_dim'); az.waic(idata, var_name='combined'). Store in log_likelihood group; samp

branches.if_observe: AttributeError 'DataArray object has no attribute data_vars' from az.waic
branches.then: you assigned a bare DataArray to idata.log_likelihood; instead assign it as a named variable inside the group and call az.waic(idata, var_name=...)

branches.if_observe: KeyError: 'chain' when waic tries to stack(sample=('chain','draw'))
branches.then: the concatenated array lost the chain dim — diagnose the concat, see next move

action: Diagnose: inspect the combined array's dims; identify that mismatched shapes broke xr.concat alignment so the (chain,draw) dims were dropped — and recognize this as a statistical signal, not just a packaging bug.
discriminates: benign shape/align bug (fixable with xr.align/broadcast) vs a deeper signal that two DISTINCT predictive tasks are being illegitimately merged

action: Reject the shortcut on first principles: explain WAIC/LOO need the POINTWISE log-lik because they approximate predictive accuracy on unseen data via per-point leave-one-out (e.g. p(y_i|y_-i) ≈ E_post[p(y_i|θ)^{-1}]^{-1}); a collapsed scalar destroys the pointwise structure CV depends on.
discriminates: totals-are-enough intuition vs the requirement for pointwise log-lik aligned to the held-out unit; also LOO/WAIC vs AIC/BIC/DIC
expected_findings: Summing to a scalar is not equivalent: LOO's PSIS importance-weighting and WAIC's pointwise variance penalty both operate per observation. AIC/DIC estimate the same elpd worse (poor for non-Gaussian posteriors) and give NO diagnostic warning when they fail.
situations: A PyMC model has two (or more) separate observed/likelihood variables (e.g. home_goals and away_goals as two Poisson likelihoods in a soccer/rugby model). Sampl
leaves: Pointwise log-lik is load-bearing: LOO uses p(y_i|y_-i) ≈ p(y_i|θ)^{-1} (importance ratios), WAIC uses pointwise mean + variance — a scalar total loses both. Ref: Gelman/Vehtari WAIC-LOO paper (arXiv:

action: Reason from the cumulative-link parameterization about what one shared threshold prior + the order constraint already buys you

expected_findings: Under H5 you would need distinct locations to encode prior order; instead, because brms enforces the ordering constraint on thresholds, a single shared prior already (1) spaces thresholds roughly uniformly, (2) can push mass to the extremes, or (3) nudge toward higher/lower categories — covering the common cases; distinct per-coef locations only matter with specific prior info about individual thresholds

action: Reason about how a latent-scale shift maps to per-category probability changes under the cumulative link, rather than treating asymmetry as a defect
discriminates: H3 (genuine mis-specification) vs H4 (expected cumulative-link mass-redistribution / zero-sum tradeoff)
expected_findings: Under H3 you'd expect a fixable error in prior or simulation. Under H4: because a zero-centered slope only shifts the single latent location, predicted category proportions move as a zero-sum redistribution — an increase in category 1 is tied to a decrease in category 5 — and categories sitting where the threshold-spacing is wide / near-saturated show little net between-condition difference; per-category overlap persists across slope-prior changes because the slope is centered at 0
situations: Working with a cumulative-link (cumulative()) ordinal model in brms. Two coupled observations: (1) after setting per-threshold Intercept priors via coef=1:K, ge
leaves: Persistent histogram overlap and the missing category-5 difference are expected: the cumulative link enforces a trade-off across categories and a zero-centered slope yields symmetric ± shifts; the rig

branches.if_observe: changing the slope prior barely changes per-category overlap
branches.then: consistent with H4 (slope centered at 0) — expected, not a bug

branches.if_observe: want to see the prior's real effect
branches.then: plot the read−noread difference / category-mass redistribution (H6), not overlapping marginal histograms

branches.if_observe: asymmetry survives even on the difference view in a way unexplained by mass-redistribution
branches.then: revisit H3 — check the simulation's threshold values / link direction

action: Switch the prior-predictive visualization to one that exposes the cross-category tradeoff and between-condition difference

expected_findings: A difference-between-conditions plot and a full category-mass plot reveal the zero-sum redistribution and make the prior's effect legible, whereas ppc_bars error bars are misleading when predictions pile on 0s/1s; ppd_ridgeplot adapted to groups shows the per-group distributions
situations: Working with a cumulative-link (cumulative()) ordinal model in brms. Two coupled observations: (1) after setting per-threshold Intercept priors via coef=1:K, ge

action: Sanity-check the naive informative prior by actually running the analysis with it (or reasoning about its weight) and judging whether it dominates the new trial data.
discriminates: H1 (trust-the-SE) vs. all discounting hypotheses (H2-H5): is the meta SE narrow enough that it overwhelms the new RCT?
expected_findings: Under H1 the prior would be fine; in practice the analyst finds the meta-SE prior is 'too informative' — it swamps the current trial, confirming the spread must be widened beyond the CI of the mean.
situations: Have a published random-effects meta-analysis of a treatment effect and want to use it as an informative prior for the treatment-effect parameter in a new Bayes
leaves: Lachlan: 'I initially specified a prior based on the meta-analysis estimate and SE, but I found in my case it was too informative.' This is the empirical correction that motivates the rest of the sear

branches.if_observe: prior dominates the new data / posterior ≈ prior
branches.then: reject H1 as primary; seek a heterogeneity-aware spread (H2) or tail-discounting (H3); reserve H1 for a sensitivity analysis only

branches.if_observe: evidence base is sparse and prior is already weak
branches.then: direct SE may be acceptable, but keep it as sensitivity analysis (AGranholm's practice)

action: Fit/inspect the random-effects model and compute the PREDICTION interval (incorporating tau), then base the prior on the prediction interval rather than the CI of the pooled mean.
discriminates: H2 (prediction interval, heterogeneity-aware) vs. H1 (CI of the mean); also exposes whether the meta-analysis's apparent confidence is an artifact of ignoring between-study variance.
expected_findings: If heterogeneity is real, the prediction interval is MUCH wider than the CI and may cross zero even when the CI is entirely on one side — a diagnostic that the pooled CI overstates certainty. With few studies tau is poorly estimated, yielding very wide intervals (weak, conservative prior).
situations: Have a published random-effects meta-analysis of a treatment effect and want to use it as an informative prior for the treatment-effect parameter in a new Bayes
leaves: Diagnostic payoff realized: pooled 95% CI was well above zero (small p), but the prediction interval was much wider and substantially crossed zero — ~10% implied chance the average effect is harmful.

branches.if_observe: prediction interval crosses zero despite a 'significant' pooled CI
branches.then: confirms meta-analysis overstates certainty; adopt prediction-interval-based prior to avoid importing false confidence

branches.if_observe: very wide prediction interval from tiny #studies / unstable tau
branches.then: accept it as appropriately weak (little influence on result), or fall back to a formal MAP prior (H5) that regularizes tau

action: Consider a Student-t prior (location = meta mean, scale = SE) and choose/penalize the df (nu) as a confidence knob; pin it down by simulating from the prior to see what effects it implies (prior predictive check).
discriminates: H3 (tail-discounting) vs. H2 (variance-based widening); whether discounting should act on tails (outlier-robust) or on the bulk variance.
expected_findings: Low nu (e.g. nu=1) gives very heavy tails / very wide prior; prior-predictive simulation reveals implausible implied effects for bad nu, giving a defensible basis for the choice rather than an arbitrary one.
situations: Have a published random-effects meta-analysis of a treatment effect and want to use it as an informative prior for the treatment-effect parameter in a new Bayes
leaves: Max Mantei: Student-t with df reflecting subjective confidence (nu=1 very wide), optionally a hierarchical prior on nu; reframe prior choice as making assumptions explicit and validate via prior predi

branches.if_observe: prior-predictive draws imply implausible effect sizes
branches.then: adjust nu/scale until implied effects are reasonable; document the simulation as justification

branches.if_observe: still uneasy about arbitrariness
branches.then: prefer a standard/citable procedure (prediction interval or formal MAP) over a hand-tuned t-prior

action: Adopt a formal Meta-Analytic-Predictive (MAP) prior workflow (RBesT package / vignette), optionally a robust mixture with a neutral component, and evaluate the trial's operating characteristics under the prior.
discriminates: H5/H4 (formal MAP + robust mixture, design-aware) vs. ad hoc H2/H3; provides the 'standard procedure to cite' the analyst asked for.
expected_findings: RBesT/MAP yields a principled informative (or robust-mixture) prior plus tools to assess design properties; clarifies that with few studies, model assumptions materially affect the prior, and that informative priors always introduce some bias — the point is to communicate, not eliminate it.
situations: Have a published random-effects meta-analysis of a treatment effect and want to use it as an informative prior for the treatment-effect parameter in a new Bayes
leaves: wds15 (RBesT author): informative priors can never avoid bias — communicate what produced the prior since it becomes part of the analysis like data; start with the RBesT introduction vignette; key val

branches.if_observe: concern the new trial won't replicate the meta-analysis
branches.then: use robust-MAP mixture (informative + neutral); mixture weight encodes degree of belief in replication

branches.if_observe: fully trust meta-analysis and just want to narrow uncertainty
branches.then: informative MAP prior, or go fully hierarchical — re-run the meta-analysis perched atop the new data and estimate jointly (jsocolar)

action: Step back and classify the inferential goal (replication-check vs. precision-narrowing) and the downstream evidence-synthesis consequences before fixing prior strength.
discriminates: H6 (goal-dependent strategy) — separates 'distrust → weak/neutral prior' from 'trust → informative prior'; also weighs double-counting risk in later systematic reviews.
expected_findings: Replication goal → strong meta-prior is inappropriate (concordance of new data with meta prediction interval is itself the quantity of interest). Precision goal → prediction-interval / MAP prior is appropriate. Evidence-synthesis lens → informative-prior result may be flagged as biased/over-precise or double-counted, so a weak/neutral primary analysis with informative secondary/sensitivity analysis is safest.
situations: Have a published random-effects meta-analysis of a treatment effect and want to use it as an informative prior for the treatment-effect parameter in a new Bayes
leaves: jsocolar: two distinct goals — replicate (don't strongly inform) vs. narrow (prediction-interval/MAP ok); concordance of prior with prediction interval ~ your belief in the meta-analysis. multinormal

branches.if_observe: goal is to test replication / trial may soon enter a systematic review
branches.then: weak or neutral primary prior; meta-informed prior only as labeled sensitivity analysis to avoid bias/double-counting

branches.if_observe: goal is to narrow uncertainty and trust the evidence base
branches.then: prediction-interval-based or formal MAP prior as primary, transparently documented

action: Reject the inference that simulated-data success exonerates the model; assert the workflow stance that a model failing on structurally-identical real data is too fragile.
discriminates: Whether the diagnosis path should focus on 'what's wrong with the data' vs 'why the model is fragile to this data'
expected_findings: Across-the-board: 'Plenty of models work with simulated data and fail with structurally identical real data.' Simulated success only proves the code runs, not that the model+data geometry is benign. Redirects effort from data-blaming to geometry.
situations: A truncated-normal model (lower bound at 0, y ~ normal(mu, sigma) T[0,]) fit to a large real dataset (~87k obs, grouped by site): step size adapts down to ~1e-9
leaves: Stated workflow assumption: 'if the model can't handle the data I have, it's too fragile' — Stan exposes the fragility, fix that and move on. Simulated truncated normal had MORE mass near 0 yet fit fi

branches.if_observe: user wants to switch distributions (e.g. 3-param Weibull)
branches.then: advise NOT to switch yet — without knowing why it's fragile you'll reproduce the problem in the new distribution

action: Request the iteration-vs-acceptance-rate trace per chain for the real data.
discriminates: Adaptation failure mode: instability (acceptance collapses, stepsize chases it down) vs a genuine high-acceptance-but-stuck state
expected_findings: Boundary/instability hypothesis: acceptance rate trace is erratic/poor, stepsize driven to ~0 trying to recover stability. Observed: 'Not good' — acceptance trace confirms adaptation is failing, consistent with an unstable region the integrator can't step through.
situations: A truncated-normal model (lower bound at 0, y ~ normal(mu, sigma) T[0,]) fit to a large real dataset (~87k obs, grouped by site): step size adapts down to ~1e-9
leaves: Acceptance plots posted for adapt_delta=0.9 / adapt_term_buffer=100 look bad; raising to 0.999 + max_treedepth=14 barely helps and chronically hits max_treedepth.

action: Expand the truncated sampling statement into its explicit log-density and inspect the danger region of that expression.
discriminates: Whether numerical blow-up comes from the truncation normalizing term under bad parameter combos
expected_findings: Truncation-instability: large negative log-cdf / steep gradients when mu is negative AND sigma small, destabilizing the integrator. Observed partial check: user reports varcomp3 not small (min ~0.7) and only mildly negative mu (to ~-2) and few such points -> exact (negative mu)x(tiny sigma) corner not the dominant trigger, but it points at the bound region as the sensitive zone.
situations: A truncated-normal model (lower bound at 0, y ~ normal(mu, sigma) T[0,]) fit to a large real dataset (~87k obs, grouped by site): step size adapts down to ~1e-9
leaves: T[0,] is equivalent to: target += normal_lpdf(y|mu,sigma) - normal_lccdf(0|mu,sigma) (thread wrote it via normal_lcdf). The normalizing term and its derivative are ill-conditioned for negative mu / sm

branches.if_observe: many obs forced near the 0 bound with negative-leaning mu
branches.then: expect the boundary-geometry overflow mechanism to dominate

action: Stratify the raw response by group (site) and look at the per-site distribution near the lower bound, not just counts.

expected_findings: Heterogeneity hypothesis: a few sites carry a disproportionate cluster of tiny near-zero values; removing them removes the pathology. Observed: 3 sites have small extreme values at 2/4/10%; dropping those 3 sites -> model fits cleanly in ~200 iterations.
situations: A truncated-normal model (lower bound at 0, y ~ normal(mu, sigma) T[0,]) fit to a large real dataset (~87k obs, grouped by site): step size adapts down to ~1e-9
leaves: Resolution found by the asker: per-site near-zero extreme-value clusters (a few hundred obs out of 87k) create the curvature the truncation makes pathological; simulated data lacked this group-localiz

action: Give the computational + statistical account of constrained lower bounds.
discriminates: Surface tuning issue vs fundamental constrained-space geometry; and a separate inferential caveat about truncation
expected_findings: Boundary-geometry hypothesis confirmed: lower bound in constrained space maps to -inf in the unconstrained space, so mass at the bound demands large steps that overflow / are unstable -> step-size collapse. Plus the statistical caveat that truncation pushes truncated mass upward, inflating the posterior mean (Bayes, unlike MLE).
situations: A truncated-normal model (lower bound at 0, y ~ normal(mu, sigma) T[0,]) fit to a large real dataset (~87k obs, grouped by site): step size adapts down to ~1e-9
leaves: Bob_Carpenter: 'lower bounds in the constrained space map to negative infinity in the unconstrained space... they tend to require large step sizes, which can be unstable w.r.t. overflow.' Statistical

action: Confirm the failure mode is multimodality before treating it as a 'too little data / run longer' problem: plot the per-chain posterior over changepoint location (softmax of log_p) and the per-chain log_p curve
discriminates: Genuinely multimodal/degenerate posterior (H2/H3) vs a single-mode-but-slow chain that merely needs more iterations vs an outright bug (H4)
expected_findings: H2/H3: chains cluster on different changepoint locations - typically some chains place it mid-document and others pile mass on the very last page(s) (the 'no real change' signature); H1: diffuse, low-information location posterior with no sharp competing modes; healthy: all chains agree on one location.
situations: A marginalized (sum-over-changepoint, dynamic-programming) Stan changepoint model with two simplex emission parameters (theta_e before the change, theta_l after

situations: A marginalized (sum-over-changepoint, dynamic-programming) Stan changepoint model with two simplex emission parameters (theta_e before the change, theta_l after

action: Replace the vague Dirichlet with a weakly-informative one matched to domain shape, and consider deliberately non-symmetric priors on theta_e vs theta_l
discriminates: Whether the non-mixing is fixable by gentle regularization / symmetry-breaking (H5/H3) vs an irreducible data limitation (H1)
expected_findings: A concentrating Dirichlet (alpha<1, e.g. 0.6, reflecting that one word-length category dominates in natural language) restores well-mixed chains and a single changepoint; alpha=1 (uniform) is conceptually simplest for debugging but mixes worse. Distinct, asymmetric priors on theta_e/theta_l are the principled cure for label-switching when no change exists.

action: Test the substantive claim, not just the location: (a) check whether theta_e and theta_l are significantly different; (b) check sensitivity of the location to the assumed NUMBER of changepoints

expected_findings: If theta_e approx theta_l the 'changepoint' is meaningless regardless of location; and choosing 1 vs 2 changepoints can yield non-overlapping locations (more changepoints almost always fit better, like adding mixture components), so a single-change result needs justification and regularization.
situations: A marginalized (sum-over-changepoint, dynamic-programming) Stan changepoint model with two simplex emission parameters (theta_e before the change, theta_l after

action: Recall the general principle: you cannot put a constraint on the output of a transformation; constraints must be declared on the parameters such that they induce the desired constraint on the transform
discriminates: whether the problem is solvable by direct <lower>/<upper> on beta vs needs a reparameterization
