action: Give the positive-definiteness/geometry argument and test whether feasible ranges of single entries collapse with dimension.
discriminates: H3 vs H2 — is it specifically the PD constraint forcing mass to the identity (one large entry 'swamps' the matrix), versus merely the uniform-volume measure shrinking high-correlation regions?
expected_findings: Under H3: as dimension grows the PD-feasible region concentrates at the identity; one large Cholesky-row element leaves little 'stick' for the rest; LKJ scores the whole matrix as a single object and is structure-agnostic.
situations: An LKJ (lkj_corr / lkj_corr_cholesky) prior on a high-dimensional correlation matrix (P on the order of 50-200) produces sampled/prior correlations that concent
leaves: spinkney: PD mass concentrates at diagonal-1s as dim grows; one large row-N element swamps the matrix; LKJ treats the matrix as one 'value' and assumes no structure (blocks etc.); R cor_swap/chol-angl

branches.if_observe: feasible single-entry range stays wide
branches.then: PD-geometry is not the binding constraint

branches.if_observe: feasible range collapses / equicorrelation bound -1/(n-1)<rho
branches.then: PD geometry confirmed as driver (H3)

action: Disambiguate a single entry's range from the joint matrix, and just run Stan to measure the central interval of an off-diagonal entry across N and across extreme eta.
discriminates: Resolves the apparent contradiction (H3 wording) and quantifies the dimension effect; tests whether extreme eta is genuinely inert.
expected_findings: A single isolated entry can range -1..1 (AR(1) rho^|m-n| and a near-empty matrix both work), but the 90% interval of x[1,2] under LKJ(1) shrinks: ~(-0.9,0.9) at N=2, (-0.5,0.5) at N=10, (-0.16,0.16) at N=100; eta=1e-10..1e-10^... barely changes N=10 from ~(-0.55,0.55).
situations: An LKJ (lkj_corr / lkj_corr_cholesky) prior on a high-dimensional correlation matrix (P on the order of 50-200) produces sampled/prior correlations that concent
leaves: Bob_Carpenter: AR(1) and isolated-entry counterexamples (single entry can be ~+/-1), then lkj_corr_rng N=2/10/100 90%-interval measurements + eta=1e-10 still ~0.55 at N=10; spinkney clarifies it's abo

branches.if_observe: single-entry counterexample
branches.then: reframe claim as joint-matrix mass, not per-entry feasibility

branches.if_observe: intervals shrink with N and not with eta
branches.then: dimension dominates eta — proceed to closed form (H4)

action: Write the marginal distribution of a single off-diagonal entry in closed form and read off the dimension-vs-eta scaling.
discriminates: H4 — pins the mechanism to an explicit shape parameter so the eta-inertness and the impossibility of uniform-on-pairs become provable, not just empirical.
expected_findings: (r_ij+1)/2 ~ Beta(eta-1+K/2, eta-1+K/2); the K/2 term swamps eta as K grows, concentrating r_ij at 0; uniform marginal (shape=1) requires eta-1+K/2=1 => K=4-2*eta, so for K>=4 no eta gives a uniform per-pair correlation.
situations: An LKJ (lkj_corr / lkj_corr_cholesky) prior on a high-dimensional correlation matrix (P on the order of 50-200) produces sampled/prior correlations that concent
leaves: jd_c: (r_ij+1)/2 ~ Beta(eta-1+K/2, eta-1+K/2); K dominates eta; uniform-on-pairs impossible for K>=4 (K=4-2eta boundary).

branches.if_observe: marginal Beta shape grows with K
branches.then: explains eta-inertness and zero-concentration analytically — H4 confirmed

action: Enumerate structure-imposing alternatives to a plain whole-matrix LKJ prior.
discriminates: H5 — which external-structure route fits the user's known/unknown structure (blocks vs. agnostic), trading off informativeness against flexibility.
expected_findings: Block/sub-block LKJ priors (better recovery when block structure is known), latent-factor models, equicorrelation/AR-style constructions, or direct canonical-partial-correlation / C-vine parameterizations (tanh-mapped partials -> Cholesky) for per-element control; each requires committing to some structure.
situations: An LKJ (lkj_corr / lkj_corr_cholesky) prior on a high-dimensional correlation matrix (P on the order of 50-200) produces sampled/prior correlations that concent
leaves: spinkney (block-wise LKJ, better simulation recovery when blocks known), jmh530 (regression approach; vine-copula / partial-correlation 'unwrapping' for flexibility; caveat blocks must be known in adv

branches.if_observe: block structure known
branches.then: LKJ-per-block / block prior

branches.if_observe: structure unknown but high correlations expected
branches.then: latent-factor model or direct partial-correlation/vine parameterization

action: Investigate the geometry the divergences flag directly (per Betancourt 'Identity Crisis'): confirm WHICH parameters are degenerate and characterize the degeneracy.
discriminates: scale-vs-RE funnel vs between-upsilon non-identifiability vs Cauchy-tail geometry
expected_findings: If between-element non-identifiability H: degeneracy lives in upsilon[i]-upsilon[j] plane tied to confounded measurement combinations, NOT in s_upsilon-vs-upsilon. If classic-funnel H: degeneracy concentrates in s_upsilon-vs-upsilon neck. Hierarchical scale likely a red herring here.
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model (interval-censored insect development times; temperature-dependent rate curve with an iid latent lack-of-fit term mapped throu
leaves: Color pairs/scatter by divergence, slice upsilon[i] vs upsilon[j] and upsilon vs s_upsilon, and connect degenerate combinations back to the measurement structure (e.g. which temperatures/blocks are ob

branches.if_observe: degeneracy is between upsilon elements tied to how temperatures are observed
branches.then: address identifiability via the measurement model (richer RE structure, e.g. autocorrelated/random-walk prior, or merging confounded effects) rather than just reparameterizing

branches.if_observe: degeneracy concentrates in the scale-vs-RE neck
branches.then: treat as a residual funnel; revisit parameterization / tighten scale prior

action: Swap the heavy-tailed Cauchy latent for a Student-t with moderate degrees of freedom as a diagnostic, then judge whether the distributional choice is statistically justified.
discriminates: Cauchy-tail-induced geometry vs a degeneracy independent of tail weight
expected_findings: If Cauchy-tail H: moderate-df Student-t squashes the divergences. If the degeneracy is structural (between-upsilon): changing tail weight does little.
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model (interval-censored insect development times; temperature-dependent rate curve with an iid latent lack-of-fit term mapped throu
leaves: Replace qcauchy mapping with a Student-t (intermediate df) latent; rerun; if divergences vanish, decide whether t is defensible for the lack-of-fit term (note TMB structure makes this extra work).

branches.if_observe: Student-t removes divergences
branches.then: adopt t after justifying it; the issue was tail geometry, not identifiability

branches.if_observe: divergences persist
branches.then: tail weight is not the cause; keep focus on between-upsilon identifiability

action: Build up from the simplest model and add complexity in iterations, watching when divergences appear.
discriminates: which model component (curve / individual variation / lack-of-fit latent) introduces the bad geometry
expected_findings: Divergences appear precisely when the upsilon lack-of-fit layer is added (consistent with 'removing lack-of-fit removes divergences'), confirming that layer as the locus.
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model (interval-censored insect development times; temperature-dependent rate curve with an iid latent lack-of-fit term mapped throu
leaves: Start from the bare development-rate curve, add individual lognormal variation, then add the upsilon lack-of-fit term last; identify the offending layer.

branches.if_observe: divergences first appear with the upsilon layer
branches.then: concentrate reparameterization/identifiability work on the lack-of-fit term

action: Rebalance the sampler: lengthen warmup and shorten sampling.
discriminates: adaptation artifact vs genuine geometric pathology
expected_findings: If it were adaptation-limited: more warmup reduces divergences. For a real funnel: little effect (and indeed only extreme adapt_delta=0.999 finally clears the few remaining ones here).
situations: A non-centered hierarchical model (interval-censored insect development times; temperature-dependent rate curve with an iid latent lack-of-fit term mapped throu
leaves: Use ~1000-1500 warmup with ~1000-1500 sampling instead of 500/4500; note this generally will NOT fix a true funnel.

branches.if_observe: more warmup clears them
branches.then: it was an adaptation artifact

branches.if_observe: no change
branches.then: confirms a geometric pathology; rely on reparameterization / adapt_delta

action: Step back from the likelihood family and reconstruct the data-generating process end-to-end before choosing a distribution.
discriminates: Whether the misfit is a missing-mechanism problem (H1/H2) or a wrong-family problem (H3), by naming which physical step each candidate distribution is supposed to represent.
expected_findings: H1/H2: enumerating cell-level biology shows overdispersion arises pre-sequencing (latent cell states, unmodeled predictors) → Gamma-Poisson/NB at the count stage. H3: the sequencing step itself looks multinomial-conditional-on-the-input-sample. The generative chain is NB(cell counts) → multinomial(sequencing draw), so a single DM precision is structurally too rigid.
situations: A compositional/count regression (RNA-seq: ~20K gene categories × ~200 samples) is fit as a Dirichlet-multinomial with a single shared precision parameter on so
leaves: Modrák walks through 7 steps (steady-state Poisson → bursting/multimodal at single-cell → bulk averaging + non-steady/heterogeneous states → unmodeled linear-predictor residual → fragmentation/RT → PC

branches.if_observe: User confirms the urn analogy (sample 1M reads from a 1B-molecule urn of 20K colors; per-patient color counts produced by an NB process)
branches.then: Lock in the NB→multinomial two-stage generative target and ask which family reproduces the observed mean-variance curve.

branches.if_observe: User cannot point to a biological mechanism for the extra dispersion
branches.then: Treat overdispersion as an unexplained-residual hack and prefer modeling the mechanism (amplification, competition) directly rather than inflating a dispersion parameter.

action: Reason about the strength of multinomial-induced cross-category correlation given the sum-to-one constraint.
discriminates: H3 (drop multinomial, use independent NB with normalization) vs H1/H2 (keep multinomial but fix its dispersion structure).
expected_findings: H3: because each gene is a tiny fraction of total reads, the negative correlations from the fixed total are negligible → a Poisson/NB approximation to the multinomial is nearly equivalent and far simpler. Counter (Bob): a finite sample that is repeatedly chopped never has an open-ended Poisson/NB count and doesn't normalize properly, so multinomial-conditional-on-input is the principled sequencing model.
situations: A compositional/count regression (RNA-seq: ~20K gene categories × ~200 samples) is fit as a Dirichlet-multinomial with a single shared precision parameter on so
leaves: Modrák notes Poisson/NB ≈ multinomial when per-category share is small and proposes neg_binomial_2 with a sample×gene normalization factor c[i,j] (median-of-ratios / GC-length corrections à la DESeq2)

branches.if_observe: Depth/information-content differences across samples are the main concern
branches.then: Show they can be absorbed by scaling the NB mean (normalization factor), so multinomial is not strictly required for that purpose.

branches.if_observe: User insists on modeling the deep-vs-shallow sequencing uncertainty generatively
branches.then: Keep the multinomial sequencing layer over latent NB/gamma counts.

action: Derive the analytic link between NB, multinomial, and Dirichlet-multinomial to expose the implied mean-variance law.
discriminates: Confirms H1 as the root cause and explains H3's normalization non-identifiability; quantifies exactly which parametrization assumption forces the linear variance scaling.
expected_findings: H1 confirmed: DM = (NB counts) conditioned on the per-sample total, exactly as Multinomial = (Poisson) conditioned on the total — but only under an NB parametrization with Var = λ(1+θ), θ shared across genes. This linear-in-λ variance is what mis-scales: it over-disperses low-λ genes and under-disperses high-λ genes relative to the NB_2 (Var = λ(1+λ/φ), quadratic) that fits RNA-seq. The shared per-sample θ also makes it non-identifiable from any constant per-sample effect — which is why softmax (sum-to-zero) is required in the DM formulation.
situations: A compositional/count regression (RNA-seq: ~20K gene categories × ~200 samples) is fit as a Dirichlet-multinomial with a single shared precision parameter on so
leaves: Modrák, building on Guimarães & Lindrooth (2007): z_{g,s}~NB(λ, θ_s) with Var=λ(1+θ_s); conditioning the multinomial draw on the total yields DM(δ_s^{-1} λ̃_{g,s}) where λ̃=β_g X_s and δ_s^{-1}=θ_s^{-

branches.if_observe: A variance law that grows ~quadratically with the mean is needed
branches.then: Use NB_2 (φ fixed) directly, or a gamma/logNormal-multinomial whose dispersion is mean-dependent — not the single-precision DM.

branches.if_observe: You must keep the closed/compositional multinomial layer
branches.then: Let precision/overdispersion be a function of the mean (or per-gene) so the implied variance can scale faster than λ, recovering the missing flexibility.

action: Refit the simplest possible model dropping the custom prior, letting brms use its default (flat / weakly-informative) priors, and compare coefficient magnitudes.
discriminates: H1 (true null) vs H2 (prior over-regularizes): if estimates stay ~0 -> null; if they jump to tens of ms with Est.Error of similar order -> the N(0,1) prior was the culprit.
expected_findings: Under H2: TOVIDIOM ~45 (CI 19-72), TOVLEX ~18 (CI -9 to 46) - real, non-trivial effects that the N(0,1) prior had crushed. Under H1: would remain near zero even with flat prior.
situations: brms gaussian mixed model where the fixed-effect coefficients all collapse to near-zero with posterior SD ~= the prior SD (e.g. estimates 0.05, 0.02, -0.03 with
leaves: brm(Total_duration_of_whole_fixations ~ TOV, cores=4, chains=4) with NO prior= argument; inspect Population-Level Effects.

branches.if_observe: coefficients jump to tens of ms once the prior is removed
branches.then: confirm prior-scale mismatch (H2); the normal(0,1) was far tighter than the coefficient scale -> either drop it or rescale the prior to the outcome scale (or standardize the outcome).

branches.if_observe: coefficients remain ~0 under default priors
branches.then: treat as genuine null / no detectable effect (H1).

action: Tighten the NUTS sampler (raise adapt_delta, raise max_treedepth, more iterations) to clear residual divergences in the hierarchical model.
discriminates: H4 (residual divergences are tuning-curable) vs a deeper funnel needing reparameterization.
expected_findings: Divergences clear and Rhat returns to 1.00 with adequate ESS, confirming the remaining issue was step-size/tree-depth tuning, not model structure.
situations: brms gaussian mixed model where the fixed-effect coefficients all collapse to near-zero with posterior SD ~= the prior SD (e.g. estimates 0.05, 0.02, -0.03 with
leaves: control = list(adapt_delta=0.99, max_treedepth=12), iter=3000; re-examine divergence count and Rhat/ESS.

branches.if_observe: divergences gone, all Rhat 1.00, ESS in thousands
branches.then: accept the fit; proceed to interpretation and prior documentation.

branches.if_observe: divergences persist after adapt_delta=0.99
branches.then: move to non-centered parameterization for the hierarchical terms (referenced: Betancourt hierarchical modeling case study).

action: Plot the prior density and align its location/scale with the outcome's natural scale before committing; reject degenerate priors.
discriminates: well-specified weakly-informative prior vs a prior that is either degenerate or on the wrong scale (the same failure mode that produced the original collapsed estimates).
expected_findings: A bound like normal(-1,0) is degenerate/one-sided and samples badly; a centered normal(-0.5,0.5) (on a standardized scale) is sane. Reinforces that prior scale must match coefficient scale - the root cause from move 1.
situations: brms gaussian mixed model where the fixed-effect coefficients all collapse to near-zero with posterior SD ~= the prior SD (e.g. estimates 0.05, 0.02, -0.03 with
leaves: Plot normal(-1,0) (degenerate: zero SD, all mass at -1) vs normal(-0.5,0.5); set prior scale relative to the response scale (hundreds of ms), document each prior's justification.

branches.if_observe: prior plotted is degenerate or far off the data scale
branches.then: rescale: standardize the outcome or widen the prior to match the ms scale; document justification per parameter.

branches.if_observe: no prior information for a term (e.g. Group / interaction)
branches.then: leave it on the brms default (weakly informative), don't force a custom prior.

action: Reframe inference around the meaning/uncertainty of each coefficient (contrast vs the reference level) using the credible interval, not a significance test.
discriminates: correct Bayesian interpretation (parameter = contrast to reference category; quantify uncertainty) vs frequentist significance dichotomy.
expected_findings: Coefficients are differences from the alphabetically-first level (COMP), which is absorbed into the Intercept; 'significance' is replaced by where the credible interval sits relative to zero plus domain judgment on magnitude.
situations: brms gaussian mixed model where the fixed-effect coefficients all collapse to near-zero with posterior SD ~= the prior SD (e.g. estimates 0.05, 0.02, -0.03 with
leaves: Read l-95% CI / u-95% CI: interval excluding 0 (e.g. TOVIDIOM 19-72) = consistent positive effect vs reference (COMP=intercept); interval spanning 0 (TOVLEX -9 to 46) = direction undetermined. Each co

branches.if_observe: CI excludes zero
branches.then: report a directional effect of that magnitude relative to the reference level, judging practical size by domain knowledge.

branches.if_observe: CI spans zero
branches.then: report uncertainty in direction rather than 'no effect'; consider whether the magnitude is still practically meaningful.

action: Clarify the meaning of the default (centered) intercept vs. user's intended interpretation; recommend a reparameterization that aligns prior meaning with intent
discriminates: whether the prior the user is about to set controls 'expected response at predictor means' (centered) vs 'expected response when raw predictors = 0' (uncentered)
expected_findings: Default Intercept = centered => prior is about response at predictor means; with 0/1 dummies, 'at means' != 'at reference level'. Using 0 + Intercept removes centering so the intercept = response at raw predictors=0 and the prior is set as class b.
situations: A brms user is confused about what prior to set on the intercept and/or coefficients. Two flavors appear: (a) the docs say the Intercept-class prior is set on a
leaves: Recommend formula y ~ 0 + Intercept + x1*x2 + (x1*x2|group): 0 drops the centered default intercept, Intercept becomes an ordinary population-level effect (class b) interpretable as y at reference cel

branches.if_observe: user wants prior to reflect belief about grand mean / response at average predictor values
branches.then: keep the default centered Intercept (class Intercept); it is easier and more intuitive to set there

branches.if_observe: user wants prior to reflect response at raw predictors=0 (reference cell, dummy coding)
branches.then: use 0 + Intercept and set prior on class b

branches.if_observe: model is non-linear (nl=TRUE)
branches.then: there is no special Intercept class; the per-parameter intercept is always class b — use 0 + factor to get a separate value per level, set priors via nlpar

action: Distinguish linear vs non-linear formula handling of the intercept and demonstrate equivalence of reference-coded vs 0+factor parameterizations
discriminates: whether the special centered Intercept machinery applies (linear) or not (non-linear), and whether 1+f vs 0+f change the fit or only the parameterization
expected_findings: In nl formulas the intercept is always class b (no centering). 1 + f estimates a reference-level intercept plus contrast; 0 + f estimates a separate intercept per level; conditional_effects shows identical fitted values within simulation error.
situations: A brms user is confused about what prior to set on the intercept and/or coefficients. Two flavors appear: (a) the docs say the Intercept-class prior is set on a
leaves: Show fit1 (b1 ~ 1 + f) vs fit2 (b1 ~ 0 + f) on a simulated 2-level exp() model; conditional_effects('x:f') overlays are identical — only the coefficient interpretation/prior placement differs, set pri

action: Resist the surface symptoms; back up and check prior support against the parameter's scale before debugging hypothesis()/sample_prior
discriminates: whether the trouble is a hypothesis() syntax / sample_prior limitation (surface) vs a fundamental prior-scale mismatch with the link function
expected_findings: binomial()/bernoulli() default to logit link => intercept and age_class coefficients are log-odds, ranging (-inf, inf). A beta(1,1) prior is constrained to [0,1], so it cannot cover the coefficient's natural range — this is the root cause; NA evidence ratios are a downstream/secondary symptom.
situations: A brms user is confused about what prior to set on the intercept and/or coefficients. Two flavors appear: (a) the docs say the Intercept-class prior is set on a
leaves: Diagnose: priors on log-odds-scale coefficients must have unbounded support; replace beta(1,1) with Gaussian/Student-t. Suggested generic default normal(0,1) for log-odds coefficients. (Also: bernoull

branches.if_observe: user intends to model on the log-odds (logit) scale (recommended)
branches.then: use unbounded priors — normal(0,1) as a generic start, Student-t for heavier tails; do NOT use beta() on class b

branches.if_observe: user truly wants a probability-scale [0,1] prior
branches.then: that requires a different link/parameterization; on logit scale beta(1,1) is invalid

branches.if_observe: asking about a real scientific analysis rather than a generic default
branches.then: go beyond normal(0,1): consult McElreath (Statistical Rethinking 2e) and Gelman et al. Bayesian Workflow (arXiv:2011.01808) for prior reasoning / prior predictive checks

action: Build a minimal reproducible pair: code the same log density two ways (implicit `<multiplier=sigma_a>` vs explicit `a = sigma_a*e_a`) on simulated hierarchical data and compare warning streams and inferences
discriminates: Spurious-artifact vs Hidden-error-in-other-path: does the no-warning model truly avoid the numerical event, or is it just masking the same event?
expected_findings: Both fit and converge to the same posterior; implicit version emits many `multiplier is 0` warnings, explicit version emits only one/two `normal_lpdf: Scale parameter is 0` warnings. Same underlying underflow surfaces differently depending on which constrain function sees the zero.
situations: A hierarchical model fit with the non-centered `vector<multiplier=sigma_a>[J] a` parameterization throws repeated warmup-only warnings "offset_multiplier_constr
leaves: scaling_1.stan (implicit multiplier) vs scaling_2.stan (a = sigma_a*e_a); R/cmdstanr sim N=1000,J=50,sigma_y=2,sigma_a=0.5; compare fit$summary() and warning counts

branches.if_observe: Same posterior, different warnings, and explicit version still emits a scale=0 warning
branches.then: Reject pure Spurious-artifact: the event is real underflow; the explicit version merely relocates the message (to normal_lpdf), so dismiss the 'hand-coded is error-free' framing

branches.if_observe: Explicit version emits zero warnings only because normal_lpdf was hand-rewritten to skip the check
branches.then: Confirm Hidden-error: the difference is suppression, not absence of underflow

action: Trace the constrain transform semantics: write out a = offset + multiplier * a_unc and reason about what happens to gradients/dimension when multiplier -> 0
discriminates: Spurious-artifact vs Dimension-collapse: is multiplier=0 a benign rounding or a degeneracy that severs the gradient?
expected_findings: At multiplier s=0, a[j] = offset for all j, so J parameters collapse to one degree of freedom; the unconstrained a decouple from the target gradient and a log(0) Jacobian adds +/-inf. Constant multiplier=0 is a genuinely bad (rank-deficient) model; dynamic underflow to 0 is the transient version of the same collapse.
situations: A hierarchical model fit with the non-centered `vector<multiplier=sigma_a>[J] a` parameterization throws repeated warmup-only warnings "offset_multiplier_constr
leaves: transform a = m + s*a_unc; Jacobian for the vector case is target += J*log(sigma_a); at s=0 -> log(0) singularity and zero gradient w.r.t. e_a

branches.if_observe: multiplier=0 collapses dimension and kills gradient
branches.then: Upgrade significance: the warning marks a real structural event, not a cosmetic artifact; keep it as a diagnostic

action: Check the constrained/unconstrained boundary: determine which variable is actually exposed to the model block after a `real<lower=0>` declaration
discriminates: Spurious-artifact (works on log scale, never zero) vs Real-underflow (constrained exp() is what enters likelihood and that underflows)
expected_findings: Stan samples log(sigma_a) unconstrained, but every iteration computes sigma_a = exp(log_sigma) and exposes only sigma_a; once log_sigma is very negative, exp() underflows to literal 0, which then enters normal_lpdf / the multiplier constrain. The log-scale argument does not save you because the exponentiated value is what the rest of the program sees.
situations: A hierarchical model fit with the non-centered `vector<multiplier=sigma_a>[J] a` parameterization throws repeated warmup-only warnings "offset_multiplier_constr
leaves: real<lower=0> sigma => underlying param is log(sigma); constrained sigma=exp(.) recomputed and exposed each iteration; to stay on log scale you must declare real log_sigma + transformed sigma=exp(log_

branches.if_observe: Constrained exp(log_sigma) is the exposed variable and underflows
branches.then: Reject the 'always works on log scale' rationale; the underflow into the likelihood is real

branches.if_observe: User proposes clamping log(sigma) >= -300
branches.then: Reject: introduces a discontinuity / constant region you cannot autodiff through; not a viable fix

action: Vary the prior on sigma_a (lognormal vs half-normal vs informative normal) and re-fit, watching whether warnings persist
discriminates: Prior-support-mismatch & Funnel-tip vs Real-underflow/step-size: is the warning caused by the prior cutting off 0, or by warmup dynamics independent of the prior?
expected_findings: Warnings persist across lognormal, half-normal, and a strong informative prior that removes the funnel tip. So the trigger is not the prior support and not the funnel neck per se -- it is the warmup step-size/leapfrog instability driving log(sigma_a) down. (Caveat per Bob: with a prior whose support excludes 0, sigma_a=0 should additionally throw at the prior, but lognormal_lpdf failed to.)
situations: A hierarchical model fit with the non-centered `vector<multiplier=sigma_a>[J] a` parameterization throws repeated warmup-only warnings "offset_multiplier_constr
leaves: add sigma_a ~ lognormal(1,1) then sigma_a ~ normal(1,1) [half-normal]; warnings essentially unchanged either way; informative prior knocking out funnel tip still warns

branches.if_observe: Warnings persist regardless of prior, including a tight informative prior
branches.then: Reject Prior-support-mismatch as the cause; attribute to warmup step-size dynamics (folk theorem)

branches.if_observe: lognormal prior does NOT throw at sigma_a=0
branches.then: Spawn a separate-bug investigation: a positive-support density is silently accepting 0

action: Attribute mechanism via HMC warmup reasoning: smaller step size -> more accurate Hamiltonian trajectories -> fewer excursions into numerically unstable (deep-funnel) regions during equilibration
discriminates: Real-underflow/Funnel vs Spurious-artifact: if a sampler-config knob deterministically controls the warning, it is a dynamics/geometry signal, not a coding artifact
expected_findings: Lowering initial step size eliminates the transient warmup warnings (at the cost of slower warmup); the warning rate tracks trajectory instability, confirming the event is the leapfrog overshooting log(sigma_a) -- a real geometry signal even when confined to warmup.
situations: A hierarchical model fit with the non-centered `vector<multiplier=sigma_a>[J] a` parameterization throws repeated warmup-only warnings "offset_multiplier_constr
leaves: folk theorem of warmup: large initial potential energy converts to kinetic energy during equilibration, large step size overshoots into unstable regions; smaller stepsize0 mitigates

branches.if_observe: Step-size reduction removes warnings
branches.then: Confirm the warning is a genuine (if transient) numerical-stability indicator tied to warmup geometry; conclude suppress-the-messenger (quiet-during-warmup option) is the only 'fix', not an epsilon offset

action: Audit the offending check in the math library: locate where sigma_a=0 should throw at the prior and confirm the constraint used
discriminates: Separate-bug vs expected behavior: is the prior correctly rejecting the boundary value?
expected_findings: lognormal_lpdf uses check_nonnegative (allows 0) where it should use check_positive_finite, since 0 is outside the lognormal support (gamma_lpdf does it correctly). This is why sigma_a=0 under a lognormal prior did not throw as expected -> a real bug, filed against stan-dev/math.
situations: A hierarchical model fit with the non-centered `vector<multiplier=sigma_a>[J] a` parameterization throws repeated warmup-only warnings "offset_multiplier_constr
leaves: lognormal_lpdf.hpp line ~26-27: check_not_nan + check_nonnegative on the random variable; should be check_positive_finite; filed stan-dev/math issue

branches.if_observe: check_nonnegative present where check_positive_finite required
branches.then: File bug; note that once fixed, both codings would throw at a lognormal prior on sigma_a=0 and neither would throw under a half-normal (which IS consistent with 0)

action: Extract draw-wise (per-MCMC-sample) total log-likelihood from the Bayesian fit; take its summary and max
discriminates: H2 (a draw beats ML) vs everything else
expected_findings: H2: at least one draw's total LL exceeds the MLE. H1/H5: max over draws is <= MLE, and the bulk are substantially below — the 'win' is NOT in any sampled parameter vector.
situations: A Bayesian Bernoulli/logistic regression (brms) reports a log predictive score (elpd_loo + p_loo, equivalently a manual lppd from fitted values) that is HIGHER/

branches.if_observe: some draw exceeds MLE

action: Reproduce the high log-score manually by plugging the model's fitted values into the Bernoulli log-density and compare to plugging posterior-mean COEFFICIENTS through the link

situations: A Bayesian Bernoulli/logistic regression (brms) reports a log predictive score (elpd_loo + p_loo, equivalently a manual lppd from fitted values) that is HIGHER/
leaves: fitted(baymod)[,1] are response-scale posterior predictive means; manual lppd on them = -1453.191; plogis(X %*% colMeans(b_)) gives -1453.282 == logLik(glm). The gap is precisely average(f(theta)) vs

branches.if_observe: fitted-value score high BUT mean-coefficient score == MLE (observed)
branches.then: confirm H5; the Bayesian point estimates DO agree with the MLE — the inflation is purely from response-scale averaging (Jensen), so glm is fine

branches.if_observe: mean-coefficient score also beats glm
branches.then: glm truly failed to find MLE — pursue H3/H4

action: Verify the IRLS solution is the MLE by re-fitting glm from a different start (the brms linear predictor) and checking it returns to the same solution; cross-check via an independent optimizer (rstanarm optimizing, prior=NULL)

situations: A Bayesian Bernoulli/logistic regression (brms) reports a log predictive score (elpd_loo + p_loo, equivalently a manual lppd from fitted values) that is HIGHER/
leaves: glm(..., etastart = brms linear predictor) converges back to identical fit; the lone high-x2 point pulls glm only marginally; canonical-link IRLS == MLE up to numerical tolerance

branches.if_observe: IRLS returns to same solution (observed)
branches.then: H4 rejected; glm is at the MLE — the apparent loss is definitional, not optimization failure

branches.if_observe: different higher-LL solution found
branches.then: real glm convergence problem; report it separately

action: Reduce to the single offending predictor (univariate y ~ x2), plot both fits, and inspect the influential high-x2 observation
discriminates: an influential-point optimization explanation (H4) vs the averaging/definitional explanation (H5)
expected_findings: H4: glm fit visibly mis-pulled by the x2=51 point and recoverable by re-optimization. H5: glm IS at the MLE; the high-x2 point pulls glm slightly more than brms, but starting IRLS from the brms predictor still returns the same MLE — so the gap is still average(f) vs f(average).
situations: A Bayesian Bernoulli/logistic regression (brms) reports a log predictive score (elpd_loo + p_loo, equivalently a manual lppd from fitted values) that is HIGHER/
leaves: univariate y ~ x2 reproduces the phenomenon; plot shows brms (red) vs glm (blue); x2=51 influential but does not change that glm == MLE; reaffirms the response-scale-averaging account

branches.if_observe: re-optimization from brms start returns same glm solution
branches.then: influential-point story does not overturn MLE-status; converge on H5

action: Characterize the exact invariance group of the factorization (enumerate which transforms leave the likelihood unchanged)
discriminates: sign-only vs. permutation vs. full rotational non-identifiability — i.e. how much structure a constraint must remove
expected_findings: Sign+permutation are visible immediately; the general statement U V' = (U R)(R^{-1} R) V' for any invertible R reveals these are special cases of a continuous rotation symmetry, so any fix must pin a full orthogonal/rotation matrix, not just signs.
situations: A Bayesian low-rank / reduced-rank regression where the coefficient matrix is factored B = U V' (rank k < min(p,q)). For k=1 an ad-hoc sign constraint works, bu
leaves: Ad-hoc fixes (signed-sum-of-squares to point V positive; positive_ordered diagonal S in Y=XUSV') address sign/scale but NOT permutation or rotation, which is why k>1 still fails.

branches.if_observe: only sign flips matter (effectively k=1 per column, well-separated)
branches.then: a per-column sign/orientation constraint may suffice

branches.if_observe: permutation + rotation present (k>1)
branches.then: need a structural constraint that fixes the whole rotation — move to triangular/Cholesky parameterization

action: Impose a triangular structure on one factor (rectangular Cholesky / lower-trapezoidal factor with positive diagonal)
discriminates: whether the non-identifiability is removed structurally vs. merely post-hoc sign-corrected
expected_findings: Lower-trapezoidal with positive diagonal: columns can't be swapped without destroying the trapezoidal zeros, and signs can't flip without making a diagonal negative — so it removes both. This is the 'gold standard' (Farouni / Leung & Drton).
situations: A Bayesian low-rank / reduced-rank regression where the coefficient matrix is factored B = U V' (rank k < min(p,q)). For k=1 an ad-hoc sign constraint works, bu
leaves: Use cholesky_factor_cov[p,k] (or hand-built lower-trapezoidal B via diags>0 + lowtrap entries, ntrap = p*k - k(k-1)/2 - k). Cost: the column order of X (or B) now carries meaning — column j only loads

branches.if_observe: concerned about ordering artifact
branches.then: consider orthogonal-matrix parameterizations (Cayley transform) as an alternative — but permutation issue persists, so often not worth it

action: Check whether each diagonal (the LQ/Cholesky diagonal) is large relative to its column
discriminates: binding constraint (well-separated factor, unimodal) vs. non-binding constraint (near-zero diagonal, constraint fails to enforce uniqueness)
expected_findings: The triangular constraint only enforces unimodality when the diagonal coefficients are LARGE relative to the rest of the column. If the model fits about equally with a diagonal positive or negative (true value ≈ 0), the constraint is cosmetic and modes return.
situations: A Bayesian low-rank / reduced-rank regression where the coefficient matrix is factored B = U V' (rank k < min(p,q)). For k=1 an ad-hoc sign constraint works, bu
leaves: Permute the columns of X so that predictors with large absolute loadings land on the diagonal: estimate beta via least squares (Y=Xβ), do QR/QRP of β' (pivoted QR for ordering), put the largest-|loadi

branches.if_observe: diagonals still near zero for some columns
branches.then: that latent factor is genuinely weak/unidentified — accept reduced effective rank or stronger shrinkage

action: Set informative, shared initial values near an approximate ML solution (same init across chains, lightly jittered)
discriminates: whether residual multimodality is the sampler exploring symmetric modes vs. a true modeling defect
expected_findings: Initializing all chains at the same approximate-ML point (from the QR-decomposed least-squares β, rescaled so A's L2 norm matches the prior expectation √(c·k)) lets chains agree; cheating with the true simulated values 'works' but is implausible for real data, hence the ML-init recipe.
situations: A Bayesian low-rank / reduced-rank regression where the coefficient matrix is factored B = U V' (rank k < min(p,q)). For k=1 an ad-hoc sign constraint works, bu
leaves: iB = L(1:k,:)'/√(numel A); iA = Q(:,1:k)·√(numel A); flip signs so diagonals positive; jitter ~N(0, 0.05) per chain. Note Stan init must be supplied as init=list(...) of parameter values, not transfor

branches.if_observe: chains agree after shared init
branches.then: non-identifiability was the cause; proceed

branches.if_observe: still diverging modes
branches.then: deeper reparameterization needed

action: Work with identifiable quantities and decompose them post-hoc, accepting residual SVD ambiguity
discriminates: what is genuinely recoverable from the posterior vs. what is constraint-artifact
expected_findings: Individual B, A are not uniquely defined (only LQ-rotated versions are sampled). The identifiable object is the product L·Q·A' (equivalently the low-rank covariance L Σ L'). Post-hoc SVD of each posterior sample gives singular vectors/values BUT SVD itself is non-identifiable (sign switches per vector; permutation when singular values cross), so cross-sample ordering becomes inconsistent.
situations: A Bayesian low-rank / reduced-rank regression where the coefficient matrix is factored B = U V' (rank k < min(p,q)). For k=1 an ad-hoc sign constraint works, bu
leaves: Interpret via L Σ L' as the low-rank covariance of the first elements (the triangular L acts like a Cholesky factor of that block); SVD-per-sample is only clean when singular values are well-separated

branches.if_observe: singular values well-separated
branches.then: per-sample SVD with sign-alignment is usable

branches.if_observe: singular values close / crossing
branches.then: ordering flips across samples — report L Σ L' or predictive quantities instead

action: Locate where the data-only deterministic quantity (Toeplitz convolution matrix) is declared/computed relative to the program blocks
discriminates: H1 (wasted recompute in transformed parameters) vs H2/H3 (genuine sampling cost)

discriminates: H4 (likelihood non-identifiability of f from a diffuse convolution) vs a benign well-identified problem

action: Run the fit with a deliberately wrong/mismatched event sequence and inspect whether the GP marginal SD (alpha) inflates to flag the misfit
discriminates: Whether uncertainty is correctly attributed to the latent function (alpha) vs being absorbed by the measurement noise sigma_obs (H5)

branches.if_observe: alpha inflates strongly under wrong sequence

action: Move the noise from a likelihood term into the GP kernel diagonal (use sigma_obs^2 on the Gram-matrix diagonal instead of a tiny jitter / separate normal likelihood) and observe the effect on alpha and on f

situations: A Stan GP model (latent 1D function f drawn via Cholesky of an exponentiated-quadratic kernel, convolved with a known binary event vector via a Toeplitz matrix,

action: Reason about MAP vs posterior mean and how reliability scales with #params relative to #observations
discriminates: When point-estimation (MAP) is adequate vs when full posterior sampling is required
expected_findings: MAP ≠ posterior mean; the objective is non-convex so a found optimum may be local; reliability degrades as #parameters grows; optimizing can work only when #parameters << #observations (works for the simplest birthday model, fails for richer ones)
situations: A Stan GP model (latent 1D function f drawn via Cholesky of an exponentiated-quadratic kernel, convolved with a known binary event vector via a Toeplitz matrix,
leaves: Timonen: optimizing targets MAP, not posterior mean; non-convex so local-optimum risk grows with dimension. Vehtari: optimizing OK when params << observations; good for simplest birthday model, not th

branches.then: use full MCMC; MAP unreliable and gives no honest uncertainty

action: Map each candidate formula to the latent structure it actually implies (pooled varying-effects vs fixed cell-means), rather than matching surface syntax.
discriminates: H1 vs H2 vs H3 — distinguishes a multilevel/partial-pooling parameterization from a fixed-effect index one; clarifies whether (1|g) is what the user wants.
expected_findings: (1|treatment)+(1|actor) IS a valid multilevel model and gives results close to ulam because ulam's a[actor] with a shared prior is itself mild pooling — but it adds an estimated group SD and shrinks, so it is NOT a fixed single-level index model. 0+factor(...) is closer to a fixed index model but has the dummy-coding pitfalls.
situations: Want to reproduce a rethinking/Stan "index" model logit(p)=a[actor]+b[treatment] in brms, where each category gets its own coefficient with a category-specific
leaves: Buerkner (post 2): 'Option 1 seems correct to me (and is also a multilevel model).' At this stage the user's stated goal is just 'reproduce m11.4', and a[actor]~N(0,1.5) in ulam behaves like a varying

branches.then: Stop — Option 1 is correct; move on to interpreting its priors.

action: Identify the unexplained rows as the group-level standard-deviation hyperparameters of the multilevel parameterization, and point to the canonical ?set_prior / brmsprior documentation for the naming scheme.
discriminates: Confirms H1/H4 — the (1|g) formula carries an SD hyperparameter (sd_<group>_Intercept) per grouping factor; their very existence signals partial pooling, i.e. this is NOT the fixed index model.
expected_findings: The empty-coef rows are the overall SD class for each grouping factor; the lone student_t(3,0,10) is the default half-Student-t prior on those group SDs, scaled to the response SD on the link scale. These are hyperpriors on shrinkage, absent from a pure fixed-effect model.
situations: Want to reproduce a rethinking/Stan "index" model logit(p)=a[actor]+b[treatment] in brms, where each category gets its own coefficient with a category-specific

action: Diagnose the dummy-coding failure mode of 0+factor(f1)+factor(f2): only the first factor becomes full cell-means; the second is contrast-coded so one level is absorbed into the (suppressed) intercept and can't get its own prior.
discriminates: H2 — explains WHY Option 2 gives different results and why one level's prior is inaccessible; rules out 0+factor() as the clean route.
expected_findings: With two factors and 0+, brms keeps all levels of the first factor but drops a reference level of the second; you cannot give the two predictors different class=b priors without per-coef enumeration, and the dropped level has no nameable coefficient.
situations: Want to reproduce a rethinking/Stan "index" model logit(p)=a[actor]+b[treatment] in brms, where each category gets its own coefficient with a category-specific

branches.then: 0+factor(f) alone is fine (full cell-means, one prior block) — no need for nl syntax.

branches.if_observe: TWO+ indexed factors needing DISTINCT priors / full cell-means each

action: Reparameterize with the brms non-linear formula: split the linear predictor into named nlpars, give each nlpar its own 0+factor design, and set one prior per nlpar.
discriminates: H3 — confirms the non-linear syntax is the faithful, pool-free translation of a[actor]+b[treatment] with predictor-specific priors.
expected_findings: bf(pulled_left|trials(1) ~ a+b, a ~ 0+actor, b ~ 0+treatment, nl=TRUE) with prior(normal(0,1.5),nlpar='a')+prior(normal(0,0.5),nlpar='b') reproduces the index model: each level of each factor gets its own coefficient, all reachable, and the two priors are cleanly separated by nlpar (no per-coef enumeration, no dropped level).

leaves: Buerkner (post 9) gives exactly this formula; user (post 10) confirms 'works like a charm.' Solomon/Kurz (post 11) adopts it. This is the terminal resolution — the canonical brms recipe for index/cell

action: Distinguish fixed cell-means (0+gender) from random-effect specifications, and explain what dropping the intercept does to a random coefficient's mean.
discriminates: Separates 'fixed per-level coefficient' (0+gender, what the index model wants) from 'random effect with/without a free mean' (0+(1|g) forces mean 0; 1+(1|g) frees it).
expected_findings: 0+(1|gender) implies the random coef has mean exactly 0 (b~Normal(0,sigma^2)); 1+(1|gender) frees the mean (b~Normal(beta,sigma^2)); but for a genuine fixed index model you want 0+gender, which gives a separate fixed coefficient per level and no shrinkage. An alternative compact multilevel form is y ~ height + (1+height||gender) (|| = uncorrelated effects).

action: Attribute the slowdown to lost automatic predictor centering and recommend manual centering of predictors (never the response).
discriminates: Efficiency, not correctness — explains why nl syntax is slower and how to recover speed without changing the model's meaning.
expected_findings: brms auto-centers predictors only in models WITH an intercept; the non-linear / intercept-suppressed (0+) parameterization skips that, hurting geometry/sampling. Centering predictors yourself restores efficiency. Do NOT center the response (breaks count/non-Gaussian structure; no benefit even for Gaussian).

action: Reframe: separate the model-to-fit from the algorithm-used-to-fit-it; ask whether Gibbs is a requirement or just the reference implementation

action: Inspect how the sum over discrete states is accumulated — linear-scale addition of log-likelihood contributions vs proper log-domain marginalization
discriminates: H3 (log-scale summation bug) vs H5 (non-identifiability) vs a likelihood-derivation error
expected_findings: Bug found: contributions were summed as if adding probabilities while they are log-probabilities. Marginalizing a sum of probabilities on the log scale requires target += log_sum_exp(loglik_terms), not target += sum(loglik_terms). Fixing this is expected to give sensible posteriors.
situations: Want to fit a Bayesian "no gold standard" diagnostic-test model (estimate disease prevalence π, test sensitivity S, specificity C from positive/negative counts)

action: Assess computational scaling of the explicit discrete sum, and consider reparameterizing to model observed frequencies/probabilities directly
discriminates: H4 — whether explicit marginalization is viable, or a frequency/multinomial parameterization is needed
expected_findings: Nested Y1×Y2 sum is O(a·b) and becomes infeasible at n in the thousands. The cleaner route models the observed cell frequencies directly: per-test positive probability p_i = (1−γ_i)(1−π) + δ_i·π (γ=specificity, δ=sensitivity), or for k binary tests a multinomial over 2^k cells — no explicit latent-count sum needed.
situations: Want to fit a Bayesian "no gold standard" diagnostic-test model (estimate disease prevalence π, test sensitivity S, specificity C from positive/negative counts)

action: Set reasonable initial values and constrain parameters to break symmetry

situations: Want to fit a Bayesian "no gold standard" diagnostic-test model (estimate disease prevalence π, test sensitivity S, specificity C from positive/negative counts)

action: Examine the conditional-independence assumption underlying the simple per-test product likelihood
discriminates: H6 — whether tests can be treated as conditionally independent given true status
expected_findings: The product/multinomial likelihood assumes conditional independence given true disease status. This is often violated: similar tests (two PCRs; X-ray vs MRI) give correlated errors, and case difficulty (heavy infection) makes all tests jointly more likely positive — structure beyond a 0/1 latent state.
situations: Want to fit a Bayesian "no gold standard" diagnostic-test model (estimate disease prevalence π, test sensitivity S, specificity C from positive/negative counts)
leaves: Independence-given-true-status is a modeling choice, not a fact. Correlated-error / random-effect (case difficulty) extensions are warranted when tests share mechanism; conditional independence can bi

action: Contrast an unconstrained-parameter model against a constrained-parameter model (e.g. real mu vs real<lower=0> sigma)
discriminates: H1/H3 vs H2 — does the discrepancy appear only when a parameter is constrained?
expected_findings: H2: with NO constrained params the log-Jacobian is 0, so jacobian=TRUE and FALSE give identical estimates; introduce real<lower=0> and they diverge. H1/H3 cannot explain why the presence of a constraint is the switch.
situations: Stan/cmdstanr optimize() returns a different point estimate with jacobian=TRUE vs jacobian=FALSE on a constrained parameter (e.g. theta in [0,1] or sigma>0), ev
leaves: Unconstrained mu model: jacobian flag has zero effect (log|J|=0). Add real<lower=0> sigma with exp() constraint: equivalent to target += sigma_unc; the +sigma_unc term IS the Jacobian, and dropping it

action: Identify which density each optimization maximizes and map to MLE vs MAP
discriminates: Confirms H2 and kills H4's polarity confusion
expected_findings: jacobian=FALSE maximizes the constrained-space density -> mode in constrained space == (penalized) MLE; jacobian=TRUE maximizes p_B(b), the unconstrained-space target density Stan uses for sampling -> MAP. Explicit priors act as penalty terms either way; they are NOT what's being added/dropped.
situations: Stan/cmdstanr optimize() returns a different point estimate with jacobian=TRUE vs jacobian=FALSE on a constrained parameter (e.g. theta in [0,1] or sigma>0), ev
leaves: Polarity (counterintuitive to many): jacobian=FALSE -> mode in CONSTRAINED space (MLE-like); jacobian=TRUE -> mode in UNCONSTRAINED space (MAP, the Bayesian target). The Jacobian is the change-of-vari

action: Work the simplest closed-form example explicitly (e.g. real<lower=0> A ~ lognormal(5,2))
discriminates: Resolves residual H4 polarity confusion with an analytic check
expected_findings: Constrained MLE: argmax lognormal(a|5,2) = exp(5-2^2)=e. Transform B=log A: p_B(b)=lognormal(exp(b)|5,2)*exp(b). jacobian=FALSE recovers b*=1 -> a*=e (matches constrained MLE). jacobian=TRUE maximizes p_B including the *exp(b) Jacobian -> different mode = MAP.
situations: Stan/cmdstanr optimize() returns a different point estimate with jacobian=TRUE vs jacobian=FALSE on a constrained parameter (e.g. theta in [0,1] or sigma>0), ev
leaves: Analytic worked example confirms: drop Jacobian -> back-transform of unconstrained mode equals the constrained-space MLE; keep Jacobian -> mode of the actually-sampled density (MAP). Pointer to Vehtar

action: Replace the ad-hoc Op with a clean general Integrate Op: make_node returns a dscalar output of the integrand's own type; perform calls scipy.quad over the scalar var; grad implements Leibniz's rule explicitly.

action: Inspect how grad() re-instantiates the Op: check that extra_vars are splatted, not passed as a single list/tuple.

action: Identify the closed-form posterior and locate where lp__-argmax lands on it (mode vs mean)
discriminates: whether lp__ is the log density in constrained-lambda space (argmax=mode) or in some transformed space (argmax shifted toward the mean)
expected_findings: Constrained-density hypothesis: argmax-lp__ = mode = sum(x)/n. Transformed-density hypothesis: argmax-lp__ = (sum(x)+1)/n = posterior mean of Gamma(sum(x)+1,n) = mode of the density expressed in log_lambda space.
situations: In Stan/rstan the MCMC draw that maximizes lp__ (lambda[which.max(mcmc$lp__)]) does NOT match the MAP/MLE returned by optimizing(). For a Poisson model with a l
leaves: Gamma(sum(x)+1,n): mode (alpha-1)/beta = sum(x)/n = 9.0, mean alpha/beta = (sum(x)+1)/n = 9.5; lp__-argmax sits exactly at the mean, signalling lp__ is being maximized in a transformed coordinate, not

branches.if_observe: argmax-lp__ = posterior mean = mode-in-log-space

action: Inspect Stan's lp__ construction: ~ drops normalizing constants, lower=0 triggers an unconstraining transform with an added log-Jacobian, and a flat prior is implicit-uniform on lambda (not on log_lambda)
discriminates: which of the three lp__ components (dropped constants / Jacobian / implicit prior scale) actually moves the argmax
expected_findings: Jacobian hypothesis: lp__ = log p(lambda) + log|d lambda / d(log lambda)| = log p(lambda) + log(lambda); maximizing this over lambda shifts the argmax up from the mode toward the mean by exactly the curvature/Jacobian contribution (1/n here). Dropped-constants hypothesis: would not move the argmax (constants don't depend on lambda).
situations: In Stan/rstan the MCMC draw that maximizes lp__ (lambda[which.max(mcmc$lp__)]) does NOT match the MAP/MLE returned by optimizing(). For a Poisson model with a l
leaves: the lower=0 → log transform adds +log(lambda) to lp__; argmax of [log Gamma-kernel + log(lambda)] = mode of the equivalent log_lambda density = posterior mean of lambda; ~ vs target+= only affects con

branches.if_observe: adding +log(lambda) to the constrained log-posterior reproduces the 9.5 argmax
branches.then: the Jacobian is the cause — confirm by removing/neutralizing it

action: Reparameterize to sample an unbounded raw parameter (real log_lambda, lambda = exp(log_lambda) in transformed parameters) and re-check where lp__ is maximal

expected_findings: Jacobian hypothesis: with no lower=0 on the SAMPLED parameter, no constraint-Jacobian is auto-added in the same way, so lp__-argmax returns to the MLE/mode 9.0. (Note this changes the prior scale, so it is a diagnostic, not necessarily the desired model.)
situations: In Stan/rstan the MCMC draw that maximizes lp__ (lambda[which.max(mcmc$lp__)]) does NOT match the MAP/MLE returned by optimizing(). For a Poisson model with a l

branches.if_observe: still 9.5

action: Localize the crash to the sampled-covariance prior, then question whether a Wishart-family prior on a covariance matrix is the right tool at all
discriminates: numerical/initialization fluke vs. structurally fragile prior choice (Wishart footgun) vs. wider prior-misspecification problem
expected_findings: Hardcoding sigma removing the exception confirms the inv_wishart-on-cov_matrix is the proximate fault. Expert recognizes Wishart/inv-Wishart on covariances as discouraged in current Stan practice ('avoid unless you really know what you're doing'). The simultaneously-wide normal(0,1000)/huge-scale priors signal a broader under-regularization, so this is not merely a numerical hiccup.

action: Inspect the actual data scale and signs before touching the model further
discriminates: geometry caused by data scale/functional form vs. caused purely by the prior/parameterization
expected_findings: Variables span huge ranges and are all strictly positive. This points to a functional-form problem (additive linear model on raw multiplicative-scale data), independent of the covariance prior. Strictly-positive + heavy dispersion is the canonical signal to model on the log scale.

action: Check for collinearity among predictors as a driver of the correlated posterior / slow exploration
discriminates: ill-conditioning from correlated predictors vs. from the hierarchical funnel
expected_findings: Predictors are fairly highly correlated with each other, which makes the posterior of the coefficients correlated and the sampler slow (treedepth saturation). Collinearity is addressable by orthogonalizing the design matrix.

action: Reclassify predictors by the level at which they vary and restructure the model into an explicit varying-intercepts/slopes hierarchy
discriminates: predictors that vary within group (genuine individual-level slopes) vs. predictors constant within group (group-level covariates that should explain the varying coefficients)
expected_findings: Only the two within-county-varying predictors (c_ly, c_lm) belong as observation-level slopes; the four county-constant predictors (m, p, nw, gp) belong as group-level predictors entering the mean of the varying intercept/slopes — the 'projection' of group effects onto the individual-level coefficients (Gelman/Hill varying-intercept/slope with group-level predictors).

action: Switch the hierarchical covariance to a non-centered parameterization

action: Inspect how the covariate is coded in the linear predictor and how the prior maps onto group means; switch from treatment/dummy contrasts to sum-to-zero (deviation) contrasts.
discriminates: Whether the one-sided prior effect is an inherent feature of Cox absolute prediction (H2/H4) or simply an artifact of the dummy parametrization (H1).
expected_findings: Under H1: dummy coding sets control = exp(-H0) with x=0 so the prior literally cannot enter the control arm; recoding as mu +/- Delta makes a prior on Delta pull both arms toward the common center, restoring the logistic-regression-like shrinkage-of-the-average behavior. Under H2/H4 the asymmetry would persist regardless of coding.
situations: Fitted a Bayesian Cox PH model in brms (family=cox), but brms has no posterior_epred / posterior survival prediction. Want absolute risk / survival probability
leaves: Sum-to-zero / deviation contrasts (contr.sum or `0 + trt` style) so each arm is mu +/- Delta; a normal(0,0.2) prior on the contrast then shifts control and treatment symmetrically. User confirms it 'w

action: Replace the fixed frequentist baseline hazard with a baseline hazard recomputed per posterior draw, plugging each draw of beta into the nonparametric (Nelson-Aalen/Breslow) estimator.
discriminates: Whether remaining discrepancies / understated uncertainty come from freezing the baseline hazard (H2) versus a coding bug in the estimator itself (H3).
expected_findings: Under H2: draw-by-draw H0(t)= sum over events d_i / sum_{j at risk} exp(x_j'beta) makes the baseline shift with beta and yields a full posterior on absolute risk, removing the 'control arm identical to flat-prior' pathology and propagating uncertainty.

leaves: Extract posterior draws (rstan::extract(brmsfit$fit) or as_draws_df), and for each draw evaluate H0(t_i)=d_i / sum_{j: t_j>=t_i} exp(x_j'beta), then survival = exp(-cumsum). Conceptually correct; impl

branches.then: Debug the manual estimator against the frequentist value at fixed beta (H3).

action: Audit the risk-set denominator term by term against the textbook formula H0(t_i)=d_i / sum_{j at risk} exp(x_j'beta).
discriminates: Bad math/coding (H3) vs a genuine estimator-definition difference (H4/H5).

Fixing the denominator to sum of exponentiated linear predictors moves the manual value from 1.472 to 1.430, much closer to 1.434.

action: Plot the full cumulative-hazard step functions (manual vs survival) per seed instead of comparing a single endpoint, and interrogate what survival::basehaz/survfit.coxph actually computes (centered flag, estimator type, ties method).
discriminates: Floating-point/centered-mean artifact (H4) vs a real estimator difference in how tied event times are handled (H5).
expected_findings: Under H4: basehaz default centered=TRUE returns a mean-population curve, not a true baseline -- a real caveat, but flipping centered=FALSE does NOT close the seed-19 gap, so it is not the main cause. Under H5: the divergence concentrates at the end of follow-up where heavy early censoring piles up ties; survfit/basehaz uses Efron for ties while the manual Nelson-Aalen equals Breslow, so the two estimators legitimately differ when ties are dense.

action: Force the frequentist survfit to the Breslow/ctype=1 cumulative hazard and compare against the manual Nelson-Aalen, and regenerate data with realistic (non-extreme) censoring.

expected_findings: survfit(fit, ctype=1) (Breslow) matches the manual Nelson-Aalen to ~1e-15 (machine precision) across all seeds, proving the manual code is correct Breslow; the survival-vs-manual difference (Efron-Breslow) collapses to <0.0016 once early censoring is realistic, well below any decision-relevant threshold.

action: Diagnose root cause: identify that an RV (lam, then ad_sum_r) makes ALL downstream pandas/numpy ops symbolic, which those libs can't reduce (H4)

action: Reason about how independent marginal priors compose into the joint prior on the linear predictor; inspect Var(eta) implied by P predictors
discriminates: H1/H3 (dimensionality + link saturation, a Var(eta) problem) vs H2 (just a single-scale problem solvable by shrinking each beta)
expected_findings: H1/H3: confirmed — the pile-up is the expected consequence of high Var(eta) through a saturating link, not a coding bug. H2: a global tightening helps numerically but is conceptually the wrong frame (you're fighting dimensionality by hand). H4: motivates moving to a joint variance-budget prior.
situations: Beta (or other GLM) regression with many (~10) standardized predictors; independent "weakly informative" priors (Intercept Normal, betas Normal(0,1), phi gamma
leaves: eta = b0 + sum_j beta_j x_j; with beta_j ~ N(0,1) independent and standardized x, Var(eta) scales roughly with P (=10), so eta has SD ~sqrt(10)+; pushed through inv_logit this saturates at 0/1. Key in

branches.then: treat it as a joint Var(eta) problem; adopt a prior that budgets total variance (R2D2/horseshoe) rather than tuning each coefficient (-> H4 move)

action: Adopt R2D2 shrinkage prior: put a prior on R^2, map to total variance W=R2/(1-R2), and split W across predictors with a Dirichlet/logistic-normal simplex
discriminates: H4 (joint variance-budget prior fixes it) vs residual H3/H6 (even with R2D2 the variance can still be too large)
expected_findings: H4: the construction caps total explained variance and shrinks most betas toward 0, expected to tame the prior predictive. BUT (foreshadowing H5/H6): naive R2D2 in a non-Gaussian GLM may still over-disperse.
situations: Beta (or other GLM) regression with many (~10) standardized predictors; independent "weakly informative" priors (Intercept Normal, betas Normal(0,1), phi gamma
leaves: R2 ~ rbeta(1,1); W = R2/(1-R2); simplex phi via Dirichlet(rep(1/theta,P)) or zero-sum logistic-normal with scale theta; per-coef scale = sqrt(W*phi); beta_j ~ N(0, scale_j). theta controls sparsity (i

branches.then: done — adopt R2D2, optionally tune theta/R2 prior

expected_findings: H6: with R2~Beta(1,1), R2 near 1 -> W huge -> overdispersed prior predictive (mhollanders recalls 'overdispersed PPCs when R2 close to 1'). H3: confirmed the lever is Var(eta). H7: Bob notes it may not matter — like a beta(0.1,0.1) prior, any data overtakes it.

action: Compare the variance-decomposition assumption (Gaussian, sigma=1) against the beta-regression pseudovariance; inspect what brms' generated Stan code actually does

leaves: User adds an s2b0 adjustment: u=plogis(b0); var=u(1-u)/(1+scale); s2b0=var/(u(1-u))^2; scales=sqrt(W*phi*s2b0). This is the Yanchenko-style/Taylor pseudovariance making W an explained-variance fractio

action: State the limits of R-hat as a screen and stress-test it conceptually (could a trivially-wrong sampler also pass?)
discriminates: H2 (R-hat is a necessary-not-sufficient screen, gameable by symmetry moves) vs interpreting pass as proof of correct sampling

action: Trace multimodality to the neg-binomial dispersion regime and the default initialization

Default init uniform[-2,2] gives ~50% chance of alpha<1 -> roughly half the chains land in the bad multimodal regime, matching 'half converge high, half stationary lower'. A uniform prior on alpha does not rescue this.

On the full continuous*binary_1*binary_2 design, even rstanarm shows low n_eff (<10%) for the group and individual intercepts -> the limitation is data/model, not code.

Once code is QR-conditioned, remaining options are: tighter prior_intercept, more/better data, many more iterations, or living with less precise intercept estimates.

action: Re-classify the divergences against the formal definition of a divergence
discriminates: H3 (true positive: integrator genuinely diverged) vs H4/H5 (false positive)

action: Pin the formal mechanism: define divergence via integrator instability and the Delta-H > 1000 early-termination threshold, then identify the regime where the flag misfires
discriminates: H3 vs H5 — true integrator instability vs benign threshold trip in high dimensions
expected_findings: Divergence = unstable symplectic integrator whose shadow-Hamiltonian level sets become noncompact (trajectory flies to infinity). Stan terminates early when Delta H > 1000 as a proxy for 'on its way to infinity'. In high-dimensional models the Hamiltonian variation can hit that threshold even on a STABLE trajectory — a genuine false positive w.r.t. 'is the trajectory actually unstable'
situations: Model throws divergent transitions; user raises adapt_delta (sometimes to 0.999) and the divergences go away. User worries the smaller step size is not eliminat
leaves: Betancourt: divergence is formally an unstable symplectic integrator (noncompact shadow-Hamiltonian level sets); the Delta H > 1000 cutoff is an early-stop proxy; in high dimensions benign Hamiltonian
