expected_findings: If only mild cancellation: log(Phi(-z)) removes the -inf. If Phi itself underflows in the lower tail: log(Phi(-z)) STILL gives -inf once -z <= -37.5 (Phi underflows to 0 there), so the fix only buys range from z~8 out to z~37.5.
situations: A user-defined custom _lpdf in Stan (here a Normal-Laplace convolution built from a Mills ratio / normal CDF) fails: first every initial value is rejected with
leaves: Replace log1m(Phi(z)) with log(Phi(-z)) (mathematically identical, 1-Phi(z)=Phi(-z)), which avoids cancellation near 1.

branches.if_observe: Still -inf for very large z (>=37.5)
branches.then: Recognize Phi has hard over/underflow limits (->1 above 8, ->0 below -37.5; Phi_approx ->1 above ~5, ->0 below ~-21.5); no Stan-native re-expression fully fixes it -> need a dedicated stable log-normal-CDF (log_ndtr-style asymptotic series, as in TFP / R's pnorm(log.p=TRUE)).

branches.if_observe: Errors only at init and disappear once sampler reaches the bulk
branches.then: Treat as an initialization problem, not a model error -> reduce |z| seen at init by improving the starting region.

action: Reduce the distance between Stan's default init region and the true scale by reparameterizing/standardizing data and parameters.
discriminates: init-region mismatch (fixable by scaling) vs an intrinsic density instability that persists in the posterior bulk
expected_findings: If init-only: standardizing makes z stay in the well-behaved range of Phi during warmup and the -inf/gradient errors disappear once the chain finds the posterior.
situations: A user-defined custom _lpdf in Stan (here a Normal-Laplace convolution built from a Mills ratio / normal CDF) fails: first every initial value is rejected with
leaves: Standardize predictors/outputs so the unconstrained init in (-2,2) is within ~a few SDs of the truth (Stan draws unconstrained init in (-2,2); 'too far' here means ~20 SDs). Optionally pass explicit i

branches.if_observe: Errors vanish after standardizing/explicit inits
branches.then: Resolved for practical purposes; the saturating-tail remains a latent risk only revisited if posterior mass sits in the extreme tail.

action: Anchor reporting on a single combined numeric diagnostic check rather than many exploratory visualizations
discriminates: what to communicate (no-problem-found) vs what to explore (find-the-problem) — visualizations are for exploration, summaries for communication
expected_findings: Recommendation: report the panel of checks bundled in check_all_diagnostics (divergences, Rhat, E-FMI/E-BFMI, tree-depth saturation, neff) plus MCSE; visuals only when a problem surfaces
situations: A practitioner running Stan dynamic HMC (4 chains, 1000 post-warmup draws each, 100+ parameters) wants to choose WHICH MCMC diagnostics to report in a scientifi
leaves: Betancourt: 'Visualizations are much better for exploring problems than trying to communicate that no problems could be found'; points to check_all_diagnostics in stan_utility.R and the markov_chain_m

branches.if_observe: audience is unfamiliar with HMC
branches.then: report the standard numeric checks + cite a Stan version so the exact algorithm is unambiguous; reporting key params in main text + all params in supplement is legitimate

action: Rule out a trivial units/percentage artifact by reading the source code of both checks
discriminates: units artifact (0.001 = 0.1%) vs a genuine semantic difference between the two cutoffs
expected_findings: If units artifact, the two would agree after rescaling; if semantic, the code shows both operate on the same neff/N fraction with genuinely different constants
situations: A practitioner running Stan dynamic HMC (4 chains, 1000 post-warmup draws each, 100+ parameters) wants to choose WHICH MCMC diagnostics to report in a scientifi
leaves: User reads bayesplot mcmc-diagnostics.R lines 461-472: neff_ratio bins are N_eff/N <= 0.1 (low) / <=0.5 (ok); confirms it is NOT a percentage — so a real 100x difference, not a units bug

branches.if_observe: code confirms both use the raw fraction neff/N
branches.then: reject the percentage hypothesis; the discrepancy is semantic and needs an authority to explain WHAT each threshold is for

action: Ask the method author to disambiguate what quantity each threshold actually guards
discriminates: estimator-trustworthiness floor (0.001) vs estimator-precision/adequacy heuristic (0.1) vs outdated criterion
expected_findings: 0.001 is a heuristic from historical empirical evidence flagging when the ESS ESTIMATOR is untrustworthy (strong autocorrelation -> noisy estimator that overestimates true ESS); 0.1 just asks whether ESS is large enough for precise estimators at Stan's default chain length
situations: A practitioner running Stan dynamic HMC (4 chains, 1000 post-warmup draws each, 100+ parameters) wants to choose WHICH MCMC diagnostics to report in a scientifi
leaves: Betancourt: neff/N<0.1 is 'extremely aggressive' (even well-behaved HMC can fail it); neff/N<0.001 flags an unreliable ESS ESTIMATOR. Two caveats: (1) MCMC-CLT may not exist so the standard error itse

branches.if_observe: a few params fail neff/N<0.1 but pass <0.001
branches.then: ESS estimator is trustworthy; decide adequacy from MCSE against the analysis's required precision, not from the 0.1 rule

branches.if_observe: params fail neff/N<0.001 or neff is NaN
branches.then: ESS estimate is untrustworthy / strong autocorrelation — suspect no CLT; first check Rhat, divergences, low E-FMI before trusting any neff or MCSE

action: Reframe the adequacy question as a direct Monte Carlo standard error tolerance rather than a fixed neff/N ratio
discriminates: principled per-analysis precision target vs arbitrary universal ratio
expected_findings: Correct in principle (ESS sets the MCMC estimator standard error), but only valid once a CLT exists and the ESS estimate is reliable; otherwise the SE/ESS is ill-defined
situations: A practitioner running Stan dynamic HMC (4 chains, 1000 post-warmup draws each, 100+ parameters) wants to choose WHICH MCMC diagnostics to report in a scientifi
leaves: Betancourt confirms the ESS->MCSE reasoning is right but conditions it on (a) existence of an MCMC-CLT and (b) a trustworthy (non-overestimated) ESS estimate; recommends inspecting MCSE directly for t

branches.if_observe: CLT existence is in doubt (large Rhat / divergences / low E-FMI)
branches.then: do not report or trust MCSE/ESS-based precision claims until those are resolved

action: Recognize the marginalized-mixture idiom and reject sampling the discrete latent directly; point to the canonical recovery sections.
discriminates: H1 (post-hoc recovery is the correct route) vs. the user's wrong instinct (declare z as a parameter).
expected_findings: Under H1: Stan has no discrete parameters, so `ordered[K] z` / `int z` cannot be a parameter — hence the crash; the documented pattern is to marginalize z out (already done) and recover it in generated quantities. Manual 5.3 (summing out the responsibility parameter / recovering posterior mixture proportions) and 7.2 change-point (categorical_rng on posterior responsibilities) are the reference idioms.
situations: A finite mixture model (here 2-component Poisson, mixture weights p, ordered rates lambda) fits fine with the discrete latent class z_n marginalized out via log
leaves: Cite Stan User's Guide 5.3 'Summing out the responsibility parameter' and 7.2 'Change point models / discrete sampling' — the latter uses categorical_rng in generated quantities to simulate the latent

branches.if_observe: User actually wants global mixture proportions
branches.then: 5.3's 'recovering posterior mixture proportions' suffices

branches.if_observe: User wants per-point class assignment
branches.then: need pointwise responsibilities, not the global-proportion recipe — proceed to next move

action: Disambiguate global proportions vs. pointwise responsibilities, then make the recovery formula explicit.
discriminates: H2 — whether 'mixture proportions p' (one global simplex) answers the question, vs. the per-observation class-membership probability r_nk.
expected_findings: Under H2: the per-point quantity is r_nk = softmax over k of [ log p_k + Poisson_lpmf(y_n | lambda_k) ] — i.e. log prior class weight plus log-likelihood of that point under component k, exponentiated/normalized. This is the Law of Total Probability / Bayes responsibility the user already coded in base R, now done inside Stan.
situations: A finite mixture model (here 2-component Poisson, mixture weights p, ordered rates lambda) fits fine with the discrete latent class z_n marginalized out via log
leaves: Per point n and class k: log-weight = log_p[k] + poisson_lpmf(y[n]|lambda[k]); normalize via log_sum_exp to get class probabilities; apply any label decision rule. This is distinct from the single glo

action: Move the discrete draw into generated quantities, sampling z_n from the per-point responsibilities each iteration.
discriminates: How to expose the discrete latent (categorical draw of z_n) vs. only reporting continuous probabilities — both valid; generated quantities makes z_n appear as posterior draws while keeping the model block continuous/marginalized.
expected_findings: Under H1: in generated quantities declare int<lower=1,upper=K> z[N]; for each i build tmp[k]=poisson_lpmf(y[i]|lambda[k])+log_p[k] and draw z[i]=categorical_logit_rng(tmp) (equivalently categorical_rng(exp(tmp-log_sum_exp(tmp)))). Caching log_p in transformed parameters lets both model and generated-quantities blocks reuse it. Recovers the same answer as the base-R explicit z-posterior, more efficiently.
situations: A finite mixture model (here 2-component Poisson, mixture weights p, ordered rates lambda) fits fine with the discrete latent class z_n marginalized out via log
leaves: transformed parameters { vector[K] log_p = log(p); } and generated quantities using categorical_logit_rng(tmp) with tmp[k]=poisson_lpmf(y[i]|lambda[k])+log_p[k]; posterior draws of z[N] now appear in

branches.if_observe: Want hard labels / class probabilities instead of stochastic draws
branches.then: report normalized exp(tmp-log_sum_exp(tmp)) (the responsibilities) and threshold, rather than categorical_logit_rng

action: Flag the missing positivity constraint on the rate and offer constrained reparameterizations.
discriminates: H3 — whether rate positivity is enforced; an ordered-but-unbounded lambda permits negative rates and can corrupt sampling/recovery.
expected_findings: Under H3: lambda should be positive for a Poisson rate. Fixes: put an ordered, unbounded prior on log(lambda) and use poisson_log_lpmf instead of poisson_lpmf; or use a positive-support prior (user adopts gamma(0.3,3); a lognormal also works). User later confirms switching the prior resolves the concern.
situations: A finite mixture model (here 2-component Poisson, mixture weights p, ordered rates lambda) fits fine with the discrete latent class z_n marginalized out via log
leaves: Either reparameterize with ordered log-lambda + poisson_log_lpmf, or use a positive-support prior on lambda (gamma / lognormal). OP changed normal(5,8) to gamma(0.3,3).

action: Correct the API misuse so iter/chains/cores actually take effect.
discriminates: Whether the run used the intended sampler settings — iter/chains in the data list are ignored as sampler controls.
expected_findings: Pass sampling(model, list(K, N, y), iter=200, chains=4, cores=4) — data list separate from sampler arguments.
situations: A finite mixture model (here 2-component Poisson, mixture weights p, ordered rates lambda) fits fine with the discrete latent class z_n marginalized out via log
leaves: rstan sampling(): data go in the list; iter/chains/cores are top-level args.

action: Condition the likelihood (and the recovery draw) on the known component instead of marginalizing it; mark unknowns and branch.
discriminates: H4 — known vs. unknown z[i]: for known i the contribution is the single-component term, not a sum over all components; for unknown i keep the log_sum_exp marginalization.
expected_findings: Under H4: pass Z_obs[N] with 0 meaning unknown; in transformed data set Z_known[i]=Z_obs[i]>0. In model: if known, target += poisson_lpmf(y[i]|lambda[Z_obs[i]]) + log_p[Z_obs[i]] (no log_sum_exp); else sum over k as before. In generated quantities: if known, z[i]=Z_obs[i]; else draw via categorical_logit_rng. This injects the label information as a hard restriction of the likelihood rather than a separate prior block.
situations: A finite mixture model (here 2-component Poisson, mixture weights p, ordered rates lambda) fits fine with the discrete latent class z_n marginalized out via log
leaves: data int<lower=0,upper=K> Z_obs[N] (0=NA); transformed data Z_known[i]=Z_obs[i]>0; model and generated-quantities branch on Z_known to use the observed component term directly. Confirmed elegant by OP

action: Disambiguate the two distributions by their data-generating structure rather than by name.
discriminates: Whether the outcome is one observed category per row (categorical) vs. a vector of counts over categories per row (multinomial).
expected_findings: If each response row holds a single chosen category -> categorical family; if each row is counts/totals across categories -> multinomial. The 'categorical = multinomial with n=1' identity (bernoulli:binomial :: categorical:multinomial) explains why ML/NLP literature conflates them.
situations: A multilevel model with an unordered multi-category outcome (6 categories) and categorical level-2 predictors plus a grouping/control variable; user is unsure w
leaves: Point to the categorical distribution as the special case of the multinomial with a single observation drawn from the simplex; cite Wikipedia 'Categorical distribution' terminology note; note non-stat

branches.if_observe: one category per row
branches.then: use brms categorical() family; do not route to multinomial/MCMCglmm

branches.if_observe: counts per row over categories
branches.then: use multinomial family

action: Reframe prior-setting as optional and selective rather than exhaustive.
discriminates: Whether convergence actually requires user priors vs. defaults being adequate; whether the goal is regularization or identification.
expected_findings: Defaults usually achieve convergence on their own; you need not (and should not blindly) set all rows. Selectively set priors on the classes that matter (e.g. population-level b, group-level sd) once you understand what each parameter is.
situations: A multilevel model with an unordered multi-category outcome (6 categories) and categorical level-2 predictors plus a grouping/control variable; user is unsure w
leaves: Direct user to set_prior() docs; recommend weakly-informative priors only on the parameters whose scale you can reason about, leaving the rest at brms defaults.

branches.if_observe: model converges with defaults
branches.then: add regularizing priors only as a deliberate modeling choice, not a requirement

branches.if_observe: hand-set priors error out
branches.then: the user is mis-specifying class/coef/dpar targets -> resolve the encoding question first

action: Anchor the prior scale to the logit/log-odds scale and the predictor's standard deviation.
discriminates: Whether the chosen prior SD is commensurate with plausible effect sizes on the log-odds scale given predictor scaling.
expected_findings: 'Scale 1' means predictor SD ~ 1 (not orders of magnitude smaller, e.g. 0.1). On a logit link, normal(0,1) is reasonable but can be too tight if patterns are extreme; there is no universal rule - it depends on inferential goal.
situations: A multilevel model with an unordered multi-category outcome (6 categories) and categorical level-2 predictors plus a grouping/control variable; user is unsure w
leaves: If inexperienced with priors, prefer a slightly wider normal(0,3)-normal(0,5) on the logit scale as an ad-hoc safe default; treat any specific number as non-authoritative.

branches.if_observe: predictors standardized to SD~1
branches.then: normal(0,1)-normal(0,3) logit priors are sensible

branches.if_observe: expect extreme/near-separation effects
branches.then: widen prior (3-5) to avoid over-shrinking

action: Correct the encoding mental model: identify how categorical predictors are coded and what parameter the prior lands on.
discriminates: Whether priors act on dummy-coded log-odds contrasts vs. on raw category labels or per-category probabilities.
expected_findings: Categorical predictors are NOT ordinal integers; brms uses the same dummy/treatment-contrast coding as base R, so each non-reference level gets its own coefficient (a contrast against the reference), and the prior is placed on that log-odds coefficient - not on a probability and not on the variable as a monolith.
situations: A multilevel model with an unordered multi-category outcome (6 categories) and categorical level-2 predictors plus a grouping/control variable; user is unsure w
leaves: Explain dummy coding -> one b coefficient per non-reference level; the prior meaning depends entirely on the chosen contrast coding; refer user to contrast-coding tutorials.

branches.if_observe: default treatment contrasts
branches.then: interpret each coefficient as a log-odds difference from the reference level and prior it accordingly

branches.if_observe: user wants a prior per level with no reference
branches.then: switch contrast/coding scheme (e.g. sum-to-zero / index coding) so each level has an interpretable parameter

action: Check the independence/repeatability assumption of the softmax categorical model against the design.
discriminates: Whether choices are independent draws (categorical OK) vs. constrained choices among alternatives (needs a choice model).
expected_findings: Without-replacement / alternative-specific choice data violate the plain categorical model's assumption that any category is available on each draw; a discrete-choice / mixed-logit model is the appropriate family (and watch for identification and red-bus/blue-bus IIA issues).
situations: A multilevel model with an unordered multi-category outcome (6 categories) and categorical level-2 predictors plus a grouping/control variable; user is unsure w
leaves: Route constrained-choice designs to a mixed-logit/discrete-choice formulation (P-1 parameterization, alternative-specific utilities) rather than a vanilla categorical regression.

branches.if_observe: independent unordered selection per row
branches.then: categorical family is fine

branches.if_observe: choice among a constrained/sampled-without-replacement set
branches.then: use a mixed-logit / choice model and beware IIA

action: Run formal out-of-sample model comparison (LOO/ELPD) between flat and varying-intercept models, treating the hierarchical model as the default given known repeated-measures structure.
discriminates: H1 vs H2 — is the hierarchical result the better model, or an artifact to distrust?
expected_findings: H1: hierarchical model dominates LOO by a large margin and is justified a priori by design → trust it. H2: if it were a convergence artifact, ELPD would not robustly favor it and R-hat would be clearly failing, not borderline.
situations: A Gamma(log) GLM on positive skewed timing data with repeated measures per unit. Adding a varying intercept (1|group) flips a population-level effect from non-s
leaves: add_criterion(test,'loo'); loo_compare — hierarchical model wins by elpd_diff ~2590 (se 40); domain knowledge says repeated measures MUST be modelled, so the flat model 'shouldn't be reported at all'.

branches.if_observe: Huge ELPD gap favoring hierarchical model AND design has known repeated measures
branches.then: Adopt hierarchical model; treat the significant within-unit effect as real (variance partitioning, not artifact). Don't even report the flat model.

branches.if_observe: ELPD ambiguous / convergence clearly broken
branches.then: Fall back to fixing sampler/priors before interpreting the contrast.

action: Judge convergence as adequate-but-improvable and prescribe a more informative prior on the random-effect SD rather than rejecting the model.
discriminates: H2 vs H3 — is poor mixing fatal (reject model) or fixable (tune prior)?
expected_findings: H2: R-hat would be substantially >1.05 and ESS collapsed → genuine non-convergence. H3: borderline R-hat + low ESS on a near-zero-information SD prior is the classic 'weakly identified variance component' signature, curable with a prior.
situations: A Gamma(log) GLM on positive skewed timing data with repeated measures per unit. Adding a varying intercept (1|group) flips a population-level effect from non-s
leaves: Expert reads R-hat ~1.0x / lowered ESS as 'overall convergence looks ok'; recommends a tighter/informative prior on the sd class to improve mixing.

branches.if_observe: R-hat near 1, modest ESS
branches.then: Keep model, set informative sd prior, rerun; do not discard the inference.

action: Decompose the meaning of the varying intercept: it captures baseline between-group variation BEFORE treatment; the fixed effect is then the within-group adjustment.
discriminates: Mechanism behind H1 — why pooling sharpens the fixed effect.
expected_findings: H1: separating between-group variance (SD term) from the within-group contrast is exactly why the contrast's CI tightens once the random intercept is present.
situations: A Gamma(log) GLM on positive skewed timing data with repeated measures per unit. Adding a varying intercept (1|group) flips a population-level effect from non-s
leaves: SD(Intercept) = amount of baseline between-basename variability prior to accounting for the intervention; population effect = within-unit shift from that random baseline. This reframing explains the s

branches.if_observe: User asks to also model differences in spread/variance, not just mean
branches.then: Note that requires a distributional (sigma/shape) sub-model — a different specification.

action: Attribute interpretation difficulty to the non-identity (log) link, not to a modeling error.
discriminates: H5 — interpretation friction is a scale issue, not misspecification.
expected_findings: H5: values that seem unintuitive become sensible once mapped through exp() to the response scale.
situations: A Gamma(log) GLM on positive skewed timing data with repeated measures per unit. Adding a varying intercept (1|group) flips a population-level effect from non-s
leaves: SD is on the log scale because link=log; this is a generic property of any GLM with a non-identity link, not a bug — exponentiate / think multiplicatively to interpret.

action: Redirect from hand-rolled estimator to an established marginal-likelihood package built to consume Stan draws
discriminates: H1 (package already exists) vs H2 (must hand-roll TI)
expected_findings: If bridgesampling fits the model class -> H1 holds, no custom code needed. If the model is one where bridge sampling itself is delicate (e.g. phylogenetics-scale), the package exists but its estimate is still as brittle as the marginal likelihood, pushing back toward H3.
situations: Want to compute model evidence (marginal likelihood) / Bayes factors for competing models in Stan/rstan; no built-in facility exists, so the user is hand-rollin
leaves: Recommend the bridgesampling R package (bridge-sampling estimator of the marginal likelihood, designed to take rstan/posterior draws); separately, loo's PSIS-LOO is floated as a cheaper predictive-com

branches.if_observe: bridgesampling runs and gives stable estimates with reasonable error
branches.then: Use it instead of the custom TI hack; H1

branches.if_observe: model class is one where marginal likelihoods are notoriously hard (high-dim, multimodal)
branches.then: Package use does not save you; estimator variance dominates -> H3

action: Audit Stan density semantics: check whether normalizing constants are being kept
discriminates: H4 (silent implementation bug) vs a genuinely correct-but-fragile estimator
expected_findings: If the user wrote ~ or *lupdf, the recorded prior/loglik are off by an unknown additive constant per term, biasing every per-temperature estimate and the integrated evidence. Switching to *lpdf removes that systematic offset.
situations: Want to compute model evidence (marginal likelihood) / Bayes factors for competing models in Stan/rstan; no built-in facility exists, so the user is hand-rollin
leaves: Stan's ~ sampling notation and the *_lupmf/*_lupdf functions SILENTLY drop normalizing constants; for evidence/marginal-likelihood work you must use target += with the full *_lpmf/*_lpdf (normalized)

branches.if_observe: code uses ~ or *lupdf for the terms feeding the evidence
branches.then: Replace with target += *lpdf to retain normalizing constants; H4 was active

branches.if_observe: code already uses fully-normalized *lpdf everywhere
branches.then: Implementation is clean; move on to estimator-variance concerns (H3)

action: Reframe brittleness as a property of the marginal-likelihood estimator's variance, then diagnose which naive estimator is being used
discriminates: H3 (estimator-variance is the true culprit) vs H2 (TI is basically fine, just slow) vs OP's belief that improper-prior avoidance + correct math suffices
expected_findings: Naive prior-mean or posterior-inverse estimators -> heavy/infinite-variance, unreliable error bars. A power-posterior / thermodynamic path with intermediate distributions reduces this BUT only if neighboring temperatures overlap well; otherwise it inherits the same variance pathology.
situations: Want to compute model evidence (marginal likelihood) / Bayes factors for competing models in Stan/rstan; no built-in facility exists, so the user is hand-rollin
leaves: Decompose the marginal likelihood into the two standard sampling estimators — prior expectation of the realized likelihood E_prior[pi(y|theta)] and posterior expectation of the inverse likelihood E_po

branches.if_observe: estimator is a single prior- or posterior-expectation (harmonic-mean-like)
branches.then: Expect infinite/huge variance; abandon for a tempered path or bridge sampling; H3 confirmed

branches.if_observe: tempered ladder used but evidence estimate unstable across runs / diverges in high dim
branches.then: Temperature spacing is too coarse — neighbors not close enough; refine ladder (TI/stepping-stone) or use adiabatic/continuous tempering; H3 in its ladder-fragility form

action: Separate inference from decision and demand calibration of the selection rule
discriminates: H5 (selection decision needs calibration) vs treating the estimated BF as a finished answer
expected_findings: If used as a thresholded selector without calibration, expect uncontrolled error rates and prior-sensitivity (Lindley/Bartlett-style). If reframed as model averaging or as inference within an encompassing model, the BF machinery is well-behaved.
situations: Want to compute model evidence (marginal likelihood) / Bayes factors for competing models in Stan/rstan; no built-in facility exists, so the user is hand-rollin
leaves: Bayes factors are valid inference and underlie model averaging / mixture reweighting, but using a BF-ratio threshold for model SELECTION is a decision problem with no built-in guarantee on false-posit

branches.if_observe: BF will gate a binary model choice with no simulation calibration
branches.then: Run a calibration study or switch to an encompassing model / model averaging; H5

branches.if_observe: goal is model averaging or mixture inference
branches.then: BFs (or an explicit mixture/encompassing model) are appropriate without a separate selection-calibration step

action: Interrogate the cost model of the built-in precision-parameterized density before optimizing around it.
discriminates: H1 (manual conversion is a real win) vs the conversion being pure overhead
expected_findings: Under H1 a manual route would help; finding: multi_normal_prec already does a Cholesky decomposition internally, so inverting then re-factoring adds work and numerical risk rather than removing it.
situations: Fitting an MVN latent with a sparse/horseshoe-regularized precision matrix in Stan; trying to avoid slow multi_normal_prec by converting the precision-Cholesky
leaves: bgoodri: 'Not really because multi_normal_prec is doing a Cholesky decomposition internally.' Kills the premise of speeding it up by inverse()+cholesky_decompose().

branches.if_observe: built-in already factors internally
branches.then: abandon precision->covariance round-trip; keep working in the precision/Cholesky-of-precision space and hand-code the density

action: Reframe an 'almost symmetric' / SPD-in-theory failure as a floating-point conditioning problem, not a construction bug.
discriminates: H2 (true asymmetry) vs H3 (floating-point loss of symmetry/SPD from a badly conditioned, inverted matrix)
expected_findings: Under H2 the off-diagonals would differ structurally; finding: they differ only in the last digits — a matrix SPD in exact arithmetic can fail Cholesky in floating point; the inverse of an ill-conditioned precision blows up (giant entries) and loses symmetry/PD.
situations: Fitting an MVN latent with a sparse/horseshoe-regularized precision matrix in Stan; trying to avoid slow multi_normal_prec by converting the precision-Cholesky
leaves: anon75146577 (D. Simpson): SPD-in-exact can fail in float; condition number of Sigma matters; 1e-12 jitter is far too small — use 1e-7 or 1e-5. User adds 1e-5/1e-7 to the diagonal; this clears the sym

branches.if_observe: bumping jitter to 1e-5/1e-7 clears the cholesky/inverse_spd rejection
branches.then: conditioning was the symmetry failure; proceed — but recognize this only masks, the real density path still needs fixing

branches.if_observe: still fails after jitter
branches.then: do forward-error / condition-number analysis of Sigma, or avoid the inverse entirely (next move)

action: Replace the matrix round-trip with the analytic MVN log-density written directly in terms of the precision Cholesky.
discriminates: whether the model even needs the covariance/its Cholesky (H1/H3 path) vs being expressible from L_prec alone
expected_findings: The N(0, Q^{-1}) log-density with Q = L L' equals (up to const) log|L|-terms minus half the quadratic form, computable from L with no inverse: target += 2*log(sum(diagonal(L))) - 0.5*dot_self(L'*x). Eliminates inverse_spd and the second Cholesky entirely.
situations: Fitting an MVN latent with a sparse/horseshoe-regularized precision matrix in Stan; trying to avoid slow multi_normal_prec by converting the precision-Cholesky
leaves: anon75146577 hand-codes: target += 2*log(sum(diagonal(L_prec))) - 0.5*dot_self(L_prec' * x), where x is the variable with precision Q=L_prec*L_prec'. (Note the log-det term uses log of the product of

branches.if_observe: switching to target += introduces NEW init errors (lambda_tilde nan, logit-prob nan)
branches.then: the numerical-symmetry issue was a red herring; pivot to NaN-source diagnosis (next moves)

action: Audit transformed-parameters assignment ORDER and check every variable is set before use (Stan executes top-to-bottom, no symbolic resolution).
discriminates: H4 (use-before-assignment / ordering) vs H5 (placeholder 9999 corruption) vs lingering H3
expected_findings: Finding: lambda_tilde is used to build L_prec before the lines that define c then lambda_tilde run — so it is NaN at use; inverse_spd/cholesky then see NaN ('m[1,2]=nan'). action: Check whether the observed coordinates fully determine the dynamical state (phase-space/identifiability check of the science, not the sampler)
discriminates: H4 (multimodality intrinsic to the dynamics) vs everything-fixable-by-modeling
expected_findings: If (regulator,target) uniquely determined the future evolution, requiring stability over time forces the system to either diverge to infinity, collapse to a point, or put all cells in one identical state — i.e. nothing interesting. action: Reconstruct the intended marginal likelihood from the source paper and compare its summation scale to the implementation

action: Sanity-check the likelihood structure against the generative process; question whether the pdf+cdf 'censoring' terms and a free mixing weight belong together

situations: Observed data is the elementwise max (or min) of two latent positive RVs (gamma/lognormal) whose shapes/locations you want to estimate, but you don't know which

This is the diagnostic signature of exchangeable-component degeneracy, not MCMC tuning failure.
situations: Observed data is the elementwise max (or min) of two latent positive RVs (gamma/lognormal) whose shapes/locations you want to estimate, but you don't know which
leaves: Symptom triad: (i) per-chain Rhat~1 yet (ii) many distinct precise modes across chains and (iii) mixing weight pinned at 0/1 -> components are exchangeable / winner identity unknown.

action: Derive the marginal density analytically via order statistics instead of patching the mixture
discriminates: max-density with known winner (pi1*Pi2) vs unknown winner (must marginalize over which component wins)
expected_findings: Known-winner: pi(x1 | x1>x2) = pi_1(x1)*Pi_2(x1). Unknown-winner max: differentiate Pr(Y<=y)=Pi_1(y)Pi_2(y) -> f_Y(y)=pi_1(y)Pi_2(y)+pi_2(y)Pi_1(y). This equals the user's pdf+cdf log_mix ONLY with the weight fixed at 1/2, NOT a free P1. So the free mixing weight was the modeling error.
situations: Observed data is the elementwise max (or min) of two latent positive RVs (gamma/lognormal) whose shapes/locations you want to estimate, but you don't know which

action: Diagnose the fixed-covariance trap: a covariance numerically evaluated only on X_train ties the latent vector to training locations, so there is no mechanism to produce values at X_test.

action: Clarify GP semantics: mean function = the value the GP reverts to away from data (zero by default); predictive uncertainty = both noise and covariance, treated as distinct uncertainty sources.
discriminates: Separates the role of mean function (extrapolation baseline) from H5 (noise-vs-covariance attribution of uncertainty).
expected_findings: Mean function K means the function decays to K (not 0) where there is no data; with a Gaussian likelihood the observation noise is the variance added to the diagonal of the cov matrix, conceptually separate from the kernel-driven covariance uncertainty.
situations: Trying to build RBF-kernel regression in PyMC: hand-coded an RBF as a parametric mean (weights * exp(-gamma*(X-center)^2)) or built a fixed MvNormal with a squa
leaves: No mean function -> reverts to 0 in gaps; constant mean K -> reverts to K. Predictive variance = kernel covariance + observation noise (noise = sigma^2 added to cov diagonal under Gaussian likelihood)

action: Locate the precise theoretical relationship (limit behavior) rather than treating the analogy as the goal
discriminates: whether BART==GP-approximation (so GP dominates) vs BART is a distinct, richer model whose GP limit is a degenerate case
expected_findings: In the infinite-tree limit BART does converge to a GP, but that limit is NOT the goal and performance on average DECREASES as trees -> infinity; the original BART paper shows accuracy rises with number of trees, peaks, then slowly declines. So 'BART = GP approximation' is the wrong frame.

action: Give the same explanation in three equivalent framings and a geometric argument
discriminates: whether the deficit is computational vs an intrinsic property of multivariate-Normal priors
expected_findings: Multivariate Normals don't do variable selection; GPs don't learn features (kernel fixes which features matter); GP priors aren't hierarchical (probability spread uniformly over a sea of equal-complexity hypotheses, unlike a Solomonoff-style prior). Geometrically: diagonalize the GP via Mercer (f = sum u_i f_i, u ~ N(0,I)); conditioning on data just cuts the typical-set sphere with a plane, yielding a lower-dim sphere with probability still spread uniformly -> no preference for sparse/simple explanations.
situations: Deciding between BART and a Gaussian Process for nonparametric function estimation; both appear to do "the same task" and BART is rumored to be an approximation
leaves: Stat/ML/AI jargon trio: 'Normals don't do variable selection', 'GPs don't learn features', 'GPs aren't hierarchical'; Mercer-diagonalization sphere-cut-by-plane geometric picture.

action: Run a model-selection probe over m using cross-validation and approximate LOO, checking agreement and saturation

Predictive accuracy keeps improving with m but often SATURATES (m=50 or 100 ~ m=200). Overfitting is mitigated because as m grows the leaf values are shrunk more (the prior tightens), so more trees does not blow up complexity unchecked.

action: Apply Bayes' theorem to compute the occupancy posterior for unobserved-state sites and compare against the formula being used

action: Probe whether the latent Z is a parameter at all, using a cross-validation / LOO thought experiment as a discriminating diagnostic

This shows Z behaves like a residual/unobserved data, NOT a parameter; the proper likelihood is P(y|psi,theta) marginal over Z, and the predictive must NOT condition on Z.

action: Decompose where the prior-induced correlation goes once you condition on data -- inspect the likelihood structure and the law-of-total-covariance argument the user themselves wrote
discriminates: prior-driven correlation that survives conditioning vs correlation that is washed out because the likelihood factorizes over the two groups
expected_findings: The binomial likelihood is binomial_lpmf(n_1|N_1,lambda_1) + binomial_lpmf(n_2|N_2,lambda_2): NO cross-term. So once each lambda is informed by its own n_i,N_i, the data pull them apart; posterior correlation collapses toward independence. Confirms likelihood-factorizes H and explains the 0.04.

action: Reclassify the model: check whether the two-group Beta(a,b) layer can actually be a hierarchical/random-effect with only two groups and one pair of observations

It is two independent binomials with a shared prior, NOT a Bayesian hierarchical model. Confirms non-identifiability H.

action: Distinguish the source of the desired dependence -- is it correlation between the two true proportions, or a selection/conditional-observation rule on whether stage 2 is observed?
discriminates: correlated parameters (joint MVN prior is the right tool) vs selection mechanism (conditioning on whether lambda_2 is observed -- does NOT by itself create lambda_1,lambda_2 correlation)
expected_findings: Conditioning observation of x_2 on x_1 exceeding a threshold does not make independently-drawn x_1,x_2 correlated -- it only makes WHETHER x_2 is observed depend on x_1. So the threshold rule is a selection effect, separate from the substantive belief that the same test's sensitivity is correlated across stages. The two must be modeled separately; you cannot compute Pr(lambda_1>0.95) inside the model (it is a posterior average over draws).

action: Enumerate viable modeling options that respect the lack of data to estimate a population correlation
discriminates: estimate-the-correlation (infeasible) vs impose-informative-prior vs collapse/pool vs sequential Bayesian updating
expected_findings: Freely estimating rho with a wide prior does nothing (no leverage); options that work: (a) impose an informative prior / fix rho, accepting it just gently couples the lambdas; (b) drop the hierarchical component and fit independently; (c) fully pool n=n_1+n_2, N=N_1+N_2, one lambda_0; (d) sequential update -- posterior from stage 1 becomes prior for stage 2, p(lambda|n_1,N_1,n_2,N_2) ∝ L(lambda|n_2,N_2) p(lambda|n_1,N_1), which is equivalent to pooling under exchangeability.
situations: A "hierarchical" Beta-Binomial (or logit-MVN) model over TWO group proportions (lambda_1, lambda_2) fit to a single pair of binomial observations. Prior-predict
leaves: Bob #10/#11: ignore the correlation or impose an informative prior; a wide/uninformative rho prior 'won't do much'; skip the hierarchical component unless there is a downstream use for the correlation

action: Reject fixed threshold transfer; derive the M-dependent threshold for nested R-hat instead

action: Reason about how much the leave-one-out posterior of the random effect moves when one point is removed, as a function of group size

leaves: Do NOT weight by in-sample category proportions. The posterior already reflects that small groups have less precise group-specific estimates. Only legitimate reweighting is by KNOWN POPULATION proport

action: Separate the joint from its marginals: plot the 2D joint (scatter/hex over the (x,y) plane) rather than 1D histograms, and check whether it is uniform on the feasible triangle.
discriminates: H3 (it's just a marginal cross-section artifact of a uniform-on-triangle joint) vs a genuinely wrong/unintended joint density.
expected_findings: Under H1/H3 the joint is uniform over the triangle (0,0)-(1,0)-(1,1); the odd marginals P(x)=2x, P(y)=2(1-y) are exactly the cross-sections of that triangle, so nothing is 'broken' — it's just not the intended conditional model.
situations: A parameter is declared with a bound that depends on another parameter (e.g. `real<lower=0, upper=x> y`), expecting an implicit conditional uniform Uniform(0,x)
leaves: nhuurre: implicit prior is 2D-uniform on the triangle; marginals are cross-sections, scatter looks uniform. OP had estimated marginals from histograms.

branches.if_observe: joint is uniform on the triangle
branches.then: the question is not 'why is it broken' but 'why does the empty-model default density equal triangle-uniform rather than conditional-uniform' — go to the Jacobian/density-accounting move.

branches.if_observe: joint is NOT uniform on the triangle
branches.then: different bug — re-examine the constraint declaration / transform.

action: Drop the 'implicit uniform prior' mental model; write the target as an explicit sum of log-density contributions and identify which term is constant.
discriminates: H1 (no implicit prior; difference is the non-constant Jacobian/lpdf term -log(x)) vs H2 (vague 'Jacobian' hand-wave).
expected_findings: `target += uniform_lpdf(.|0,b)` equals a constant (can be dropped) ONLY when b is constant; with constant bounds the omitted term is constant so the empty model already encodes uniform. With variable bound x, `uniform_lpdf(y|0,x) = -log(x)` is NOT constant, so omitting it changes the density — the empty model is missing exactly that -log(x).
situations: A parameter is declared with a bound that depends on another parameter (e.g. `real<lower=0, upper=x> y`), expecting an implicit conditional uniform Uniform(0,x)
leaves: martinmodrak (accepted): Stan never imposes an implicit prior; a Stan program is just a function computing log-posterior up to an additive constant. uniform_lpdf(x|0,1)=const → ignorable; uniform_lpdf

branches.if_observe: bound is a constant
branches.then: omitted uniform_lpdf is constant → empty model == intended uniform; the implicit-prior intuition accidentally holds.

branches.if_observe: bound depends on a parameter
branches.then: must write the dependent uniform_lpdf (= -log(upper) for Uniform(0,upper)) explicitly, or reparameterize.

action: Account precisely for the constrained-transform Jacobian of the variable-bound parameter, and reconcile the forward-sampling pushforward with the target-density view.
discriminates: whether the residual confusion is a missing/duplicated Jacobian term vs a genuine inconsistency in H1.
expected_findings: The empty model's density for the variable-bound y carries a +log(x) Jacobian from the scaling-by-upper-bound transform; this +log(x) cancels the -log(x) that a true Uniform(0,x) would contribute. Net: the empty model = (uniform on x giving f_X=2x i.e. log2+log x) combined so terms cancel to a constant → uniform-on-triangle. The forward sqrt(U) draw is the change-of-variables Z~U, X=sqrt(Z) giving f_X=2x — same 2x marginal.
situations: A parameter is declared with a bound that depends on another parameter (e.g. `real<lower=0, upper=x> y`), expecting an implicit conditional uniform Uniform(0,x)
leaves: spinkney: with Z~U, X=sqrt(Z) → F_X=x^2, f_X=2x; target increment log2+log(x) (from x) plus -log(x) (dependent uniform on y) → the log(x) terms cancel, leaving a constant ⇒ same as doing nothing ⇒ tri

branches.if_observe: hand-coded log-post still not uniform
branches.then: you've either double-counted or dropped the +log(x) Jacobian of the scale-by-x transform; align it with the canceling -log(x).

action: Derive the intended marginal by integrating out x, then realize it either by adding the dependent lpdf or by inverse-CDF reparameterization.
discriminates: H1 intended-model density vs H4 reparameterization route; confirms what 'correct' looks like.
expected_findings: Intended marginal f_Y = ∫_y^1 (1/x)·1 dx = -ln(y), with CDF F_Y = y - y ln(y); inverse-CDF sampling needs LambertW. Two valid Stan realizations: (a) keep variable bound + `target += -log(x)` equivalently `y~uniform(0,x)`; (b) put y on [0,1] and add `target += log(-log(y))`, or sample via lambertWm1.
situations: A parameter is declared with a bound that depends on another parameter (e.g. `real<lower=0, upper=x> y`), expecting an implicit conditional uniform Uniform(0,x)
leaves: spinkney: f_Y=-ln(y); F_Y=y - y ln(y); inverse via lambertWm1; Stan: declare y in [0,1], `target += log(-log(y))` reproduces the correct marginal. JohnnyZoom4H's earlier suggestion (sample on fixed su

action: Reframe the derivation in clean BDA notation and rebuild it from the chain rule as an explicit numbered argument
discriminates: H3 (trivial chain-rule consequence) vs H1 (genuine incorrectness needing a different foundation)
expected_findings: Under H3: prior-draw theta_sim ~ p(theta) then y_sim ~ p(y|theta_sim) gives joint p(theta,y); posterior draws theta^(m) ~ p(theta|y_sim) also give joint p(theta,y); hence theta_sim and theta^(m) are i.i.d. from p(theta|y_sim) -> rank of theta_sim among theta^(m) is uniform. Under H1: the chain rule rebuild would break or need extra assumptions.

action: De-overload the density notation, distinguishing prior/posterior/marginal symbols, and read off what computes each variable's conditional density

action: Switch to a brute-force generative/simulation-based reliability estimator (parametric-bootstrap-within-posterior)
discriminates: outcome-family-specific analytic formula vs a general estimator that works for any derived quantity and likelihood
expected_findings: For each posterior draw: use group-level multivariate structure (means, SDs, correlation matrix) to simulate subjects, compute true DQI (Psi_true); simulate many session-like datasets, re-fit in Stan with group structure as prior to get estimated DQI (Psi_est) and its within-subject SD; then reliability Phi = sigma(Psi_true)/(sigma(Psi_true)+mean sigma_within(Psi_est)). Yields a FULL POSTERIOR on reliability and test-retest correlations for any DQI and any outcome family.
situations: Hierarchical/mixed model with per-subject random intercepts and slopes (DV ~ 1+IV + (1+IV|SUBJECT)); analyst wants a full posterior on an ICC-like test-retest r

action: Diff the refactored likelihood against the original loop semantics; check the loop index actually varies over observations.

action: Stabilize sampling and then recommend respecification rather than parameter-by-parameter tuning
discriminates: workable mitigations vs accepting unresolvable degeneracy
expected_findings: Practical fixes that help the SAMPLER (not the identifiability): use init='adapt_diag' to avoid jittered start that the sensitive model can't tolerate; increase tune (e.g. 5000); center priors near plausible values so they are 'agnostic but not problematic'. The deeper fix is to reparameterize so the model is less sensitive and priors can be informative on identifiable quantities (e.g. on beta-gamma directly), or add an informative prior on gamma
situations: Simulated data from a deterministic curve mu = beta/(beta-gamma)*(exp(t*(beta-gamma))-1)+1 with known true (beta, gamma); fitting a Normal likelihood in PyMC to
leaves: Default jittered init perturbs the start point and breaks on models sensitive to tiny parameter changes => use adapt_diag. These tame the sampler but do NOT remove the dependency; the only structural

branches.if_observe: adapt_diag + more tune + centered priors -> clean sampling but persistent joint uncertainty
branches.then: report as expected; identifiability needs model/prior change, not sampler tuning

branches.if_observe: still diverging/erroring
branches.then: reparameterize the model around identifiable combinations

action: Isolate the offending term by comparing the model logp at the start point against the data — comment out the likelihood and draw one sample (tune=0, draws=1) to inspect the deterministic prediction P, then count observations where predicted p contradicts the data
discriminates: Whether the -inf is coming from the likelihood term (p hits 0/1 against conflicting data) vs a prior/transform issue
expected_findings: H1/H2: a nonzero count of cells with P==0.0 while observed R!=0 (here 120 impossible observations) confirms the Bernoulli logp is -inf; H3: would show finite logp everywhere and point elsewhere
situations: Hierarchical probit/psychometric model (subject-level params feeding a normal-CDF link into a Bernoulli likelihood) ported from JAGS to PyMC; NUTS fails immedia
leaves: trace = pm.sample(tune=0, draws=1, chains=1) with likelihood commented; np.sum((trace['P']==0.0) & (R!=0)) -> 120. Also Discounting.check_test_point() to see which named term is -inf (h was -inf).

branches.if_observe: count of impossible (p=0 vs data=1) cells is >0 / h term is -inf
branches.then: p is saturating; investigate WHY the link returns exactly 0 (move to underflow check)

branches.if_observe: count is 0 but still bad energy
branches.then: look at priors/transforms (Uniform bounds, Bound transform) for the -inf term instead

action: Probe the link function in isolation at extreme inputs to distinguish true model misspecification from floating-point underflow of the CDF
discriminates: H1 (finite-precision underflow of the analytic CDF) vs H2 (genuinely pathological start values that a testval tweak would fix)
expected_findings: H1: evaluating Phi at increasingly negative inputs returns exactly 0.0 below a threshold (Phi(-13,-11,-9)=0.0; Phi(-7)~1.3e-12), i.e. the math is fine but float64 underflows; the true probability is tiny-but-positive
situations: Hierarchical probit/psychometric model (subject-level params feeding a normal-CDF link into a Bernoulli likelihood) ported from JAGS to PyMC; NUTS fails immedia
leaves: Phi(np.array([-13.,-11.,-9.,-7.])).eval() -> [0,0,0,1.28e-12]; anything < ~-9 underflows to 0 on the probability scale

branches.if_observe: Phi underflows to literal 0 below ~-9
branches.then: diagnosis is underflow, not bad model; fix by moving the likelihood to log/logit scale so the tiny probability is represented as a finite large-negative log

branches.if_observe: Phi returns sensible nonzero values everywhere
branches.then: reconsider H2/H3 — start values or hierarchical geometry rather than the link

action: Reformulate the Bernoulli likelihood to consume a log/logit-scale linear predictor using stable log-CDF helpers instead of the raw probability
discriminates: Confirms the reformulation removes the -inf without changing the model (H1 fix) vs revealing a deeper problem
expected_findings: H1 fix: check_test_point()'s likelihood term becomes finite (here h goes from -inf to ~-68138); invlogit(logit_P) reproduces the original P everywhere except the cells that had underflowed to 0
situations: Hierarchical probit/psychometric model (subject-level params feeding a normal-CDF link into a Bernoulli likelihood) ported from JAGS to PyMC; NUTS fails immedia
leaves: log_P = normal_lcdf(0,1,z); log_1m_P = log1mexp(-log_P); pm.Bernoulli('h', logit_p=log_P-log_1m_P, observed=R). Validate: pm.math.invlogit(logit_P) matches original P except underflow cells.

branches.if_observe: likelihood test point is now finite and model starts sampling
branches.then: primary blocker resolved; move on to sampling-quality diagnostics

branches.if_observe: still -inf or new NaNs
branches.then: check log1mexp domain / sign conventions and that z is built on the correct scale

action: Attack residual divergences with standard hierarchical sampler/geometry levers and tighten the weakly-identified scale prior
discriminates: H3 (sampler step-size/funnel geometry) vs H4 (likelihood rigidity) — does pushing target_accept and constraining the noise-scale prior remove divergences or only dent them
expected_findings: H3: raising target_accept to ~0.9 and tightening the alpha_sigma prior reduces divergence count somewhat but does not eliminate them; the alpha-scale parameter remains high-uncertainty / low-ESS
situations: Hierarchical probit/psychometric model (subject-level params feeding a normal-CDF link into a Bernoulli likelihood) ported from JAGS to PyMC; NUTS fails immedia
leaves: pm.sample(target_accept=0.9, more tune); tighten alpha_sigma to Uniform(0,5). Helps a little but hundreds of divergences persist and alpha_sigma stays poorly identified.

branches.if_observe: divergences mostly gone after target_accept/prior tightening
branches.then: accept as a geometry issue; consider non-centered parameterization of subject params

branches.if_observe: divergences persist despite target_accept and tighter scale prior
branches.then: suspect the likelihood itself is too rigid (H4) — a single conflicting choice in a saturated zone forces the slope to collapse

action: Robustify and stabilize the link: handle the immediate-reward edge case, swap the saturating normal CDF for a logistic link, and add a lapse-rate floor so the function asymptotes at epsilon / 1-epsilon instead of 0/1
discriminates: H4/H5 — whether giving the likelihood a small irreducible error floor (and a gentler-tailed link) lets outlier choices be absorbed without the slope going to zero, removing the divergence-driving rigidity
expected_findings: H5: logistic link decays gently (e.g. logistic(-100)~3.7e-44, logistic(-20)~2e-9) instead of underflowing to 0, improving numerics; H4: a per-subject lapse rate epsilon~Beta(2,51) lets the model tolerate inconsistent responses without collapsing alpha, reducing divergences
situations: Hierarchical probit/psychometric model (subject-level params feeding a normal-CDF link into a Bernoulli likelihood) ported from JAGS to PyMC; NUTS fails immedia
leaves: V_A=A when DA==0 (immediate reward). logistic(x,alpha)=1/(1+exp(-alpha*x)); Phi(x,alpha,eps)=eps+(1-2*eps)*logistic(x,alpha); eps=pm.Beta('eps',2,51,shape=participants). In PyMC the logistic CDF is pm

branches.if_observe: lapse-rate + logistic link removes divergences and fits stabilize
branches.then: adopt the robustified psychometric likelihood; note real human data still needs case-by-case tuning

branches.if_observe: logistic link alone (no lapse rate) does not fix divergences
branches.then: the lapse-rate floor — not the link swap — is the load-bearing change; ensure target_accept=0.9 is also set

action: Reframe prior elicitation: reject 'priors from the data' in favor of design/domain-based weakly-informative priors
discriminates: Whether reasonable priors require seeing the data (empirical) or can be derived from experiment design plus the model's link-scale semantics
expected_findings: Empirical route reuses the flat-fit posterior as a prior -> overconfidence / double-use of data. Design route exploits that shifted-lognormal coefficients are log multiplicative changes, so a plausible max fold-change between predictor extremes bounds the coefficient with no data needed.
situations: A brms penalized-spline GAM (shifted-lognormal model, mpg ~ s(wt)) converges poorly / throws a divergence under the default flat priors. The user wants principl
leaves: Shifted-lognormal coefficients are log(multiplicative change in mean). Rule of thumb: max_predictor_difference * coef < log(20) for <20-fold change; since log(20)/2 ~= 1.5, use normal(0, 1.5/max_predi

branches.if_observe: Domain gives a plausible fold-change bound across the predictor range
branches.then: Set normal(0, log(fold)/2 / max_predictor_difference) on the b coefficient; verify it would be defensible without having seen the data

branches.if_observe: No domain bound available
branches.then: Fall back to prior predictive checks (prior_only=TRUE) and tune until implied effect sizes look plausible

action: Decode the brms/mgcv spline parametrization by inspecting the design matrix and consulting the penalized-spline = random-effect equivalence
discriminates: Whether swt_1 is the wiggliness/penalty parameter or the unpenalized linear (null-space) basis coefficient
expected_findings: Inspecting make_standata()$Xs and plotting it against the raw predictor shows Xs is a (centered/rescaled) LINEAR transform of wt -> swt_1 is the linear trend term, not a wiggliness control. sds(swt_1) is the SD of the penalized (range-space) basis functions and is what governs wiggliness.
situations: A brms penalized-spline GAM (shifted-lognormal model, mpg ~ s(wt)) converges poorly / throws a divergence under the default flat priors. The user wants principl
leaves: Run dd <- make_standata(brmsformula(mpg ~ s(wt)), data); plot(dd$Xs, wt) -> straight line, confirming swt_1 = linear trend of (rescaled) wt. Second-derivative penalty leaves constant+linear in the nul

branches.if_observe: Xs is a straight-line function of the predictor
branches.then: Treat swt_1 as the linear-trend coefficient (put a Gaussian/t prior on it) and sds(swt_1) as the wiggliness SD (separate prior)

branches.if_observe: Negative swt_1 estimate
branches.then: Interpret as a negative linear trend of (rescaled) wt on the log mean, not as anything about wiggliness

action: Validate the chosen priors via prior predictive simulation of the smooth itself
discriminates: Whether the joint prior on (b, sds) implies plausible curve wiggliness and effect sizes vs. implausibly wild or overly rigid smooths
expected_findings: Sampling with prior_only=TRUE and plotting conditional_smooths()/conditional draws shows the prior-implied family of curves; the y-axis range reveals implied effect sizes and the curvature reveals wiggliness, which can be matched to domain expectations.
situations: A brms penalized-spline GAM (shifted-lognormal model, mpg ~ s(wt)) converges poorly / throws a divergence under the default flat priors. The user wants principl
leaves: Set separate priors: a Gaussian/t on swt_1 (linear basis) and a prior on the sds term (wiggliness SD). Fit with prior_only=TRUE, then plot prior draws via conditional_smooths(); check (a) wiggliness a

branches.if_observe: Prior smooths too wiggly
branches.then: Tighten the sds prior toward 0 (more smoothing)

branches.if_observe: Prior-implied effect sizes (y-axis range) too large
branches.then: Tighten the swt_1 / coefficient prior

action: Reframe: take the WORKING idiomatic library model as ground truth and ask whether the two log-densities are actually equal, rather than asking whether HMC failed
discriminates: H1 (sampler/geometry fault) vs H2/H3 (the hand-coded density encodes a different distribution)
expected_findings: If H1, the idiomatic model would ALSO degrade with dimension/geometry; instead it samples cleanly to 1000d -> geometry is not the cause and the bad run must be a mis-specified target, pointing at H2/H3
situations: Two formulations of the SAME target (Neal's funnel) disagree under HMC/NUTS: a hand-written unnormalized log_prob / custom potential energy gives bad samples, w
leaves: Idiomatic centered funnel sampling cleanly to 1000d is itself surprising (centered funnel is the canonical HMC failure), but the decisive contrast is internal: same sampler, two densities, divergent r

branches.if_observe: idiomatic version is clean while hand-coded is bad
branches.then: stop blaming HMC; audit the hand-written log_prob term-by-term against the library density

branches.if_observe: both versions degrade similarly
branches.then: revisit H1/geometry and reparameterization (non-centered funnel)

action: Expand each library Normal log-density analytically and diff it against the hand-written expression, focusing on terms that depend on the scale parameter v
discriminates: H2 (missing log-scale/normalizer term) vs H4 (plumbing/broadcasting) vs H3 (var-vs-std)
expected_findings: Each x_i ~ N(0, exp(v)) contributes -x_i^2/(2 exp(v)) - (1/2)v plus a constant; the user's hand-coded res = -sum x^2/(2 exp(v)) - v^2/18 OMITS the summed -v/2 (the per-dimension -log std). That omission is exactly a missing volume/normalizer term that varies with v, so it distorts the v-marginal -- the funnel's hard direction
situations: Two formulations of the SAME target (Neal's funnel) disagree under HMC/NUTS: a hand-written unnormalized log_prob / custom potential energy gives bad samples, w
leaves: Corrected term: res = (-(x**2)/(2*exp(v)) - v/2).sum() - v**2/18. Note the missing term scales with dimension D (D*(-v/2)), so the bug worsens precisely as dim grows, explaining why the hand-coded run

branches.if_observe: missing per-dimension v-dependent term found
branches.then: add it; that is the bug, not HMC

branches.if_observe: expressions already match
branches.then: fall back to H4: check reshape/broadcasting and the M upper-bound offset in the potential plumbing

action: Pin down whether exp(v) is variance or std, and set the log-scale coefficient accordingly
discriminates: H3: -v/2 (exp(v)=variance, std=exp(v/2)) vs -v (treating exp(v) as std)
expected_findings: Idiomatic model used scale = sqrt(exp(v)) = exp(v/2), so exp(v) is the VARIANCE; log std = v/2, so the per-dim normalizer correction is -v/2, NOT -v (a reviewer's first patch using -std/-v overshoots)
situations: Two formulations of the SAME target (Neal's funnel) disagree under HMC/NUTS: a hand-written unnormalized log_prob / custom potential energy gives bad samples, w
leaves: Final agreed term -v/2 per dimension; this matches scale=sqrt(exp(v)). A proposed fix written as '- std' (i.e. -v) is itself wrong by a factor of 2 in the exponent -- the var-vs-std trap

branches.if_observe: scale was sqrt(exp(v))
branches.then: use -v/2 per dim

branches.if_observe: scale defined directly as exp(v)
branches.then: exp(v) is std, correction would be -v per dim -- keep coefficient consistent with the chosen scale definition

action: Weigh hand-coded potential vs idiomatic library distributions for correctness and debuggability
discriminates: when the hand-coded path is justified vs when it just invites silent normalizer/broadcasting bugs
expected_findings: Normalization is irrelevant to HMC (only gradients of log-density matter), so a hand-coded unnormalized potential is valid in principle; BUT for any target expressible with library distributions, the idiomatic form is preferred because the per-dimension log-Jacobian/normalizer and broadcasting are handled automatically and bugs are far harder to spot in hand-written densities
situations: Two formulations of the SAME target (Neal's funnel) disagree under HMC/NUTS: a hand-written unnormalized log_prob / custom potential energy gives bad samples, w
leaves: Reserve hand-coded log_prob / custom potential_energy (subclass HMC/NUTS, or a custom TorchDistribution whose log_prob returns the unnormalized density, or the Bernoulli(exp(logp - M)) injection trick

branches.if_observe: target expressible as a generative model
branches.then: use idiomatic library distributions (ground-truth, fewer bugs)

branches.if_observe: only an unnormalized potential is available
branches.then: use custom potential / custom-distribution route, and validate it against a known case (like the funnel) before trusting it

action: Clarify the non-linear-model estimand: the fitted equation is the top observable line; lower lines define how Tprev/Se/Sp are built, so Tprev must be reconstructed from its own linear predictor draws before applying inv_logit
discriminates: user back-transformation error vs genuine model misfit
expected_findings: If purely a transformation error, correctly reconstructed Tprev aligns with the no-logit / truePrev() JAGS benchmark (~0.37). If misfit, Se collapses (~0.6, below its beta(10,1) prior) and Tprev stays inflated regardless.
situations: A non-linear binomial brms model for true-prevalence estimation (Count|trials(total) ~ Tprev*Se + (1-Tprev)*(1-Sp), with Tprev ~ species + (1|site), Se+Sp ~ 1,
leaves: extract posterior draws of the Tprev linear predictor (species + (1|site)) via fitted(mod, nlpar='Tprev') / prepare_predictions, then inv_logit; do not inv_logit the raw summary coefficient; also note

branches.if_observe: corrected reconstruction still gives inflated Tprev and depressed Se
branches.then: treat as identifiability problem, not transformation; pursue Se+Sp constraint

branches.if_observe: no-logit model already returns Tprev~0.37 matching JAGS truePrev()
branches.then: localize remaining trouble to the inv_logit + RE interaction, not the prevalence math

action: Diagnose the brms centered-Intercept parameterization as the reason bounded beta priors do not act as intended under identity link
discriminates: centered-intercept parameterization artifact vs genuine prior misspecification vs RE problem
expected_findings: Under H-centered-intercept: the ~0+species (no-intercept) model samples cleanly with correct estimates while the intercept version diverges and ignores bounds; logit-link version shows identical priors across species (no identity-link bound conflict). This isolates the bug.
situations: A non-linear binomial brms model for true-prevalence estimation (Count|trials(total) ~ Tprev*Se + (1-Tprev)*(1-Sp), with Tprev ~ species + (1|site), Se+Sp ~ 1,
leaves: brms mean-centers population predictors and sets the Intercept prior on the centered intercept (default student_t(3,0,2.5)), so beta(2,2,lb=0,ub=1) on class='Intercept' is not the bound the user think

branches.if_observe: ~0+species clean but +species diverges
branches.then: confirm centered-Intercept artifact; fix by beta(2,2) on intercept + normal(0,0.5) on species (allowing negative), not bounded beta on species

branches.if_observe: logit link removes the discrepancy
branches.then: confirm identity-link x bounded-beta interaction as the mechanism

action: Impose the sensitivity/specificity identifiability constraint Se+Sp>1 (and keep the linear predictor inside [0,1]) to break label-switching / multimodality
discriminates: Se/Sp non-identifiability (multimodal likelihood) vs residual RE-prior/funnel issues
expected_findings: If the constraint resolves the divergences/rejections, the no-gold-standard identifiability was the driver. If divergences persist, residual cause is the RE funnel / too-few-levels.
situations: A non-linear binomial brms model for true-prevalence estimation (Count|trials(total) ~ Tprev*Se + (1-Tprev)*(1-Sp), with Tprev ~ species + (1|site), Se+Sp ~ 1,
leaves: Osupplisson: reparameterize Se ~ inv_logit(exp(gamma_se)), Sp ~ inv_logit(exp(gamma_sp)) so both lie in (0.5,1) and Se+Sp>1 automatically (Se>0.5,Sp>0.5 is mild else it is a coin toss); avoid hard lb=

branches.if_observe: constraint + decentered intercept gives clean simple model but RE still diverges
branches.then: treat RE as the remaining issue: test necessity rather than force convergence

action: Test whether the random effect is supported by the data using SBC, ICC, Bayes-R2, and formal LOO/WAIC model comparison fixed-vs-random
discriminates: RE genuinely unsupported/misspecified vs RE needed but mis-priored
expected_findings: If LOO favors the random model and ICC is non-trivial, keep the RE but fix its prior; if comparisons show no gain and SBC reproduces the pathology under a no-RE generative process, the RE is the misspecification.
situations: A non-linear binomial brms model for true-prevalence estimation (Count|trials(total) ~ Tprev*Se + (1-Tprev)*(1-Sp), with Tprev ~ species + (1|site), Se+Sp ~ 1,
leaves: rule-of-thumb >=5-6 levels for a stable variance component (only 3 sites/species here); ICC<~0.07 suggests RE not useful (performance::icc); add_criterion(bayes_R2); loo_compare(fit_fixed, fit_random)

branches.if_observe: loo favors random model AND only tiny RE-SD (e.g. beta(2,50)) removes divergences
branches.then: WARNING (jd_c): do not use the SD prior as a tuning knob to delete divergences; justify priors via scientific knowledge + prior predictive checks, since shrinking SD to ~0 makes the RE meaningless even if it improves sampling

branches.if_observe: ICC tiny and no LOO improvement
branches.then: drop the random intercept; the fixed-effects model is adequate

action: Reframe the symptom: treat extreme sampler settings as a model/data-fit red flag, not a runtime knob
discriminates: model-data pathology vs genuine hardware/runtime ceiling
expected_findings: If pathology: no setting will rescue it cheaply and Rhat/n_eff stay marginal (observed). If truly just big-but-fine: runtime scales with params/data and diagnostics are clean (NOT observed — diagnostics are poor).
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: Highest Rhat 1.07, lowest n_eff 53 despite adapt_delta=0.999999999 / max_treedepth=13; flag a~normal(11,1) as slightly odd but harmless

branches.if_observe: diagnostics stay bad at extreme settings
branches.then: stop tuning the sampler; investigate identifiability/geometry

action: Refit the same model spec with a trusted reference implementation (brms) on a representative sample
discriminates: coding/indexing bug in bespoke Stan vs intrinsic model-data difficulty
expected_findings: If bug: brms converges fine and differs from user code. If intrinsic: brms reproduces the same poor Rhat/ESS (this is what happened).
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: brms reproduced identical convergence diagnostics => code is exonerated, problem is model-vs-data

branches.if_observe: brms also fails to converge
branches.then: rule out code bug; localize to data/geometry/identifiability

branches.if_observe: brms converges well
branches.then: diff user Stan against brms-generated code for the discrepancy

action: Build a simulator that draws from the model (hyperparams->params->data) so 'true' values are known, then fit a tiny simulated dataset
discriminates: data-driven non-identifiability vs a model that is fine when data matches assumptions
expected_findings: If model is fine on matched data, the real data violates an assumption (e.g. no slope info); if it also fails on simulated data, the parameterization itself is unidentified
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: Recommend SBC-style simulate-and-recover on a tiny set to enable full pairs/everything-vs-everything plotting

branches.if_observe: recovers cleanly on simulated data
branches.then: the real data is the culprit — hunt for groups where predictors don't vary

action: Tie the geometry to the data structure: groups with no variation in the (binary) predictor cannot identify a slope, so that slope's posterior collapses toward the prior and tangles with the intercept
discriminates: low obs-per-group in general vs specifically no within-group predictor variation as the driver
expected_findings: Convergence degrades with FEWER obs per group and with MORE no-variance groups; group-size heterogeneity alone is NOT the cause (user's own tests confirmed)
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: User's tests: differing group sizes don't hurt, but low average obs/group does; intercepts-only model converges in 32min/Rhat1.009 — slopes on uninformative groups are the break point

branches.if_observe: removing varying slopes restores fast clean fit
branches.then: the varying-slope-on-uninformative-groups is confirmed as the cause

action: Decouple the unidentified slopes from the likelihood via a masked predictor: x_2 = x for groups with variance, 0 otherwise, then estimate (x_2 | group)
discriminates: whether the fix is dropping data vs surgically severing uninformative parameters from the likelihood
expected_findings: Masked parameters' posterior == prior (no likelihood contribution) => easy for sampler, geometry simplifies, while informative groups still get real slopes
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: Proposed x_2 masking trick (untested caveat); parameter stays in model but is data-disconnected so posterior equals prior

branches.if_observe: user notes predictor is binary so some groups are all-0/all-1 already
branches.then: exactly the no-variance case the mask targets; mask isolates those groups' slopes

action: Check applicability of the Cauchy tangent-transform to constrained (half-)Cauchy scale priors
discriminates: valid speedup vs misapplied trick
expected_findings: Tangent transform applies to the FULL unconstrained Cauchy; the lower=0 bound already thins the tail, so the trick adds little here. Heavy-tailed scale prior is itself questionable.
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: Niko: half-Cauchy lower=0 transform already thins tail; prefer exponential(1) (or Weibull) over half-Cauchy for the scale; Vehtari cites Rhat/ESS Cauchy experiments showing the difference is big

branches.if_observe: scale prior is half-Cauchy (lower=0)
branches.then: skip tangent reparam; switch to exponential/Weibull scale prior

action: Triage compute knobs vs the real bottleneck (geometry); note memory is not maxed and small-sample fits show the same bad diagnostics
discriminates: runtime-bound (parallelism helps) vs mixing-bound (parallelism does not fix Rhat/ESS)
expected_findings: More chains/cores and map-reduce reduce wall-clock but do NOT cure poor Rhat/ESS caused by a hard posterior; same diagnostics on N<3000 samples confirm it's not memory/scale
situations: A nominally "simple" hierarchical linear model (Gaussian outcome, crossed random intercepts AND slopes, non-centered + Cholesky) needs absurd tuning (adapt_delt
leaves: Defer MPI/map-reduce/extra-chains; memory not the issue (512GB, small samples same diagnostics); fix identifiability first

branches.if_observe: small-sample fit has same bad Rhat/ESS
branches.then: rule out memory/scale; problem is geometry, not compute

action: Clarify the role of theta and the data-partition structure before proposing any code
discriminates: theta-as-covariance-hyperparameter vs theta-as-input/covariate; one-GP vs two-GP partition of the data
expected_findings: If theta were a lengthscale-type hyperparameter, standard prior+cov-func suffices (no special handling). The user clarifies theta sits in the x-slot of the covariance/mean — confirming the input-position hypothesis and ruling out the trivial path.
situations: Implementing Kennedy-O'Hagan Bayesian model calibration with a GP in PyMC3, where an unknown parameter theta must enter the GP as a covariate (input to the cova
leaves: Q: 'are they regular covariance function parameters like lengthscale?' -> answer: no, theta plays the role of x in the covariance matrix, same theta for each z_i; data splits into z_i (unknown theta)

branches.if_observe: theta is a covariance hyperparameter
branches.then: ordinary GP with prior on hyperparam; done

branches.if_observe: theta sits in the input/covariate slot
branches.then: must construct the GP input matrix symbolically including the latent theta

action: Validate the additive/partitioned GP composition empirically rather than by assertion
discriminates: whether the partitioned/additive GP construction actually recovers parameters vs is mis-specified
expected_findings: Construction is a valid use case; recommend a recovery simulation. User later reports the partitioned-GP composition 'works'.
situations: Implementing Kennedy-O'Hagan Bayesian model calibration with a GP in PyMC3, where an unknown parameter theta must enter the GP as a covariate (input to the cova
leaves: Advice: 'run some simulation to see if it can recover the parameters' — parameter-recovery check on simulated data is the arbiter.

action: Pin down the generative model — write mean m(x,theta)=x^T theta and the anisotropic ExpQuad covariance over (x,theta) jointly, plus the exact data/covariate lists
discriminates: x-can-be-dropped (information already in y) vs (x,theta) jointly required because the covariance and mean both depend on both and theta must be inferred
expected_findings: Because both mean and covariance depend on theta and theta must be inferred, theta must appear in the live input matrix; it cannot be folded away. This forces the symbolic-input requirement.
situations: Implementing Kennedy-O'Hagan Bayesian model calibration with a GP in PyMC3, where an unknown parameter theta must enter the GP as a covariate (input to the cova

action: Probe the role of the initialization intercept: ask whether the ordinary per-subject random intercept can double as the process kickoff, and whether the first point needs its own likelihood term distinct from later points.

action: Examine the data-flow of the 'observed' argument: does the quantity passed to the likelihood depend on the model's random variables?

action: Reformulate as a direct generative model of the observed binary mask: map PDE temperature to per-cell fire probability and use a Bernoulli likelihood on the FIXED observed mask
discriminates: fixes H3 (and sidesteps H2's Beta-endpoint trap) by giving a proper data-generating process

Now the model outputs a 0/1 mask matching the data type; IoU distribution comes 'for free' from posterior predictive samples. To inject spatial structure, center a process on the PDE output (mu=T_pred) with a structured covariance (diagonal + adjacent off-diagonal), or convolve.

action: Read the square (N×N) shape as a diagnostic signal of a broadcasting/shape error in the likelihood, not a cosmetic quirk
discriminates: Confirms the shape-bug hypothesis and ties it to the likelihood: the categorical/multinomial p needs (N,K), and an (N,)×(N,) outer-broadcast is producing N×N — so the regression output geometry is incompatible with a categorical observation
expected_findings: Under shape-bug: the (85,85) is an accidental outer product from missing reshape; p should be (85,4). Under likelihood-mis-spec: same root cause — Multinomial's p/value were fed the wrong-rank tensor, hence the init -inf. Resolves once a reshape((-1,1)) (or building p as (N,K)) is added.
