branches.then: discrete scheduler bug at that thread count — focus there

action: Test within-setting reproducibility: rerun the SAME thread count with the SAME seed and compare draws bitwise
discriminates: RNG/nondeterminism hypothesis vs deterministic-but-thread-count-dependent bug
expected_findings: Nondeterminism hypothesis: two identical-config runs differ. Bug hypothesis: all.equal(fit, fit_rerun) == TRUE — fully reproducible at fixed thread count, so the variation is a deterministic function of thread count, not random.
situations: A model using map_rect for within-chain parallelization gives different posteriors when only the number of threads (STAN_NUM_THREADS) is changed, holding data,
leaves: Sys.setenv(STAN_NUM_THREADS=2) run twice, seed=42 → all.equal(as.matrix(fit2), as.matrix(fit2_2)) == TRUE. Confirms determinism; rules out RNG nondeterminism. The bug is a deterministic, thread-count-

action: Independent reproduction by a maintainer from CmdStan, sweeping thread counts
discriminates: User-side configuration error vs library-level bug; also pins the exact triggering thread count
expected_findings: User-error hypothesis: maintainer cannot reproduce. Bug hypothesis: maintainer reproduces and finds N=1,3,4,5 line up identically but N=2 does NOT — a single anomalous thread count.
situations: A model using map_rect for within-chain parallelization gives different posteriors when only the number of threads (STAN_NUM_THREADS) is changed, holding data,
leaves: syclik reproduces from CmdStan: threads 1,3,4,5 identical; threads=2 deviates. Independent repro + the N=2-specific signature confirms a library bug in the concurrent map_rect scheduler.

branches.if_observe: cannot reproduce
branches.then: back to user-side config diff

branches.if_observe: reproduces, anomaly localized to N=2
branches.then: minimize to a bug report

action: Minimize to a trivial map_rect program (empty shards, dummy mapped function) and run at the triggering thread count
discriminates: Whether the defect is in shard work-partitioning/result-assembly itself vs something model-specific
expected_findings: Scheduler-bug hypothesis: minimal program with shards=3 under N=2 throws a size-mismatch in assembling shard outputs (e.g. 'Rows of left-hand-side (21) and right-hand-side (20) must match') and/or a malloc 'pointer being freed was not allocated' / abort — i.e. wrong result buffer sizing from thread scheduling.
situations: A model using map_rect for within-chain parallelization gives different posteriors when only the number of threads (STAN_NUM_THREADS) is changed, holding data,
leaves: Minimal map_rect model with shards=3 under STAN_NUM_THREADS=2 errors with assign row-mismatch (21 vs 20) and a malloc free error. Demonstrates the concurrent scheduler mis-partitions/mis-assembles sha

branches.if_observe: minimal repro errors only at N=2
branches.then: file Math issue; defect is in std::thread scheduling of shards

branches.if_observe: MPI backend also fails
branches.then: deeper map_rect logic bug, not just threading scheduler

action: Propose a per-observation shared latent-error vector with an MVN prior, one component entering each outcome's linear predictor
discriminates: Whether residual cross-outcome correlation can be induced by a shared continuous latent term that HMC can sample
expected_findings: Shared-latent: gives a clean correlation channel via the MVN covariance, fits in Stan, scales in HMC. Copula: alternative but needs CDFs and is messy for discrete. Stacked-RE: equivalent reframing as multilevel intercepts.
situations: Want a joint model for two (or more) outcomes of different types (e.g. continuous Gaussian Y1 and binary logistic Y2) that allows residual dependence between th
leaves: epsilon_n in R^3 ~ MVN(0, Sigma); Y1 ~ normal(eps1 + alpha + beta*x, exp(log sigma + eps2)); Y2 ~ bernoulli(logit^-1(eps3 + gamma + delta*u)). Bob's reply post 2.

branches.if_observe: User only wants the means/linear predictors to covary, not the variance
branches.then: Drop the variance-perturbing component (eps2); keep only location latent terms per outcome — the variance perturbation was an over-inclusion ('I just thought you wanted all parameters to covary')

branches.if_observe: User worries n*k latent params blow up effective parameters / slow sampling / instability
branches.then: Reassure: HMC-NUTS scales very well in random-effect dimension if geometry is benign (cite fits with 400K-1M random effects in ~30 min); the partial-pooling MVN prior constrains them, so it is not unstable despite weak per-unit constraints

action: Name the concrete copula pathologies for mixed discrete-continuous outcomes
discriminates: Whether 'copula is messy' is a vague aesthetic complaint or a real identifiability/uniqueness problem
expected_findings: Real problems: copula is NOT unique when a margin is discrete (arbitrary choices needed) and copulas restrict the attainable corr(Y1,Y2) range; offset by the benefit that marginals stay exactly standard.
situations: Want a joint model for two (or more) outcomes of different types (e.g. continuous Gaussian Y1 and binary logistic Y2) that allows residual dependence between th
leaves: Harrell post 3: non-uniqueness with discrete margins + correlation-range restriction. Counter-pointer (Andrew, post 4): mixed continuous-discrete margins are handled 'trivially' for a Gaussian copula

action: Probe marginalization / interpretability tradeoff of the latent-error approach
discriminates: Whether integrating out the shared latent error leaves the familiar standard univariate model (copula's main selling point) or distorts it
expected_findings: Shared-latent: marginalizing out epsilon is hard/intractable and the implied marginal for one outcome may NOT resemble the standard model (a real downside). Copula: by construction preserves the exact standard marginals. You don't need to marginalize to USE the fitted model, but you lose the clean marginal interpretation.
situations: Want a joint model for two (or more) outcomes of different types (e.g. continuous Gaussian Y1 and binary logistic Y2) that allows residual dependence between th
leaves: Bob post 2 ('hard to marginalize out epsilon, no closed-form direct definition'); Harrell post 3 acknowledges 'the resulting marginal may not resemble standard models — a problem copulas don't have'.

action: Reframe as a stacked long-format model: each outcome is a row for the same unit, induce within-unit correlation via ordinary MVN random intercepts
discriminates: Whether standard random-effects software (brms) can produce the cross-outcome correlation without bespoke copula code
expected_findings: Stacked-RE: works (Chib & Winkelmann 2001 correlated-count precedent; Conor reports success on 11 Poisson outcomes via MVN on log latent intercepts, letting Stan estimate the correlation matrix directly with no special factorization). Caveats surfaced by Harrell: hard when outcomes have dramatically different scales, and harder to assign totally different predictors per outcome than in the explicit-latent approach.
situations: Want a joint model for two (or more) outcomes of different types (e.g. continuous Gaussian Y1 and binary logistic Y2) that allows residual dependence between th
leaves: Andrew post 8 brms proof-of-concept gist for correlated binomial/Poisson; Conor posts 12/14 — MVN on log random intercepts across 11 Poisson margins, Stan estimates correlations directly.

branches.if_observe: Outcomes are on dramatically different scales
branches.then: Flag as a potential failure mode of the stacked approach (Harrell post 9)

branches.if_observe: Need different predictor sets per outcome
branches.then: Prefer the explicit per-outcome latent-injection form over the stacked form, which makes outcome-specific predictors awkward

action: Offer the conditional / autologistic alternative: put one outcome (or its residual) as a covariate in the other model
discriminates: Whether to model dependence via a latent shared term vs. directly conditioning one outcome on the other (and whether latent residuals are identifiable for binary outcomes)
expected_findings: Conditional approach sidesteps the poorly-identified latent-residual autoregression for binary outcomes (autologistic analogy: lagged/observed outcomes as covariates are better identified than latent-residual AR). Cost: introduces directionality / conditioning order, and breaks clean joint/marginal interpretation.
situations: Want a joint model for two (or more) outcomes of different types (e.g. continuous Gaussian Y1 and binary logistic Y2) that allows residual dependence between th
leaves: Jacob post 10: autologistic analogy — for binary, latent-residual AR is poorly identified, use lagged outcomes as covariates instead.

branches.if_observe: Outcomes are measured over time on a regular schedule (Markov-type)
branches.then: Lagged-covariate conditioning is standard and easy; state-occupancy probabilities recoverable by marginalization, preserving ITT in trials after the first period (Harrell post 11)

branches.if_observe: Outcomes are on different follow-up schedules, or one is censored time-to-event and another is ordinal at a fixed time
branches.then: Conditional/lagged approach breaks down: unclear what to condition on, which outcome is the conditioner, and hard to marginalize back to a marginal (e.g. ordinal-at-2-months vs time-to-death-over-1-year) — prefer the symmetric shared-latent or copula form

action: Use copula's direct CDF instead of an observation-level latent error for that margin
discriminates: Which dependence mechanism is even feasible when a margin cannot accept an observation-level random effect
expected_findings: Shared-latent fails: a negative binomial is already a Poisson-with-observation-level-RE, so you cannot add another observation-level RE on it (brms book result). Copula works: the negative-binomial CDF is computable directly in Stan, so the copula route remains available where the latent-RE route is blocked.
situations: Want a joint model for two (or more) outcomes of different types (e.g. continuous Gaussian Y1 and binary logistic Y2) that allows residual dependence between th
leaves: Matthijs post 15 — negative binomial = Poisson + obs-level RE, so obs-level MVN effects can't be layered on it; NB CDF computable directly, so copula remains usable.

action: Disambiguate the estimand before testing any claim — separate the MLE (likelihood-only) from Bayes estimators (posterior mean/median/MAP)
discriminates: whether the question is even well-posed; redirects target from MLE to posterior mean (H1 reframed)
expected_findings: H1/H2/H3: claim only meaningful once estimand fixed to posterior mean. H4: even after fixing, no estimator guarantees the ordering.
situations: User holds the intuition that a non-uniform (informative) prior always "shrinks" the estimate: i.e. the posterior mean is supposed to land somewhere between the
leaves: Point out priors do not affect the MLE at all; they yield OTHER estimators (posterior mean, median, MAP). The claim must be restated about the posterior mean. Also flag that for none of these estimato

branches.if_observe: user agrees they mean posterior mean lands between MLE and prior mean
branches.then: proceed to stress-test the general claim H1 with a counterexample

action: Construct a minimal multimodal-prior counterexample to refute the fully general claim
discriminates: H1 (general truth) vs H4 (false in general)
expected_findings: H1: predicts posterior mean in [0, 49.5]. H4: posterior mean goes negative, violating the bound — H1 falsified.
situations: User holds the intuition that a non-uniform (informative) prior always "shrinks" the estimate: i.e. the posterior mean is supposed to land somewhere between the
leaves: Lognormal prior (unimodal, but right-skewed so mean > mode) with a likelihood that concentrates between the prior's mode and its mean. Because the prior mean sits in the long tail, the posterior mean

Standard-normal likelihood; prior = equal-weight mixture of N(-1) and N(+100). Prior mean is ~+49.5 but the posterior mean gets pulled NEGATIVE — outside the [MLE, prior mean] interval. The far mode c

branches.if_observe: posterior mean lands outside [MLE, prior mean]
branches.then: H1 rejected; user retreats to a narrower sufficient condition (unimodality)

action: Identify the conjugate / pseudo-observation structure under which the posterior mean is provably a convex combination of prior and data means
discriminates: H3 (conjugate pseudo-obs sufficient) vs H4 (only a heuristic)
expected_findings: H3: convex-combination algebra guarantees the ordering within this family. H4: holds, but the condition is very restrictive and not general.
situations: User holds the intuition that a non-uniform (informative) prior always "shrinks" the estimate: i.e. the posterior mean is supposed to land somewhere between the
leaves: If the prior can be written as n0 'fake' observations from the same distribution as the n real observations (conjugate exponential-family / pseudo-count form), the posterior mean is a weighted average

branches.if_observe: condition is conjugate-only and assumes equal pseudo-count weight across all coordinates
branches.then: accept as a narrow sufficient condition, not a general law; H3 holds only locally

action: Construct a general spike-in-likelihood argument showing the ordering can be broken for essentially ANY prior/likelihood pair
discriminates: whether H4 (false in general) is robust, not just dependent on exotic priors
expected_findings: H4: confirms the property fails for an arbitrary base model via a constructive perturbation — it is at most a heuristic.
situations: User holds the intuition that a non-uniform (informative) prior always "shrinks" the estimate: i.e. the posterior mean is supposed to land somewhere between the
leaves: Take any prior+likelihood with prior mean != posterior mean. Insert a narrow finite spike in the likelihood between the prior and posterior means — narrow enough to leave the posterior essentially unc

branches.if_observe: spike moves MLE but not posterior
branches.then: general counterexample established; property is a heuristic only

action: Invoke reparameterization (in)variance: contrast the MLE (invariant) with the posterior mean (not invariant)
discriminates: H1/H2/H3 (any parameterization-free truth) vs H4 (the very notion of 'between' is parameterization-dependent)
expected_findings: H4: the ordering is not even a well-defined invariant property; it depends on parameterization. Multidim caveat limits the constructive claim but reinforces that no general invariant law exists.
situations: User holds the intuition that a non-uniform (informative) prior always "shrinks" the estimate: i.e. the posterior mean is supposed to land somewhere between the
leaves: The MLE location is invariant under monotone reparameterization; prior and posterior MEANS are NOT (the mean of a transformed variable != transform of the mean). So one can reparameterize a model to p

branches.if_observe: 1-D reparameterization repositions the MLE relative to the means
branches.then: property is parameterization-dependent; abandon as a general law

branches.if_observe: multidimensional case where reparam couples prior and posterior changes
branches.then: no clean construction guaranteed, but also no general guarantee the property holds — open under multivariate assumptions

action: Locate the idea in prior art — map the Dirichlet-weight scheme onto the fractional-random-weight (Bayesian) bootstrap and onto existing Bayesian-IPW/importance-weighted-bootstrap work
discriminates: whether this is a known equivalence (FRW = Dirichlet-weighted likelihood) vs a genuinely new mechanism, and which existing implementation path to borrow
expected_findings: FRW bootstrap (Xu et al. 2020) and importance-weighted moment matching (Paananen et al.) already use such weights; the IPW blog (Heiss/Nafa) injects posterior weights row-by-row into the outcome model — so the *weighting* is known, the open part is doing it self-contained per-iteration inside Stan
situations: Want to do a "fully Bayesian" bootstrap (Bayesian / fractional-random-weight bootstrap: Dirichlet(1)·N weights summing to N) inside a Stan/brms model so each po
leaves: References: Xu et al. 2020 American Statistician; Paananen et al. 2021 IWMM (only ordinary bootstrap, but works with fractional weights); Heiss blog + ajnafa Latent-Bayesian-MSM repo (posterior weight

branches.if_observe: equivalence holds and an existing per-draw-weight C++ trick exists
branches.then: reuse/adapt that C++ routine for self-contained per-iteration Dirichlet draws

branches.if_observe: no existing self-contained-per-iteration approach
branches.then: treat the per-iteration RNG injection as the novel piece to build

action: Diagnose why the precomputed-weight-matrix approach fails and decide to generate weights internally instead of passing them as data
discriminates: whether the bottleneck is the size/growth of the passed-in weight matrix (data-side) vs the likelihood itself
expected_findings: weight matrix grows with N × draws and couples design+outcome stages; expert (ajnafa) confirms it 'doesn't scale well and runs into sampling issues' — motivating sampling weights at the design stage / internally and passing only location+scale, or drawing weights inside the model
situations: Want to do a "fully Bayesian" bootstrap (Bayesian / fractional-random-weight bootstrap: Dirichlet(1)·N weights summing to N) inside a Stan/brms model so each po
leaves: ajnafa's more scalable Latent-Bayesian-MSM passes only weight location+scale as input data; for the pure bootstrap, weights are independent of all parameters so they can simply be drawn inside each it

branches.if_observe: weights independent of model parameters (pure bootstrap case)
branches.then: draw them internally per iteration — no need to pre-store a matrix

branches.if_observe: weights depend on a design-stage model (IPW)
branches.then: pass summary (location/scale) and reconstruct, not the full matrix

action: Probe Stan's execution model and reject naive in-language RNG; route the RNG through an external C++ user_header
discriminates: where a fresh random draw can legally and correctly live in Stan's evaluation lifecycle (model block runs per-gradient-eval; transformed data / generated quantities run at different cadences)
expected_findings: the built-in Dirichlet sampler 'would lead to loud complaints' in the model block; an external C++ function (declared in functions block, defined in a .hpp via user_header) can return a vector and bypass the restriction
situations: Want to do a "fully Bayesian" bootstrap (Bayesian / fractional-random-weight bootstrap: Dirichlet(1)·N weights summing to N) inside a Stan/brms model so each po
leaves: C++ header dirichlet_rng_wrapper using stan::math::dirichlet_rng + boost mt19937; declared in Stan functions block via brms-stancode rewrite (transform_stan_code injects function decls, comments out t

branches.if_observe: RNG called naively on every model-block evaluation
branches.then: weights change within a single HMC trajectory -> inconsistent log-density/gradients -> broken sampler; must gate the draw

action: Introduce a side-channel iteration counter so the C++ RNG caches the draw and refreshes it only when the iteration index advances
discriminates: per-leapfrog-eval freshness (wrong, corrupts trajectory) vs once-per-iteration freshness (correct bootstrap behaviour)
expected_findings: a static iteration counter incremented from the generated-quantities block (which runs once per accepted iteration) lets the wrapper detect a new iteration and only then regenerate; otherwise it returns the cached last_draw — yielding fixed weights per iteration, fresh weights across iterations
situations: Want to do a "fully Bayesian" bootstrap (Bayesian / fractional-random-weight bootstrap: Dirichlet(1)·N weights summing to N) inside a Stan/brms model so each po
leaves: static itct + add_iter() called from generated quantities; dirichlet_rng_wrapper compares itct vs last_itct, regenerates only on change, caches last_draw; weights = dirichlet_rng_wrapper(alpha)*N

branches.if_observe: counter incremented in generated quantities runs once per iteration
branches.then: weights frozen within iteration, refreshed between -> correct

branches.if_observe: no gating / counter
branches.then: weights redrawn every gradient eval -> divergences / non-convergence

action: Run HMC diagnostics and compare the bootstrap posterior to the ordinary fixed-data fit
discriminates: correct implementation (clean diagnostics + sensibly inflated uncertainty) vs broken RNG injection (divergences, bad E-BFMI, non-convergence) vs no effect (identical to fixed fit)
expected_findings: 0 divergences, no max-treedepth hits, E-BFMI ~2 across chains; posterior wider/heavier-tailed than the ordinary brms fit (Intercept Est.Error 0.88 -> 1.46, shape 0.61 -> 0.94), Rhat ~1.01, lower but adequate ESS — matching the expected extra bootstrap variability
situations: Want to do a "fully Bayesian" bootstrap (Bayesian / fractional-random-weight bootstrap: Dirichlet(1)·N weights summing to N) inside a Stan/brms model so each po
leaves: diagnostic_summary() num_divergent all 0, ebfmi ~1.9-2.0; reassemble cmdstan csv into an rstan/brms object (rstan::read_stan_csv, empty=TRUE brm, rename_pars) for standard summary/plotting; heavier ta

branches.if_observe: clean diagnostics + inflated, heavier-tailed posterior
branches.then: implementation validated; per-iteration Dirichlet-weight bootstrap works (still 'unclear whether useful')

branches.if_observe: divergences / poor E-BFMI
branches.then: suspect the iteration-gating (weights changing mid-trajectory) and revisit the counter logic

branches.if_observe: posterior identical to fixed fit
branches.then: suspect weights not actually varying across iterations (counter never advancing / draw cached too aggressively)

action: Diagnose whether the symptom is a numerical (Stan log-prob) issue or a modeling-assumption issue
discriminates: numerical-hack-fixable vs structural model assumption violated
expected_findings: Under H_drop/H_meas-error: it is NOT a Stan log-arithmetic quirk — using ln(x) as predictor implicitly assumes x>0, so x=0 means ln(mu)=-inf => mu=0 => y=0 with certainty; epsilon is a hack that hides a modeling inconsistency
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: ln(x_ij) as predictor implies the model genuinely says x=0 -> y=0; allowing y>0 at x=0 contradicts the model's own generative assumptions, so the fix is in the model, not in epsilon

branches.if_observe: user confirms x=0 -> y=0 is desired behavior
branches.then: show the x=0 rows are informationless and can be dropped (next move)

branches.if_observe: user truly needs y>0 at x=0
branches.then: the predictor x must be replaced by a latent variable (measurement-error model)

action: Decompose the likelihood contribution of x=0 rows by cases on y
discriminates: whether x=0 rows add information vs are degenerate/contradictory
expected_findings: If x=0 & y=0: log-lik = 0 and gradient wrt all params = 0 (posterior identical with or without the row). If x=0 & y!=0: log-lik = -inf (data contradicts model) regardless of parameters. Either way the rows carry no usable gradient information.
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: x=0 rows can be excluded with zero effect on the posterior (y=0) or because they make the model -inf (y>0); dropping them is correct, not a hack

branches.if_observe: all x=0 rows have y=0
branches.then: safe to drop; no information lost

branches.if_observe: some x=0 rows have y>0 and matter scientifically
branches.then: the deterministic x->y link is wrong; move to latent-variable formulation

action: Reframe x as a noisy measurement of a latent expression lambda (measurement-error / errors-in-variables model)
discriminates: noiseless-predictor model vs latent-shared-cause model
expected_findings: Introduce latent lambda_ij with x_ij ~ NB(exp(lambda_ij), phi) and put lambda in the linear predictor (ln mu = a + a_i + lambda + ln cellsize); both modalities generated from a shared latent expression. This removes the log(0) issue structurally and matches the two-assay domain story.
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: Errors-in-variables: model both x and y as noisy reads of a common latent lambda_ik (per gene x cell-type), bridging the two unmatched datasets via per-cell-type means; theoretically appealing though

branches.if_observe: too expensive with full latent lambda
branches.then: seek an efficient approximation of the same theoretically-appealing model

branches.if_observe: latent lambda count is large / weakly identified
branches.then: constrain degrees of freedom (simplex) — later move

action: Rule out a confounder (relative-vs-absolute quantification mismatch) before tuning model internals
discriminates: artifactual disagreement (normalization) vs genuine gene-specific distortion to be modeled
expected_findings: scRNA-seq is relative (read depth splits over cells) while smFISH is closer to absolute; if uncorrected, disagreement is mostly a normalization artifact. Fix by expressing both as relative expression over the same shared gene panel before modeling.
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: Check normalization scope: compute relative expression over the same set of co-measured genes for BOTH modalities so they share a baseline; only then is residual disagreement meaningful signal

branches.if_observe: user already normalized to shared-gene relative expression
branches.then: discrepancy is real; proceed to model the distortion (calibration curve)

branches.if_observe: not yet normalized
branches.then: fix normalization first — likely removes much of the disagreement

action: Recognize the model as a (large) linear regression and reason about identifiability from its parameterization
discriminates: hard latent-variable inference vs ordinary GLM with fixed effects
expected_findings: With lambda_ik treated as fixed effects, the model is ln mu^T = (1-T)a^A + (1-T)a_i^A + T a_i^B + ln lambda_ik + ln cs^T — a linear model equivalent to R formula ~0 + technology + gene:technology + gene:cell_type with cellsize as offset. Identifiability hinges on enough replicates per cell-type x technology; feasible for hundreds of genes, slow but doable for low thousands.
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: Rewrite the hierarchical/indicator model as a dummy-coded linear regression to expose that it is a standard GLM; fittability is governed by replicate counts and priors, not exotic structure

branches.if_observe: enough replicates per cell-type/technology
branches.then: model is well-identified; ML or Bayesian both viable

branches.if_observe: too many genes / too few replicates
branches.then: reduce dof (simplex on lambda) and/or rely on priors

action: Address degrees-of-freedom redundancy and the prediction-time inference problem
discriminates: over-parameterized lambda vs properly-constrained latent simplex; and whether prediction needs re-inference
expected_findings: Relative expression => implied negative cross-gene correlation; theoretically multinomial(counts)/Dirichlet, but for N~200 the effect is minor (multinomial gave no empirical gain). Cleaner fix: keep NB noise but remove one dof by declaring lambda a simplex (built-in Stan type). For new genes lacking y, prediction DOES require inferring lambda from x — zero counts should give an UPPER BOUND on expression, not force lambda=0; noise in predicted y must grow as read counts shrink.
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: Constrain lambda to a simplex to remove redundant dof; accept that prediction on y-less genes requires a light per-gene inference of lambda from x (not a pure plug-in), with read-count-dependent dispe

branches.if_observe: non-linear technology relationship suspected (saturation/detection limit)
branches.then: fit a flexible calibration curve (spline or GP), not an assumed sigmoid

branches.if_observe: low-count genes predicted with overconfident y
branches.then: make dispersion depend on expression / read count so low counts -> wider intervals

action: Interpret hierarchy pathology as misspecification/identifiability signal rather than trusting good-looking PPC
discriminates: genuine good fit vs under-identified/redundant-parameterization + failed VI approximation
expected_findings: Varying intercepts should center near 0; intercepts pulled far from 0 with inflated sigma and an offsetting global intercept indicate a redundant/removed intercept or location non-identifiability (intercept vs random-effect mean trade off). Pareto-k~20 means the VI approximation is unreliable — good PPC/trace do not vouch for it. Needs exact code/data inspection; user should not over-trust the fit.
situations: A multilevel count model uses ln(x_ij) of an observed predictor as a linear-predictor offset (e.g. ln(mu_ij) = a + a_i + ln(x_ij) + ln(cellsize)). When data poi
leaves: Intercept-shift + huge sigma + opposed global/random intercepts = non-identified location (intercept absorbed into random effects); combined with Pareto-k~20 the VI posterior is untrustworthy regardle

branches.if_observe: global and random intercepts cancel (opposite shifts)
branches.then: additive non-identifiability — pin a reference / sum-to-zero constraint, recheck

branches.if_observe: Pareto-k stays large
branches.then: VI is failing; switch to NUTS and re-diagnose before believing the fit

action: Conceptually separate the two diagnostic regimes; establish PPC and MCMC convergence diagnostics answer orthogonal questions
discriminates: whether good PPC can rescue/override bad convergence diagnostics (it cannot) vs whether the two should agree
expected_findings: A wrong posterior that is consistent with (a subset of) the true posterior still generates data that look fine under PPC; PPC passing is fully compatible with broken sampling. Toy: true post normal(0,3), sampler yields normal(1,0.1) -> terrible inference but plausible replicated data.
situations: Single-chain HMC/NUTS fit shows bad MCMC diagnostics (high divergences, very low BFMI, bad R-hat) yet posterior predictive checks look good and the original/sim
leaves: PPC diagnoses model misspecification (fit to data); divergences/BFMI/R-hat/ESS diagnose whether chains converged to the correct posterior GIVEN the model. They are independent axes. PPC is also a 'fis

branches.if_observe: user still treats good PPC as evidence the fit is fine
branches.then: reassert ordering: first pass MCMC diagnostics, THEN do PPC; PPC on an unconverged fit is meaningless

action: Impose a precedence rule on the workflow
discriminates: whether to debug convergence first or interpret the (untrustworthy) posterior first
expected_findings: Convergence diagnostics must pass before PPC is interpretable; otherwise you are doing PPC on samples from the wrong distribution.
situations: Single-chain HMC/NUTS fit shows bad MCMC diagnostics (high divergences, very low BFMI, bad R-hat) yet posterior predictive checks look good and the original/sim
leaves: Rule: get a model that passes R-hat, ESS, divergences, BFMI FIRST; only then use PPC to check the converged model against the data-generating process.

action: Flag the single-chain blind spot in the diagnostic itself
discriminates: whether 'parameters look OK' reflects real convergence vs an insensitive single-chain R-hat
expected_findings: Single chain makes R-hat much less sensitive; cannot detect between-chain non-mixing / multimodality. 'OK-looking' params are not reassuring.
situations: Single-chain HMC/NUTS fit shows bad MCMC diagnostics (high divergences, very low BFMI, bad R-hat) yet posterior predictive checks look good and the original/sim
leaves: Run >=2 (ideally 3-4) parallel chains so R-hat and trace plots can detect non-mixing; single-chain R-hat is structurally weak. User is compute-bound (5 vs 20 days) so settles on >=2 chains as a compro

branches.if_observe: user is compute-constrained and resists 4 chains
branches.then: accept >=2 chains as strictly better than 1; frame as compute-vs-risk-of-wrong-inference tradeoff

action: Localize the regression: identify the change that broke a previously-healthy model
discriminates: newly introduced pathology (QR reparameterization) vs a pre-existing latent problem
expected_findings: Model sampled fine before QR decomposition was added -> QR step is the prime suspect; isolate it.
situations: Single-chain HMC/NUTS fit shows bad MCMC diagnostics (high divergences, very low BFMI, bad R-hat) yet posterior predictive checks look good and the original/sim
leaves: User reports it sampled fine pre-QR-decomposition. QR reparameterization is the suspect; check the QR construction / priors on the transformed coefficients.

action: Shrink the problem: fit a simpler model and/or a data subset to iterate fast
discriminates: code/parameterization bug (reproduces on subset) vs data-scale-only effect
expected_findings: Most model pathologies reproduce on a small subset and small model, enabling fast debugging; folk theorem links the bad geometry to the slow sampling — fixing the model also speeds it up.
situations: Single-chain HMC/NUTS fit shows bad MCMC diagnostics (high divergences, very low BFMI, bad R-hat) yet posterior predictive checks look good and the original/sim
leaves: Standard scaling-down workflow: subset (e.g. 100/500/1000/2000 rows) + simplified model to debug convergence/code (not final estimates), then scale up. References: Gelman et al. 'Bayesian workflow' pr

branches.if_observe: bad diagnostics reproduce on the small subset
branches.then: debug parameterization (QR, priors, non-centering) on the cheap fit before returning to full scale

branches.if_observe: model still must run at full scale or remains slow
branches.then: consider an approximate method (e.g. INLA for multilevel models); validate small-model agreement Stan-vs-INLA, then run INLA at full scale

action: Propose removing one normalization: apply the availability mask on the latent/logit scale and softmax ONCE (mask*mus -> softmax), instead of softmax-then-renormalize
discriminates: whether the redundant normalization is the cause of the aliasing/ridge
expected_findings: If double normalization is the culprit, single-normalization should tighten identifiability and improve mixing.
situations: Hierarchical multinomial/softmax regression (categorical outcome over parties x districts x dates, with a zero-inflation mask for unavailable categories): NUTS
leaves: latent_p = parties_available * mus_parties; slot_prob = softmax(latent_p)

branches.if_observe: masking-then-single-softmax turns absent categories into prob 0.5 instead of 0 (softmax of 0 is not 0)
branches.then: naive single softmax breaks the zero-inflation semantics -> need a mask that maps absent categories to -inf on the latent scale (or otherwise zero post-softmax), not a literal product mask; keep iterating until one normalization both samples well AND yields exact zeros

action: Reduce model depth: drop the weakly-identified top (city) level and fit varying intercepts+slopes (MvNormal with LKJ cov) at the district level only
discriminates: whether an extra hierarchical level is the part that won't identify vs the normalization being the sole issue
expected_findings: Collapsing to 2 levels should markedly improve sampling if the 3rd level was the unidentified piece; mixing improves 'considerably' here.
situations: Hierarchical multinomial/softmax regression (categorical outcome over parties x districts x dates, with a zero-inflation mask for unavailable categories): NUTS
leaves: m_var_effects: per-party MvNormal(ab, chol) over (Ndistricts,3) with LKJCholeskyCov, pivot fixed at constant

action: Reason about where scale is identifiable under a normalized (softmax) link: fix/standardize sigma only at the level absorbed by the normalization, keep hierarchical variance where it survives (slopes / lower levels)
discriminates: which variance parameters are identifiable vs which are aliased by softmax shift/scale invariance
expected_findings: Sigmas at the highest latent level (directly absorbed by softmax normalization) are not meaningful (set to 1); shrinkage/pooling migrates to the slopes — you keep hierarchy without the unidentified noise term.
situations: Hierarchical multinomial/softmax regression (categorical outcome over parties x districts x dates, with a zero-inflation mask for unavailable categories): NUTS
leaves: Set highest-level latent sigma to 1; push hierarchical structure onto slopes; don't blanket-fix all sigmas

action: Separate convergence diagnosis from model-adequacy diagnosis: run PPC and ask whether the DGP is captured, not whether the sampler converged
discriminates: sampler/identifiability problem (now resolved) vs structural mis-specification of the generative model
expected_findings: Good traces + bad PPC => the remaining failure is model structure, not sampling. Time-constant latent probabilities can't reproduce temporal variation across the 12 elections.
situations: Hierarchical multinomial/softmax regression (categorical outcome over parties x districts x dates, with a zero-inflation mask for unavailable categories): NUTS
leaves: Diagnose 'time-ignorant' latent p; predictions overconfident because variation across dates is unmodeled

action: Add a temporal/autocorrelated latent process (autoregressive ~ state-space ~ continuous-state HMM) so latent probabilities evolve over elections
discriminates: static vs dynamic latent-probability model for capturing temporal swings
expected_findings: An autoregressive/state-space temporal component should let latent preferences drift across dates and fix the overconfident, time-flat PPC.
situations: Hierarchical multinomial/softmax regression (categorical outcome over parties x districts x dates, with a zero-inflation mask for unavailable categories): NUTS
leaves: AR == linear-Gaussian state space == continuous-state HMM; final time-aware model samples smoothly with good diagnostics and PPC

action: Gauge divergence count against the known horseshoe funnel geometry instead of treating divergences as a generic blocker
discriminates: benign-funnel-neck (H1) vs a count large enough to threaten the inference you actually care about
expected_findings: Under H1, ~20 out of ~15000 post-warmup draws is small; the unexplored region is the deep-shrinkage neck whose exact exploration does not affect the predictive quantities projpred needs, so it is acceptable to proceed
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: For the regularized horseshoe the posterior typically has a funnel shape; the poorly-explored narrow part flagged by divergences is the region you usually do not care about. ~20 divergences is 'not mu

branches.if_observe: divergence count is small and confined to the high-shrinkage neck
branches.then: accept and proceed to projpred; do not chase adapt_delta/treedepth further

branches.if_observe: divergences are many or co-occur with bad Rhat / biased low-shrinkage region
branches.then: treat as genuine mis-specification and move to tightening the prior (next move)

action: Tighten the horseshoe's heavy components using domain prior information rather than nudging sampler controls
discriminates: prior-mis-specification (H2) vs sampler-tuning deficit (H3)
expected_findings: Shrinking the slab scale (to the plausible magnitude of non-zero weights) and increasing slab degrees-of-freedom makes the slab more informative about the tails, shallows the funnel, and removes divergences; this works where further adapt_delta/treedepth tuning does not
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: Make slab_scale smaller based on carefully-considered prior information about the likely scale of non-zero coefficients, and make slab_df larger to make the prior more informative about the tails.

branches.if_observe: tightened slab removes divergences and posterior is stable
branches.then: keep the horseshoe with the informed slab and proceed

branches.if_observe: still cannot make the regularized horseshoe behave after slab tuning
branches.then: switch prior family (next move)

action: Choose the prior from assumptions about the coefficient-magnitude distribution; if horseshoe is intractable, substitute an alternative sparsifying prior
discriminates: horseshoe-specific funnel pathology (H4) vs a real need for sparsity that a better-behaved prior can also express
expected_findings: Any prior matching the assumed coefficient distribution is valid in the projpred reference model; the R2-D2 prior has empirically worked when the regularized horseshoe could not be made to behave, often sampling more cleanly
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: Pick the prior to match your assumptions about coefficient magnitudes. R2-D2 (Zhang et al. 2022) is a practical fallback that has worked where the regularized horseshoe could not be tuned to behave.

branches.if_observe: alternative prior samples cleanly and reflects intended sparsity
branches.then: adopt it as the reference-model prior

action: Re-evaluate the collinearity-handling strategy against what projpred is designed to do
discriminates: manual correlation-pruning (H5, suboptimal) vs letting the projection-predictive method select among correlated predictors
expected_findings: Pre-screening can drop predictors that are jointly useful; projpred can select the most useful among correlated predictors, so pre-screening is unnecessary. With many highly-correlated predictors, build the reference model via supervised PCA (per Piironen & Vehtari) instead of dropping columns
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: Do not drop both members of a correlated pair — they may jointly predict the target. Let projpred select among correlated predictors. For many highly-correlated predictors, make the reference model wi

branches.if_observe: many strongly-correlated predictors
branches.then: use a supervised-PCA reference model rather than column removal

action: Suspect search-method instability in the L1 path before trusting the size-selection curve
discriminates: true predictive plateau/degradation vs an artifact of the L1 search path in high-dimensional/correlated data
expected_findings: Switching from the default method='l1' to method='forward' yields a more reliable/monotone-looking selection path; the apparent late-size degradation is likely a search artifact, not evidence that more terms help
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: Re-run cv_varsel with method='forward' (instead of the default L1 search) when the selection curve looks anomalous (e.g. metric going negative again at larger sizes).

branches.if_observe: forward search gives a cleaner curve and consistent suggested size
branches.then: trust the forward-search selection

branches.if_observe: curve still anomalous
branches.then: re-examine reference-model quality / whether full model beats the null at all

action: Replace dichotomized 'good vs bad' testing with reporting actual predictive distributions and ELPD comparisons against a plausible (not empty) baseline
discriminates: null-hypothesis/p-value framing vs predictive-performance framing appropriate to projpred
expected_findings: Prefer showing the actual values/distributions over thresholding; compare ELPD to another plausible model rather than to the intercept-only null (loo::loo_compare won't directly do this cross-dataset comparison, so write custom ELPD-comparison code); use domain knowledge to judge what counts as good accuracy
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: All proposed checks are 'fine' but avoid p-values and good/bad thresholds; show actual predictive distributions; compare ELPD against a plausible alternative model, not the empty model; loo_compare ca

branches.if_observe: a plausible competing model exists
branches.then: compute ELPD difference vs it with custom code and report the distribution

branches.if_observe: only intercept-only available
branches.then: report predictive distributions and domain-judged accuracy rather than a p-value

action: Locate the missing-variable requirement as a brms/newdata quirk rather than a projpred logic error
discriminates: projpred actually using unselected terms (would be alarming) vs a brms newdata-formula artifact requiring all original predictors present
expected_findings: Only the selected solution_terms are used in the projection; the error is a brms specialty. Workaround: fill unselected predictors in newdata with a dummy value (e.g. 0 for numeric) — results are unaffected since only selected terms enter
situations: High-dimensional Gaussian regression in brms (~670 rows, ~155 scaled predictors) using a regularized horseshoe prior, horseshoe(par_ratio=0.1), on the coefficie
leaves: Set the unselected predictors in newdata to a dummy value (0 for numeric); this is a brms newdata requirement, later fixed upstream in brms PR #1459. Multiple imputation is not natively supported: pro

branches.if_observe: error persists after dummy-filling
branches.then: check predictor names match the model formula exactly

action: Inspect the functional relationship the user imposed between mu, lambda and z; check dimensional/temporal consistency
discriminates: H1 (just needs ordering) vs H2 (the mu = pinv(lambda'·lambda)·lambda'·z construction is structurally backwards)
expected_findings: If H2: mu is declared time-invariant yet is being solved from time-varying lambda and z — inconsistent; the natural direction is to write lambda as a function of mu and z, not mu from lambda. The indicator/STEP hack is a symptom of fighting a mis-specified parameterization.
situations: A 2-component Gaussian mixture with time-varying mixing weights (lambda_t) constrained so the mixture mean equals a smooth spline z_t = B·theta. User wants to e
leaves: Betancourt/stijn principle: solve the constraint in the well-posed direction. lambda_t = (mu2 - z_t)/(mu2 - mu1), which automatically lies in (0,1) iff mu1 < z_t < mu2 and mu2>mu1. Then ordering is en

branches.if_observe: mu intended time-invariant but derived from time-varying quantities
branches.then: Reparameterize: make lambda deterministic (transformed param) from mu and z; drop the stochastic eta/softmax layer

action: Trace how the lambda-in-(0,1) requirement propagates back onto the spline coefficients theta
discriminates: whether the new parameterization is feasible at init (H3) — does a constraint `lower=mu[1], upper=mu[2]` on z translate to a satisfiable region for theta?
expected_findings: Init failure: 'y_mean[1] is -1.026 but must be >= 0.469' — declaring z with bounds mu[1],mu[2] while z is the deterministic image B·theta means an arbitrary theta draw violates the bound; the constraint cannot be imposed by simply declaring z bounded because theta is the free parameter, not z.
situations: A 2-component Gaussian mixture with time-varying mixing weights (lambda_t) constrained so the mixture mean equals a smooth spline z_t = B·theta. User wants to e
leaves: Constraint must be rewritten in terms of theta (mu1 < B·theta < mu2 elementwise), which is NOT achievable by inverting B; this is the hard part. Confirms the reformulation shifted the difficulty rathe

branches.if_observe: log-prob fails at initial value on the z bound
branches.then: Recognize the constraint lives on theta, not z; either reparameterize theta into the feasible polytope or step back to question the model

action: Reframe from 'fix the constraint' to diagnosing the global posterior geometry of an exchangeable mixture that also fits variances
discriminates: H1 (small residual label-switch to be cleaned up) vs H4 (deep non-identifiability: modes joined by valleys with mode-collapse)
expected_findings: Multiple chains disagreeing — chain1 mu=(5,10), chain2 swapped (10,5), chain3 mu[1]~-500 (collapsed/runaway) — is the signature of H4, not a nuisance. The erratic chain is information: it shows the sampler reaching parts of the posterior (valleys/collapsed modes) the 'good' chains miss. 'Stan just makes the poor identifiability easier to see.'
situations: A 2-component Gaussian mixture with time-varying mixing weights (lambda_t) constrained so the mixture mean equals a smooth spline z_t = B·theta. User wants to e
leaves: Betancourt: exchangeable mixtures with free component variances are incredibly poorly identified — components collapse onto each other or one component expands to explain multiple clusters, all equall

branches.if_observe: one chain erratic, others 'nice'
branches.then: Do NOT discard/restart the bad chain; read it as evidence the posterior has unexplored valleys/collapse modes — the model, not the sampler, is the problem

branches.if_observe: means swap between chains only
branches.then: pure label switching — orderable; but check variances before concluding it's only that

action: Decompose the posterior topology and set the bar for 'good': absence of divergences is necessary, not sufficient
discriminates: whether non-divergent + bimodal marginals means the fit is trustworthy (H1-resolved) vs still-unidentified relative mode weights (H4 persists)
expected_findings: Even a clean run cannot tell you the relative importance/weight of the two modes — exactly the physically meaningful quantity for the aerosol application. Bimodal marginals look fine but mode-weight is non-identified.
situations: A 2-component Gaussian mixture with time-varying mixing weights (lambda_t) constrained so the mixture mean equals a smooth spline z_t = B·theta. User wants to e
leaves: The model is at best useful for exploratory configuration (LDA-like), not for full Bayesian inference where relative mode importance matters. 'No divergences' ≠ 'well-identified'.

branches.if_observe: marginals bimodal, no divergences, but goal needs mode weights
branches.then: Insufficient — need a generative, exchangeability-breaking model, not just a clean sampler

action: Pivot to generative model-building: break exchangeability with domain-informed priors and build up from the simplest sub-model
discriminates: H6 (informative non-overlapping priors identify it) and H5 (missing spline identifying constraint) — which interventions actually remove divergences and stabilize chains
expected_findings: (a) Informative priors that place the two components in non-overlapping mean/variance ranges (e.g. characteristic particle scales) break exchangeability and make the model fit well — same trick that works in similar physical applications. (b) Re-introducing the sum-to-zero constraint on the B-spline coefficients beta (an identifying constraint for the additive spline level, which the user had dropped) removes ALL divergent transitions in a 4-chain x 5000-iter run.
situations: A 2-component Gaussian mixture with time-varying mixing weights (lambda_t) constrained so the mixture mean equals a smooth spline z_t = B·theta. User wants to e
leaves: Two complementary identifying moves: (1) generative non-overlapping component priors (break label exchangeability via the likelihood/prior, not via order-stats or rejection); (2) sum-to-zero on spline

branches.if_observe: sum-to-zero on beta re-added -> divergences vanish
branches.then: additive non-identifiability of spline level was a real contributor; keep it, but verify mode-weight still under-determined

branches.if_observe: components given non-overlapping informative priors
branches.then: exchangeability broken at the generative level; model becomes well-posed for the physical interpretation

branches.if_observe: physical interpretation needs mode importances
branches.then: prefer a generative model that doesn't exhibit multimodality at all over patching an exchangeable mixture

action: Reframe the question: explain that variance attribution is not a fixed-pie zero-sum allocation -- adding predictive covariates automatically pulls variance off the intercepts.
discriminates: H1 (fixed-pie misconception) vs the analyst's stated plan; establishes that the fix is to ADD covariates, not REMOVE the grouping.
expected_findings: If H1 is the issue, the analyst's worry dissolves once they see the mechanism; the high intercept SD is expected behavior of an intercept-only model, not pathology.
situations: A multilevel (brms) model on country-year panel data with only random intercepts ((1|country)+(1|year)) and a censored response yields very high R2 (~0.94) domi
leaves: Tell the analyst NOT to drop (1|country). Mechanism: if you add covariates that explain between-country variation, the apparent between-country SD will shrink on its own while total explanatory streng

branches.if_observe: analyst still confused about WHY the model reattributes
branches.then: give the likelihood-geometry argument (next move)

branches.if_observe: analyst's covariates are country-constant
branches.then: note one x per group reduces to a regression on the group means -- still works, intercept SD shrinks

action: Invoke likelihood geometry: a tall-skinny residual normal has higher density than a short-wide one, so the model minimizes residual SD whenever covariates can absorb the variation.
discriminates: Confirms H1 is a misconception and supplies the generative reason; rules out the idea that intercepts 'block' covariates.
expected_findings: The (1|A) intercept SD behaves exactly like a residual term -- it absorbs everything only in the absence of predictors and yields automatically when predictors are added.
situations: A multilevel (brms) model on country-year panel data with only random intercepts ((1|country)+(1|year)) and a censored response yields very high R2 (~0.94) domi
leaves: Analogy to plain Gaussian regression: with no predictors the residual 'explains' 100% of variance; adding predictive x makes the model prefer attributing variation to bx and shrinking sigma_residual,

action: Probe the justification for the grouping: is the country effect about correlated residuals, or just an omitted effect? Compare random-effects vs fixed-effects vs skeptical-prior fixed effects.
discriminates: H2 (clustering/non-independence) vs H6 (fixed-vs-random is a modeling choice, not a correctness requirement).
expected_findings: Either framing fits the data similarly here; both random and fixed country effects represent the by-country 2012 starting level. The grouping should stay in; only its parameterization is a judgment call.
situations: A multilevel (brms) model on country-year panel data with only random intercepts ((1|country)+(1|year)) and a censored response yields very high R2 (~0.94) domi
leaves: Debate surfaces: (a) design-driven view -- use the most complex random-effects structure justifiable, because repeated country observations are not independent. (b) counter-view (Harrell): the within-

branches.if_observe: 178 country levels, sparse per level
branches.then: favor partial pooling or skeptical-prior fixed effects to avoid noisy per-country estimates

branches.if_observe: countries are themselves of inferential interest and few
branches.then: fixed effects are defensible

action: Sanity-check the censoring claim by INSPECTING the raw y values per country rather than trusting the cens label; also flag the R2-on-censored-data caveat.
discriminates: H4 (mislabeled-missing vs true censoring) and H3 (R2 artifact) vs the assumption that the high R2 reflects real explained structure.
expected_findings: If H4 holds, a constant-value block is visually obvious in a per-country y plot; those rows artificially inflate between-country structure and the censored-likelihood is mis-applied.
situations: A multilevel (brms) model on country-year panel data with only random intercepts ((1|country)+(1|year)) and a censored response yields very high R2 (~0.94) domi
leaves: Direct data inspection reveals 17 small countries pinned at a flat y=-1.26 for ALL years -- a constant sentinel value. This pattern is the signature of MISSING data imputed with a default (then standa

branches.if_observe: flat constant block confirmed
branches.then: treat as missing -- exclude or model missingness explicitly; do not treat as cens

branches.if_observe: values truly clustered at a known detection limit
branches.then: censoring is legitimate; keep cens() but distrust bayes_R2

action: Plot y over time by country/continent to locate the structure that intercepts CANNOT capture, and aim covariates there.
discriminates: H5 (real signal is dynamic/temporal heterogeneity) vs the framing that covariates should reduce the static intercept variance.
expected_findings: High R2 comes mostly from country intercept + shared dip shape; what is left for covariates is the recovery-trajectory heterogeneity, not the baseline level.
situations: A multilevel (brms) model on country-year panel data with only random intercepts ((1|country)+(1|year)) and a censored response yields very high R2 (~0.94) domi
leaves: By-country/by-continent line plot shows: (i) a level difference (African countries on average lower) -- already captured by either fixed or random intercepts; (ii) a 2013 dip with most countries stabi

branches.if_observe: recovery heterogeneity dominates residual
branches.then: consider time-varying covariates, random slopes on year, or a dynamic/AR specification, not just more level predictors
